On a recent holistic concept-unlearning benchmark covering ten concepts across artistic style, intellectual property, celebrity, and NSFW categories, PURE significantly reduces target leakage under paraphrased and adversarial prompts while preserving retain concepts close to the unedited model, yielding the best overall forget-retain trade-off among evaluated methods.

</details>


### [211] [SAFE-Diff: Scale-Aware Attention and Feature-Dispersive Diffusion with Uncertainty Estimation for Contrast-Enhanced Breast MRI Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25767)
*Tianyu Zhang,Xinglong Liang,Jarek van Dijk,Luyi Han,Chunyao Lu,Antonio Portaluri,Xinghe Xie,Yaofei Duan,Nika Rasoolzadeh,Xin Wang,Yuan Gao,Muzhen He,Yue Sun,Jonas Teuwen,Tao Tan,Ritse Mann*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of synthesizing high-fidelity contrast-enhanced MRI for breast cancer screening, focusing on complex lesion textures and heterogeneous enhancement patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safer and more efficient breast cancer screening by synthesizing high-fidelity contrast-enhanced MRI, avoiding the risks and costs associated with actual contrast agent administration.

Method: The paper proposes a method to synthesize contrast-enhanced MRI that handles complex lesion textures and heterogeneous enhancement patterns, though specific technical details are not provided in the abstract.

Result: The abstract does not provide specific results; it only states the clinical value and ongoing challenges of the synthesis task.

Conclusion: Synthesizing high-fidelity contrast-enhanced MRI is clinically valuable but remains challenging due to complex lesion textures and heterogeneous enhancement patterns.

Abstract: Synthesizing high fidelity contrast enhanced MRI is clinically valuable for safer and more efficient breast cancer screening, yet remains challenging due to complex lesion textures and heterogeneous enhancement patterns.

</details>


### [212] [DRFusion: Drift-Resilient Temporally Consistent Infrared-Visible Video Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25775)
*Xingyuan Li,Haoyuan Xu,Shulin Li,Xiang Chen,Zhiying Jiang,Jinyuan Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A drift-resilient infrared-visible video fusion method using history-conditioned motion generation, Stabilized History Guidance, Soft Temporal Anchoring, and Decoupled Structure-Motion Adaptation to address temporal inconsistency and error accumulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Infrared and visible video fusion suffers from temporal inconsistency, ghosting artifacts, and error accumulation in autoregressive diffusion models, limiting comprehensive dynamic scene perception.

Method: Proposes (1) Stabilized History Guidance and Soft Temporal Anchoring for spectral-filtering-based temporal consistency, (2) Decoupled Structure-Motion Adaptation with two-stage training and latent refinement to bridge pre-trained priors and structural constraints.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in both fusion quality and temporal stability.

Conclusion: Reformulating video fusion as history-conditioned motion generation with spectral filtering and decoupled adaptation effectively resolves drift and temporal inconsistency issues.

Abstract: Infrared and visible video fusion is essential for achieving comprehensive perception in dynamic scenes. However, maintaining temporal consistency remains a formidable challenge. Conventional methods relying on optical flow often suffer from geometric rigidity and ghosting artifacts. Moreover, standard diffusion-based fusion models typically operate in a frame-by-frame manner; when extended to autoregressive settings, they lack intrinsic temporal constraints and are prone to severe error accumulation and drifting, where minor artifacts amplify over time. To address these limitations, we propose a drift-resilient video fusion method that reformulates the task as history-conditioned motion generation. We introduce Stabilized History Guidance and Soft Temporal Anchoring to reframe temporal consistency as spectral filtering, implicitly aggregating motion dynamics without rigid alignment. Furthermore, our Decoupled Structure-Motion Adaptation strategy bridges pre-trained priors and structural constraints via two-stage training and latent refinement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in both fusion quality and temporal stability.

</details>


### [213] [OMGTex: One-stage Multi-style Facial Texture Reconstruction without Geometry Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25778)
*Zitong Xiao,Yuda Qiu,Zisheng Ye,Xiaoguang Han*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OMGTex is an end-to-end diffusion-based framework that reconstructs high-quality, editable facial UV textures from multi-style images without relying on 3D geometry priors, using gradient-guided refinement and semantic-aware diffusion training, supported by a new multi-style dataset CANVAS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing texture reconstruction methods suffer from (1) fragility due to reliance on inaccurate 3D geometry priors, especially under occlusions or stylized domains, and (2) lack of semantic disentanglement, preventing region-specific editing and style transfer.

Method: Proposes a geometry-free pipeline directly mapping 2D face images to editable UV textures via diffusion. Key techniques: (1) gradient-guided refinement at inference to correct UV misalignment, (2) a novel training paradigm leveraging semantic distribution of diffusion models for semantic-aware editing, and (3) construction of CANVAS dataset for multi-style texture reconstruction.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple facial texture benchmarks, enabling robust, style-consistent, and editable texture reconstruction across diverse domains.

Conclusion: OMGTex is the first geometry-free inference framework for facial texture reconstruction that simultaneously achieves robustness, style consistency, and editability across diverse domains, with strong benchmark performance.

Abstract: We propose OMGTex, an end-to-end diffusion-based framework for reconstructing high-quality and editable facial UV textures from multi-style facial images. Existing texture reconstruction methods face two major limitations: (1) Fragility due to reliance on 3D geometry priors, which are difficult to estimate accurately, especially under facial occlusions or in stylized domains; and (2) A lack of semantic disentanglement, inhibiting region-specific texture editing and style transfer. Our work addresses both challenges simultaneously.
  Our core innovation is a geometry-free pipeline that directly maps a 2D face image to its corresponding editable UV texture. We introduce two key techniques: First, to address the challenge of UV misalignment common in diffusion generation, we introduce a gradient-guided refinement strategy at inference time, which explicitly corrects structural consistency. Second, we leverage the inherent semantic distribution capability of diffusion models and design a novel training paradigm to enhance this tendency, enabling semantic-aware editing of facial texture. Furthermore, to address the data scarcity in multi-style texture reconstruction, we construct CANVAS, the first comprehensive paired texture reconstruction dataset covering realistic and diverse stylized domains.
  To the best of our knowledge, OMGTex is the first geometry-free inference framework that achieves robust, style-consistent, and editable facial texture reconstruction across diverse domains. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple facial texture benchmarks.

</details>


### [214] [VertiCue-Bench: Diagnosing Whether MLLMs Use Height Cues to Resolve 2D Ambiguity in Remote Sensing Natural Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25784)
*Jing Huang,Duanchu Wang,Junjie Yang,Zihang Cheng,Cheng Li,Lin Cui,Zhouyi Wu,Di Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VertiCue-Bench is a new benchmark testing whether MLLMs can use 3D canopy height data (CHMs) to resolve ambiguities in remote sensing. Results show models can read height cues but fail to integrate them into semantic reasoning, revealing a geometry-to-semantics gap.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing remote sensing benchmarks are 2D-centric and fail in natural environments due to spectral confusion, where different ecological regions look similar optically but differ in vertical structure. The authors want to test if current MLLMs actually leverage 3D structural data (CHMs) for semantic disambiguation.

Method: The authors introduce VertiCue-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark with 1,534 instances across 17 tasks, designed to disentangle low-level height perception from ambiguity-aware semantic reasoning. They evaluate 14 MLLMs (general and remote-sensing-specialized) using counterfactual modality testing.

Result: MLLMs show emerging competence in reading raw CHM height cues but largely fail to translate geometric perception into reliable semantic reasoning. They often underperform RGB-only baselines when joint constraints are required, revealing a perception-reasoning dissociation.

Conclusion: VertiCue-Bench exposes a critical geometry-to-semantics gap in natural scene understanding for MLLMs, providing actionable insights for advancing geospatial MLLMs.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently shown promising progress in geospatial reasoning. However, existing remote sensing benchmarks remain largely 2D-centric, evaluating models primarily on optical appearance. In natural environments, this paradigm breaks down due to severe spectral confusion, where ecologically distinct regions share similar textures but differ fundamentally in vertical structure. In such cases, explicit 3D structural data, such as Canopy Height Models (CHMs), become essential geometric evidence for semantic disambiguation. Yet, it remains unclear whether current MLLMs can genuinely leverage vertical cues to resolve appearance-level ambiguity. To address this gap, we introduce VertiCue-Bench, the first diagnostic benchmark for CHM-grounded geospatial reasoning. VertiCue-Bench comprises 1,534 carefully curated instances across 17 tasks, explicitly disentangling low-level height perception from ambiguity-aware semantic reasoning. Evaluations on 14 state-of-the-art general and remote-sensing-specialized MLLMs, combined with counterfactual modality testing, reveal a striking perception-reasoning dissociation. While models exhibit emerging competence in reading raw CHM height cues, they largely fail to translate geometric perception into reliable semantic reasoning, often underperforming RGB-only baselines when joint constraints are required. Overall, VertiCue-Bench exposes a critical geometry-to-semantics gap in natural scene understanding, offering actionable insights for advancing geospatial MLLMs.

</details>


### [215] [Addressing Exacerbated Attention Sink for Source-Free Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25799)
*Shuai Yi,Yixiong Zou,Yuhua Li,Ruixuan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper identifies that standard few-shot fine-tuning in Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CDFSL) exacerbates the attention sink problem in VLMs, where the model relies too heavily on "simple tokens" (those already close to target classes) while neglecting "hard tokens." The authors propose a token re-weighting method to dynamically balance token importance, achieving state-of-the-art results on four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior works overlooked the attention sink phenomenon in CDFSL scenarios. The authors discover that standard target-domain few-shot fine-tuning significantly worsens attention sink, harming class discriminability.

Method: The authors propose a dynamic token re-weighting approach that adjusts token importance based on their relevance to target-domain classes during fine-tuning, suppressing simple tokens and enhancing hard tokens to reduce sink tokens.

Result: The method achieves new state-of-the-art performance on four benchmark datasets, validating the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

Conclusion: The attention sink problem in CDFSL is a form of shortcut learning for domain adaptation. The proposed token re-weighting effectively mitigates this issue, improving model discriminability across domains.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP have shown impressive generalization capabilities, yet their potential for Cross-Domain Few-Shot Learning (CDFSL) remains underexplored, where the model needs to transfer source-domain information to target domains with scarce training data. While the attention sink phenomenon has been observed in VLMs for certain tasks, its role in CDFSL scenarios has not been studied. In this paper, we uncover a critical issue overlooked by prior works: standard target-domain few-shot fine-tuning in CDFSL significantly exacerbates the attention sink problem, leading to poor discriminability across classes. To understand this phenomenon, through extensive experiments, we interpret it as the model's shortcut learning for domain adaptation: to overcome the huge domain gap between the source and target domains, the model shows a high tendency to push tokens that are initially closer to target-domain classes (i.e., simple tokens) to be even closer to these classes, exacerbating the attention sink and wasting the capability of learning other discriminative but initially further tokens (i.e., hard tokens). To address this, we propose a novel approach to dynamically re-weight tokens according to their relevance with target-domain classes during the target-domain finetuning, which explicitly suppresses the model's reliance on these simple tokens and enhances the learning of hard tokens, reducing sink tokens and enhancing discriminability. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets validate the rationale of our method, demonstrating new state-of-the-art performance. Our codes are available at https://github.com/shuaiyi308/TIR.

</details>


### [216] [PixelWizard: Towards Efficient High-Fidelity Video Generation at Ultra-Large Spatial Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25801)
*Wenxue Li,Jingjing Ren,Peng Zhang,Tian Ye,Daiguo Zhou,Jian Luan,Lei Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PixelWizard is a framework for high-resolution video generation that hierarchically decouples global structure from fine details, using compact spatiotemporal anchors and Noise-Span Aligned Shortcut Training to achieve 10x faster 2K/4K video generation while maintaining structural stability and visual quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-resolution video generation suffers from optimization instability and prohibitive computational costs due to massive token expansion, which biases optimization toward local textures at the expense of global coherence, causing structural collapse and high training/inference latency.

Method: (1) Hierarchically decouples global structure modeling from fine-grained detail synthesis via a compact spatiotemporal anchor. (2) Noise-Span Aligned Shortcut Training (NSAST) to enable large-step traversal of generation trajectory. (3) Exponential Index-Biased Sampling and Adaptive Noise-Span Calibration to align optimization with shifted noise schedules of high-resolution grids.

Result: Superior visual quality with over 10x acceleration of generative sampling for native 2K/4K videos, without heavy distillation overhead.

Conclusion: PixelWizard effectively addresses the coupled bottleneck of optimization instability and high computational cost in high-resolution video generation, enabling practical and efficient high-resolution video synthesis.

Abstract: High-resolution video generation faces a coupled bottleneck of optimization instability and prohibitive computational costs. The massive expansion of the token sequence not only biases optimization toward local textures at the expense of global coherence, leading to structural collapse, but also imposes prohibitive training costs and severe inference latency. To address this, we propose PixelWizard, a framework that hierarchically decouples global structure modeling from fine-grained detail synthesis. PixelWizard first establishes a compact spatiotemporal anchor to concentrate dense structural priors, which then guides fine-grained generation at high resolution. This mitigates the local optimization bias to ensure structural stability without compromising high-frequency details. Leveraging this structural stability, we introduce Noise-Span Aligned Shortcut Training to break the inference bottleneck. By explicitly modeling the step size, this mechanism allows the model to traverse the generation trajectory with large steps. Crucially, we incorporate Exponential Index-Biased Sampling and Adaptive Noise-Span Calibration to align optimization with the shifted noise schedules of high-resolution grids, ensuring robust few-step inference without incurring the heavy overhead of distillation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PixelWizard achieves superior visual quality while accelerating the generative sampling of native 2K/4K videos by over 10x.

</details>


### [217] [Rethinking VLM Representation for VLA Initialization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25802)
*Weifeng Lin,Siyuan Huang,Hao Li,Tingwei Chen,Ruichuan An,Xinyu Wei,Jianbo Liu,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper investigates how to best initialize Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models from pretrained VLMs, finding that preserving the original VLM representation is crucial; embodied VQA benefits are conditional, LoRA outperforms full finetuning for initialization, and robot-data pretraining with staged LoRA yields the strongest results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models commonly use pretrained VLMs as backbones, but it is unclear what kind of VLM representation is actually useful for VLA initialization. The authors aim to systematically understand the design space of VLA initialization.

Method: The study formulates VLA initialization as a controlled representation-design problem along three axes: (1) capability-level embodied VQA supervision, (2) parameter-update strategy (LoRA vs. Full Finetune), and (3) robot-data pretraining. Comparative experiments are conducted across these dimensions.

Result: Original pretrained VLM representation is a key source of action performance. Embodied VQA gains are not uniform—they depend on downstream bottlenecks and are not additive across domains. LoRA provides more reliable initialization than Full Finetune. Robot-data pretraining further improves performance, with staged LoRA-based training being the strongest variant.

Conclusion: Effective VLM-to-VLA adaptation should inject action-relevant embodied and robot-trajectory signals while preserving the pretrained VLM representation that remains useful for action learning.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models widely adopt pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) as policy backbones, yet it remains unclear what kind of pretrained VLM representation is useful as a VLA initialization. In this paper, we study VLA initialization as a controlled representation-design problem along three axes: capability-level embodied VQA supervision, parameter-update strategy, and robot-data pretraining. Our experiments show that the original pretrained VLM representation is a key source of action performance. However, embodied VQA adaptation does not yield uniform gains: its benefit depends on downstream bottlenecks, and gains from different capability domains are not simply additive. For update strategy, LoRA provides a more reliable initialization than Full Finetune, indicating that overly reshaping the pretrained representation can weaken VLA initialization. Robot-data pretraining further improves VLA initialization, with the strongest variant obtained by staged LoRA-based training. Together, these findings suggest that effective VLM-to-VLA adaptation should inject action-relevant embodied and robot-trajectory signals while preserving the pretrained VLM representation that remains useful for action learning.

</details>


### [218] [ATV-Net: Adaptive Triple-View Network with Dynamic Feature Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25803)
*Hsin-Jui Pan,Sheng-Wei Chan,Meng-Qian Li,Chun-Po Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATV-Net improves ResNet-based semantic segmentation by using three complementary receptive-field views (micro, local, scout) with an adaptive decision gate, achieving 80.31% mIoU on Cityscapes without transformers or complex context modules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how far a classical ResNet-based segmentation model can be improved by modifying only the segmentation head, challenging the trend toward transformer-based architectures.

Method: ATV-Net uses three views: micro (point-wise), local (neighborhood/boundary), scout (enlarged context), fused via an Adaptive Decision Gate that dynamically selects receptive-field responses, plus a global coordination layer.

Result: 80.31% mIoU on Cityscapes validation set, demonstrating competitive accuracy with simpler CNN-based approach.

Conclusion: Classical CNN-based segmentation with simple receptive-field views and adaptive fusion remains competitive, showing transformer-style global attention is not strictly necessary for high accuracy.

Abstract: Recent semantic segmentation research has increasingly moved toward stronger context modeling, dense attention, and transformer-based architectures. Although these models achieve impressive performance, classical CNN-based segmentation pipelines remain attractive because of their simplicity, efficiency, and ease of implementation. This paper revisits a practical question: how far can a ResNet-based segmentation model be improved by only modifying the segmentation head? We propose ATV-Net, an Adaptive Triple-View Network that strengthens a ResNet-101 backbone using three simple but complementary receptive-field views. The micro view captures point-wise semantic responses, the local view models neighborhood structures and object boundaries, and the scout view provides enlarged contextual cues. Instead of fusing these views with fixed weights, ATV-Net introduces an Adaptive Decision Gate that dynamically selects receptive-field responses according to input scene characteristics. A compact global coordination layer is further applied to improve spatial and semantic consistency. Experiments on the Cityscapes validation set show that ATV-Net achieves 80.31\% mIoU. This result suggests that classical CNN-based segmentation is still far from obsolete: with simple receptive-field views and adaptive fusion, a ResNet-based pipeline can reach a competitive accuracy level without relying on transformer-style global attention or overly complex context modules.

</details>


### [219] [Event-to-Video Reconstruction using Spatio-Temporal and Frequency-Enhanced Deep Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25804)
*Ramna Maqsood,Paulo Nunes,Luís Ducla Soares,Caroline Conti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MSFET-E2V is a multiscale frequency-enhanced transformer model for event-to-video reconstruction that uses cross-domain attention combining spatio-temporal features with wavelet-based frequency representations, plus lightweight wavelet-enhanced skip blocks, achieving superior reconstruction quality with fewer parameters, less GPU memory, and faster inference than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event cameras lack dense intensity frames, making conventional computer vision methods difficult to apply directly. Existing E2V reconstruction methods (CNNs/transformers) operate mainly in the spatial domain and struggle to recover fine structural details while suppressing severe reconstruction artifacts.

Method: Proposes MSFET-E2V with: (1) a cross-domain attention module that fuses spatio-temporal features with frequency-aware representations from discrete wavelet transform (DWT), capturing both local and global structures via low- and high-frequency components; (2) a lightweight wavelet-enhanced skip block for artifact suppression and detail refinement through joint spatial-frequency domain processing.

Result: Achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on multiple real-world event datasets, with significant gains in reconstruction quality. Compared to existing transformer-based methods, it also reduces parameters, GPU memory usage, and inference time.

Conclusion: The proposed frequency-enhanced approach effectively addresses the limitations of spatial-only attention in E2V reconstruction, offering a more efficient and higher-quality solution for converting event streams to video frames.

Abstract: Event cameras offer significant advantages over conventional frame-based counterparts, including high temporal resolution, low latency, and energy efficiency. These characteristics make them suitable for high-speed and high-dynamic range scene acquisition scenarios; however, the lack of dense intensity frames limits the direct applicability of conventional computer vision methods for scene understanding. Event-to-video (E2V) reconstruction seeks to bridge this gap by converting asynchronous event streams into a sequence of synchronous video frames. Existing E2V reconstruction methods based on convolutional neural networks and transformers operate primarily in the spatial domain and often struggle to recover fine structural details while suppressing severe reconstruction artifacts. To address these issues, we propose MSFET-E2V, a novel multiscale frequency-enhanced transformer model. At its core lies a cross-domain attention module, which fuses spatio-temporal features with frequency-aware representations derived from the discrete wavelet transform. Unlike prior methods relying solely on spatial attention, our approach effectively captures both local and global structures by taking into account low- and high-frequency components, enhancing detail preservation and robustness across various motion scenarios. Furthermore, we propose a lightweight wavelet-enhanced skip block that serves as a skip connection, facilitating artifact suppression and structural detail refinement through joint spatial-frequency domain processing. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MSFET-E2V achieves superior performance over state-of-the-art methods on multiple real-world event datasets, offering significant gains in reconstruction quality. Moreover, compared to the existing transformer-based method, our proposed model significantly reduces the number of parameters, the GPU memory usage, and inference time.

</details>


### [220] [An Analysis Focused on Womens Safety: Can VAD Models Be Enhanced by a Multi-modal Dataset?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25806)
*Sangeeta,Maddikuntla Sai Prajwal,Debi Prosad Dogra,Kamalakar Vijay Thakare,Hyungjoo Jung,Ig-Jae Kim,Heeseung Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces ExtrAnom, a multi-modal video anomaly detection dataset focused on women-centric crimes (chain snatching, stalking, harassment, etc.), containing 1001 videos with textual descriptions, including low-light, low-resolution, and long-shot scenarios, and demonstrates that existing datasets are insufficient for detecting such anomalies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crimes against women are under-addressed in video anomaly detection research. Existing datasets lack women-centric anomalies (e.g., chain snatching, stalking), and contain only well-lit, high-resolution, close-shot videos, failing to represent real-world surveillance conditions.

Method: The authors propose ExtrAnom, a multi-modal dataset of 1001 videos (500 normal, 501 anomalous) with 5 women-centric crime types, covering diverse conditions (low-light 8%, low-resolution 13%, long-shot 15%, daylight 64%). Each video includes 4 textual descriptions (1 human, 3 LLM-generated). The dataset is benchmarked against SOTA methods and existing VAD datasets.

Result: Existing datasets (XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, UCA) are insufficient for training models to detect women-centric anomalies. ExtrAnom enables cross-modal and VLM-based validation for women's safety anomaly detection.

Conclusion: A women-centric anomaly detection dataset is needed for accurate detection of subtle and overt crimes against women. ExtrAnom fills this gap and supports multi-modal anomaly detection research.

Abstract: Women's safety and security are paramount for a modern society. Crimes against women occur in daylight as well as in low-light conditions. Often, such events are captured through real-world surveillance cameras that operate at lower resolutions. Despite substantial progress in CV-related research, video anomaly detection (VAD) focused on women's safety has not yet been adequately addressed. Existing video anomaly datasets contain well-lit, high-resolution, close-shot videos, and fail to represent women-centric anomalies such as chain snatching, stalking, inappropriate touch, and other subtle forms of crime against women. To address these problems, we propose the ExtrAnom dataset, a new multi-modal benchmark containing 1001 videos with textual descriptions, 500 normal and 501 anomalous, classified into 5 different types of women-centric crimes. The dataset comprises low-light (8%), low-resolution videos (13%), long-shot (15%), along with daylight (64%) anomalous videos. And it covers anomalous events like stalking (3.9%), chain snatching (17.6%), kidnapping (7.3%), assassinations (2.3%), harassment (18.9%), and normal (50%). Each video is supplemented with 4 textual annotations, including one human-generated and three LLM-generated descriptions, enabling cross-modal and VLM-based validations. The aim of creating a women-centric dataset is to accurately detect the women-centric anomaly patterns, which are possible to observe visually. The dataset supplements the VLMs to accurately generate video-level descriptions. ExtrAnom has been benchmarked against popular unimodal and multi-modal VAD datasets (e.g., XD-Violence, UCF-Crime, and UCA) and SOTA methods. Experiments reveal that the existing datasets are insufficient to train models for detecting women-centric anomalies.

</details>


### [221] [Data-driven Head Motion Generation through Natural Gaze-Head Coordination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25810)
*Xiaohan Liu,Yilin Wen,Yusuke Sugano*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: First data-driven approach for modeling temporal gaze-head coordination from large-scale in-the-wild facial videos, using a conditional VAE to generate realistic gaze-conditioned head motions, applied to facial video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To model temporal gaze-head coordination - a crucial non-verbal communication aspect that has not been emphasized in facial video generation, using a data-driven approach from natural in-the-wild videos.

Method: Automatic pipeline extracts gaze/head motions using off-the-shelf gaze estimators; generative conditional Variational Autoencoder (cVAE) captures probabilistic correlation and temporal dynamics of gaze-head coordination.

Result: Human evaluation and quantitative comparisons show statistically significant preference for the proposed method over baseline approaches, demonstrating effectiveness and natural head motion generation.

Conclusion: The first data-driven method effectively models gaze-head coordination, enabling more realistic facial video generation with natural head movements correlated to gaze input.

Abstract: We present the first data-driven approach to model temporal gaze-head coordination from large-scale in-the-wild facial videos. To obtain training data for generalizable learning, we propose an automatic pipeline that extracts natural yet diverse gaze and head motions with off-the-shelf appearance-based gaze estimators. To capture the probabilistic correlation and temporal dynamics of gaze-head coordination, we build our model on a generative conditional Variational Autoencoder for plausible yet diverse gaze-conditioned head motion generations. We further apply our framework to gaze-controlled facial video generation, where we enable video generation with natural and realistic head motion correlated to the input gaze - an aspect that has not been emphasized before. Human evaluation and quantitative comparisons demonstrate our method's effectiveness and validate our design choices, with evaluators showing statistically significant preference for our approach over baseline methods.

</details>


### [222] [[CLS] is Not Enough: Multi-Label Recognition via Patch-Level Inference and Adaptive Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25821)
*Akang Wang,Xili Deng,Zhanxuan Hu,Yi Zhao,Yonghang Tai,Huafeng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PIAA is a training-free multi-label image recognition framework that enhances CLIP by performing patch-level predictions with adaptive aggregation, achieving over 6% mAP improvement on NUS-WIDE without any gradient updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CLIP's [CLS] token as a single global representation cannot faithfully encode multiple objects with varying scales, contexts, and co-occurrence patterns in multi-label recognition.

Method: PIAA uses (1) Patch-level Inference with enhanced discriminability via semantic entanglement mitigation and unsupervised visual classifier learning, followed by (2) Adaptive Aggregation to consolidate patch scores into final predictions—all training-free.

Result: Over 6% mAP improvement on NUS-WIDE benchmark over representative baselines with minimal extra computation.

Conclusion: Patch-level inference with adaptive aggregation effectively addresses CLIP's limitations in multi-label recognition without requiring any fine-tuning or gradient updates.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models such as CLIP exhibit strong zero-shot recognition capability by aligning images with textual concepts, yet they often underperform on multi-label recognition where multiple objects co-exist. A key bottleneck is that the [CLS] token, as a single global visual representation, is insufficient to faithfully encode diverse targets with varying scales, contexts, and co-occurrence patterns. To address this limitation, we present a new multi-label image recognition framework, termed PIAA, which formulates prediction as Patch-level Inference followed by Adaptive Aggregation. Specifically, we first enhance patch-wise predictions from two complementary perspectives: (i) mitigating semantic entanglement in the visual encoder to obtain more discriminative patch representations, and (ii) learning an unsupervised visual classifier to narrow the vision-language modality gap. We then introduce an adaptive aggregation module that consolidates patch-level scores into the final multi-label prediction. Notably, the entire pipeline is fully training-free, requiring no gradient updates or parameter fine-tuning. Experiments show that our method achieves strong improvements with minimal extra computation, exceeding a 6% mAP gain on the challenging NUS-WIDE benchmark over representative baselines. Code is available at https://github.com/akang-wang/PIAA.

</details>


### [223] [SAM3-Assisted Training of Lightweight YOLO Models for Precision Pig Farming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25860)
*Marcos Vinicius Mendes Faria,Thiago Borges Pereira,Isabella C. F. S. Condotta,Thiago Meireles Paixão,Francisco de Assis Boldt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fully automated knowledge distillation pipeline using SAM 3 to generate zero-shot pseudo-labels for training YOLOv8 detectors, eliminating manual annotation. On the PigLife dataset, SAM 3-trained YOLOv8m achieves 79.4% mAP with 200× faster inference than the teacher model, and matches human performance in low-occlusion scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-performance foundation models (e.g., SAM 3) are too computationally heavy for edge deployment, while lightweight models (e.g., YOLO) require prohibitive manual annotation. This work aims to bridge this gap by leveraging foundation models as zero-annotation-cost supervisors.

Method: A fully automated knowledge distillation pipeline that uses SAM 3 as an offline auto-annotator to generate zero-shot pseudo-labels, which are then used to train efficient YOLOv8 detectors for real-time inference on edge devices.

Result: SAM 3-trained YOLOv8m achieves 79.4% mAP without human intervention, with ~200× inference speedup over SAM 3. In low-occlusion scenarios, AP50 exceeds 99%, matching human-annotated benchmarks.

Conclusion: Foundation models can serve as effective zero-annotation-cost supervisors, enabling scalable edge computing solutions for smart agriculture and Precision Livestock Farming.

Abstract: Deep learning-based object detection has revolutionized Precision Livestock Farming (PLF), yet a critical barrier remains: high-performance Foundation Models (such as SAM 3) are too computationally intensive for edge deployment, while lightweight models (like YOLO) require prohibitive manual annotation efforts. This work proposes a fully automated knowledge distillation pipeline that leverages the Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM 3) to generate zero-shot pseudo-labels for training efficient YOLOv8 detectors. By treating SAM 3 as an offline auto-annotator, we eliminate the manual labeling bottleneck, producing models capable of real-time inference on resource-constrained hardware. We systematically evaluate this approach on the PigLife dataset, comparing SAM 3-supervised models against human-annotated baselines. Results demonstrate that a SAM 3-trained YOLOv8m achieves a mean Average Precision (mAP) of 79.4% without human intervention, while reducing inference latency by approximately 200$\times$ compared to the teacher model. Furthermore, stratified analysis reveals that in low-occlusion scenarios, the automated pipeline achieves detection rates comparable to human benchmarks ($AP_{50} > 99\%$). These findings indicate that foundation models can serve as effective, zero-annotation-cost supervisors, enabling scalable edge computing solutions for smart agriculture.

</details>


### [224] [MuNet: A Mutualistic Network for Joint 3D Human Mesh Recovery and 3D Clothed Human Reconstruction from Single Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25861)
*Yunqi Gao,Leyuan Liu,Yuhan Li,Changxin Gao,Jingying Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MuNet is a unified framework that jointly optimizes 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction from single images using graph convolutional networks with a mutualistic mechanism, achieving state-of-the-art performance on six benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction are inherently related but have been studied in isolation, overlooking potential gains from joint optimization.

Method: (1) Uses 2-manifold graphs as a unified representation; (2) End-to-end graph convolutional network that progressively deforms an initial graph into a mesh and refines it into clothed human model; (3) Mutualistic mechanism enabling reciprocal interaction between the two tasks during training.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on both 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction across Human3.6M, 3DPW, MPI-INF-3DHP, THuman2.0, CAPE, and RenderPeople datasets.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling both tasks with mutual learning significantly improves performance on each individual task, validating the benefit of unified optimization.

Abstract: 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction are inherently related, yet they have long been studied in isolation, thereby overlooking the potential gains of joint optimization. To overcome this limitation, we propose to address these two tasks within a unified framework, which allows their mutual dependencies to be effectively exploited. Building on this idea, we propose MuNet, a mutualistic network for joint 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction from single images. First, we adopt 2-manifold graphs as a unified representation for all 3D models, enabling consistent modeling across 3D human mesh recovery and clothed human reconstruction. Second, we design an end-to-end graph convolutional network that progressively deforms an initial graph into a 3D human mesh and refines it into a detailed 3D clothed human model. Third, we introduce a mutualistic mechanism that allows reciprocal interaction between the two tasks {during training}, where 3D human mesh recovery provides guidance for 3D clothed human reconstruction, and reconstruction feedback refines the 3D human mesh recovery. We extensively evaluate MuNet on six benchmark datasets for 3D human mesh recovery and 3D clothed human reconstruction, including Human3.6M, 3DPW, MPI-INF-3DHP, THuman2.0, CAPE, and RenderPeople. Experimental results demonstrate that MuNet achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks across all datasets. The code of MuNet is released for research purposes at https://github.com/starVisionTeam/MuNet.

</details>


### [225] [WBench: A Comprehensive Multi-turn Benchmark for Interactive Video World Model Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25874)
*Kaining Ying,Hengrui Hu,Siyu Ren,Jiamu Li,Fengjiao Chen,Ziwen Wang,Xuezhi Cao,Xunliang Cai,Henghui Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: WBench is a comprehensive multi-turn benchmark for evaluating interactive world models across five dimensions (video quality, setting adherence, interaction adherence, consistency, physics compliance), containing 289 test cases with 1,058 interaction turns. It covers diverse scenes, styles, perspectives, and four interaction types. Evaluation of 20 models shows no single model excels across all dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for interactive world models cover only partial competencies with no unified standard for systematic evaluation, creating a gap that WBench aims to fill.

Method: WBench comprises 289 test cases and 1,058 interaction turns covering diverse scenes, styles, subjects, and perspectives. It supports four interaction types: navigation, subject action, event editing, and perspective switching. Navigation unifies text, 6-DoF pose, and discrete-action control. Evaluation uses 22 automatic sub-metrics combining specialist vision models with large multimodal models, validated against human judgments.

Result: Across 20 state-of-the-art models, no single model performs strongly across all five dimensions. The benchmark provides detailed diagnostic insights into characteristic strengths, weaknesses, and open challenges of each model.

Conclusion: WBench establishes a unified standard for systematic evaluation of interactive world models, revealing that current models still have significant room for improvement across multiple dimensions. The benchmark offers diagnostic insights to guide future research.

Abstract: Interactive world models are advancing rapidly, yet existing benchmarks cover only part of the required competencies, leaving no unified standard for systematic evaluation. To fill this gap, we introduce WBench, a comprehensive multi-turn benchmark for interactive world model evaluation along five dimensions, namely video quality, setting adherence, interaction adherence, consistency, and physics compliance. WBench contains 289 test cases and 1,058 interaction turns, where each case specifies a world setting and a multi-turn interaction sequence, covering diverse scenes, styles, subjects, and both first- and third-person perspectives, together with four interaction types, including navigation, subject action, event editing, and perspective switching. For navigation, WBench unifies text, 6-DoF pose, and discrete-action control, enabling evaluation of models with different native input interfaces. Evaluation uses 22 automatic sub-metrics that combine specialist vision models with large multimodal models, and all metrics are validated against human judgments. Across 20 state-of-the-art models, we find that no single model performs strongly across all dimensions. We provide detailed diagnostic insights into the characteristic strengths, weaknesses, and open challenges of each model. Code and data are available at https://github.com/meituan-longcat/WBench.

</details>


### [226] [DyCoRM: Dynamic Criterion-Aware Reward Modeling for Text-to-Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25876)
*Jiaying Qian,Ziheng Jia,Qian Zhang,Zicheng Zhang,Jiayi Guo,Junqi Zhang,Guangtao Zhai,Xiongkuo Min*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DyCoRM is a dynamic criterion-aware reward model for T2I generation that assesses images based on task-relevant, fine-grained user criteria rather than static dimensions, supported by a new dataset (DyCoDataset-20K), benchmark (DyCoBench-1K), and selection method (DyCoPick).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: User demands for T2I generation are shifting toward images that satisfy specific requirements, but existing reward models rely on static, general evaluation dimensions and fail to capture task-relevant, fine-grained criteria that users actually care about.

Method: Proposes DyCoRM, a reward model that grounds task-relevant dynamic criteria and performs criterion-aware preference comparison; constructs DyCoDataset-20K with dynamic criteria and criterion-level annotations; derives DyCoBench-1K for evaluation; introduces DyCoPick for applying criterion-aware reward modeling to image selection.

Result: Establishes the first reward modeling framework for dynamic and fine-grained evaluation in T2I generation, with benchmark and dataset contributions.

Conclusion: Dynamic, criterion-aware reward models are needed for better alignment with user-specific requirements in T2I generation, and DyCoRM provides a foundational framework for this direction.

Abstract: With the continued advancement of text-to-image (T2I) generation, producing high-quality images is becoming increasingly attainable; consequently, user demands are shifting toward images that better satisfy their specific requirements. As reward models play an increasingly important role in assessing whether generated images align with user preference, this trend introduces an important challenge for reward modeling: rather than relying solely on static and general evaluation dimensions, reward models should account for the task-relevant and fine-grained criteria through which users assess whether generated images meet their specific requirements. To address this challenge, we propose DyCoRM, a dynamic, criterion-aware reward model that grounds task-relevant criteria and performs criterion-aware preference comparison. To support this setting, we construct DyCoDataset-20K, which provides dynamic criteria together with criterion-level annotations, and further derive DyCoBench-1K, a benchmark for systematically evaluating reward models under dynamic criteria. We further introduce DyCoPick, which applies criterion-aware reward modeling to selecting T2I images. Our contributions establish the first reward modeling framework for dynamic and fine-grained evaluation and practical application in T2I generation.

</details>


### [227] [SP-MoMamba: Superpixel-driven Mixture of State Space Experts for Efficient Image Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25892)
*Wenbin Zou,Yawen Cui,Yi Wang,Lap-Pui Chau,Liang Chen,Jinshan Pan,Huiping Zhuang,Guanbin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SP-MoMamba introduces a superpixel-driven mixture of state space experts for content-aware super-resolution, replacing rigid scanning with semantic-level grouping to improve reconstruction quality and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Mamba-based SR methods use data-agnostic rigid scanning (fixed grid reshaping of 2D images into 1D sequences), which disrupts spatial-semantic topology and introduces artifacts.

Method: (1) Superpixel-driven State Space Model (SP-SSM) compresses semantically homogeneous regions into high-order tokens. (2) Multi-Scale Superpixel Mixture of State Space Experts (MSS-MoE) uses dynamic routing for scale-specific experts. (3) Local Spatial Modulation Expert (LSME) preserves high-frequency details during global abstraction.

Result: Superior reconstruction fidelity and better efficiency-performance trade-off compared to state-of-the-art efficient SR methods on standard benchmarks.

Conclusion: Transforming rigid scanning into semantic-level interaction via superpixels is effective for content-aware super-resolution, balancing performance and efficiency.

Abstract: State space models (SSMs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for efficient single-image super-resolution (SR) due to their linear complexity and long-range modeling capabilities. However, existing Mamba-based methods typically rely on data-agnostic rigid scanning, which reshapes 2D images into 1D sequences over a fixed grid, inevitably disrupting spatial-semantic topology and introducing artifacts. Inspired by the \textbf{Gestalt perceptual grouping theory}, we propose \textbf{SP-MoMamba}, a superpixel-driven mixture of state space experts designed for content-aware SR. Our core idea is to transform the traditional rigid scanning into a \textbf{semantic-level interaction} by treating superpixels as fundamental units. Specifically, we introduce the \textbf{Superpixel-driven State Space Model (SP-SSM)}, which compresses semantically homogeneous regions into high-order tokens to preserve global topological consistency. To address the conflict between fixed scanning scales and diverse semantic granularities, we develop the \textbf{Multi-Scale Superpixel Mixture of State Space Experts (MSS-MoE)}. This module utilizes a dynamic routing mechanism to adaptively assign scale-specific experts, effectively capturing multi-scale textures while reducing computational redundancy. Furthermore, to prevent the loss of high-frequency details during global abstraction, we introduce a \textbf{Local Spatial Modulation Expert (LSME)} to complement the global modeling, ensuring a precise reconstruction of sharp edges and fine structures. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that SP-MoMamba achieves superior reconstruction fidelity and a more favorable efficiency-performance trade-off compared to state-of-the-art efficient SR methods.

</details>


### [228] [R5DGS: Semantic-Aware 4D Gaussian Splatting with Rigid Body Constraints for Efficient Dynamic Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25909)
*Denis Gridusov,Maxim Popov,Sergey Kolyubin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: R5DGS is a physics-informed 4D Gaussian Splatting framework with compact Identity Encoding vectors for object-level semantic awareness, supporting open-vocabulary text queries and achieving 11 FPS speedup in extrapolation via centroid-level rigid-body dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing physics-informed Gaussian Splatting methods lack semantic awareness and suffer from high computational overhead, limiting their applicability in robotics, AR/VR, and digital twins.

Method: (1) Augments physics-driven 4D Gaussians with Identity Encoding vectors for Gaussian-to-object association. (2) Builds offline CLIP-based object lookup table for open-vocabulary text-prompted object retrieval. (3) Proposes rigid-body inference constraint predicting dynamics only for object centroids, propagating motion via relative transformations.

Result: Achieves an 11 FPS speedup during extrapolation while maintaining trajectory plausibility.

Conclusion: R5DGS enables efficient, semantically-aware dynamic scene reconstruction and extrapolation with improved computational efficiency.

Abstract: Reconstructing and predicting dynamic 3D scenes from multi-view videos is a foundational task for robotics, AR/VR, and digital twins. Recent physics-informed Gaussian Splatting methods achieve impressive future frame extrapolation but lack semantic awareness and suffer from large computational overhead. We introduce $\textbf{R5DGS}$, a framework that augments a physics-driven 4D Gaussian representation with compact Identity Encoding vectors, enabling precise Gaussian-to-object association. By constructing an offline CLIP-based object lookup table, we support open-vocabulary text prompting to retrieve and render object-specific Gaussians across arbitrary timestamps and viewpoints. Furthermore, we propose a rigid-body inference constraint that predicts and integrates physical dynamics exclusively for object centroids, propagating motion to associated Gaussians via relative transformations. This optimization yields a 11 FPS speedup during extrapolation without compromising trajectories plausibility.

</details>


### [229] [Closed-Loop Bidirectional Prompting for Adversarial Robustness of Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25922)
*Xiao Liu,Jiaxiang Liu,Boci Peng,Boren Hu,Yusong Wang,Xiwen Chen,Prayag Tiwari,Liming Zhang,Mingkun Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Closed-Loop Bidirectional Prompting (CBP) with Semantic Anchors to defend VLMs against adversarial perturbations by cyclically denoising visual features with textual semantics and adaptively updating prompts, achieving SOTA robustness on 11 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial defenses for VLMs are unidirectional or static, failing to exploit bidirectional cross-modal complementarity and instance-adaptive protection, leaving them vulnerable to perturbations that disrupt cross-modal alignment.

Method: Closed-Loop Bidirectional Prompting: uses a Semantic Anchor as stable prior, bootstraps textual semantics to denoise visual representations, then uses refined visuals for instance-adaptive prompt updates, forming a dynamic feedback loop on frozen encoders.

Result: Evaluated on 11 datasets, achieves state-of-the-art robustness and strong base-to-new generalization with favorable computation-accuracy trade-off.

Conclusion: Bidirectional cross-modal feedback with semantic anchoring effectively rectifies adversarial feature corruption, offering a practical and robust adaptation paradigm for frozen VLMs.

Abstract: Vision Language Models adapt well to downstream tasks but are highly vulnerable to adversarial perturbations that disrupt cross-modal semantic alignment. Existing defenses are largely unidirectional or structural, failing to exploit bidirectional cross-modal complementarity and instance-wise adaptive protection. To overcome the limitations of unidirectional and static defenses in adversarial settings, we propose Closed-Loop Bidirectional Prompting, casting robust adaptation as cross-modal agreement recovery via a dynamic feedback loop on frozen encoders. A Semantic Anchor is introduced as a stable prior to constrain cyclic updates and mitigate perturbation-induced feature corruption. Through anchor-based bootstrapping, textual semantics denoise visual representations, while the refined visuals enable instance-adaptive prompt updating, yielding a rectified and robust consensus. Extensive evaluations across 11 datasets validate state-of-the-art robustness and strong base-to-new generalization, while maintaining a favorable trade-off between computational cost and accuracy.

</details>


### [230] [Where Concept Erasure Should Occur: Concept-Layer Alignment in Text-to-Video Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25941)
*Yiwei Xie,Ping Liu,Zheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CLEAR is a framework that identifies layers in diffusion transformers where concepts are naturally separated from non-targets, then explicitly enforces concept-layer alignment for more precise concept erasure in text-to-video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Text-to-video diffusion transformers encode semantic information unevenly across model depth, making effective concept erasure difficult because outside certain depths, concept and non-target signals remain strongly entangled.

Method: CLEAR formulates layer selection as an optimization problem over concept-non-target separability, introducing a separability-aware objective to favor layers with stronger concept-non-target separation.

Result: Experiments on large-scale text-to-video diffusion models show that enforcing concept-layer alignment leads to more precise concept suppression while preserving generative quality.

Conclusion: Concept erasure can be reframed as identifying representational depths where concept-non-target separation naturally emerges, and CLEAR effectively operationalizes this principle.

Abstract: Text-to-video diffusion transformers encode semantic information unevenly across model depth, which constrains effective concept erasure. We identify a representational bottleneck, termed concept-layer topological alignment, under which target concepts exhibit higher separability at certain representational depths. Outside these depths, concept and non-target signals remain strongly entangled, limiting the effectiveness of depth-specific erasure. This observation reframes concept erasure as the problem of identifying representational depths where concept-non-target separation naturally emerges. Motivated by this structural constraint, we introduce CLEAR, a separability-driven optimization framework for concept erasure that explicitly enforces concept-layer alignment. CLEAR operationalizes this principle by formulating layer selection as an optimization problem over concept-non-target separability, rather than relying on layer-agnostic or heuristic choices. To enable this, we introduce a separability-aware objective that favors layers exhibiting stronger concept-non-target separation. Experiments on large-scale text-to-video diffusion models demonstrate that enforcing concept--layer alignment leads to more precise concept suppression while preserving overall generative quality.

</details>


### [231] [EchoPilot: Training-Free Ultrasound Video Segmentation via Scale-Space Semantic Prompting and Reliability-Gated Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25944)
*Ruiqiang Xiao,Zhaohu Xing,Yijun Yang,Zhenyan Han,Weiming Wang,Kaishun Wu,Lei Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EchoPilot is a training-free framework for ultrasound video segmentation using a single first-frame point click and anatomical category name. It combines a frozen VLM, VFM, and promptable video segmentor with two novel techniques—Scale-Space Semantic Prompting (optimal view selection via S.E.E.D. criterion + auxiliary point prompts) and Reliability-Gated Memory update (selective memory freeze to prevent drift)—achieving SOTA on three ultrasound datasets including a new placenta dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing promptable foundation models fail on ultrasound video segmentation due to: (1) insufficient spatial context from a single point causing scale ambiguity, and (2) greedy memory updates amplifying early errors into temporal drift.

Method: EchoPilot combines a frozen VLM (semantic localization), VFM (dense geometric features), and promptable video segmentor. It introduces: (1) Scale-Space Semantic Prompting with S.E.E.D. criterion for optimal view selection and auxiliary point generation, and (2) Reliability-Gated Memory update that selectively freezes the memory bank under uncertain predictions.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across three ultrasound video datasets (including a new fetal placenta dataset with 671 annotated frames), consistently outperforming training-free baselines and finetuned specialists under the sparse-interactive setting.

Conclusion: A training-free, single-point-interaction approach can achieve reliable ultrasound video segmentation by combining VLM+VFM guidance with smart prompt selection and error-gated memory management.

Abstract: Ultrasound video segmentation is clinically valuable yet difficult due to speckle noise, weak boundaries, and rapid anatomical deformation. Recent promptable foundation models enable point-guided segmentation, but their direct deployment in ultrasound remains unreliable: a single point provides insufficient spatial context to resolve scale ambiguity, and greedy memory updates amplify early errors into severe temporal drift. We present EchoPilot, a training-free framework for ultrasound video segmentation under sparse first-frame interaction, requiring only a single point click and an anatomical category name. EchoPilot orchestrates a frozen medical vision-language model (VLM) for semantic localization, a vision foundation model (VFM) for dense geometric feature extraction, and a promptable video segmentor for mask prediction and propagation. To resolve initialization ambiguity, we propose Scale-Space Semantic Prompting, which first selects an optimal contextual view via a parameter-free S.E.E.D. (Semantic Energy-Entropy Density) criterion, and then synthesizes geometrically precise auxiliary point prompts from dense foundation features without additional user interaction. To reduce propagation drift, a Reliability-Gated Memory update is further introduced to selectively freeze the segmentor's memory bank under uncertain predictions, preventing error accumulation. We also contribute the first dynamic fetal placenta ultrasound video segmentation dataset with 671 annotated frames. Across three ultrasound video datasets, EchoPilot achieves state-of-the-art performance under the sparse-interactive setting, consistently outperforming training-free baselines and finetuned specialists.

</details>


### [232] [A Pedestrian-Vehicle Interaction Benchmark and Annotation Framework for Unstructured Scenes via Uncalibrated Cameras](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25947)
*Haoyang Peng,Qian Hu,Songan Zhang,Ming Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces PINNS, a dataset annotation framework and dataset for pedestrian-vehicle interaction prediction in unstructured scenes, using uncalibrated surveillance camera videos. It covers diverse regions, scenarios, and conditions, providing trajectory and scene-level data to address the lack of public datasets for dense, heterogeneous interactions in unstructured environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing datasets for pedestrian-vehicle interaction prediction are mostly based on structured road data, failing to represent dense, heterogeneous interactions in unstructured and semi-structured environments. This scarcity hinders research and autonomous driving safety in complex mixed traffic scenarios.

Method: The authors propose a dataset annotation framework using video data from uncalibrated surveillance cameras. The PINNS dataset covers multiple countries/regions, diverse traffic scenarios, and varying seasons, lighting, and weather conditions. It focuses on dense pedestrian-vehicle interactions and is annotated per Chinese Association of Automation standards, including trajectory and scene-level information.

Result: A publicly available dataset (PINNS) designed for complex, unstructured scenes with dense pedestrian-vehicle interactions, featuring extensibility, diverse conditions, and standardized annotations. The paper also analyzes current challenges in heterogeneous agent trajectory prediction, demonstrating the dataset's necessity.

Conclusion: The proposed framework and dataset are expected to advance trajectory prediction and autonomous driving research in complex mixed traffic scenarios. PINNS is publicly released to facilitate further work.

Abstract: Predicting the interaction between pedestrian and vehicle is essential for autonomous driving safety in unstructured and semi-structured scenarios; however, this task is severely hindered by the scarcity of public datasets that feature dense pedestrian-vehicle interactions. Most current studies rely on structured road data, leaving the complex, heterogeneous interactions found in unstructured environments insufficiently represented and researched. In this paper, we propose a dataset annotation framework based on video data from uncalibrated surveillance cameras and present PINNS (Pedestrian-vehicle Interaction dataset from uNcalibrated cameras in uNstructured Scenes). The dataset covers multiple countries and regions, includes diverse typical traffic scenarios, and considers variations in seasons, lighting conditions, and weather. It focuses on complex scenes with dense pedestrian-vehicle interactions and is designed to be easily extensible. The dataset is constructed and annotated according to the standard issued by the Chinese Association of Automation, providing both trajectory data and corresponding scene-level information. Furthermore, this paper analyzes current challenges and research directions in heterogeneous agent trajectory prediction, shows the necessity and usefulness of the proposed dataset. We hope our framework and dataset will facilitate research on trajectory prediction and autonomous driving in complex mixed traffic scenarios. PINNS is publicly available at https://github.com/Songan-Lab.

</details>


### [233] [VEN-VL: A Visual Ensemble MoE Framework for Effective and Efficient Multi-Modal Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25952)
*Yinghao Wu,Zhuoyan Luo,Yiyao Yu,Zhaojian Yu,Yujiu Yang,Xiao-Ping Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VEN-VL uses a visual ensemble MoE framework with an "enrich then compact" strategy to improve multimodal understanding by unifying visual representations from different perspectives, progressively compacting them with adaptive routers, and incorporating reconstruction supervision, achieving strong performance with few condensed tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing efficient multimodal methods suffer performance degradation due to high compression ratios of single visual clues and reliance on heuristic pruning with coarse attention alignment, creating bottlenecks in information capacity and density.

Method: VEN-VL follows an "enrich then compact" principle: (1) enrich information capacity by unifying visual representations from different perspectives, (2) progressively compact tokens using adaptive routers in specialized visual experts (MoE), (3) incorporate reconstruction supervision to preserve crucial information.

Result: The method demonstrates superiority in complex visual tasks using few information-condensed tokens, effectively bridging the gap between performance and efficiency.

Conclusion: VEN-VL effectively addresses the performance-efficiency trade-off in multimodal understanding by enriching visual information before compacting it, showing that careful information management with ensemble MoE can maintain high performance even with highly condensed token representations.

Abstract: Despite the remarkable progress achieved by recent efficient methods in accelerating multimodal understanding, they still suffer from noticeable performance degradation. Their emphasis on the high compression ratio of a single visual clue and reliance on the heuristic pruning strategy with coarse attention alignment incurs a bottleneck on the information capacity and density of visual tokens. Addressing this limitation, we propose VEN-VL, a visual ensemble MoE framework for effective and efficient perception following the enrich then compact principle. Specifically, we first enrich the information capacity by unifying the visual representations of different perspectives, and then progressively compact it with adaptive routers in specialized visual experts to enhance the information density. Furthermore, we incorporate the reconstruction ability of vanilla structure via explicit visual supervision, facilitating crucial information preservation. Experimental results demonstrate our superiority in complex visual tasks with few information-condensed tokens, which effectively bridges the gap between performance and efficiency.

</details>


### [234] [RAPTOR+: A Visually Grounded Vision-Language Framework to Improve Clinical Trust and Auditability in Automated Cancer Referral Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25956)
*Sofiat Abioye,Ufaq Khan,Shazad Ashraf,Anusha Jose,Benjamin Wallace,William Poulett,Adam Byfield,Lukman Akanbi,Muhammad Bilal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RAPTOR+ extends the original RAPTOR system by using Vision-Language Models for end-to-end understanding of suspected CRC referral forms, eliminating the separate OCR stage. Fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B achieves 96.1% Reading Accuracy and 60.6% Strict Safety, far outperforming zero-shot models like Gemini 2.5 Flash (92.6% accuracy but only 1.2% Strict Safety), demonstrating that task-specific fine-tuning is critical for verifiable evidence grounding in clinical document understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urgent suspected CRC referrals create operational bottlenecks due to semi-structured clinical documents requiring manual review and transcription. The original RAPTOR's reliance on a separate OCR stage made it vulnerable to handwriting, layout variation, and loss of visual evidence linkage.

Method: RAPTOR+ is a multimodal extension using Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for end-to-end referral understanding. The authors evaluate fine-tuned VLMs, commercial and open-source zero-shot VLMs, and the original OCR-based pipeline on 223 curated CRC urgent referral forms, using a grounding-aware evaluation framework measuring both extraction accuracy and evidence localisation.

Result: Fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B achieved 96.1% Reading Accuracy and 60.6% Strict Safety. Gemini 2.5 Flash achieved 92.6% Reading Accuracy but only 1.2% Strict Safety, revealing a clear grounding gap in zero-shot models.

Conclusion: Task-specific fine-tuning is essential for reliable, auditable clinical document understanding. RAPTOR+ enables extracted referral decisions to be linked to visual evidence, supporting safer and more efficient cancer referral triage.

Abstract: Urgent suspected colorectal cancer (CRC) referrals create operational bottlenecks because semi-structured clinical documents often require manual review and transcription. The original RAPTOR system used Large Language Models for structured extraction but relied on a separate OCR stage, making it vulnerable to handwriting, layout variation, and loss of visual evidence linkage. We present RAPTOR+, a multimodal extension that uses Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for end-to-end referral understanding. We evaluate fine-tuned VLMs, commercial and open-source zero-shot VLMs, and the original OCR-based pipeline on 223 clinically curated CRC urgent referral forms. We also introduce a grounding-aware evaluation framework that measures both extraction accuracy and evidence localisation. Results show a clear grounding gap in zero-shot models. Gemini 2.5 Flash achieved 92.6% Reading Accuracy but only 1.2% Strict Safety. In contrast, fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B achieved 96.1% Reading Accuracy and 60.6% Strict Safety, substantially improving verifiable evidence grounding. These findings show that task-specific fine-tuning is essential for reliable, auditable clinical document understanding. RAPTOR+ enables extracted referral decisions to be linked to visual evidence, supporting safer and more efficient cancer referral triage.

</details>


### [235] [Context-driven Missing-Modality Learning for Robust Medical Diagnosis with Image-Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25968)
*Tianling Liu,Lequan Yu,Tong Han,Liang Wan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes CMML framework that uses Cascade Residual Transformer-based Autoencoder with learnable context tokens to synthesize missing modalities and align heterogeneous representations, achieving SOTA results on three medical datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal medical diagnosis suffers from arbitrary missing modalities in clinical practice, and existing methods either discard missing data (information loss) or fail to capture complex inter-modal dependencies when synthesizing them.

Method: CMML framework with (1) Cascade Residual Transformer-based Autoencoder (CRTA) using learnable context tokens as dataset-level semantic priors to capture inter-modal dependencies and synthesize missing representations, enriched by modality-specific memory banks; (2) transforming context tokens into instance-adaptive semantic references to align original and synthesized representations; (3) class-aware contrastive refinement for discriminative cues.

Result: Outperforms SOTA methods on Derm7pt (1.26% AUC improvement), ODIR (0.97%), and MEN (1.32%) datasets.

Conclusion: The CMML framework effectively handles arbitrary missing modalities through sequential modality synthesis and semantic alignment, achieving robust multimodal diagnosis in real-world clinical scenarios.

Abstract: While multimodal data integrating diverse imaging and clinical tabular records is crucial for accurate medical diagnosis, the arbitrary absence of specific modalities is prevalent in clinical practice, severely degrading the performance of multimodal models. Existing methods either discard missing modalities, leading to information loss, or struggle to synthesize them without capturing complex inter-modal dependencies. To address these limitations, we propose a novel Context-driven Missing-Modality Learning (CMML) framework, which sequentially performs modality synthesis and semantic alignment to achieve robust diagnosis under arbitrary missing conditions. Specifically, we design a Cascade Residual Transformer-based Autoencoder (CRTA) that leverages learnable context tokens acting as dataset-level semantic prior to capture inter-modal dependencies and synthesize key missing representations. These representations are further enriched by modality-specific memory banks. To resolve the discrepancy between original available and synthesized representations, we transform the learned context tokens into instance-adaptive semantic references by infusing multimodal representations from the CRTA's outputs. This reference guides the alignment of heterogeneous modality representations into a unified space, where class-aware contrastive refinement is finally applied to explore discriminative diagnostic cues. Extensive evaluations on skin lesion (Derm7pt), ocular disease (ODIR), and meningioma (MEN) datasets demonstrate that CMML significantly outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, yielding AVG AUC improvements of 1.26%, 0.97%, and 1.32%, respectively.

</details>


### [236] [PathWISE: Multi-Agent Cancer Pathway Triaging Ontology Learning from Clinical Flowcharts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25970)
*Sofiat Abioye,Ufaq Khan,Shazad Ashraf,Mohammed Adil Butt,Andrew D. Beggs,Adam Byfield,Anusha Jose,William Poulett,Ben Wallace,Junaid Qadir,Muhammad Bilal*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PathWISE is a five-phase pipeline combining LLM agents with deterministic auditing and a Java compiler to convert clinical flowchart pathways (e.g., UK NHS cancer pathways) into validated, executable HL7 CQL libraries deployable as FHIR CDS Hooks services, achieving 100% compilation success and zero hallucinated terminology codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clinical pathways exist as visual flowcharts (spatial topology, arrows, colors, font weights) that encode critical triage logic but remain inaccessible to computational systems, preventing automated decision support.

Method: A five-phase pipeline: four LLM-based agents extract flowchart structure into a typed directed graph; deterministic depth-first search enumerates all paths; structured semantic audit checks computability per node; terminology-constrained CQL definitions verified by the official Java CQL-to-ELM compiler; routing logic covers 100% of patient journeys. Non-deterministic LLM inference is confined to knowledge extraction; graph math and compiler underpin all verification.

Result: Tested on 5 UK NHS cancer pathways; audits up to 183 nodes; 544 structured governance findings across 4 categories; 100% syntactic compilation success; zero hallucinated terminology codes for dictionary-covered concepts; UNCOMPUTABLE nodes get false placeholders preserving compilability while flagging governance gaps.

Conclusion: PathWISE successfully transforms non-computable visual clinical pathways into validated, executable CQL artifacts, confining LLM risk to the knowledge extraction phase while relying on deterministic methods for verification.

Abstract: Clinical pathways are disseminated as visual flowcharts where spatial topology, arrow direction, colour coding, and font weight encode critical triage logic that remains inaccessible to computational systems. We present PathWISE, a five-phase pipeline combining four LLM-based agents with a deterministic depth-first search auditor and a Java compiler critic, transforming these non-computable artefacts into validated, executable HL7 Clinical Quality Language (CQL) libraries deployable as FHIR CDS Hooks services. Purpose-built agents extract flowchart structure into a typed directed graph, perform deterministic path enumeration, conduct a structured semantic audit of every node's computability, generate terminology-constrained CQL definitions verified by the official Java CQL-to-ELM compiler, and produce routing logic covering 100% of enumerated patient journeys. Demonstrated across five UK NHS cancer pathways (colorectal, lung, skin, upper GI, and breast), PathWISE audits up to 183 nodes (182 under the Hybrid configuration), identifies 544 structured governance findings across four issue categories, achieves 100% syntactic compilation success, with UNCOMPUTABLE nodes receiving false placeholders that preserve compilability while surfacing governance gaps for clinical review, and produces zero hallucinated terminology codes for dictionary-covered concepts. Critically, PathWISE confines non-deterministic LLM inference to knowledge extraction while deterministic graph mathematics and a standard compiler underpin every verification step.

</details>


### [237] [LLaVA-OneVision-2: Towards Next-Generation Perceptual Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25979)
*Xiang An,Yin Xie,Feilong Tang,Yunyao Yan,Huajie Tan,Didi Zhu,Changrui Chen,Xiuwei Zhao,Bin Qin,Kaicheng Yang,Yifei Shen,Yuanhan Zhang,Kaichen Zhang,Wenkang Zhang,Zheng Cheng,Nansen Zhang,Chunsheng Wu,Chunjiang Ge,Zimin Ran,Dehua Song,Chunyuan Li,Shikun Feng,Ming Hu,Zhangquan Chen,Junbo Niu,Bo Li,Ziyong Feng,Ziwei Liu,Zongyuan Ge,Jiankang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LLaVA-OV-2 is a vision-language model that uses codec-stream tokenization for efficient long-video understanding, achieving state-of-the-art results across video, spatial, and tracking benchmarks, including a 44.8-point margin over Qwen3-VL-8B on the new JumpScore benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models struggle with efficient long-video understanding due to fixed temporal grouping and insufficient spatial grounding in dense, high-frequency motion scenarios.

Method: Codec-stream tokenization (bit-cost dynamics for adaptive temporal grouping + motion-residual cues for spatial saliency), Windowed Attention for local computation, 3D RoPE for unified spatiotemporal coordinates, and large-scale open supervision data (8M video + 4M spatial samples).
