Result: LLaVA-OV-2-8B achieves 74.9 mAP on JumpScore (vs. Qwen3-VL-8B's 30.1), +4.3 avg on video tasks, +5.3 on spatial tasks, +15.6 on tracking tasks over Qwen3-VL-8B, and +9.7 points improvement via codec-stream over frame sampling on JumpScore.

Conclusion: Codec-stream tokenization is an effective paradigm for unified perception across video understanding, temporal/spatial grounding, and tracking, significantly outperforming strong baselines under matched token budgets.

Abstract: We introduce LLaVA-OneVision-2 (LLaVA-OV-2), the most capable vision-language model in the LLaVA-OneVision series to date, achieving superior performance across a broad range of multimodal benchmarks. The model builds on a native OneVision-Encoder and incorporates Windowed Attention for efficient local computation while maintaining native resolution. Its key advance is codec-stream tokenization: it treats compressed video as a continuous bit-cost stream, where bit-cost dynamics determine adaptive temporal groups, and motion-residual cues select salient spatial evidence into compact visual canvases. This allocation concentrates a limited token budget on event-bearing content, enabling more stable long-video token compression than fixed groups of pictures. A shared 3D RoPE further places codec canvases, sampled frames, and images in a unified spatiotemporal coordinate system. Furthermore, we build the LLaVA-OV-2 data and training stack around large-scale open supervision: approximately 8M re-captioned video samples for pretraining, a 4M-sample spatial corpus for fine-tuning. We also introduce JumpScore, a temporal-localization benchmark targeting fine-grained grounding in high-frequency, densely repeated motion, a regime underrepresented by existing video evaluations. A standout capability of LLaVA-OV-2 is its unified perception across video understanding, temporal grounding, spatial grounding, and manipulation-trace reasoning. On JumpScore, LLaVA-OneVision-2-8B reaches 74.9 JumpScore mAP, surpassing Qwen3-VL-8B (30.1) by +44.8 points; under matched visual-token budgets on the same benchmark, codec-stream inputs improve temporal grounding over frame sampling by +9.7 points. Across standard benchmarks, LLaVA-OneVision-2-8B further outperforms Qwen3-VL-8B by +4.3 average points on video tasks, +5.3 on spatial tasks, and +15.6 average J&F on tracking tasks.

</details>


### [238] [Towards 3D heart mesh generation using contactless radar imaging and physics-informed neural network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26003)
*Jinye Li,Chenxi Fu,Minghang Zheng,Yang Liu,Xiahai Zhuang,Qingchao Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAR2Mesh is a novel framework that reconstructs high-fidelity 3D cardiac geometry from mmWave SAR data using coarse-to-fine mesh deformation, outperforming traditional radar and image-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous, non-invasive cardiac monitoring is needed but MRI lacks portability; mmWave radar/SAR offers privacy-preserving point-of-care potential, but existing methods fail to reconstruct high-fidelity 3D cardiac geometry with continuous surface topology.

Method: SAR2Mesh uses a coarse-to-fine mesh deformation process initialized with a topological template, a geometry-aware feature projection module for multi-view 3D-to-2D sampling, and a physics-informed radar loss for consistency with raw radar echoes.

Result: SAR2Mesh significantly outperforms existing image-based baselines, achieving accurate and physically consistent cardiac reconstructions on the first large-scale paired SAR-mesh dataset (Cardiac Mesh-SAR).

Conclusion: The coarse-to-fine mesh deformation approach with explicit topology preservation and physics-informed loss effectively bridges the gap between sparse radar point clouds and continuous 3D cardiac geometry reconstruction.

Abstract: Cardiac function evaluation necessitates continuous, non-invasive monitoring, a capability limited in MRI. Millimeter-wave (mmWave) radar and its Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) mode offer a privacy-preserving and portable point-of-care clinical applications. However, reconstructing high-fidelity 3D cardiac geometry from SAR remains an open challenge. Traditional radar methods generate sparse point clouds that lack continuous surface topology. Meanwhile, direct application of optical reconstruction networks performs poorly due to the severe speckle noise and ambiguous boundaries inherent in SAR images. To bridge this gap, we propose SAR2Mesh, a novel framework that reformulates the task as a coarse-to-fine mesh deformation process. By initializing with a topological template, our approach explicitly preserves anatomical connectivity through progressive mesh deformation.We introduce a geometry-aware feature projection module to extract multi-view features via 3D-to-2D sampling, and a physics-informed radar loss to enforce consistency between the predicted geometry and raw radar echoes. Furthermore, we present Cardiac Mesh-SAR, the first large-scale paired SAR-mesh dataset. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAR2Mesh significantly outperforms existing image-based baselines, achieving accurate and physically consistent cardiac reconstructions.

</details>


### [239] [MAGIC: Multimodal Alignment & Grounding-aware Instruction Coreset for Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26004)
*Shristi Das Biswas,Kaushik Roy*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MAGIC is a training-free, forward-only coreset selection method for multimodal instruction tuning of LVLMs. It uses three intrinsic signals (Multimodal Gain, Bridging Relevance, Skill-Neuron Signatures) in a three-stage pipeline to select compact, behaviorally faithful subsets, achieving superior performance (~100-102% relative to full fine-tuning) with 73.7% runtime reduction at 20% budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal instruction tuning datasets contain substantial redundancy, low visual dependency, and imbalanced coverage of reasoning behaviors; uniform or naive score-based subsampling yields suboptimal training subsets.

Method: MAGIC combines three intrinsic VLM signals: Multimodal Gain (likelihood improvement from visual input), Bridging Relevance (answer-token grounding sharpness over visual tokens), and Skill-Neuron Signatures (top-activated feed-forward neurons). Pipeline: (1) filter low-gain examples, (2) rank by normalized quality objective, (3) bucket-wise budget allocation over discrete neuron signatures to preserve skill coverage.

Result: On LLaVA-665K and Vision-Flan, at 20% budget, MAGIC achieves 100.3% (LLaVA-665K) and 101.6% (Vision-Flan-186K) relative performance vs full fine-tuning, with 73.7% wall-clock time reduction, consistently outperforming strong baselines.

Conclusion: MAGIC provides an efficient, training-free, forward-only coreset selection that preserves multimodal skill coverage and achieves full-data-level performance with substantially reduced computational cost.

Abstract: Instruction tuning of large vision-language models (LVLMs) increasingly depends on massive multimodal corpora, yet these datasets contain samples with substantial redundancy, low visual dependency, and highly imbalanced coverage of multimodal reasoning behaviors. As a result, uniform subsampling or naive score-based selection often yields suboptimal training subsets. We introduce MAGIC, a training-free, forward-only coreset selection method designed to construct compact yet behaviorally faithful subsets for multimodal instruction tuning. MAGIC is built on three intrinsic signals extracted from a pretrained VLM: Multimodal Gain, which measures the likelihood improvement obtained from visual input; Bridging Relevance, which captures the sharpness of answer-token grounding over visual tokens; and Skill-Neuron Signatures, which characterize the functional computation elicited by each sample via top-activated feed-forward neurons. MAGIC combines these signals in a three-stage pipeline: filtering low-gain examples, ranking candidates by a normalized quality objective, and performing bucket-wise budget allocation over discrete neuron signatures to preserve latent multimodal skill coverage. This formulation avoids backpropagation, auxiliary selector training, and expensive clustering in continuous activation spaces, while remaining efficient and easily deployable in existing VLMs. Across LLaVA-665K and Vision-Flan datasets, and transfer settings to large target models, LLaVA-1.5-7B and -13B, MAGIC consistently improves over strong baselines under matched 20% budgets: it achieves 100.3% relative performance to full finetuning on LLaVA-665K and 101.6% relative performance on Vision-Flan-186K, while yielding a 73.7% reduction in wall-clock run time.

</details>


### [240] [STORM: Internalized Modeling for Spatial-Temporal Reasoning in Video-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26014)
*Yiming Liang,Yixiao Chen,Yiyang Zhou,Yixuan Wang,Shoubin Yu,Andong Deng,Fuxiao Liu,Qin Zhang,Chen Chen,Mohit Bansal,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STORMS is a two-stage framework that teaches LVLMs to reason through bounded continuous latent trajectories instead of explicit textual chain-of-thought, improving video reasoning accuracy while reducing inference overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video reasoning methods rely on externalized reasoning (textual CoT, keyframe selection, frame reinsertion, or external tools), which increases latency and complexity, and forces temporal-visual evidence to be serialized into text or repeatedly re-encoded.

Method: Two-stage framework: (1) Aligns latent tokens with thought-video representations from generated videos to ground latent states in dynamic visual evidence; (2) Trains with answer-only supervision to internalize reasoning without step-by-step annotations. At inference, performs bounded latent rollout without regenerating videos or using external tools.

Result: Experiments on VideoMME, MVBench, TempCompass, and MMVU show improved video reasoning accuracy with substantially reduced inference overhead compared to tool or video-generation-based reasoning pipelines.

Conclusion: Visual reasoning can occur implicitly before verbalization through internalized latent trajectories, offering an efficient alternative to explicit textual reasoning chains for video understanding.

Abstract: Many video reasoning tasks require tracking motion, temporal order, and evolving visual states across frames. Existing methods built on large vision-language models (LVLMs) often address this challenge by externalizing reasoning through textual chain-of-thought (CoT), keyframe selection, repeated frame reinsertion, or external tool use. While effective, such pipelines increase inference-time latency and engineering complexity, and they force temporal-visual evidence to be serialized into text or repeatedly re-encoded from frames. Inspired by the intuition that visual reasoning can occur implicitly before verbalization, we propose STORMS (Spatial-Temporal reasOning via inteRnalized Modeling), a two-stage framework that teaches LVLMs to reason through bounded continuous latent trajectories instead of explicit textual CoT. In Stage I, STORMS aligns latent tokens with thought-video representations derived from generated videos, grounding the latent states in dynamic visual evidence. In Stage II, the model is further trained with answer-only supervision, encouraging the reasoning process to be internalized without step-by-step annotations. Generated thought videos are used only during training; at inference, STORMS performs a bounded latent rollout without regenerating videos, reinserting frames, or invoking external visual tools. Experiments on VideoMME, MVBench, TempCompass, and MMVU show that STORMS improves video reasoning accuracy while substantially reducing inference overhead compared with tool or video-generation-based reasoning pipelines.

</details>


### [241] [A Multimodal 3D Foundation Model for Light Sheet Fluorescence Microscopy Enables Few-Shot Segmentation, Classification, and Deblurring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26026)
*Adina Scheinfeld,Haotan Zhang,Shang Mu,Rudolf L. M. van Herten,Lucas Stoffl,Ali Erturk,Zhuhao Wu,Johannes C. Paetzold*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a 3D foundation model for Light Sheet Fluorescence Microscopy (LSM) data, pretrained on diverse volumetric images using masked reconstruction and image-text alignment. The model achieves strong few-shot performance on downstream segmentation, classification, and deblurring tasks, reducing annotation burden while outperforming baselines in both automated metrics and expert evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LSM produces rich 3D biological data, but supervised deep learning is costly and hard to scale due to data size, dimensionality, and annotation burden. Despite abundant unannotated LSM volumes, foundation models for this modality remain underexplored due to computational challenges and complexity of volumetric representation learning.

Method: The authors pretrain a 3D foundation model on a large curated collection of LSM images (multiple organisms, stains, protocols). They jointly optimize for (1) masked reconstruction and (2) image-text alignment to learn transferable volumetric representations. The pretrained backbone is then adapted via few-shot fine-tuning for downstream tasks.

Result: The model achieves consistent improvements over baselines on segmentation, classification, and deblurring tasks, measured both by standard evaluation metrics and by domain expert assessment. Pretraining drastically reduces annotation requirements while improving performance.

Conclusion: Foundation model pretraining can effectively reduce annotation burden and improve performance across diverse LSM analysis tasks. The work highlights the potential of self-supervised and multimodal pretraining for volumetric microscopy data. Code and model weights are publicly released.

Abstract: Light sheet fluorescence microscopy (LSM) enables high-resolution, three-dimensional (3D) imaging of biological specimens, providing rich volumetric data for studying cellular organization, pathology, and vascular networks. However, the size, dimensionality, and annotation burden of LSM data make supervised deep learning approaches costly and difficult to scale. Additionally, despite the abundance of unannotated LSM volumes, foundation models for this modality remain underexplored due to computational challenges and the complexity of volumetric representation learning. In this work, we introduce a 3D foundation model for LSM data, pretrained on a large curated collection of 3D images spanning multiple organisms, stains, and imaging protocols. We learn transferable volumetric representations by jointly optimizing for masked reconstruction and image-text alignment. The pretrained backbone drastically reduces the annotation burden, enabling efficient, few-shot adaptation for varied downstream tasks. We evaluate this approach on downstream segmentation, classification, and deblurring. Our results demonstrate consistent improvements over baselines, (1) when measured using standard evaluation metrics and (2) when rigorously assessed by domain experts. This highlights the potential of foundation model pretraining to reduce annotation requirements while improving performance across diverse LSM analysis tasks. Pretrained model weights and code for pretraining and finetuning are publicly available: https://github.com/AdinaScheinfeld/lsm_fm_public_repo.git.

</details>


### [242] [Everything at Every Scale: Scale-Invariant Diffusion with Continuous Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26032)
*Zixin Jessie Chen,Zhuo Chen,Archer Wang,Jeff Gore,William T. Freeman,Congyue Deng,Marin Soljačić*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SKILD unifies image generation and continuous super-resolution in a single unconditional diffusion model by leveraging scale invariance, with the forward process attenuating content from fine to coarse scales using spectrum-matched noise. The same trained model performs both tasks by varying the starting timestep only, outperforming conditional models on ImageNet super-resolution (2×-8×) and achieving strong results on CIFAR-10 and physical Ising model reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To unify image generation and super-resolution—two tasks that both reverse information loss across scales—into a single unconditional framework, eliminating the need for task-specific architectures, conditioning, or retraining per scale factor.

Method: Leverages scale invariance in natural images and physical systems. Forward process attenuates image content from fine to coarse scales while injecting spectrum-matched Gaussian noise, making scale an explicit coordinate of diffusion dynamics. The reverse process performs both generation and continuous super-resolution by varying the starting timestep.

Result: FID 2.65 and Inception Score 9.63 on unconditional CIFAR-10; 2×-8× super-resolution on ImageNet from a single unconditional checkpoint, outperforming conditional models; reconstructs critical Ising models with accurate connected four-point correlations.

Conclusion: Scale invariance enables a truly unified diffusion framework for generation and super-resolution without any task-specific modifications, demonstrating strong performance across both natural images and physical systems.

Abstract: Creating images from noise is image generation; reconstructing fine details from coarse inputs is super-resolution. Despite their practical differences, both can be understood as reversing information loss across scales. We introduce $\textbf{SKILD}$, a $\textbf{S}$cale-invariant $\textbf{K}$-Space $\textbf{I}$mage $\textbf{L}$earning $\textbf{D}$iffusion model that unifies generation and continuous super-resolution within a single unconditional framework. Both natural images and critical physical systems exhibit scale invariance, and we leverage it to design a forward process that attenuates image content from fine to coarse scales while injecting spectrum-matched Gaussian noise, making scale an explicit coordinate of the diffusion dynamics. The same trained reverse process performs generation and continuous super-resolution by varying only the starting timestep: $\textit{no task-specific architecture, no conditioning branch, no classifier-free guidance, no retraining per scale factor}$. Empirically, SKILD reaches FID $2.65$ and Inception Score $9.63$ on unconditional CIFAR-10, performs $2\times$--$8\times$ super-resolution on ImageNet from a single unconditional checkpoint while outperforming conditional models across perceptual metrics, and reconstructs critical Ising models whose connected four-point correlations closely track the ground truth.

</details>


### [243] [DRScaffold: Boosting Dense-Scene Reasoning in Lightweight Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26038)
*Xinrui Shi,Kai Liu,Ziqing Zhang,Jianze Li,Anqi Li,Yulun Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces DRBench (dense-scene reasoning benchmark) and DRScaffold (structured supervision framework) to improve lightweight VLMs' multi-step grounded reasoning in cluttered environments, showing 3B model can outperform frozen 32B model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lightweight VLMs fail systematically in dense-scene reasoning because existing training signals provide no explicit grounding between reasoning steps and visual entities, causing fluency without visual faithfulness.

Method: (1) DRBench: 14,573 questions across 2,943 images organized into 5 task categories over 3 progressive reasoning layers. (2) DRScaffold: SFT framework decomposing supervision into 4 causally ordered stages enforcing grounded reasoning without architectural changes.

Result: Substantial gains on DRBench across 3 lightweight VLMs while preserving/improving general benchmarks. Qwen2.5-VL-3B + DRScaffold surpasses frozen Qwen2.5-VL-32B on DRBench.

Conclusion: Structured supervision can substitute for a significant portion of model scale in dense-scene reasoning, demonstrating that explicit grounding via decomposed training signals is a viable path for lightweight models.

Abstract: Lightweight vision-language models perform competitively on standard benchmarks yet fail systematically in dense-scene reasoning, where multiple objects, attributes, and relations must be jointly grounded and resolved through multi-step inference. Such capability is critical for real-world applications where models must reliably interpret cluttered environments. Yet existing training signals provide no explicit grounding between reasoning steps and the underlying visual entities and relations, leaving lightweight models free to generate fluent but visually unanchored reasoning chains. To address this gap, we first introduce DRBench, a benchmark of 14,573 questions across 2,943 images, organized into five task categories spanning three progressive reasoning layers. Building on DRBench, we propose DRScaffold, a supervised fine-tuning framework that decomposes the supervision target into four causally ordered stages, enforcing grounded reasoning without architectural modification. Experiments on three lightweight VLMs demonstrate substantial gains on DRBench while preserving or improving performance on general-purpose benchmarks. Notably, Qwen2.5-VL-3B trained with DRScaffold surpasses the frozen Qwen2.5-VL-32B on DRBench, demonstrating that structured supervision can substitute for a significant portion of model scale in dense-scene reasoning. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/irene-shi/DRScaffold .

</details>


### [244] [Paris 2.0: A Decentralized Diffusion Model for Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26064)
*Ali Rouzbayani,Bidhan Roy,Marcos Villagra,Zhiying Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Paris 2.0 is the first video generation model trained using decentralized computation, achieving significant improvements over monolithic training (2x better FVD scores).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the open problem of temporally coherent video generation under decentralized training, building on Paris 1.0 which showed image generation could be trained without a monolithic GPU cluster.

Method: Decentralized computation training recipe building upon Paris 1.0 (Decentralized Diffusion Model), applied to low-resolution text-to-video generation.

Result: Paris 2.0 achieves ~2.0x improvement in FVD (279.01 vs 561.04) compared to monolithic model with matched compute budget, along with improved CLIP text-video similarity and aesthetic scores.

Conclusion: Decentralized training can effectively produce temporally coherent video generation, closing a previously open problem in the field.

Abstract: We present Paris 2.0, the first video generation model pre-trained through decentralized computation. Its training recipe builds upon Paris 1.0 (arXiv:2510.03434), the first ever open-weight Decentralized Diffusion Model (DDM), which showed that image generation can be trained without a monolithic GPU cluster. However, temporally coherent video generation had remained an open problem under decentralized training, and Paris 2.0 closes it.
  In low-resolution text-to-video training, against a monolithic model trained on the same data under a matched total compute budget, Paris 2.0 cuts Frechet Video Distance (FVD) from 561.04 to 279.01, a ~2.0x improvement, and lifts CLIP text-video similarity and aesthetic score.

</details>


### [245] [Channel-wise Vector Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26089)
*Wei Song,Tianhang Wang,Yitong Chen,Tong Zhang,Zuxuan Wu,Ming Li,Jiaqi Wang,Kaicheng Yu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CVQ replaces patch-level VQ with channel-level quantization for image tokenization, enabling a "next-channel prediction" autoregressive framework (CAR) that generates images by progressively refining visual details from coarse structure to fine details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional VQ tokenizes images into spatial patch grids, which limits codebook utilization and fails to model images in a progressively detailed manner. The authors propose a channel-wise paradigm to better align with how images naturally compose—from global structure to fine details.

Method: CVQ quantizes each channel of the feature map (rather than each spatial patch), representing an image as discrete levels of visual detail. Based on this, CAR performs autoregressive "next-channel prediction" to generate images by first sketching global structure, then refining fine-grained attributes.

Result: CVQ achieves 100% codebook utilization with a 16K+ codebook size without special tricks, and significantly improves reconstruction quality over conventional VQ. CAR achieves DPG score of 86.7 and GenEval score of 0.79 for text-to-image generation.

Conclusion: Channel-wise tokenization is a promising alternative to patch-wise VQ, offering better codebook utilization and enabling a more intuitive autoregressive generation process that mimics human artistic workflows, with strong empirical results in both reconstruction and text-to-image generation.

Abstract: We present Channel-wise Vector Quantization (CVQ), a novel image tokenization paradigm that replaces patch-wise tokens with channel-wise tokens. Unlike conventional vector quantization, which assigns a discrete token to each patch feature vector, CVQ quantizes each channel of the feature map. This formulation represents an image as discrete levels of visual details, rather than as a grid of spatial patches. Based on CVQ, we introduce a new visual autoregressive framework with "next-channel prediction". Instead of rendering images patch by patch in raster order, our Channel-wise Autoregressive (CAR) model predicts image channels sequentially, producing progressively enriched visual details. Specifically, it first sketches global structure and then refines fine-grained attributes, akin to a human artist's workflow. Empirically, we show that: (1) CVQ achieves 100% codebook utilization with a 16K+ codebook size without any bells and whistles, and substantially improves reconstruction quality over conventional VQ; and (2) CAR attains a DPG score of 86.7 and a GenEval score of 0.79, demonstrating strong effectiveness for text-to-image generation.

</details>


### [246] [Pixel-Level Pavement Distress Assessment Using Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26095)
*Logan Dewick,Bibesh Pyakurel,Kong Pheng Yang,Nazim Choudhury,M. G. Sarwar Murshed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper presents a Mask R-CNN-based pavement distress analysis system evaluated on a custom field-collected dataset (UWGB-StreetCrack). The best model (ResNet-101 FPN backbone) achieved 87.04% F1 score and closely matched ground-truth crack-area fraction (2.164% vs 2.170%), outperforming a YOLO detector baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated pavement distress assessment requires precise localization of thin, branching, and irregular cracks for maintenance-relevant quantification, which goes beyond image-level classification or coarse bounding box detection.

Method: Used Mask R-CNN instance segmentation with five Detectron2-based backbone variants on a custom field-collected dataset (UWGB-StreetCrack) with polygon annotations. A CSPDarknet53-based YOLO detector was also adapted for comparison.

Result: Best model (Mask R-CNN ResNet-101 FPN): 84.23% precision, 90.04% recall, 87.04% F1 score. Predicted crack-area fraction 2.164% vs ground-truth 2.170%. YOLO baseline achieved only 27.5% precision and 20.7% recall.

Conclusion: Instance segmentation is a practical direction for field pavement imagery and aggregate crack-area estimation, but challenges remain in annotation consistency, class imbalance, confounder rejection, and mask-level benchmarking.

Abstract: Automated pavement distress assessment requires more than image-level classification or coarse bounding box detection, demanding precise localization of thin, branching, and irregular cracks to achieve the geometric precision necessary for maintenance-relevant quantification. This paper presents a vision-based pavement distress analysis system based on Mask R-CNN instance segmentation and evaluates it on UWGB-StreetCrack, a custom field-collected roadway image dataset acquired with a vehicle-mounted smartphone and manually annotated with polygon labels for longitudinal cracks, transverse cracks, alligator cracks, and potholes. Five Detectron2-based Mask R-CNN backbone variants were considered under a consistent fine-tuning protocol. The best-performing model, Mask R-CNN with a ResNet-101 FPN backbone, achieved 84.23% precision, 90.04% recall, and an F1 score of 87.04% under the project-specific bounding-box matching protocol. The same model produced an aggregate predicted crack-area fraction of 2.164%, closely matching the 2.170% ground-truth crack-area fraction. To contextualize the segmentation system against a detector-oriented alternative, a CSPDarknet53-based YOLO detector was also adapted and retrained on the dataset, reaching 27.5% precision and 20.7% recall on the validation protocol. The results show that instance segmentation is a practical direction for field pavement imagery and aggregate crack-area estimation, while also exposing open challenges in annotation consistency, class imbalance, confounder rejection, and mask-level benchmarking.

</details>


### [247] [InstructSAM: Segment Any Instance with Any Instructions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26102)
*Yuqian Yuan,Wentong Li,Zhaocheng Li,Yutong Lin,Juncheng Li,Siliang Tang,Jun Xiao,Yueting Zhuang,Wenqiao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InstructSAM unifies VLM and SAM3 for multi-instance segmentation under arbitrary instructions via learnable instance queries and hybrid-attention, achieving strong results on instruction-driven segmentation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for instruction-driven instance segmentation rely on complex agentic pipelines or lack compositional reasoning, motivating the need for a unified, end-to-end framework that handles multi-instance segmentation under free-form instructions.

Method: InstructSAM formulates instruction-driven segmentation as set-structured query prediction, injecting learnable instance queries into a VLM with hybrid-attention between queries, visual tokens, and instruction tokens, then projecting LLM-conditioned queries into SAM3's detector query space for single-pass multi-instance segmentation.

Result: The 2B-scale InstructSAM outperforms prior end-to-end methods and SAM3's agentic pipeline on complex instruction-driven and phrase-level referring segmentation benchmarks, with efficient single-pass multi-instance prediction.

Conclusion: InstructSAM provides an effective unified framework for instruction-driven multi-instance segmentation by bridging VLM reasoning with SAM3's segmentation capability, supported by a new large-scale dataset Inst2Seg.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce InstructSAM, a unified and streamlined framework designed for multi-instance segmentation under arbitrary instructions. We formulates instruction-driven instance segmentation as a set-structured query prediction problem and propose an explicit reasoning-to-instance query interface that elegantly bridges a vision-language model (VLM) and SAM3. Specifically, a bank of learnable instance queries is injected into the VLM and contextualized with instruction and visual information, enabling each query to serve as an instance-aware slot. A hybrid-attention mechanism further promotes interaction among these queries, visual tokens, and instruction tokens, improving instance enumeration and reducing duplicate predictions. The resulting LLM-conditioned queries are projected into SAM3's detector query space to drive accurate multi-instance segmentation in a single forward pass. This design equips SAM3 with high-level instruction understanding, compositional reasoning, and instance-level set prediction without modifying its core architecture. To support training and evaluation, we further construct Inst2Seg, a high-quality and large-scale instruction-based instance segmentation dataset and benchmark that couples free-form instructions with instance-level masks. Extensive experiments show that only 2B-scale InstructSAM achieves strong results across complex instruction-driven and phrase-level referring segmentation benchmarks, outperforming prior end-to-end methods and SAM3's agentic pipeline while enabling efficient single-pass multi-instance prediction.

</details>


### [248] [Global Structure-from-Motion Meets Feedforward Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26103)
*Linfei Pan,Johannes Schönberge,Marc Pollefeys*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a hybrid Structure-from-Motion pipeline that combines classical SfM methods with feedforward 3D reconstruction approaches, achieving state-of-the-art results by leveraging complementary strengths of both paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical SfM methods fail in challenging scenarios (low texture, limited overlap, symmetries), while feedforward approaches have scalability, accuracy, and robustness limitations in standard settings. The motivation is to combine both to overcome their respective weaknesses.

Method: A new SfM pipeline that systematically integrates classical and feedforward methods to leverage their complementary strengths across different reconstruction scenarios.

Result: State-of-the-art results achieved across multiple datasets and a wide range of scenarios.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach successfully combines the best of both worlds, overcoming limitations of each paradigm individually, and is released as open-source.

Abstract: Structure-from-Motion -- the process of simultaneously estimating camera poses and 3D scene structure from a collection of images -- remains a central challenge in computer vision, with many open problems yet to be solved. Recent advances in feedforward 3D reconstruction have made significant strides in overcoming persistent failure cases of classical SfM methods, particularly in scenarios characterized by low texture, limited overlap, and symmetries. However, while feedforward approaches excel in these challenging conditions, they often face limitations regarding scalability, accuracy, or robustness, and typically fall short of classical methods in standard reconstruction settings. In this work, we systematically analyze these limitations and propose a new Structure-from-Motion pipeline by combining the respective strengths of classical and feedforward methods. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets show the benefits of our approach, achieving state-of-the-art results across a wide range of scenarios. We share our system as an open-source implementation at https://github.com/colmap/gluemap.

</details>


### [249] [EVIDENT: Routing MLLM Adaptation through Entity-Grounded Visual Evidence for Cross-Domain Video Temporal Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26104)
*Geo Ahn,Jiwook Han,Youngrae Kim,Joonseok Lee,Jinwoo Choi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EVIDENT improves cross-domain robustness in Video Temporal Grounding by anchoring adaptation through explicit entity-level visual evidence, using an Entity Bottleneck Adapter, Entity-Binding Distillation loss, and Entity-to-eVidence gating.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning MLLMs for VTG improves in-domain performance but degrades under domain shift due to visual domain shift disrupting the coupling between temporal localization knowledge and entity-attention capability.

Method: Three components: (i) Entity Bottleneck Adapter compressing visual tokens into entity slots, (ii) Entity-Binding Distillation loss instilling objectness priors, (iii) Entity-to-eVidence gating using entities as evidence for temporal localization.

Result: Consistently improves out-of-domain robustness while maintaining competitive in-domain performance with modest parameter overhead on cross-domain VTG benchmarks.

Conclusion: Entity-level grounding serves as an effective inductive bias for generalizable temporal localization across domains.

Abstract: Fine-tuning MLLMs for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) often improves in-domain performance but degrades sharply under domain shift. In this work, we find that this failure is primarily driven not just by unseen query concepts, but by visual domain shift, which prevents the model from coupling its learned temporal localization knowledge with its inherent entity-attention capability. To address this, we introduce EVIDENT, a parameter-efficient adaptation framework that anchors temporal grounding in the inherent entity-attention of pre-trained MLLMs by routing VTG adaptation through explicit visual entity evidence. EVIDENT consists of three components: (i) an Entity Bottleneck Adapter that transforms dense visual tokens into compact entity-level slots, (ii) an Entity-Binding Distillation loss that instills objectness priors into the semantically unstructured MLLM visual space, guiding each slot to bind to a coherent entity, and (iii) an Entity-to-eVidence gating mechanism that leverages the captured entities as evidence, steering the model to localize moments containing query-relevant entities. Together, these components enable VTG fine-tuning to rely on entity-grounded evidence rather than brittle dataset shortcuts. Experiments on cross-domain VTG benchmarks show that EVIDENT consistently improves out-of-domain robustness while preserving competitive in-domain performance with modest parameter overhead. These results suggest that entity-level grounding is an effective inductive bias for generalizable temporal localization.

</details>


### [250] [On-Policy Adversarial Flow Distillation for Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26105)
*Yang Luo,Shengju Qian,Xiaohang Tang,Zirui Zhu,Yong Liu,Xin Wang,Yang You*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AFD is an on-policy framework for distilling black-box video generators into autoregressive students using prompt-paired adversarial discrimination and forward-process flow-matching, without requiring teacher scores, latents, or denoising trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distilling black-box video teachers into autoregressive students is difficult due to off-policy supervision, incompatible architectures, and lack of access to teacher scores or latents. Existing methods (supervised fine-tuning, score distillation, adversarial imitation) fail in this heterogeneous black-box setting.

Method: AFD proposes: (1) on-policy rollout of student and teacher on same prompts, (2) a prompt-paired Bradley-Terry discriminator to estimate clean-sample teacher-student discrepancy, (3) converting on-policy advantage into forward-process flow-matching updates on student's noised states, providing dense velocity-field supervision without needing teacher scores, latents, or step alignment.

Result: AFD consistently improves motion- and physics-sensitive generation across two causal AR student families while preserving general video quality. Ablations confirm the importance of adaptive on-policy feedback and forward-process credit assignment.

Conclusion: AFD provides a practical, training-stable route for distilling proprietary or heterogeneous black-box video generators into efficient autoregressive students, requiring only clean teacher videos and student rollouts.

Abstract: Autoregressive video generators are attractive for streaming, long-horizon, and interactive applications, but distilling strong black-box teachers into causal students remains difficult. The student must learn under its own rollout distribution, whereas practical teachers may expose only prompt-conditioned completed videos and may differ in architecture, capacity, temporal design, and sampling schedule. This interface makes supervised fine-tuning off-policy, score-based distillation inapplicable, and direct adversarial imitation too sparse for denoising-time credit assignment. We propose Adversarial Flow Distillation (AFD), an on-policy framework for heterogeneous black-box video distillation. AFD queries the teacher and rolls out the current student on the same prompts, trains a prompt-paired Bradley-Terry discriminator to estimate clean-sample teacher-student discrepancy, and converts the resulting on-policy advantage into forward-process flow-matching updates on the student's own noised states. Thus, AFD provides dense velocity-field supervision while requiring no teacher scores, latents, denoising trajectories, step alignment, or reverse-chain reinforcement learning. Experiments across two causal AR student families show that AFD consistently improves motion- and physics-sensitive generation while preserving general video quality, and ablations validate the importance of adaptive on-policy feedback and forward-process credit assignment. The method requires only clean teacher videos and student rollouts, providing a practical route for distilling proprietary or heterogeneous video generators into efficient autoregressive students.

</details>


### [251] [Reinforcing Few-step Generators via Reward-Tilted Distribution Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26108)
*Yushi Huang,Xiangxin Zhou,Ruoyu Wang,Chi Zhang,Jun Zhang,Tianyu Pang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RTDMD is a two-stage framework that combines distribution matching distillation with reward-guided RL to align few-step diffusion models with human preferences, achieving state-of-the-art image generation in 4 steps on SD3, SD3.5, and FLUX.2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent few-step diffusion distillation models can generate images efficiently, but aligning them with human preferences (reward optimization) remains a challenge.

Method: RTDMD has two stages: (1) AC-DMD performs subinterval-wise distribution matching with a consistency regularizer; (2) jointly optimizes distribution matching + reward maximization via a hybrid policy gradient combining GRPO-style estimation and direct reward backpropagation, plus SubGRPO to reduce variance.

Result: RTDMD establishes new SOTA results across preference, aesthetic, and compositional metrics with only 4 inference steps, outperforming prior few-step text-to-image methods on SD3, SD3.5, and FLUX.2.

Conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework effectively unifies distribution matching distillation with reward-guided RL, enabling efficient and preference-aligned few-step image generation.

Abstract: Recent advances in few-step diffusion distillation have enabled efficient image generation, yet aligning these models with human preferences remains challenging. We propose Reward-Tilted Distribution Matching Distillation (RTDMD), a two-stage framework that unifies distribution matching distillation with reward-guided reinforcement learning for few-step flow generators. We show that minimizing the KL divergence to a reward-tilted teacher distribution naturally decomposes into a distribution matching term and a reward maximization term. In the first stage, we introduce Ambient-Consistent Distribution Matching Distillation (AC-DMD), which performs subinterval-wise distribution matching and augments the fake score objective with a consistency regularizer to help the fake score model track the shifting generator distribution under limited updates. In the second stage, we jointly optimize both terms: for the reward maximization term, we derive a hybrid policy gradient that combines a GRPO-style estimator for the stochastic intermediate transitions with direct reward backpropagation through the deterministic final step, and further introduce step-subset GRPO (SubGRPO) to reduce variance. Experiments on SD3, SD3.5, and FLUX.2 demonstrate that RTDMD establishes new state-of-the-art results across preference, aesthetic, and compositional metrics with only 4 inference steps, outperforming previous few-step text-to-image generation methods. Code and models are available at https://github.com/Harahan/RTDMD.

</details>


### [252] [Helix4D: Complex 4D Mesh Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26109)
*Jiraphon Yenphraphai,Jianqi Chen,Jian Wang,Gordon Qian,Sergey Tulyakov,Rameen Abdal,Raymond A. Yeh,Peter Wonka,Chaoyang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Helix4D extends Trellis2 from image-to-3D to video-conditioned 4D dynamic mesh generation, using sliding-window cross-frame attention and a 4D temporal encoding that repurposes low-frequency spatial RoPE bands, enabling high-quality results with complex topology changes, transparent materials, thin structures, and inner surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video-to-4D methods struggle with complex topology changes, transparent materials, thin structures, and inner surfaces. The authors aim to adapt Trellis2's expressive representation from image-to-3D to video-conditioned 4D generation while preserving its pretrained quality on rare cases.

Method: (a) Sliding-window cross-frame attention anchored on the first frame (generated by base Trellis2) to enable frame-local information sharing while preserving pretrained quality. (b) 4D temporal encoding that repurposes redundant low-frequency spatial RoPE bands for time, extending from 3D to 4D with no additional parameters.

Result: Extensive experiments show effectiveness for high-quality dynamic mesh generation on ActionBench and the authors' own challenging complex dynamics set.

Conclusion: Helix4D successfully addresses key limitations of current video-to-4D methods by inheriting Trellis2's quality and extending it to 4D with efficient temporal encoding, enabling generation of complex dynamic meshes.

Abstract: Current video-to-4D methods struggle with complex topology changes, transparent materials, thin structures, and inner surfaces. We present Helix4D, a dynamic mesh generation framework by inheriting the expressive representation of Trellis2, adapting it from image-to-3D to video-conditioned 4D generation. Our design arises from two key questions: (a) how to enable Trellis2's frame-local attention to share information across frames while preserving its pretrained quality on rare cases such as transparent objects and inner surfaces, and (b) how to inject temporal information into a purely 3D positional encoding without breaking pretrained capabilities. We address (a) with a sliding-window cross-frame attention and anchor on the first frame. The first frame is generated by the base Trellis2 model and injected into our model, letting it inherit Trellis2's quality in rare cases through cross-frame attention. We address (b) with a 4D temporal encoding that repurposes redundant low-frequency spatial RoPE bands for time, extending the encoding from 3D with no additional parameters. Extensive experiments show the effectiveness of Helix4D for high-quality dynamic mesh generation on ActionBench and our own challenging complex dynamics set.

</details>


### [253] [Squeezing Capacity from Multimodal Large Language Models for Subject-driven Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26111)
*Shuhong Zheng,Aashish Kumar Misraa,Yu-Teng Li,Yu-Jhe Li,Igor Gilitschenski*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new subject-driven image generation method conditions diffusion models on MLLMs that jointly encode text and images, augmented with VAE-based identity conditioning and a Dual Layer Aggregation module, achieving better identity preservation and reduced copy-paste artifacts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing subject-driven image generation methods encode text and reference images separately, limiting cross-modal reasoning and causing copy-paste artifacts. Recent MLLM-based approaches improve instruction following but neglect identity preservation.

Method: Conditions diffusion models on MLLMs that jointly encode text and reference images, augmented with VAE-based identity conditioning. A Dual Layer Aggregation (DLA) module aggregates multi-level MLLM features. A multi-stage denoising strategy balances semantic MLLM information and fine-detail VAE identity during inference.

Result: The approach harmonizes multimodal understanding with identity preservation, mitigates copy-paste issues, and achieves superior human preference performance on subject-driven image generation.

Conclusion: Joint encoding via MLLMs with VAE identity augmentation and DLA aggregation effectively bridges the gap between instruction following and identity preservation in subject-driven image generation.

Abstract: Subject-driven image generation aims to synthesize new images that preserve the identity of the given subject while following textual instructions. Existing approaches often encode text and reference images separately. This limits cross-modal reasoning abilities and causes copy-paste artifacts. Recent frameworks that connect multimodal models and diffusion models improve instruction following, but largely overlook identity preservation. To address these limitations, we condition diffusion models on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) that jointly encode text and reference images, and augment it with VAE-based identity conditioning. A novel Dual Layer Aggregation (DLA) module is designed to aggregate multi-level MLLM features for optimal conditioning, and a multi-stage denoising strategy is applied to progressively balance the semantic information from MLLM and fine-detail identity from VAE during inference. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach harmonizes multimodal understanding with identity preservation, mitigates copy-paste issues, and achieves superior performance regarding human preference on subject-driven image generation. Our project website is available at https://zsh2000.github.io/squeeze-mllm-subject-gen/.

</details>


### [254] [TriSplat: Simulation-Ready Feed-Forward 3D Scene Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26115)
*Weijie Wang,Zimu Li,Jinchuan Shi,Zeyu Zhang,Botao Ye,Marc Pollefeys,Donny Y. Chen,Bohan Zhuang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TriSplat is a feed-forward network that reconstructs 3D scenes using oriented triangle primitives instead of Gaussians, enabling direct export of simulation-ready meshes from a single forward pass without post-hoc conversion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing feed-forward splatting methods rely on Gaussian primitives, requiring expensive post-processing to extract usable meshes for downstream tasks like simulation, physics reasoning, and embodied interaction—especially problematic in pose-free settings.

Method: TriSplat predicts oriented triangle primitives by: (1) constructing geometry normals from predicted point maps, (2) refining with an image-conditioned normal head, (3) converting to stable local frames for triangle param., (4) using mono-normal bootstrap, opacity, and blur scheduling for stable training and surface sharpening.

Result: On RealEstate10K and DL3DV, TriSplat produces more geometry-faithful reconstructions than Gaussian feed-forward baselines while maintaining competitive novel-view rendering quality.

Conclusion: Triangle primitives enable direct ingestion by physics engines, collision detectors, and rendering pipelines without conversion, making TriSplat a practical simulation-ready feed-forward reconstruction solution.

Abstract: Sparse-view 3D reconstruction is increasingly addressed with feed-forward splatting networks that predict explicit primitives directly from images. Yet most existing methods remain centered on Gaussian primitives and expose surfaces only indirectly: extracting a usable mesh for downstream simulation, physics reasoning, or embodied interaction still requires expensive post-hoc steps that break the feed-forward promise. This limitation is especially pronounced in pose-free settings, where scene structure and camera parameters must be estimated jointly from sparse observations. We present TriSplat, a feed-forward reconstruction network that represents scenes with oriented triangle primitives and directly exports simulation-ready mesh scenes from a single forward pass. Given input images, the network predicts local 3D point maps, triangle attributes, camera poses, and optional intrinsics. Rather than regressing triangle orientation as an unconstrained latent variable, our approach constructs geometry normals from the predicted point maps, refines them with an image-conditioned normal head, and converts them into stable local frames for triangle parameterization. A mono-normal bootstrap schedule further stabilizes early training, while opacity and blur scheduling progressively sharpens the learned surface representation for direct mesh extraction. Experiments on RealEstate10K and DL3DV show that this representation produces more geometry-faithful reconstructions than Gaussian feed-forward baselines while maintaining competitive novel-view rendering quality. Because the rendering primitives are themselves surface triangles, the output can be directly ingested by physics engines, collision detectors, and standard rendering pipelines without any conversion, making it a practical simulation-ready solution for feed-forward 3D scene reconstruction.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [255] [Radio Environment Mapping with World Models for Active Measurement Control: Should Networks Dream of Optimal Control?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24028)
*Jernej Hribar,Ljupcho Milosheski,Ryoichi Shinkuma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a world-model-inspired active REM construction method that uses a dreaming mechanism to sequentially select measurement locations, achieving up to 5x RMSE reduction over GP-based interpolation in few-shot RSSI map reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing REM construction methods are passive, relying on interpolation or static uncertainty models without an explicit mechanism to reason about how future measurements affect reconstruction quality under a limited measurement budget.

Method: Formulates REM construction as a sequential decision-making problem; uses a world model with an internal representation of the radio environment and a dreaming mechanism to simulate the impact of candidate measurements, actively selecting measurement locations under a budget.

Result: On real indoor RSSI data, the proposed method outperforms Gaussian Process-based interpolation in the few-shot regime, achieving up to a fivefold reduction in RMSE with the same number of measurements.

Conclusion: World models represent a powerful paradigm for sample-efficient radio environment mapping and intelligent model-based sensing in 6G and beyond networks.

Abstract: Radio Environment Maps (REMs) have the potential to serve as an important enabler for intelligent modeling and control in emerging AI-native 6G networks. Despite significant progress, most REM construction methods remain passive, relying on interpolation or static uncertainty models and lacking an explicit mechanism to reason about how future measurements will affect reconstruction quality under a limited measurement budget. In this paper, we formulate REM construction as a sequential decision-making problem and propose a world-model-inspired framework for active Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI) map reconstruction. By learning an internal representation of the radio environment and employing a dreaming mechanism to simulate the impact of candidate measurements, the proposed approach actively selects measurement locations under a limited budget. Experimental results on real indoor RSSI data demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms Gaussian Process-based interpolation in the few-shot regime, achieving up to a fivefold reduction in Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) with the same number of measurements. These results highlight the potential of world models as a powerful paradigm for sample-efficient radio environment mapping and intelligent model-based sensing in 6G and beyond networks.

</details>


### [256] [LWM-CDE: A Representation Space for Wireless Data Reasoning and Transferability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24077)
*Sadjad Alikhani,Akshay Malhotra,Shahab Hamidi-Rad,Ahmed Alkhateeb*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces LWM-CDE, a dataset similarity framework using a pretrained wireless foundation model's feature space with contrastive and geometry-shaping losses to assess transferability and data similarity efficiently, outperforming existing metrics across wireless communication tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning models in wireless communications suffer from generalization challenges due to location/environment-specific signal structures, high data diversity across deployments, and limited real-world data. Existing similarity and transferability metrics are computationally expensive and inconsistent.

Method: LWM-CDE fine-tunes dataset embeddings of a pretrained wireless foundation model using contrastive learning combined with geometry-shaping losses, creating a structured manifold where distance reliably indicates transferability.

Result: LWM-CDE achieves stronger correlation with empirical transfer performance and higher computational efficiency compared to existing metrics on wireless benchmarks.

Conclusion: The learned representation space enables effective decision-making for source dataset selection, label-aware augmentation, and budgeted pretraining, demonstrating broad utility across wireless communication applications.

Abstract: Machine learning deployments in real-world wireless communication tasks face significant generalization challenges due to location and environment-specific signal structure, high diversity in data across different deployments, and limited availability of real-world data. Current approaches for assessing data similarity between training and inference (deployment) distributions, as well as evaluating model transferability, suffer from high computational costs and inconsistent performance, leaving critical model deployment and model life cycle management decisions without a principled foundation. To address this, we introduce a dataset similarity framework built upon the feature space of a pretrained wireless foundation model. Our method, LWM-CDE (Contrastive learning of Dataset Embedding), fine-tunes the dataset embeddings of the foundation model using a combination of contrastive and geometry-shaping losses, creating a structured manifold where distance reliably indicates transferability. Extensive experiments on wireless benchmarks show that LWM-CDE achieves stronger correlation with empirical transfer performance than existing metrics while being more computationally efficient. The learned representation space supports more effective and data-efficient decision-making for tasks like source dataset selection, label-aware augmentation, and budgeted pretraining, demonstrating its broader utility across different wireless communication applications.

</details>


### [257] [Feasibility Study of VLC-Based Collective Perception for Vehicular Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24246)
*Kosuke Nakano,Shan Lu,Takaya Yamazato*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes using Visible Light Communication (VLC) to enhance Collective Perception (CP) in V2X systems, addressing spectrum scarcity in the 5.9 GHz band. Three experiments (stationary indoor/outdoor and driving up to 90 km/h) show VLC-based CP is feasible and promising.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Only 30 MHz of radio spectrum is allocated for V2X in regions like the US and Japan, which is insufficient to support Collective Perception effectively.

Method: The authors integrate VLC into V2X systems as a complementary optical channel, conducting three experiments: 1) application-level delay measurement in stationary indoor, 2) communication range evaluation in stationary outdoor, and 3) driving experiments at speeds up to 90 km/h.

Result: VLC-based CP is feasible under all tested conditions, demonstrating potential to address spectrum scarcity.

Conclusion: VLC-based CP could serve as a promising solution to spectrum scarcity in the 5.9 GHz band for future V2X communications.

Abstract: This study explores the use of Visible Light Communication (VLC) in Collective Perception (CP), a technology that enables vehicles and infrastructure to share sensor information to help reduce traffic accidents. Recent advances in Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication have spurred growing research interest in CP. However, in regions such as the United States and Japan, only 30 MHz of radio spectrum is allocated for V2X, which is insufficient to effectively support CP. In this paper, we propose integrating VLC into V2X systems to enhance CP, complementing the existing 5.9 GHz band for V2X communications. VLC can coexist with wireless systems that use radio waves, providing an additional optical channel for data exchange. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first study to investigate VLC for CP. We evaluate the feasibility of VLC-based CP through three experiments. First, we measured the application-level delay of a VLC-based CP system in a stationary indoor environment. Next, we evaluated its communication range in a stationary outdoor setting. Finally, to assess robustness under realistic conditions, we conducted driving experiments at vehicle speeds up to 90 km/h. The results demonstrate that VLC-based CP is feasible and could serve as a promising solution to spectrum scarcity in the 5.9 GHz band for future V2X communications.

</details>


### [258] [A Learning Method for Optimal Chirp Predistortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24256)
*Averal N. Kandala,Ali M. Niknejad*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces a "chart & chirp" method for one-shot VCO tuning curve estimation and predistortion, achieving low FM error (50-51 kHz RMS) and predicting nonlinearity-induced spurs via DFT analysis, with simulation results showing IF SNDR of ~40 dB (R<23m) and ~24 dB (R=92m) in radar models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide an alternative to LMS-based background calibration loops for VCO tuning curve estimation and predistortion, addressing nonlinearity-induced spurs and phase noise in FMCW radar systems.

Method: Uses a cycle-counting FDC for VCO tuning curve estimation, a linearly interpolating QDAC for predistortion actuation, an LLSE-based learning process for generating predistorted DAC codes, and DFT analysis to predict nonlinearity-induced spurs.

Result: Achieves 50 kHz and 51 kHz RMS FM error for 5 µs and 20 µs chirps respectively; deterministic phase error dominates at > -70 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz offset; IF SNDR ~40 dB for R<23m and ~24 dB for R=92m.

Conclusion: The chart & chirp method is effective for one-shot VCO calibration, with nonlinearity-induced phase error dominating random phase noise and limiting radar IF SNDR at specific ranges.

Abstract: This article introduces the "chart & chirp" method of one-shot, in situ VCO tuning curve estimation, learning, and predistortion, which provides an alternative to prevailing LMS-based background calibration loops. The proposed approach utilizes a cycle-counting FDC to estimate the VCO tuning curve and a linearly interpolating QDAC to actuate the predistortion. An LLSE-based learning process generates predistorted DAC control codes for any physically achievable chirp parameters. In simulation, 50 and 51 kHz of RMS FM error is achieved for 5 and 20 us chirps, respectively. The locations of nonlinearity-induced spurs in the IF spectrum are predicted via analysis of the DFT of FM error in generated chirps, with higher frequency DAC updates weakening these spurs and moving them out of the IF band of interest. Simulation in a coherent, monostatic radar model reveals that deterministic phase error from the chirp nonlinearity arises at a level above -70 dBc/Hz at 1 MHz offset, dominating random phase noise and setting the IF SNDR for R < 23 m and R = 92 m around 40 dB and 24 dB, respectively.

</details>


### [259] [A Digital Twin Platform Enabling Monolithic Crystal-Free Bluetooth Low Energy Single-Chip Sensor Motes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24277)
*Brandon P. Hippe,Jacob N. Louie,Dingyu Zhou,Alfonso Cortés,Filip Maksimovic,Tengfei Chang,David C. Burnett*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a digital twin ecosystem for Bluetooth Low-Energy (BLE) with physical-layer control, enabling pre-silicon validation and development of novel wireless devices. Tested with crystal-free SoC sensor motes, the system achieves -82 dBm receiver sensitivity, exceeding BLE specs, and supports multiple RF front ends and digital basebands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-power wireless SoCs are crucial for environmental monitoring but face high capital/labor costs. Novel wireless architectures lack commercial development platforms, making pre-silicon validation challenging and failed tapeouts unacceptable when cost is critical.

Method: The authors propose a digital twin ecosystem for BLE with PHY control, demonstrated using crystal-free single-chip sensor motes. The system operates with multiple RF front ends and digital basebands, including an SDR with synthesized RTL/embedded firmware, and an FPGA-based digital baseband with existing crystal-free SoC front end.

Result: The system communicates sensor data with commercial BLE devices and achieves receiver sensitivity up to -82 dBm, exceeding the minimum BLE specification.

Conclusion: This digital twin approach is extendable to other hardware and protocols, enabling inexpensive, reusable validation and verification tools for novel wireless devices.

Abstract: Low-power wireless-capable systems-on-chips (SoCs) are critical for researching many of our current environmental issues. The scale at which these devices are needed for many applications necessitates innovation in their design to reduce the various capital and labor costs involved with operating an extensive sensor network. This can be difficult for devices with novel wireless architectures, as many emerging architectures lack commercially available development platforms. This makes pre-silicon validation challenging, and the impact of a failed tapeout is unacceptable when the cost is of primary concern for these devices. In this work, we propose a digital twin ecosystem for Bluetooth Low-Energy (BLE) with physical-layer (PHY) control intended for novel device development and demonstrated through use with crystal-free single-chip sensor motes. We present this system operating with multiple RF front ends and digital baseband implementations, including a commercially available Software Defined Radio (SDR) with synthesized RTL and embedded firmware, along with an existing crystal-free SoC front end and FPGA digital baseband. These configurations are shown to be capable of communicating sensor data with commercially available BLE devices and achieving receiver sensitivities up to -82 dBm, exceeding the minimum BLE specification. This approach is extendable to other hardware and communication protocols and promises to enable inexpensive, reusable validation and verification tools for novel wireless devices.

</details>


### [260] [Posterior-Aware Differential Channel Tracking for Reliable Single-Stream DAB+ Passive Radar](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24385)
*Kai Wu,Brendan Hall,Zhongqin Wang,Andrew Zhang,Jay Guo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a posterior-probability-aware differential CSI tracking method for DAB+ passive radar that reduces CSI estimation error by over 15 dB and improves target-to-background ratio by more than 11 dB compared to prior art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: DAB+ signals are attractive for passive radar, but CSI estimation is challenging due to differentially encoded communication symbols across time, where wrong symbol transition estimates cause persistent multiplicative errors in the CSI sequence.

Method: The method formulates single-stream DAB+ passive radar as a posterior-probability-aware differential CSI tracking problem. It uses previously tracked CSI as a channel prior, performs prediction-aided maximum a posteriori detection, converts posterior transition reliability into observation uncertainty, and applies linear minimum mean squared error (LMMSE) fusion. A reliability-informed CSI fusion strategy preserves weak target information.

Result: Simulations show over 15 dB reduction in CSI estimation error and over 11 dB improvement in median target-to-background ratio in random fading scenes. Experiments in Sydney demonstrate improved range-Doppler maps for commercial aircraft sensing.

Conclusion: The proposed method effectively addresses the differential encoding challenge in DAB+ passive radar, delivering significant performance gains in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Abstract: Digital audio broadcasting plus (DAB+) is an attractive illuminator for passive radar because it provides persistent, high-power, and geographically widespread very high frequency (VHF) orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) signals. A channel state information (CSI) sensing approach can convert a single received DAB+ stream into a CSI sequence for radar sensing, avoiding the need for a separately received reference signal in conventional passive radars. However, CSI estimation in DAB+ is challenging due to the differentially encoded communication symbols across time. A wrong symbol transition estimation leads to a persistent multiplicative error in the sequential CSI sequence within a DAB+ frame. This paper formulates single-stream DAB+ passive radar as a posterior-probability-aware differential CSI tracking problem. The proposed method uses the previously tracked CSI as a channel prior, performs prediction-aided maximum a posteriori detection of current symbol, converts posterior transition reliability into observation uncertainty, and applies linear minimum mean squared error fusion to obtain a stable tracking CSI. A reliability-informed CSI fusion strategy is also introduced to preserve weak target information. Theoretical analysis is provided, showing guaranteed performance again in symbol and CSI estimation. Simulation results show that the proposed method can reduce CSI estimation error by over 15~dB compared with prior art. It also improves median target-to-background ratio by more than 11~dB in random fading scenes. Experiments in Sydney, Australia demonstrate improved range-Doppler maps for commercial aircraft sensing.

</details>


### [261] [Simulation-Driven Ensemble Machine Learning for Robust and Generalizable Path Loss Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24655)
*Ahmed P. Mohamed,Byunghyun Lee,Yaguang Zhang,Christopher R. Anderson,David J. Love,James V. Krogmeier*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes an ensemble machine learning framework for path loss prediction that combines real measurements with lidar-based synthetic data and SMOTE augmentation, achieving up to 50% MAE reduction compared to models trained on real data alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning for path loss prediction degrades when measurement data are scarce, which is a common practical limitation in wireless network planning.

Method: An ensemble-based framework integrating (1) lidar-based simulator for synthetic data with broad spatial coverage, (2) dynamically weighted ensemble balancing simulation and real measurements, and (3) SMOTE for data augmentation through interpolation between measurements.

Result: Up to 50% reduction in MAE compared to models trained solely on real data, and up to 25% improvement relative to models trained exclusively on synthetic data, especially for cross-environment generalization across urban, suburban, residential, industrial, and rural settings.

Conclusion: Combining simulation-based synthetic data with SMOTE effectively overcomes data scarcity and enhances generalization, offering a robust and practical solution for cost-effective wireless network planning.

Abstract: Machine learning has emerged as a promising approach to path loss prediction, yet its effectiveness often degrades when measurement data are scarce. To address this limitation, we propose an ensemble-based machine learning framework that integrates real measurements with synthetic data generated using a lidar-based simulator. The simulator provides broad spatial coverage through static path loss values that capture terrain variations and physical obstacles in the propagation environment. A dynamically weighted ensemble then combines simulation results with measured data, balancing the contribution of both data sources and improving generalization across diverse environments. To further mitigate the effects of limited measurements, we incorporate the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE), a data augmentation technique that synthesizes additional samples through interpolation between measurements while preserving their statistical properties. By leveraging simulation data, SMOTE, and engineered propagation features, the proposed framework captures geographical and physical variability, enabling adaptability across urban, suburban, residential, industrial, and rural environments. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves up to a 50% reduction in mean absolute error (MAE), compared with models trained solely on real data, and up to a 25% improvement relative to models trained exclusively on synthetic data, particularly for cross-environment generalization. These findings highlight the effectiveness of combining simulation-based synthetic data with SMOTE to overcome data scarcity and enhance the model's generalization ability. Overall, the proposed framework provides a robust and practical solution for path loss prediction across diverse environments with limited measurement data, supporting cost-effective planning and optimization of wireless networks.

</details>


### [262] [Time Segmented Beamforming via Dynamic Programming: Theory and Implementation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24825)
*Manan Mittal,Ryan M. Corey,Diego Cuji,John R. Buck,Andrew C. Singer*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper introduces a temporally segmented distortionless response beamformer that dynamically adapts covariance matrix estimation windows to local stationarity, improving Capon beamforming in non-stationary acoustic environments with moving interferers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional batch Capon beamforming assumes stationarity within fixed windows, but in dynamic acoustic environments with time-varying interferers, this assumption fails—moving interferers smear the sample covariance matrix and degrade nulling capabilities.

Method: Inspired by segmented least squares (fitting piecewise polynomials with penalty for excessive segmentation), the method extends practical Capon beamforming by incorporating data-driven temporal segmentation to dynamically adapt SCM estimation windows to local stationarity.

Result: The proposed approach minimizes output power while tracking time-varying interferers, overcoming the limitations of fixed-length or excessively long batch windows in non-stationary settings.

Conclusion: The temporally segmented distortionless response beamformer offers a principled, adaptive framework for effective beamforming in dynamic acoustic environments where interferers move over time.

Abstract: In dynamic acoustic environments with time-varying interferers, effective beamforming requires identifying stationary regions over time. The Capon beamformer, a whitened matched filter constrained to maintain unity gain in the desired direction, theoretically relies on the instantaneous ensemble covariance matrix. Practical implementations rely on the batch Capon (or Sample Matrix Inversion), which estimates the sample covariance matrix (SCM) by averaging over a block of snapshots. This practical approach implicitly assumes that the data within the batch window is stationary and can be coherently combined. In non-stationary settings, a batch approach that averages over fixed or excessively long windows fails, as moving interferers smear the SCM and degrade the beamformer's nulling capabilities. To address this, this paper introduces a temporally segmented distortionless response beamformer. Inspired by the segmented least squares method, which fits piecewise polynomials to data while penalizing excessive segmentation to prevent overfitting, the framework extends practical Capon beamforming by incorporating data-driven temporal segmentation. This formulation minimizes output power while dynamically adapting the SCM estimation windows to local stationarity, offering a principled approach to tracking time-varying interferers.

</details>


### [263] [Lattice Modulo Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24974)
*Yhonatan Kvich,Yonina C. Eldar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a lattice-theoretic framework for modulo sampling of multidimensional bandlimited signals, extending recovery guarantees and algorithms from component-wise modulo ADCs to any lattice. Using lattices with smaller normalized second moments (e.g., hexagonal in 2D, E8 in 8D) reduces reconstruction MSE by lowering folded signal power and quantization error, achieving 16.7% and 57% MSE reduction respectively, with a topological interpretation linking folding geometry to surface genus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard modulo ADCs fold signals component-wise into a square domain, limiting recovery to independent one-dimensional problems. The authors aim to extend this to any lattice structure to leverage better geometric properties for improved reconstruction performance.

Method: A lattice-theoretic framework is developed that generalizes modulo sampling to arbitrary lattices. Recovery guarantees are extended requiring the same sampling rate as the component-wise case, and algorithms are adapted for high-dimensional lattice settings. The framework is evaluated using hexagonal lattice (2D) and E8 lattice (8D).

Result: The hexagonal lattice reduces MSE by 16.7% over the square lattice at the same inradius in 2D. The E8 lattice achieves 57% MSE reduction in both additive and quantization noise for 8D signals. Higher-dimensional lattices provide substantially better second moments compared to the hypercube.

Conclusion: Lattice selection beyond the standard hypercube significantly improves modulo sampling performance. The framework offers both theoretical guarantees and practical gains, with a topological connection to surface genus and potential hardware implementation via comparator circuits.

Abstract: We propose a lattice-theoretic framework for modulo sampling of multidimensional bandlimited signals. Standard modulo analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) fold the signal component-wise into a square domain, reducing the recovery problem to independent one-dimensional problems. We extend the recovery guarantees to any lattice, requiring the same sampling rate as in the standard component-wise modulo setting. We also extend existing recovery algorithms to the general highdimensional lattice setting.
