Selecting a lattice with a smaller normalized second moment reduces the reconstruction mean squared error (MSE) through two complementary mechanisms: it lowers the folded signal power, which reduces the absolute noise energy at a fixed signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and it reduces the quantization error when a matched lattice quantizer is applied. Higher-dimensional lattices offer better second moment compared to the hypercube lattice, with gains that grow substantially with dimension. Instantiating the framework in two dimensions with the hexagonal lattice reduces the MSE relative to the square at the same inradius by 16.7%. Furthermore, simulations on 8-dimensional signals using the E8 lattice to achive 57% in both additive and quantization noise. A topological interpretation connects each folding geometry to a surface whose genus reflects the lattice complexity, and reveals a natural hardware implementation via comparator circuits.

</details>


### [264] [HHK: A Hardware-Oriented Cross-Location PPG Key Generation Architecture for Body Area Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24991)
*Jose Ilton de Oliveira Filho*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: HHK is a hardware-oriented PPG-based key generation architecture for body area networks, implemented on FPGA with 48 μJ/event, using IBI extraction, Gray-coded quantization, and polar code fuzzy commitment, achieving up to 69.8% key agreement across head, wrist, and ankle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BANs need lightweight session key establishment, but traditional public key crypto is too costly for constrained wearable nodes. A low-power, hardware-friendly key generation method is needed.

Method: Extracts inter-beat intervals (IBIs) from green-light PPG at multiple body sites; aligns beat timestamps; applies Gray-coded equal-frequency quantization; uses rate-1/3 polar code fuzzy commitment (N=128, K=42) for error reconciliation. Implemented as synthesizable RTL on Xilinx XC7Z020.

Result: 18,760 LUTs, 20,971 FFs, 48 μJ per key generation, 0.4 μW average power over 120s window; 86.0-90.1% raw IBI bit agreement, 51.3-69.8% key agreement across 16 participants (216 hours of ambulatory data).

Conclusion: HHK is the first synthesizable RTL key generation architecture for BANs validated on ambulatory data across multiple body locations, demonstrating feasible hardware-efficient biometric key agreement.

Abstract: Body area networks (BANs) require lightweight session key establishment, yet public key exchange imposes computation and energy costs that exceed the budgets of deeply constrained wearable nodes. This brief presents HHK, a hardware-oriented cross-location photoplethysmography (PPG) key generation architecture for BANs. The proposed datapath extracts inter-beat intervals (IBIs) from green-light PPG at multiple body sites, aligns beat timestamps across locations, applies Gray-coded equal-frequency quantization, and employs a rate-1/3 polar code fuzzy commitment (N=128, K=42) to reconcile residual timing mismatches. Post-implementation synthesis on a Xilinx XC7Z020 maps the complete datapath to 18,760 lookup tables and 20,971 flip-flops with no multipliers or embedded memories, giving 48 uJ per key generation event and 0.4 uW average power over a 120-second acquisition window. Validation across 16 participants from a real ambulatory dataset (216 hours; head, wrist, and ankle) yields 86.0-90.1% raw IBI bit agreement and 51.3-69.8% key agreement. To the best of our knowledge, HHK is the first synthesizable register-transfer level (RTL) key generation architecture for BANs validated across multiple body locations on ambulatory data.

</details>


### [265] [A Unified KLD Framework for Duplexity and Deployment Paradigms in Cell-Free mMIMO-ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25018)
*Yousef Kloob,Mohammad Al-Jarrah,Emad Alsusa*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper presents a unified analytical framework comparing deployment and duplexing paradigms in distributed cell-free massive MIMO ISAC systems, analyzing four configurations with realistic impairments, using Kullback-Leibler divergence and GLRT to show FD outperforms HD with sufficient SI suppression, and shared deployment improves radar but increases communication-radar coupling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a unified analytical framework for comparing deployment (separated vs shared) and duplexing (HD vs FD) paradigms in distributed CF-mMIMO ISAC systems under realistic impairments, enabling direct comparison of communication and radar performance on a common scale.

Method: Four configurations (separated/shared AP deployment × HD/FD operation) are analyzed with residual SI, imperfect interference cancellation, and clutter. KLD serves as a unified metric; a GLRT framework produces closed-form expressions linking KLD to detection probability. Monte Carlo simulations verify the expressions.

Result: FD operation achieves substantial gains over HD when sufficient SI suppression and interference cancellation quality are maintained, while preserving strong radar detection. Shared deployment enhances radar performance via larger effective aperture but exhibits tighter communication-radar coupling than separated deployment.

Conclusion: The results establish deployment guidelines and quantitative design thresholds for next-generation CF-mMIMO ISAC systems, revealing trade-offs between radar and communication performance under different configurations.

Abstract: This paper develops a unifying analytical framework for comparing deployment and duplexing paradigms in distributed cell-free massive multiple-input multiple-output (CF-mMIMO) integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems. The system comprises distributed access points (APs) serving multiple downlink and uplink users while simultaneously detecting radar targets. Four configurations are analysed - separated and shared AP deployment under half-duplex (HD) and full-duplex (FD) operation, each incorporating realistic impairments: residual self-interference (SI) from transmit-receive leakage, imperfect interference cancellation due to channel estimation errors, and clutter. Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD) is applied to serve as a unified measure, enabling direct comparison of communication and radar performance on a common scale. A generalised likelihood ratio test (GLRT) framework is developed to produce closed-form expressions linking KLD to detection probability. Monte Carlo simulations are used to verify our expressions, which demonstrate that FD operation achieves substantial gains over HD, provided sufficient SI suppression and IC quality are maintained, while preserving strong radar detection. It is also shown that shared deployment enhances radar performance via a larger effective aperture but exhibits tighter communication-radar coupling than separated deployment. These results establish deployment guidelines and quantitative design thresholds for next-generation CF-mMIMO ISAC systems.

</details>


### [266] [CSPMNet: Pareto-Efficient Automatic Modulation Classification With Learnable Complex Subband Phase Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25099)
*Ruixiang Zhang,Zinan Zhou,Yezhuo Zhang,Guangyu Li,Xuanpeng Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CSPMNet is a lightweight neural network for automatic modulation classification that uses complex subband filters and phase-motion modules to achieve competitive accuracy with much lower model complexity than existing deep learning approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep-learning AMC methods improve accuracy by expanding generic neural backbones, increasing deployment cost and limiting suitability for resource-constrained receivers. There is a need to bridge the gap between recognition performance and model efficiency.

Method: Proposes CSPMNet with three components: (1) learnable complex subband filters to extract frequency-selective baseband responses while preserving I/Q coupling, (2) an amplitude-preserving phase-motion module to capture multi-lag temporal rotation dynamics per subband, and (3) a lightweight temporal classifier for efficient sequence aggregation.

Result: On public RadioML benchmarks, CSPMNet achieves highly competitive recognition accuracy while requiring substantially lower model complexity than many existing AMC models.

Conclusion: CSPMNet effectively bridges the gap between recognition performance and model efficiency, making it suitable for resource-constrained receivers and noncooperative spectrum monitoring.

Abstract: Automatic modulation classification (AMC) is an essential technique for noncooperative spectrum monitoring and intelligent wireless receivers. However, practical AMC models must identify modulation formats from short and noisy I/Q observations while maintaining low computational and storage overhead. Existing deep-learning approaches often improve recognition accuracy by expanding generic neural backbones, which increases deployment cost and weakens their suitability for resource-constrained receivers. To bridge the gap between recognition performance and model efficiency, this letter proposes a Complex Subband Phase-Motion Network, designated as CSPMNet, for lightweight AMC from raw I/Q samples. Specifically, learnable complex subband filters are introduced to adaptively extract frequency-selective baseband responses while preserving the algebraic coupling between in-phase and quadrature components. Then, an amplitude-preserving phase-motion module captures multi-lag temporal rotation dynamics within each subband, and a lightweight temporal classifier performs efficient sequence aggregation. Rigorous experimental evaluations on public RadioML benchmark datasets demonstrate that CSPMNet achieves highly competitive recognition accuracy while requiring substantially lower model complexity than many existing AMC models.

</details>


### [267] [A 100 GHz Wideband Reconfigurable Intelligent Surface Based on Orthogonal Polarization and Sub-Array Partitioning Concepts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25138)
*Ruiqi Wang,Behrooz Makki,Atif Shamim*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper demonstrates a wideband PCB-based reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) operating around 100 GHz, using an orthogonal-polarization slot-coupled patch structure with subarray partitioning to mitigate switch parasitic effects. A 12×8 prototype achieves ~10 dB gain enhancement from 86-100 GHz and ~5 dB from 100-106 GHz with only 0.165 W power consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sub-terahertz frequencies (around 100 GHz) offer huge bandwidth for ultra-high data rates but suffer from severe propagation loss and blockage. Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces (RIS) can dynamically shape EM waves to enhance coverage, but realizing them with low-cost PCB technology at these frequencies is challenging due to fabrication limits, switch availability, switch parasitic effects, and control complexity.

Method: The design combines an orthogonal-polarization slot-coupled patch structure with subarray partitioning to mitigate switch-induced parasitic effects, reduce the number of RF switches, and simplify control. AlGaAs SP3T bare-die switches are integrated via optimized bond-wire interconnections. A six-subarray structure with 4×4 elements per subarray was designed, and a 12×8 prototype was fabricated and experimentally characterized.

Result: Measured gain enhancement of ~10 dB from 86-100 GHz and ~5 dB from 100-106 GHz, with a low power consumption of 0.165 W.

Conclusion: The results validate the feasibility of practical wideband PCB-based reconfigurable intelligent surfaces for sub-terahertz wireless systems, demonstrating a promising low-cost solution for coverage enhancement at these frequencies.

Abstract: The sub-terahertz frequency band offers extremely large bandwidth and enables ultra-high data rates for future wireless applications. However, severe propagation loss and blockage significantly limit coverage at these frequencies. Reconfigurable intelligent surfaces can dynamically shape EM wave propagation and provide a promising solution for coverage enhancement. Realizing such surfaces using standard printed circuit board technology is attractive due to its low cost and scalability, but it remains challenging around 100 GHz because of fabrication limits, limited switch availability, large switch size compared with the unit cell, switch parasitic effects, and high control complexity. In this work, we demonstrate a wideband PCB-based reconfigurable intelligent surface operating around 100 GHz. The design combines an orthogonal-polarization slot-coupled patch structure with subarray partitioning to mitigate switch-induced parasitic effects, reduce the required number of RF switches, and simplify the control architecture. The reconfigurability is achieved using AlGaAs SP3T bare-die switches integrated through optimized bond-wire interconnections. For proof of concept, a six-subarray structure with 4 by 4 elements per subarray is designed for different beamforming angles, and a 12 by 8 prototype is fabricated and experimentally characterized. The measured results show a gain enhancement of about 10 dB from 86 to 100 GHz and about 5 dB from 100 to 106 GHz, while maintaining a low power consumption of 0.165 W. These results validate the feasibility of practical wideband PCB-based reconfigurable intelligent surfaces for sub-terahertz wireless systems.

</details>


### [268] [An Extended Object Poisson Multi-Bernoulli Filter with Zero-Inflated Poisson Model Using Belief Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25316)
*Yuxuan Xia,Xueqi Qiu,Hyowon Kim,Chaoqun Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an efficient particle BP implementation of the extended object PMB filter under the ZIP measurement model, achieving superior accuracy and speed compared to existing sampling-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To efficiently handle empty measurement sets in extended object tracking by leveraging the ZIP model, and to overcome the computational burden of existing sampling-based implementations of the PMB filter.

Method: The method factorizes the joint posterior over objects with detection variables and uses a dual representation of data association (object-oriented and measurement-oriented variables), replacing global hypothesis constraints with local consistency factors for BP. A particle-based implementation is used where the undetected Poisson intensity is analytic and detected Bernoulli components are particle-based.

Result: Simulation results show the proposed method outperforms existing sampling-based extended object PMB filter with ZIP model in both estimation accuracy and runtime.

Conclusion: The particle BP implementation of the ZIP-PMB filter provides a more efficient and accurate solution for extended object tracking with empty measurement sets.

Abstract: This paper presents an efficient implementation of the extended object Poisson multi-Bernoulli (PMB) filter under the zero-inflated Poisson (ZIP) object measurement model using particle belief propagation (BP). The ZIP measurement model separates a Bernoulli object detection event from the conditional Poisson generation of object measurements, enabling principled handling of empty measurement sets. Building upon the PMB mixture posterior, we present a factorized joint posterior over set of objects with object detection variables and a dual representation of data association using both object-oriented and measurement-oriented association variables. Notably, this representation replaces the implicit high-order global hypothesis constraint by local consistency factors, yielding a factor graph amenable to BP. In addition, we present a particle-based implementation, in which the Poisson intensity for undetected objects is analytic, whereas the single object densities of Bernoulli components for the detected objects are represented using particles. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method has superior performance than existing sampling-based implementations of extended object PMB filter with ZIP model in terms of both estimation accuracy and runtime.

</details>


### [269] [A Token/KV-Cache Communication Media Selection and Resource Allocation Strategy for Multi-Agent Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25422)
*Lipeng Dai,Luping Xiang,Kun Yang*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a joint communication-media selection and wireless resource allocation framework to minimize end-to-end latency in LLM-powered multi-agent systems over 6G networks, demonstrating adaptive trade-offs between token-based and KV-cache-based interaction under varying computational and channel conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-empowered multi-agent cooperation in 6G networks generates heavy east-west traffic. Prior work often ignores realistic wireless constraints and communication overhead, while heterogeneous interaction media (NL, token-based, KV-cache) incur different inference/transmission costs, creating an inherent E2E latency trade-off that needs joint optimization.

Method: The authors analytically characterize the latency trade-off between token-based and KV-cache-based transmission, formulate a joint optimization problem for media selection and wireless resource allocation, and develop a low-complexity JMSRA algorithm to minimize E2E latency in embodied multi-agent settings.

Result: Neither token-based nor KV-cache-based transmission is uniformly optimal; performance depends on computational resources and channel conditions. The proposed JMSRA scheme achieves markedly reduced E2E latency compared to NL-only and KV-cache-only baselines by adaptively coordinating interaction media and bandwidth allocation over heterogeneous links.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing communication-media selection and wireless resource allocation enables efficient and robust multi-agent collaboration in future wireless networks, adapting to heterogeneous system conditions.

Abstract: The convergence of large language models (LLMs) with 6G networks is fostering a paradigm of autonomous multi-agent cooperation, which in turn is expected to substantially increase east-west traffic. Although latent-space interaction mechanisms can enable more efficient collaboration than symbolic natural-language (NL) exchanges, prior work often abstracts away the associated communication overhead under practical wireless constraints. In embodied multi-agent settings, heterogeneous interaction media incur disparate inference and transmission costs, thereby inducing an inherent end-to-end (E2E) latency trade-off. To address this, we propose a joint design that integrates communication-media selection with wireless resource allocation. Through analytical characterization and simulation-based evaluation, we show that neither token-based transmission nor key-value (KV) cache-based transmission is uniformly optimal across operating regimes, as performance depends critically on system parameters such as available computational resources and channel conditions. Accordingly, we formulate a joint optimization problem aimed at minimizing the E2E latency of multi-agent collaboration and develop a low-complexity joint media selection and resource allocation (JMSRA) algorithm. Numerical results further confirm that, by adaptively coordinating the interaction media and bandwidth allocation over heterogeneous links, the proposed scheme achieves markedly reduced E2E latency relative to conventional NL-only and KV-cache-only baselines, enabling efficient and robust multi-agent collaboration in future wireless networks.

</details>


### [270] [Deep Machine Learning in MIMO Communication Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25458)
*Mohammad Reza Ghavidel Aghdam,Alireza Naghavi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an autoencoder-based end-to-end MIMO communication system that jointly optimizes transmitter, receiver, and channel under Rayleigh fading conditions, achieving lower bit error rates than conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve MIMO communication systems by replacing conventional block-based processing with an end-to-end deep learning approach that can better handle real-world wireless impairments like noise and fading.

Method: An autoencoder framework jointly optimizes transmitter, receiver, and channel with Rayleigh fading integrated into the training process, using a novel optimization procedure tailored for deep learning-based MIMO systems.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate significantly lower BER across various SNR levels compared to conventional block-based processing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end learning approach shows strong potential for more efficient and reliable wireless communication systems.

Abstract: This paper presents an innovative approach to enhancing machine learning based communication systems, specifically focusing on multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) configurations using autoencoders. We optimize the transmitter, receiver, and channel simultaneously under conditions of noise and channel fading, aiming to minimize the bit error rate (BER). By incorporating the Rayleigh fading channel a widely recognized model for wireless channel impairments into the autoencoder framework, we directly train the communication system to handle real world conditions. We introduce a novel optimization process tailored for deep learning-based MIMO communication, and thoroughly analyze the resulting BER performance across various signal to noise ratio (SNR) levels. Our simulation results reveal that the proposed end-to-end wireless communication system achieves significantly lower BER compared to conventional block-based processing methods, highlighting its potential for more efficient and reliable wireless communication.

</details>


### [271] [From Denoising to Decision Making: A Survey on Diffusion Model-Enabled Deep Reinforcement Learning for Wireless Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25531)
*Nguyen Cong Luong,Zeping Sui,Jie Cao,Min Xu,Nguyen Duc Hai,Zhihao Dong,Nguyen Duc Duy Anh,Qiushi Zhao,Nguyen Quoc Khanh,Zhe Fu,Shaohan Feng,Dusit Niyato,Bo Ma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper surveys the integration of diffusion models (DMs) with deep reinforcement learning (DRL) for wireless network resource management, highlighting how DM-enabled DRL policies overcome traditional DRL limitations by capturing complex, multimodal action structures. It covers theoretical foundations, algorithms, and applications across mobile edge computing, UAV-assisted systems, vehicular networks, AIGC-driven systems, resource allocation, physical-layer security, and robotics/UAV planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional DRL methods suffer from unimodal policy distributions, inefficient exploration in high-dimensional action spaces, and poor adaptability to dynamic wireless environments. Diffusion models' ability to model complex, multi-modal data distributions offers a path to overcome these limitations.

Method: A comprehensive survey approach: first providing theoretical background on diffusion models, then categorizing and reviewing DM-enabled DRL algorithms, followed by systematic application reviews across multiple wireless network domains.

Result: DM-enabled policies substantially enhance decision quality by capturing complex, discontinuous, and multimodal action structures inherent in wireless resource management, outperforming conventional DRL approaches.

Conclusion: DM-DRL integration is a rapidly growing research direction with demonstrated success across diverse wireless applications; future research directions are highlighted for continued advancement.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (DRL) has long been a promising solution for sequential resource management in wireless networks. However, conventional DRL methods are fundamentally limited by their reliance on unimodal policy distributions, inefficient exploration in high-dimensional action spaces, and poor adaptability to dynamic and heterogeneous environments. Meanwhile, diffusion models (DMs) as one of the most powerful families of generative AI have demonstrted remarkable capabilities in modeling complex, multi-modal data distributions across diverse domains. The integration of DMs and DRL has opened a new and rapidly growing research direction, in which DM-enabled policies substantially enhance decision quality by capturing the complex, discontinuous, and multimodal action structures inherent in wireless resource management. In this paper, we present a comprehensive survey of DM-enabled DRL algorithms and their applications for various issues in wireless networks. Particularly, we first provide the theoretical background of DM and present different DM-enabled DRL algorithms. We then systematically review applications of DM-enabled DRL for across computation offloading in mobile edge computing, UAV-assisted, vehicular, and AIGC-driven systems, as well as wireless resource allocation, physical-layer security, and robotics and UAV planning. We conclude the paper by higlight future research directions.

</details>


### [272] [Projected multi-reference alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25533)
*Amnon Balanov,Josh Katz,Tamir Bendory,Dan Edidin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper shows that for projected multi-reference alignment (MRA), the first three moments of noisy observations suffice to recover the dihedral orbit of an unknown signal in the high-noise regime, with sample complexity scaling as n ≳ σ⁶, and validates this through EM and moment-based optimization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motivated by structural biology applications, the authors aim to recover an unknown signal from noisy samples that undergo random cyclic shifts and a fixed projection that discards orientation information, addressing the challenge of determining the dihedral orbit under high noise.

Method: The paper reduces projected MRA to reflection-invariant phase-coupling structure of dihedral MRA at the moment level. In Fourier-cosine coordinates, the first moment gives the mean, the second gives Fourier magnitudes, and selected third moments yield cosine phase-coupling relations (dihedral bispectrum), enabling constructive recovery from moments up to order three. Finite-sample experiments compare EM, direct moment optimization, and direct Fourier-cosine moment optimization.

Result: The first three moments determine a generic dihedral orbit in the high-noise regime. Experiments show both EM and direct moment optimization achieve sample-complexity scaling n ≳ σ⁶, consistent with theoretical predictions.

Conclusion: The third-moment sample-complexity scaling n ≳ σ⁶ holds for projected MRA, and both moment-based and EM approaches are effective in the high-noise regime for dihedral orbit recovery.

Abstract: Motivated by structural biology applications, we study the projected multi-reference alignment (MRA) model, in which an unknown signal is observed through noisy samples, each generated by applying a random cyclic shift followed by a fixed projection. The projection merges reflection-symmetric index pairs, thereby discarding orientation information. The goal is to recover the dihedral orbit of the signal. We prove that in the high-noise regime, the first three moments of the projected observations determine a generic dihedral orbit. The main mechanism is a reduction, at the moment level, from projected MRA to the reflection-invariant phase-coupling structure of dihedral MRA. In Fourier-cosine coordinates adapted to the projection, the first moment determines the mean component, the second moment determines the Fourier magnitudes, and selected third moments yield the cosine phase-coupling relations appearing in the dihedral bispectrum. These relations lead to a constructive recovery scheme from moments up to order three. We complement the population theory with finite-sample experiments comparing expectation--maximization (EM), direct moment optimization, and direct Fourier-cosine moment optimization. The results show that, in the high-noise regime, both EM and direct moment optimization are consistent with the predicted third-moment sample-complexity scaling $n \gtrsim σ^6$, where $n$ is the number of observations and $σ^2$ is the noise variance.

</details>


### [273] [Time-Varying Parametric Channel Estimation With CP Decomposition Tensor Processing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25593)
*Enrique T. R. Pinto,André L. F. de Almeida,Markku Juntti*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fast parametric channel estimation (PCE) algorithm for time-varying frequency-selective channels using CP decomposition and tensor processing, achieving near-optimal performance with about 10x faster execution than the SAGE benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PCE is crucial for ISAC in 6G systems but is computationally demanding, motivating the need for lower-complexity solutions.

Method: A fast PCE algorithm based on canonical polyadic (CP) decomposition, tensor processing, ESPRIT-based initialization, component refinement, and exact line-search alternating coordinate descent. Two variants: fully digital and hybrid receiver architectures.

Result: The proposed method outperforms a CP-based baseline and achieves performance close to multiple-start SAGE, with about one order of magnitude shorter execution time.

Conclusion: The proposed algorithm offers a compelling trade-off between estimation accuracy and computational efficiency for ISAC systems.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) is a key use case for sixth-generation (6G) wireless systems, where parametric channel estimation (PCE) plays a central role in enabling sensing, localization, and channel equalization in high-mobility scenarios. However, PCE is typically more computationally demanding than conventional channel estimation, which motivates the development of lower-complexity solutions. In this letter, we propose a fast PCE algorithm for time-varying and frequency-selective (TVFS) channels based on canonical polyadic (CP) decomposition and tensor processing, combined with ESPRIT-based initialization, component refinement, and exact line-search alternating coordinate descent. Two variants are presented: one for fully digital and another for hybrid receiver architectures. Numerical results show that the proposed method clearly outperforms a related CP-based baseline while achieving estimation performance close to a multiple-start SAGE benchmark at a substantially lower computational cost, with about one order of magnitude shorter execution time.

</details>


### [274] [Robust Quantum-MUSIC for DoA Estimation Using Rydberg Atomic Receiver Arrays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25688)
*Sourav Banerjee,Neel Kanth Kundu,Prajwalita Borah*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes Robust Quantum-MUSIC (RobQMUSIC) that uses ℓ1-norm phase retrieval via IRLS to make quantum wireless sensing with Rydberg receivers robust to outliers, outperforming standard Quantum-MUSIC under faulty conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantum-MUSIC for Rydberg receiver DoA estimation works well but collapses under outlier measurements from hardware faults, saturation, or adversarial interference due to its ℓ2-norm phase retrieval sensitivity.

Method: Replaces ℓ2-norm with ℓ1-norm in the phase retrieval step, solved via an Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) scheme embedded within the alternating minimization loop, without increasing structural complexity.

Result: RobQMUSIC matches Quantum-MUSIC accuracy under ideal conditions and maintains robust performance across wide outlier contamination levels where Quantum-MUSIC fails entirely.

Conclusion: The ℓ1-norm formulation with IRLS effectively robustifies quantum wireless sensing for DoA estimation against outliers with no added complexity.

Abstract: Quantum wireless sensing using Rydberg atomic receivers enables high-sensitivity signal acquisition direction-of-arrival (DoA) estimation. However, it suffers from a fundamental limitation, where only the magnitude of the received signal is observable. The recently proposed Quantum-MUSIC algorithm addresses this problem by recovering phase information through alternating minimization and subsequently applying the MUSIC algorithm for DoA estimation. However, the existing approach relies on an $\ell_2$-norm phase retrieval step, making it highly sensitive to outlier measurements produced by hardware faults, sensor saturation, or adversarial interference. In this letter, we propose a \emph{Robust Quantum-MUSIC} (RobQMUSIC) framework that replaces the $\ell_2$-norm with an $\ell_1$-norm formulation. The resulting weighted phase-retrieval problem is solved efficiently via an Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) scheme embedded within the alternating minimization loop, requiring no increase in structural complexity relative to the baseline algorithm. Simulation results demonstrate that RobQMUSIC achieves near-identical DoA estimation accuracy to Quantum-MUSIC under ideal conditions, while maintaining robust performance over a wide range of outlier contamination levels at which Quantum-MUSIC fails entirely.

</details>


### [275] [The Symmetric Location Problem: a Song of Efficiency and Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25870)
*Stefano Fortunati*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This Lecture Note introduces semiparametric statistics to the Signal Processing community, highlighting its ability to achieve both statistical efficiency and robustness (distribution-freeness) by estimating finite-dimensional parameters while handling infinite-dimensional nuisance parameters, illustrated through the symmetric location problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To introduce the Signal Processing community to semiparametric statistics, a powerful but under-utilized tool that can reconcile statistical efficiency and robustness (distribution-freeness), filling a gap between parametric and non-parametric approaches in SP applications.

Method: The paper focuses on the symmetric location problem as a case study, developing semiparametric estimation methodology that handles finite-dimensional parameters of interest while treating infinite-dimensional nuisance parameters (e.g., noise density).

Result: Symmetric location problem methodology is developed, which the authors claim can be extended to more general semiparametric estimation problems like location vector and covariance matrix estimation in elliptical data.

Conclusion: Semiparametric statistics offers a unique advantage over parametric and non-parametric methods by simultaneously achieving statistical efficiency and robustness (distribution-freeness), applicable to numerous SP domains including source localization, time synchronization, array signal processing, and distributed sensor networks.

Abstract: The aim of this Lecture Note is to introduce the Signal Processing (SP) community to a powerful yet still under-utilised tool: the semiparametric statistics. In short, the semiparametric framework allows us to estimate or perform hypothesis testing on a finite-dimensional parameter in the presence of an infinite-dimensional nuisance parameter (i.e. a function), such as the density of the noise. Clearly, this framework is general enough to include almost every SP application.
  Remarkably, as the title suggests drawing on George R. R. Martin's famous book series, the greatest advantage of semiparametric statistics over parametric and non-parametric ones lies in the fact that it is able to reconcile two seemingly dichotomous concepts: statistical efficiency and robustness. Here, robustness is understood in the sense of distribution-freeness, that is the estimation performance must be robust with respect to the lack of knowledge of the functional form of the generating data distribution.
  To explain exactly what this means, in this Lecture Note we will focus our attention on the famous and fundamental symmetric location problem.
  The symmetric location problem is a fundamental problem that can be found (in various forms) in countless areas of SP: source localization, time synchronization, array signal processing, and distributed sensor networks, just to name a few. Furthermore, it is important to note that the methodology we will develop for this specific problem can be extended to much more general semiparametric estimation problems, such as the estimation of the location vector and covariance matrix in elliptical data.

</details>


### [276] [Alt-CC-PINN: An Alternating Optimization Framework with Implicit Neural Representation for Microwave Inverse Scattering Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26042)
*Shilong Sun*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Alt-CC-PINN framework decouples microwave physical field evolution from neural network inference using an alternating optimization strategy with PR-CG and batched 2D-FFT, achieving robust high-contrast target reconstruction in microwave inverse scattering imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantitative reconstruction of high-contrast targets in MISI suffers from severe multiple scattering effects and ill-posedness, making traditional joint optimization prone to local minima.

Method: Proposes Alt-CC-PINN with cross-correlated loss: (1) PR-CG algorithm updates contrast sources analytically; (2) batched zero-padded 2D-FFT ensures efficiency; (3) deep learning optimizer updates neural representation alternately.

Result: Superior reconstruction fidelity and robustness demonstrated on simulated and measured data, effectively overcoming local minima in high-contrast and low-SNR environments under frequency-hopping probing.

Conclusion: Alt-CC-PINN provides a powerful and reliable computational electromagnetic solver for practical microwave imaging systems.

Abstract: Microwave inverse scattering imaging (MISI) is a crucial computational technique in microwave nondestructive evaluation and near-field microwave sensing systems. However, quantitative reconstruction of high-contrast targets remains a formidable challenge due to severe multiple scattering effects and the inherent ill-posedness of electromagnetic inverse problems. To overcome this fundamental bottleneck in computational microwave imaging, this paper proposes an alternating optimization framework based on cross-correlated physics-informed neural network (Alt-CC-PINN). This architecture deeply decouples the evolution of the microwave physical field from the neural-network-based dielectric parameter inference, replacing traditional joint optimization with a hybrid alternating engine. Specifically, the method employs an analytical Polak-Ribière conjugate gradient (PR-CG) algorithm driven by a cross-correlated loss to optimally update the contrast sources, and deploys batched zero-padded 2D-FFT to ensure high computational efficiency. Subsequently, a deep learning optimizer is utilized to update the continuous neural representation. Extensive validations based on simulated and measured data demonstrate that Alt-CC-PINN effectively overcomes the local minima problem in high-contrast and low-signal-to-noise-ratio (SNR) environments. It exhibits superior reconstruction fidelity and robustness under the frequency-hopping probing strategy, providing a powerful and reliable computational electromagnetic solver for practical microwave imaging systems.

</details>


### [277] [Blind SNR Estimation for FSO Communication Systems with Deep Fading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26063)
*Humberto V. Q. Melo,Robson A. Colares,Darli A. A. Mello*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper evaluates M2M4 and EVM methods for real-time SNR estimation in FSO communication systems under deep fading, finding M2M4 reliably tracks SNR and is suitable for transceiver adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate reliable real-time SNR estimation methods for FSO communication systems that experience deep fading, enabling adaptive transceiver operations.

Method: Experimental setup with controlled deep fading conditions was used to compare the M2M4 (moment-based) and EVM (error vector magnitude) methods for SNR estimation.

Result: The M2M4 estimator reliably tracks the SNR profile under deep fading conditions, while the evaluation compared it against the EVM method.

Conclusion: The M2M4 method is suitable for real-time SNR estimation and can effectively trigger transceiver adaptation in FSO communication systems subject to deep fading.

Abstract: We evaluate the M2M4 and EVM methods for real-time SNR estimation in FSO communication systems subject to deep fading. Using an experimental setup with controlled deep fading, we show that the M2M4 estimator reliably tracks the SNR profile, making it suitable for triggering transceiver adaptation.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [278] [RED: Adaptive Real-Time DAG Scheduling for Robotic Inference under Environmental Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24044)
*Zexin Li,Tao Ren,Johnathan Liu,Xiaoxi He,Cong Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RED is a real-time scheduling framework for multi-task DNN workloads on resource-constrained robots that adapts to dynamic environmental changes while preserving timing guarantees, using deadline-aware scheduling and MIMONet graph reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robots in dynamic environments face runtime changes (new tasks, shifting precedence, evolving workloads) that degrade performance, especially under tight resource and real-time constraints for multi-task inference.

Method: RED uses a deadline-aware scheduler with intermediate sub-deadlines to handle evolving computation graphs and asynchronous inference, plus a workload refinement and graph-reconstruction procedure that aligns MIMONet's shared-parameter structure with schedulability requirements.

Result: Evaluated on NVIDIA Jetson and Apple M-series platforms with navigation-oriented workloads, RED shows consistent improvements in throughput, deadline satisfaction, robustness to interference, adaptability, and runtime overhead over existing methods.

Conclusion: RED effectively addresses real-time scheduling for multi-task robotic workloads under dynamic environmental changes, demonstrating practical benefits across multiple hardware platforms.

Abstract: Robots deployed in dynamic environments must contend with environment-driven changes that reshape computation at runtime: new tasks may appear, precedence relations can shift, and overall workload structure evolves, all of which degrade performance, especially when multi-task inference is required under tight resource and real-time budgets. We present RED, a real-time scheduling framework for multi-task deep neural network workloads on resource-constrained robotic platforms that adapts to Robotic Environmental Dynamics (RED) while preserving end-to-end timing guarantees under modeling assumptions. The core of RED is a deadline-aware scheduler that assigns intermediate sub-deadlines, allowing it to accommodate evolving computation graphs and asynchronous inference induced by unpredictable conditions. The framework also supports flexible deployment of MIMONet (multi-input multi-output neural networks), commonly used in multi-tasking robots to alleviate memory pressure through weight sharing. RED explicitly leverages this shared-parameter property via a workload refinement and graph-reconstruction procedure that aligns MIMONet structure with schedulability requirements, improving compatibility and efficiency. We implement RED on NVIDIA Jetson family platforms and on an Apple M-series MacBook and evaluate it on navigation-oriented workloads representative of real robotic scenarios. Experiments show consistent gains over existing methods in throughput, deadline satisfaction, robustness to interference, adaptability, and runtime overhead.

</details>


### [279] [MASt3R-Nav: WayPixel Navigation in Relative 3D Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24111)
*Vansh Garg,Rohit Jayanti,Krish Pandya,Sarthak Chittawar,Siddharth Tourani,Muhammad Haris Khan,Sourav Garg,Madhava Krishna*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a novel map representation called pixel-relative connectivity for visual navigation, which maintains geometric accuracy without requiring global consistency, enabling robust navigation across multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional 3D maps require globally-consistent geometry which is expensive and brittle, while topological graphs lack geometric understanding and limit navigation to simple teach-and-repeat. The authors aim to bridge this gap between geometric accuracy and flexibility.

Method: The method constructs a pixel-level connectivity map from image sequences using inter-image pixel correspondences in relative 3D coordinate systems. It performs global path planning by approximating and sparsifying intra-image pixel connectivity, deriving a "WayPixel Costmap" representation, and training a controller conditioned on this costmap to predict trajectory rollouts.

Result: The dense pixel-level costmap based on relative geometry proves to be a more accurate conditioning variable for control prediction than image- and object-level counterparts. The system demonstrates strong performance on four types of navigation tasks in both simulation and real-world demonstrations.

Conclusion: Pixel-relative connectivity offers a compelling middle ground between globally-consistent 3D maps and topological graphs, achieving geometric accuracy without global consistency requirements, enabling a highly capable navigation system.

Abstract: Visual navigation ability is strongly tied to its underlying representation of the world. Unlike classical 3D maps that require globally-consistent geometry, image- or object-relative topological graphs almost entirely do away with geometric understanding. But, this comes at the cost of navigation capability, often limiting it to merely teach-and-repeat. In this work, we propose a novel map representation in the form of pixel-relative connectivity, which is geometrically accurate but does not require global geometric consistency. Inspired by recent progress in 3D grounded image matching, we construct a map from an image sequence through inter-image connectivity based on pixel correspondences in the relative 3D coordinate systems of individual image pairs. We then use this pixel-level graph to perform global path planning by approximating and sparsifying intra-image pixel connectivity. Through this, we derive a ''WayPixel Costmap'' representation and train a controller conditioned on it to predict a trajectory rollout. We show that this dense pixel-level costmap based on relative geometry is a more accurate conditioning variable for control prediction than its image- and object-level counterparts. This enables a highly capable navigation system, as validated on four types of navigation tasks in the simulator and through real world demonstrations.

</details>


### [280] [Anisotropic Diffusion-Driven Ergodic Coverage in Multi-Robot Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24125)
*Thales C. Silva,Anoop Kiran,Nora Ayanian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces an anisotropic diffusion formulation for multi-robot ergodic search that generalizes prior isotropic (heat equation) approaches, using Perona-Malik diffusion to generate potential fields that better direct agent movement according to the goal density distribution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ergodic search methods using isotropic diffusion (heat equation) propagate error uniformly in all directions, ignoring changes in the desired distribution. This limits flexibility and efficiency in multi-robot systems.

Method: The authors propose an anisotropic diffusion formulation of the ergodicity problem using Perona-Malik diffusion to generate potential fields, which includes the heat equation as a special case. Agent movement is directed by the gradient of this diffusion solution.

Result: The approach generalizes previous results that used radial basis functions and heat equation solutions. Simulations in different scenarios demonstrate the methodology's effectiveness.

Conclusion: This anisotropic diffusion approach provides a more flexible and general framework for ergodic multi-robot search, overcoming limitations of isotropic methods by better adapting to goal density distributions.

Abstract: We consider the problem of combining potential field and ergodic search on multi-robot systems. Traditional ergodic search algorithms use metrics for ergodicity that account for the desired distribution at different scales. Recently, a heat equation-driven ergodic approach was proposed, which adds flexibility to the smoothing of the ergodic metric. However, such an approach, as it is an isotropic diffusion, propagates the error uniformly in all directions, regardless of changes in the desired distribution. We introduce a general class of anisotropic diffusion formulation of the ergodicity problem, which generates a potential field for the ergodic search. We demonstrate that this approach generalizes previous results, which consider radial basis functions and the solution of the heat equation to represent the difference between the goal density distribution and the covered trajectories. In our solution, the agent movement is directed using the gradient of the solution of the Perona-Malik diffusion, and our formulation includes the heat equation as a special case. We demonstrate the methodology with a series of simulations in different scenarios.

</details>


### [281] [Investigating the Effect of a Series Elastic Actuation Retrofit to Black-Box Actuators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24127)
*Ivan Tregear,Ayhan Aktas,Ferdinando Rodriguez y Baena*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A low-cost custom Series Elastic Actuation (SEA) module retrofitted to a black-box actuator improved force control bandwidth from 10.32 Hz to 30.32 Hz (2.93X), outperforming a commercial sensor by 7.63% at a fraction of the cost (25 GBP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional stiff actuators lack compliance, causing poor force control and potential damage in uncertain environments. The authors aim to introduce compliance via SEA while mitigating non-linearities like backlash and static friction in black-box actuators.

Method: A custom torsional Series Elastic element was designed via Finite Element (FE) analysis (stiffness: 2155.4 Nm/rad) and retrofitted to a black-box actuator. Open-loop and closed-loop force control bandwidths were measured using SEA feedback and a commercial force sensor.

Result: The SEA module increased bandwidth from 10.32 Hz to 30.32 Hz (2.93X improvement). It outperformed a commercial force sensor by 7.63% while costing only 25 GBP.

Conclusion: Retrofitting a low-cost custom SEA module can significantly improve force control performance and bandwidth in robotic actuators, rivaling or exceeding expensive commercial sensors.

Abstract: In robotic applications, actuators are typically designed to be stiff with minimal backlash to ensure precision and repeatability. However, this limits compliance, leading to potential damage and poor force control in uncertain environments. Series Elastic Actuation (SEA) introduces compliance to enhance disturbance rejection and enable force measurement via Hooke's Law but reduces system bandwidth.
  A custom Series Elastic (SE) element was retrofitted to a black-box actuator to mitigate non-linearities like backlash and static friction. Integrating the SE element enabled high-fidelity force measurements, improving force control bandwidth and performance.
  A torsional SE element was designed through Finite Element (FE) analysis, yielding a stiffness of 2155.4 Nm/rad. Open-loop force control bandwidth was measured for the original motor and the SEA-integrated configuration, while closed-loop bandwidth was assessed using feedback from the SEA and a commercial force sensor. The SEA module increased bandwidth from 10.32 Hz to 30.32 Hz, a 2.93X improvement. Additionally, it outperformed the commercial sensor by 7.63% despite costing 25 GBP, a fraction of the price.

</details>


### [282] [Afford-VLA: Action-Aligned Visual Planning via Internalized Affordance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24203)
*Runze Wang,Yuqian Fu,Yu Li,Tao Lin,Tianwen Qian,Mohamed Elhoseiny,Bo Zhao,Yanwei Fu,Yu-Gang Jiang,Xiangyang Xue*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Afford-VLA internalizes task-conditioned affordance as a visual planning interface within VLA models using learnable <AFF> tokens, achieving SOTA on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models lack sufficient spatial reasoning for determining where to interact in complex scenes. Current visual planning approaches rely on weak coupling with action prediction (e.g., global cues, symbolic representations, or external signals).

Method: Proposes Afford-VLA with learnable <AFF> tokens to query task-relevant regions, decode affordance masks from multimodal features, and convert them into compact embeddings that directly condition action generation. Uses joint optimization of affordance pathway with action prediction.

Result: Consistent state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and SimplerEnv simulation benchmarks, along with strong real-world results.

Conclusion: Internalizing affordance as action-aligned visual planning provides a powerful paradigm for improving VLA systems by tightly coupling perception and action.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown strong potential for generalist robot manipulation, yet they remain limited by insufficient spatial reasoning, particularly in determining where to interact in complex visual scenes. While recent efforts introduce various forms of visual planning to address this issue, existing approaches either rely on global geometric cues, symbolic intermediate representations, or externally generated visual signals, which are often weakly coupled with downstream action prediction. In this work, we revisit visual planning in VLA systems and argue that effective planning should be local, visually grounded, internally generated, and directly aligned with action. Based on this insight, we propose Afford-VLA, a unified framework that internalizes task-conditioned affordance as an explicit visual planning interface within VLA models. Concretely, we introduce learnable <AFF> tokens to query task-relevant interaction regions, decode affordance masks from multimodal features, and convert them into compact embeddings that directly condition action generation. This design enables affordance to be both generated and utilized within the VLA, forming a tightly coupled perception-action pathway. To further support this integration, we adopt a training strategy that allows the affordance pathway to be jointly optimized with action prediction, improving its effectiveness for downstream control. We evaluate our method on multiple simulation benchmarks, including LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and SimplerEnv, achieving consistent state-of-the-art performance, along with strong real-world results. These findings demonstrate that internalizing affordance as action-aligned visual planning provides a powerful paradigm for improving VLA systems.

</details>


### [283] [ECo-MoE: Embodiment-Conditioned Mixture of Experts Increases the Evolvability of Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24225)
*Yibin Wang,Muhan Li,Zihan Guo,Sam Kriegman*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a co-optimization framework (Evo-MoE) that jointly evolves robot designs (genotypes) and a mixture of neural control experts, where different body plans activate different sensorimotor circuits, enabling efficient modular co-design and "evo by demo" — using pretrained experts to guide evolution toward desired morphologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing co-design algorithms are either inefficient (training individual policies per robot) or overly conservative (monolithic universal controllers). The authors aim for a scalable middle ground that preserves ancestral knowledge while allowing modular adaptation.

Method: A mixture of control experts (neural modules) is gated by latent coordinates of each robot's design (phenotype). Evolution co-optimizes a distribution of latent design vectors (genotypes) alongside the expert mixture, enabling different body plans to activate/deactivate different sensorimotor circuits.

Result: The approach enables modular overhaul of controller parts for new species without disrupting other expert knowledge, and allows pretrained expert policies to be plugged in to guide evolution ("evo by demo") toward desired morphological traits in latent space.

Conclusion: The "evo by demo" process can steer freeform evolution toward canonical structures defined by pretrained models, offering a scalable, modular co-design alternative between individual-policy and monolithic-controller extremes.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce a model of evolution and learning in robots that co-optimizes a distribution of latent design vectors (genotypes) and a mixture of control experts (neural modules), which are gated by the latent coordinates of each decoded design (phenotype). This provides a scalable alternative to co-design algorithms that either train an individual policy for every robot, which is inefficient, or a monolithic universal controller for all robots, which results in overly conservative structures and behaviors. Our approach lies somewhere between these two extremes, preserving ancestral knowledge in a unified yet modular framework in which different body plans activate and deactivate different combinations of learned sensorimotor circuits for goal-directed behavior. This allows one part of the controller to be overhauled to better suit new species of designs as they emerge without disrupting the hard-earned knowledge contained within other expert modules. It also allows pretrained expert policies to be directly plugged into the mixture, which can steer evolution into otherwise unexplored areas of latent space containing desired morphological traits. We refer to this process as "evo by demo" and explore how it may be used to guide freeform evolution toward canonical structures defined by the pretrained model. Videos and code can be found at: https://eco-moe.github.io.

</details>


### [284] [AcroRL: Learning Aggressive Quadrotor Inversion using Bidirectional Thrust](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24301)
*Gabriel Rodriguez,Henri Sayag,Abhishek Rathod,John Stecklein,Siddharth Saha,Christopher Barngrover,Wennie Tabib*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A learning-based framework using reinforcement learning enables quadrotors to perform compact, position-constrained inversions with bidirectional thrust, achieving 32% lower position RMSE and 57% less settling time than optimization-based baselines, validated in simulation and hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior geometric control approaches for bidirectional thrust quadrotors struggle with actuator saturation and motor reversal delay during inversions, requiring heuristic thrust posture scheduling and waypoint tuning.

Method: A learning-based framework with separate RL policies for nominal-to-inverted and inverted-to-nominal transitions, modulating a constant reference trajectory while remaining compatible with traditional trajectory generation and tracking.

Result: In JAX-based simulation: 32% reduction in position RMSE and 57% reduction in settling time vs. strongest optimization baseline. Hardware: position RMSE below 0.35m across multiple yaw configurations, with successful circular flight in both regimes.

Conclusion: The proposed learning-based approach effectively handles bidirectional thrust quadrotor inversions with superior performance, hardware feasibility, and compatibility with existing trajectory pipelines, with open-source implementation provided.

Abstract: Bidirectional thrust grants quadrotors a second equilibrium condition and increased control authority, expanding the envelope of possible aggressive maneuvers and enabling inverted flight, perching, and sensing. Prior geometric control approaches extend differential flatness through Hopf fibration-based attitude representations to support bidirectional thrust, but struggle with actuator saturation and motor reversal delay during inversions, requiring heuristic thrust posture scheduling and waypoint tuning. We propose a learning-based framework that modulates a constant reference trajectory to perform compact, position-constrained quadrotor inversions while remaining compatible with traditional trajectory generation and tracking across flight regimes. Separate policies are trained via reinforcement learning for nominal-to-inverted and inverted-to-nominal transitions. In JAX-based simulation, the proposed method achieves the lowest position deviation and settling time across all evaluated baselines, reducing position root mean square error (RMSE) by 32% and settling time by 57% relative to the strongest optimization-based baseline. Hardware experiments demonstrate successful inversion across multiple yaw configurations with position RMSE below 0.35m, and compatibility with downstream trajectory generation and control through circular flight in both regimes. Additionally, we provide an open-source implementation of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [285] [Terrain-Adaptive Grouser Wheel for Optimal Planetary Exploration: Design and Experimental Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24311)
*Vincent Griffo,Yashwanth Kumar Nakka*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces [Anonymized Robot Name], a planetary rover wheel with continuously adjustable grouser height. Through 750 trials across 4 terrains, adaptive deployment reduced slip by 30-58% and improved travel time/energy by up to 77.4% compared to fixed configurations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Planetary rovers face mobility challenges on varying terrains (gradients, granularity). While multimodal wheel designs have explored stiffness/compliance/diameter adjustments, full wheel grouser-adjustable designs remain unexplored, despite grouser height being crucial for granular terrain performance.

Method: A multimodal wheel with continuously adjustable grouser height was designed. Evaluated across 4 surfaces (vinyl flooring, coarse rock, pea gravel, sand) under two packing states. 750 experimental trials were conducted comparing adaptive vs. fixed grouser configurations. A simplified scaling analysis was developed to relate terrain granularity to optimal grouser height.

Result: Adaptive deployment reduced slip by 30.0-58.0% and improved travel time and energy consumption by up to 77.4% on granular terrains. No single grouser height minimized slip across all terrains. The scaling analysis was validated, showing a relationship between terrain granularity and optimal grouser height.

Conclusion: Fixed-wheel systems have inherent limitations for planetary exploration across diverse terrains. Grouser-adaptive morphology (like [Anonymized Robot Name]) is an effective solution for enhancing rover mobility in challenging extraterrestrial environments.

Abstract: Planetary rovers operating in extraterrestrial environments often encounter significant mobility challenges due to varying terrain features such as gradients and granularity. While recent works in multimodal wheel design have explored adjustments in stiffness, compliance, and diameter as a means to improve terrain adaptability, full wheel grouser-adjustable designs remain largely unexplored. Grousers are a compelling feature to actuate, as granular terrains tend to require increased grouser height for improved wheel performance. As a result, we introduce [Anonymized Robot Name], a multimodal wheel capable of continuously adjusting its grouser height for terrain adaptation. The platform was evaluated across four representative surfaces, including vinyl flooring, coarse rock, pea gravel, and sand under two packing states, spanning a range of granular conditions. Results from 750 experimental trials demonstrate that adaptive deployment reduces slip by 30.0--58.0\% and improves travel time and energy consumption by up to 77.4\% in granular regimes relative to fixed configurations. Using the terrain trial data, a simplified scaling analysis was developed and validated, suggesting a relationship between terrain granularity and optimal grouser height for the tested configuration. No single grouser height minimized slip across all terrains, underscoring the limitations of fixed-wheel systems commonly used for planetary exploration. This observation reinforces the potential of grouser-adaptive morphology, such as [Anonymized Robot Name], as an effective solution for enhancing rover mobility across diverse and mobility-challenging extraterrestrial environments.

</details>


### [286] [IsaacIPC: Coupling High-Fidelity Simulation and Realistic Rendering for Contact-Rich Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24339)
*Qixin Liang,Zhongqing Han*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: IsaacIPC is a GPU-accelerated robotic simulation framework coupling IPC with IsaacSim for realistic rigid-deformable simulation, featuring GMCP for improved tactile contact-pressure resolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable real-time realistic rendering for robotic simulation with both visual fidelity and accurate tactile contact-pressure distributions, addressing limitations in existing simulation frameworks for deformable objects and tactile sensing.

Method: (1) IsaacIPC: Maps simulated deformation between simulation and visual meshes, coupling GPU-accelerated IPC with IsaacSim/Lab. (2) GMCP: A geometric mortar contact potential defining barrier potentials over contact samples on tactile surfaces for better contact-pressure distribution resolution.

Result: GMCP evaluated on contact benchmarks; IsaacIPC demonstrated on quadruped robot, dexterous hand, and UMI gripper in rigid-deformable robotic simulations.

Conclusion: IsaacIPC with GMCP enables realistic rendering and accurate tactile sensing for robotic simulation, supporting data collection and policy evaluation tasks.

Abstract: We present IsaacIPC, a robotic simulation framework that couples GPU accelerated incremental potential contact (IPC) with IsaacSim/Lab. IsaacIPC maps simulated deformation between simulation and visual meshes, enabling real-time realistic rendering with applications to data collection and policy evaluation. For tactile sensing, we introduce the geometric mortar contact potential (GMCP), which defines a barrier potential over contact samples on tactile surfaces to better resolve contact-pressure distributions. We evaluate GMCP on contact benchmarks and demonstrate IsaacIPC on rigid-deformable robotic simulations including a quadruped robot, a dexterous hand, and a universal manipulation interface (UMI) gripper.

</details>


### [287] [PACT: Proactive Asking for Continual Task Assistance in Human-Robot Collaboration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24350)
*Chengbo He,Sheng Li,Chenyang Ma,Bochao Zou,Li Sun,Jiansheng Chen,Junliang Xing,Yuanchun Shi,Huimin Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PACT is a proactive ask-or-act framework for continual human-robot collaboration that decides when to seek clarification vs. act, using interaction history to improve assistance under partial observations, outperforming passive inference methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human traits and routines are unknown initially in long-term collaboration, making passive infer-then-act approaches ineffective and inefficient under partial observations.

Method: PACT (Proactive Asking for Continual Task Assistance) framework that evaluates contextual sufficiency using current observations and accumulated interaction history, implemented via reinforcement learning, with a clarification utility metric to balance accuracy vs. frequency of clarification.

Result: In multi-day embodied collaboration scenarios, PACT consistently improves both assistance accuracy and clarification utility compared to passive inference baselines.

Conclusion: Proactive asking is important for continual human-robot collaboration, enabling robots to progressively adapt to users over time while balancing assistance reliability and clarification burden.

Abstract: Robotic assistants in long-term human-robot collaboration need to assist users under partial observations while leveraging cross-day interaction history. However, human traits and routines are often unknown at the beginning of collaboration, making passive infer-then-act assistance ineffective and inefficient. To address this challenge, we study a cross-day proactive asking setting for continual task assistance and propose PACT (Proactive Asking for Continual Task Assistance), an ask-or-act framework that determines whether clarification should be sought before taking action. PACT leverages current observations together with accumulated interaction history to evaluate contextual sufficiency, enabling the robot to provide more reliable assistance and progressively adapt to the user over time. We implement its primary learned instantiation using reinforcement learning and evaluate alternative instantiations under the same framework. To assess such behavior, we further introduce a clarification utility metric that quantifies the trade-off between assistance accuracy and the frequency of clarification requests. Experiments in multi-day embodied collaboration scenarios demonstrate that, compared with passive inference baselines, PACT consistently improves both assistance accuracy and clarification utility, highlighting the importance of proactive asking in continual human-robot collaboration.

</details>


### [288] [RoboHitch: Learning Visual Affordance from Disordered Keypoints for Hitch Knots Tying](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24394)
*Jiahui Zuo,Boyang Zhang,Fumin Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboHitch is a framework for robotic knot tying that uses disordered 3D keypoints and RGB images (instead of explicit topological tracking) to learn hitch knot tying from human demonstrations, achieving success under self-occlusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic knot tying methods rely on precise topological state tracking with ordered keypoints and explicit edge connectivity, which are prone to failures from tracking drift and topology mismatch during bending and crossings.

Method: A dynamic Graph Autoencoder extracts geometric features from untracked keypoints, a Convolutional Autoencoder captures visual context, and a bidirectional cross-attention mechanism fuses these modalities to predict pick and place affordances.

Result: Real-world experiments demonstrate successful completion of hitch knots under self-occlusions, showing effectiveness and generalizability.

Conclusion: RoboHitch eliminates the need for explicit topological ordering in knot tying, enabling more flexible manipulation under occlusion.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation of deformable linear objects (DLOs) presents significant challenges due to complex dynamics and frequent self-occlusions. Existing robotic knot tying methods typically rely on precise topological state tracking with ordered keypoints and explicit edge connectivity. This reliance makes them prone to failures due to tracking drift and topology mismatch caused by repeated bending and crossings during knot formation.To address these limitations, we introduce RoboHitch, a novel framework that learns to perform hitch knot tying from human demonstrations using only disordered 3D keypoints and RGB images. This eliminates the need for explicit topological order, allowing for more flexible manipulation. Our method employs a dynamic Graph Autoencoder to extract geometric features from untracked keypoints, complemented by a Convolutional Autoencoder that captures essential visual context. A bidirectional cross-attention mechanism then fuses these modalities to jointly predict pick and place affordances, facilitating implicit reasoning about the rope's state and enabling knot tying under occlusion.Real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and generalizability of our approach, successfully completing hitch knots in scenarios with self-occlusions.

</details>


### [289] [Smoother Action Chunking Flow Policy via Prior-Corrected Orthogonal Trust-Region Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24433)
*Kai Fang,Hailong Pei,Xuemin Chi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: POTR improves robot action-chunking by introducing prior-corrected weights and orthogonal trust-region constraints to reduce discontinuous action transitions, outperforming RTC guidance on LIBERO benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RTC guidance for action-chunking in robot policies has weak weight schedules at intermediate timesteps and unconstrained correction directions that introduce transverse perturbations, leading to discontinuous action transitions.

Method: POTR proposes (1) incorporating a data-prior scale σ_d into the RTC guidance weight for stronger intermediate-time correction, and (2) decomposing the guidance vector into parallel/perpendicular components to the denoising velocity, with the perpendicular component constrained within a trust region.

Result: On LIBERO with π_{0.5}, POTR improves success rate and consistently reduces chunk-boundary discontinuity, acceleration, and jerk compared to RTC.

Conclusion: Prior-corrected weight provides main correction gain, while orthogonal trust region further improves stability.

Abstract: Flow-matching robot policies commonly use action-chunking inference for efficient closed-loop control, but chunk boundaries can introduce discontinuous action transitions. Existing RTC guidance improves continuity by injecting correction signals during denoising, yet its weight schedule is weak at intermediate timesteps and its unconstrained correction direction may introduce transverse perturbations. We propose POTR, a **p**rior-corrected **o**rthogonal **t**rust-**r**egion guidance method. First, we incorporate a data-prior scale $σ_d$ into the RTC guidance weight, yielding stronger intermediate-time correction. Second, we decompose the guidance vector into components parallel and perpendicular to the denoising velocity, and constrain the perpendicular component within a trust region. On LIBERO with $π_{0.5}$, POTR improves success rate and consistently reduces chunk-boundary discontinuity, acceleration, and jerk compared with RTC. Ablations show that the prior-corrected weight provides the main correction gain, while the orthogonal trust region further improves stability.

</details>


### [290] [Vision-Guided Outdoor Flight and Obstacle Evasion via Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24449)
*Shiladitya Dutta,Aayush Gupta,Varun Saran,Avideh Zakhor*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A sensorimotor policy using stereo-vision depth and VIO enables autonomous quadcopter navigation through unknown obstacles to reach goal points, trained via reinforcement learning with privileged learning and curriculum fine-tuning, achieving zero-shot transfer to novel environments and platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quadcopters require continuous pilot control in complex GNSS/telemetry-denied environments, limiting their autonomous deployment. The authors aim to enable fully autonomous navigation without external positioning systems.

Method: The policy uses a pre-trained autoencoder (perception head) + LSTM (planning/control) network. Training is two-stage: (1) supervised learning from optimal trajectories via a global motion planner, (2) curriculum-based fine-tuning with RL. Domain randomization and reward shaping bridge sim-to-real gap.

Result: Successful zero-shot transfer in outdoor experiments to both unseen obstacle environments and an unseen drone platform, demonstrating robustness to domain shift and noise.

Conclusion: The proposed sensorimotor policy effectively enables autonomous quadcopter navigation in GNSS/telemetry-denied environments, with sim-to-real transfer achieved without additional real-world training.

Abstract: Although quadcopters boast impressive traversal capabilities enabled by their omnidirectional maneuverability, the need for continuous pilot control in complex environments impedes their application in GNSS and telemetry-denied scenarios. To this end, we propose a novel sensorimotor policy that uses stereo-vision depth and visual-inertial odometry (VIO) to autonomously navigate through obstacles in an unknown environment to reach a goal point. The policy is comprised of a pre-trained autoencoder as the perception head followed by a planning and control LSTM network which outputs velocity commands that can be followed by an off-the-shelf commercial drone. We leverage reinforcement and privileged learning paradigms to train the policy in simulation through a two-stage process: 1) initial training with optimal trajectories generated by a global motion planner acting as a supervisory backbone, 2) further fine-tuning in a curriculum environment. To bridge the sim-to-real gap, we employ domain randomization and reward shaping to create a policy that is both robust to noise and domain shift. In outdoor experiments, our approach achieves successful zero-shot transfer to both obstacle environments and a drone platform that were never encountered during training.

</details>


### [291] [Polymander II: an amphibious salamander-inspired robot with contact and flow sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24465)
*Qiyuan Fu,Sudong Lee,Andrea Grillo,Jonathan Arreguit,Louis Gevers,Josie Hughes,Auke J. Ijspeert*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a Hall-effect sensor system for a salamander-inspired amphibious robot that simultaneously captures foot contact forces and hydrodynamic forces at >500Hz, along with proprioceptive data at 100Hz, enabling amphibious locomotion and feedback control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Few existing amphibious robots can sense interactions with both terrestrial and aquatic environments, limiting their adaptability across different terrains.

Method: The authors use compact Hall-effect sensors embedded in multiple positions on a salamander-inspired robot to sense foot contact forces and lateral hydrodynamic forces. Two bus lines enable simultaneous acquisition of exteroceptive (>500Hz) and proprioceptive (100Hz) information, with sensor positioning separate from measured objects aiding waterproofing.

Result: The robot demonstrated successful traversal of amphibious environments with high-frequency sensor feedback (exteroceptive >500Hz, proprioceptive 100Hz), and showed potential for feedback-controlled complex locomotion tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed Hall-effect sensing approach provides an effective, compact, and waterproofable solution for amphibious robots to sense multimodal environmental interactions, enabling more adaptive locomotion control.
