Abstract: Robots benefit from sensory information to coordinate body movement, gain robustness against perturbations, and transit between different modes to adapt to various terrains. However, few amphibious robots can sense interactions with both terrestrial and aquatic environments. In this paper, we present a solution that uses Hall-effect sensors to sense foot contact forces and lateral hydrodynamic forces on a salamander-inspired amphibious robot. With two bus lines, the robot can simultaneously acquire this exteroceptive information at more than 500 Hz and proprioceptive information, such as joint positions and loads, at 100 Hz. The Hall-effect sensors used are compact, making them suitable for embedding in multiple positions within a robot, and exhibit high sensitivity to small forces. Moreover, because the sensor can be positioned separately from the measured object, waterproofing can be implemented with relative ease. Our tests demonstrate the robot's capabilities in traversing amphibious environments and its potential in using feedback control for more complex locomotion tasks.

</details>


### [292] [Elevator-LIO: Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry for Multi-Floor Navigation under Elevator-Induced Non-Inertial Motion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24495)
*Yifan Zhang,Yudong Huang,Yuchong Zhang,Changze Li,Haoran Liu,Ming Yang,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Elevator-LIO is a LiDAR-inertial odometry framework that enables continuous robot localization during elevator travel by decoupling robot and elevator motion models, using mode-dependent iterated error-state Kalman filtering, event-triggered updates for drift suppression, and adaptive voxel downsampling, achieving <1cm height error across 79 elevator rides in 20 real-world sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LIO systems fail during elevator travel due to non-inertial frame dynamics, leading to localization gaps or failures in cross-floor robotic tasks.

Method: Decoupled state-estimation model (robot + elevator motion) embedded in a mode-dependent iterated error-state Kalman filter; elevator mode manager using LiDAR ranging statistics for event detection; event-triggered zero-velocity/zero-acceleration updates; adaptive voxel downsampling for scale changes.

Result: Tested on 20 real-world sequences with 79 elevator rides; terminal height error <1cm in 17 sequences; outperforms existing systems on elevator sequences; remains competitive on Hilti 2022/2023 standard indoor benchmarks.

Conclusion: Elevator-LIO provides continuous, accurate robot localization during elevator travel, solving a critical gap in cross-floor SLAM, with robustness to large spaces, long vertical travel, dynamic interference, and mirror reflections.

Abstract: This paper presents Elevator-LIO, a LiDAR-inertial odometry framework designed to achieve continuous robot localization during elevator travel, thereby supporting cross-floor robotic tasks. To address the state-estimation problem in non-inertial frames, Elevator-LIO establishes a decoupled state-estimation model that separately models the robot motion relative to the elevator and the elevator motion itself, and embeds it into a mode-dependent iterated error-state Kalman filter framework. This framework degenerates to conventional LIO estimation in ordinary indoor environments, while enabling the propagation and constrained update of elevator-related states in elevator non-inertial environments, thereby achieving continuous and stable localization. An elevator mode manager detects elevator entry and exit events using LiDAR ranging statistics and estimated states, and introduces event-triggered zero-velocity and zero-acceleration updates when the elevator stops to suppress accumulated vertical drift. In addition, this paper adopts an adaptive voxel downsampling strategy to maintain a stable number of effective points under significant environmental scale changes. We conduct extensive experiments on 20 real-world sequences containing 79 elevator rides, including practical challenges such as large-scale spaces, long vertical travel, dynamic pedestrian interference, and mirror reflections. The results show that Elevator-LIO maintains continuous localization accuracy in all sequences, with terminal height error below 1 cm in 17 sequences. In contrast, existing representative localization systems perform poorly on these elevator sequences. Tests on the Hilti 2022/2023 datasets further show that the proposed method remains competitive in standard indoor scenarios. The project page is available at https://xiaofan4122.github.io/Elevator_LIO_Page/.

</details>


### [293] [MuGen: Multi-Skill Generative Locomotion Controller for Humanoid Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24592)
*Yusen Feng,Xiang Wang,Heyuan Yao,Zixi Kang,Xinyu Huo,Boyang Yu,Pengyun Qiu,Ruijie Zhao,Baoquan Chen,Libin Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MuGen is a data-driven framework that uses VQ-VAEs and model-based RL to enable humanoid robots to learn and deploy multi-skill locomotion, including tracking and mimicking unseen human motions from example sequences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable humanoid robots to perform expressive, human-like locomotion motions by learning from heterogeneous human motion data, moving beyond pre-programmed or single-skill walking controllers.

Method: The framework uses vector-quantized autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) combined with model-based reinforcement learning to create a generative latent representation of locomotion. A teacher-student learning framework with a novel policy distillation strategy is employed to train a deployable student policy.

Result: The framework enables the robot to track and mimic unseen human motions, achieve accurate execution across diverse motions, and reuse the learned latent space for other tasks.

Conclusion: MuGen effectively bridges human motion data and robot control, providing a scalable approach for multi-skill locomotion on humanoid robots.

Abstract: This paper presents MuGen, a data-driven framework for learning and deploying multi-skill locomotion on humanoid robots. MuGen enables a robot to perform expressive motions like humans under the guidance of example motion sequences. To achieve this, we employ vector-quantized autoencoders (VQ-VAEs) trained with model-based reinforcement learning, resulting in a generative representation of locomotion that captures key patterns of human motion from hours of heterogeneous human performance data. We employ a teacher-student learning framework and develop a new policy distillation strategy to enable a deployable student policy learning this efficient latent representation. This policy allows the robot to track and mimic unseen human motions and further enables the robot to reuse the learned latent space for other tasks. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework through a diverse set of motions and accurate execution.

</details>


### [294] [PoseRefer: Pathway-Local Parameters for Semantically Grounded Reference Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24622)
*Anna Deichler*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MM-Conv captures natural co-speech gesture from dyadic VR interaction; a decoupled late-fusion architecture fusing pose and text pathways shows that fusion with frozen MiniLM category embeddings outperforms unimodal baselines, but reveals that fused grounding accuracy can be inflated by category-representation artifacts unless pathways are architecturally decoupled.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D grounding benchmarks fail to capture natural co-speech gesture (descriptions are post-hoc, gestures templated or staged). The paper aims to study realistic pose-language fusion for referring expression comprehension.

Method: Introduces MM-Conv dataset (dyadic VR interaction with full-body mocap and 3D scene graphs). Uses a decoupled late-fusion architecture where pose and text pathways share no learned parameters; evaluates with controlled ablations and a learned scalar gate.

Result: Fusion with frozen MiniLM category embeddings achieves 31.9% top-1 accuracy, outperforming unimodal baselines. The learned scalar gate flips between opposing policies depending on whether the text pathway has category access.

Conclusion: Fusion-accuracy claims for semantic grounding systems are indistinguishable from category-representation artifacts unless pathways are architecturally decoupled. Decoupled architecture is a reliability diagnostic.

Abstract: A robot resolving ``put the cup on that one'' must fuse gesture, language, and scene geometry, yet 3D grounding benchmarks only partially capture this regime: descriptions are written post-hoc, gestures are templated, or pointing is staged for the camera. MM-Conv captures natural co-speech gesture from dyadic VR interaction alongside full-body motion capture and 3D scene graphs. We use it to evaluate pose-language fusion with a decoupled late-fusion architecture in which pose and text pathways share no learned parameters. The two choices together make category, pose, and text contributions easier to isolate through controlled ablations. Fusion with frozen MiniLM category embeddings exceeds pose alone and the best text-only pathway on every reference type, reaching 31.9% top-1. The learned scalar gate flips between opposing policies depending on whether the text pathway has category access. This is a reliability diagnostic: fusion-accuracy claims for semantic grounding systems are indistinguishable from category-representation artifacts unless pathways are architecturally decoupled.

</details>


### [295] [Towards Low-Gravity Planetary Exploration using Reinforcement Learning for Walking, Jumping, and In-flight Attitude Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24643)
*Jørgen Anker Olsen,Kostas Alexis*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper develops RL-based locomotion policies for a quadruped robot to walk, jump, and control its attitude in Mars gravity, enabling obstacle traversal via coordinated jumping and reorientation, with results showing 90° attitude control in 2.6 seconds and jumps over 3 meters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable quadruped robots to navigate challenging planetary terrain (especially Mars) by overcoming obstacles larger than themselves through coordinated jumping and precise in-flight reorientation.

Method: Reinforcement learning policies are developed for walking, vertical jumping, forward jumping, and in-flight attitude control, tailored to reduced Martian gravity. Policies are deployed on a quadruped robot with a 5-bar leg design, with Sim2Real transfer tested on the Olympus quadruped.

Result: Achieved 90° attitude reorientation in 2.6 seconds (real-world), and simulated jumps of 3.1 m vertical and 3.9 m forward under Martian gravity.

Conclusion: RL-based policies enable dynamic locomotion and obstacle traversal on Mars, with promising Sim2Real transfer for attitude control.

Abstract: This paper presents reinforcement learning (RL) policies for dynamic quadrupedal locomotion in planetary exploration scenarios. Building on a taskoptimized quadruped with a 5-bar leg design, we develop RL policies for walking, vertical jumping, forward jumping, and in-flight attitude control, explicitly tailored to the reduced gravity on Mars. These policies jointly enable such robots to overcome obstacles larger than themselves through coordinated jumping and precise in-flight reorientation for safe landings. We demonstrate Sim2Real transfer of the attitude control policy on the Olympus quadruped through single-axis reorientation tests, while all locomotion policies are validated in simulation. A complete Mars exploration mission scenario demonstrates coordinated policy deployment across challenging terrain. Experimental results show 90° attitude reorientation in 2.6 seconds, with simulations demonstrating 3.1 meter vertical jumps and 3.9 meter forward jumps under Martian gravity conditions. - Supplementary video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qlSJ3P87A4A

</details>


### [296] [Sum of Costs Diffusion with Dynamic Guidance for Motion Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24690)
*Aysu Aylin Kaplan,Özgür Erkent*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A diffusion-based motion planning method for robotic manipulation that uses gradient-guided denoising with dynamic start step selection to generate collision-free trajectories with high generalization capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing motion planning methods (classical and deep learning) struggle to generalize across diverse robotic manipulation settings.

Method: Uses diffusion models where the denoising process is guided by the gradient of the total collision cost, with a dynamic approach for selecting the start step of gradient guidance.

Result: Achieved the highest performance on diverse test settings in the Mπnets dataset compared to competing methods, demonstrating robust generalization.

Conclusion: Dynamic gradient-guided diffusion models effectively overcome generalization issues in motion planning for robotic manipulation.

Abstract: The motion planning problem for robotic manipulation can be addressed through classical or deep learning approaches. Existing methods face significant challenges in generalizing to diverse settings. In this study, we present a method with high generalization capability that generates collision-free trajectories using diffusion models where the denoising process is guided by the gradient of the total collision cost. We are also presenting a dynamic approach for choosing start step of the gradient guidance. Experimental results demonstrate that guiding the diffusion model dynamically with the sum of collision costs offers more robust performance by overcoming the generalization issues faced by competing methods. The proposed model demonstrates its effectiveness by achieving the highest performance on diverse test settings in M$π$nets\ dataset among the compared methods.

</details>


### [297] [Geometric Workspace Analysis and Transmission-Aware Dynamics of a Serial Spherical Tool for Microsurgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24760)
*Anestis Mablekos-Alexiou,Lyndon da Cruz,Christos Bergeles*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A kinematic and transmission-aware design framework for a spherical mechanism with an extra translational DOF for microsurgery, including analytical workspace formulation, dynamics-informed methodology for self-locking transmissions, and validated open-source software for friction identification and inverse dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable rapid, insight-driven design of microsurgical robotic tools by addressing the lack of analytical workspace formulations and dynamics-informed methods for mechanisms with self-locking transmissions.

Method: Analytical workspace formulation for geometric insight and rapid axis orientation selection; dynamics-informed methodology for torque evaluation in self-locking transmission systems; open-source software package for friction identification and inverse dynamics.

Result: Experiments on a vitreoretinal surgery robot validated the predictive capability of the models and demonstrated practical utility for engineering design.

Conclusion: The proposed framework enables efficient and accurate design of spherical mechanisms with translational DOF for microsurgery, supported by validated models and open-source tools.

Abstract: We present a kinematic and transmission-aware design framework for a serial spherical mechanism with an additional translational degree of freedom for microsurgery. The first contribution is an analytical workspace formulation that provides geometric insight into reachable motion and enables rapid selection of rotation axis orientations without numerical optimization. The second contribution is a dynamics-informed methodology for mechanisms driven by self-locking transmissions, supporting evaluation of torque requirements for a prescribed workspace geometry. The framework is accompanied by an open-source software package for friction identification and inverse dynamics analysis. Experiments on a purpose-built robotic tool for vitreoretinal surgery validate the predictive capability of the models and demonstrate their practical utility for engineering design.

</details>


### [298] [Enhanced INS/GNSS State Estimation using GNSS-Based Acceleration Measurements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24767)
*Gal Versano,Itzik Klein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes using past GNSS measurements with a motion model to extract vehicle acceleration, then integrating this into INS/GNSS fusion filters to improve positioning accuracy. Real-world tests show 11-21% RMSE improvement over standard methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard INS/GNSS fusion relies on GNSS position updates, which provide limited observability of orientation and inertial sensor errors, especially during low-dynamic motion.

Method: Past GNSS measurements are combined with a motion model to extract vehicle acceleration information, which is then integrated into the INS/GNSS fusion filter.

Result: Consistent positioning accuracy improvements: 11.40% and 20.74% mean position RMSE improvements on two real-world unmanned ground vehicle datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed acceleration-aided approach enhances robustness and accuracy of INS/GNSS navigation compared to standard position-aided filtering.

Abstract: Accurate and reliable navigation is essential for autonomous ground vehicle operations. Standard INS/GNSS fusion relies on GNSS position updates, which provide limited observability of orientation and inertial sensor error states, particularly during low-dynamic motion. In this work, we propose utilizing past GNSS measurements alongside a motion model to extract meaningful vehicle acceleration information. This acceleration measurement is then integrated into the INS/GNSS filter to improve its robustness and accuracy. The proposed approach is evaluated on two real-world unmanned ground vehicle datasets collected from different mobile platforms and inertial sensor grades. Results demonstrate consistent positioning accuracy improvements relative to the standard position-aided filter, with mean position root mean square error improvements of 11.40 % and 20.74 % on the two datasets, respectively.

</details>


### [299] [MR-LiDAR: A Multi-Resolution Roadside LiDAR Benchmark for Perception Diagnostics and Deployment Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24777)
*Shunlai Cui,Peng Cao,Yuan Zhu,Yongjiang He,Jiacheng Yin,Xiao Huo,Gang Cao,Xiaobo Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MR-LiDAR is a controlled multi-resolution benchmark (16, 32, 80, 128 beams) for roadside perception. It reveals that 80-beam LiDAR with optimized beam distribution can match 128-beam LiDAR, and provides practical guidance for cost-effective LiDAR selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LiDAR model selection is critical for balancing perception capability and deployment cost in roadside sensing, but there is a lack of empirical benchmarks comparing perception performance across different LiDAR configurations.

Method: Authors collected point clouds and annotations using 16-, 32-, 80-, and 128-beam LiDARs in identical roadside scenarios, then systematically analyzed how beam count, beam distribution, distance, object category, and occlusion affect perception performance.

Result: All factors (beam count, distribution, distance, object category, occlusion) substantially impact perception. An 80-beam LiDAR with optimized beam distribution can match or outperform a 128-beam LiDAR with uniform distribution, challenging the assumption that more beams always yield better perception.

Conclusion: The work provides a diagnostic benchmark and practical reference guide for cost-effective LiDAR selection in roadside perception applications.

Abstract: LiDAR model selection is a critical issue in roadside sensing systems, as it directly determines both perception capability and deployment cost. However, the lack of empirical benchmarks for comparing perception performance across different LiDAR configurations has greatly constrained scientific sensor selection and deployment planning. To address this gap, we present MR-LiDAR, a controlled multi-resolution LiDAR benchmark for roadside perception diagnostics. Using 16-, 32-, 80-, and 128-beam LiDARs in identical roadside scenarios, we collect point clouds and ground-truth annotations for diverse traffic participants, including vehicles and vulnerable road users (VRUs), across varying distances. This controlled design isolates intrinsic LiDAR specifications, particularly beam count and beam distribution, as the key variables for precise performance diagnostics. Based on MR-LiDAR, we conduct systematic empirical analyses to examine how beam count, beam distribution, target distance, object category, and vehicle occlusion affect LiDAR perception performance. The results reveal that all of these factors have substantial impacts. In particular, contrary to the common assumption that higher beam counts always yield better perception, we show that an 80-beam LiDAR with optimized beam distribution can match or even outperform a 128-beam LiDAR with uniform beam distribution. In addition, we provide a practical reference guide for LiDAR selection, including target point-count statistics and detection performance comparisons based on two widely used detection algorithms. This work offers a diagnostic benchmark and practical guidance for determining cost-effective LiDAR configurations in roadside perception applications.

</details>


### [300] [Manifold-Constrained MPPI: Real-Time Sampling-Based Control Under Hard Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24813)
*Seulchan Lee,Sanghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MC-MPPI is a real-time sampling-based control framework that combines VAE-based latent-space planning with QP-based execution-level correction to enforce hard manifold equality constraints for closed-chain manipulation, outperforming standard MPPI at 100 Hz.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MPPI uses cost-based soft penalties that cannot guarantee hard-constraint satisfaction, limiting its applicability to highly constrained tasks like closed-chain manipulation.

Method: Decouples constrained optimal control into latent-space planning (VAE learns low-dimensional manifold representation for MPPI to generate near-feasible trajectories) and execution-level correction (QP controller resolves residual manifold mismatch in a single solve).

Result: Operates at 100 Hz on a 14-DoF closed-chain dual-arm system in simulation and real-world; reliably navigates dynamic environments while maintaining hard equality constraints; significantly outperforms baselines in tracking accuracy.

Conclusion: VAE-based latent planning enables efficient linearization of equality constraints, allowing single-solve QP correction instead of iterative projection, making hard-constraint MPPI feasible and fast for real-time robotic control.

Abstract: Sampling-based model predictive control methods, such as Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI), offer derivative-free optimization and robustness in complex robotic systems. However, standard MPPI relies on cost-based soft penalties that cannot guarantee hard-constraint satisfaction, severely limiting its applicability to highly constrained tasks such as closed-chain manipulation. To address this, we propose Manifold-Constrained MPPI (MC-MPPI), a real-time sampling-based control framework that enforces manifold-based equality constraints while preserving the computational advantages of MPPI. The key idea is to decouple the constrained optimal control problem into latent-space planning and execution-level correction. At the planning stage, a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) learns a low-dimensional latent representation of the constraint manifold, enabling MPPI to efficiently generate near-feasible candidate trajectories without per-sample modification. Since this reference enables accurate linearization of the equality constraints, an execution-level Quadratic Programming (QP) controller resolves the residual manifold mismatch in a single solve rather than through iterative projection. Experiments on a 14-DoF closed-chain dual-arm system in both simulation and real-world settings demonstrate that MC-MPPI operates stably at 100 Hz, reliably navigates dynamic environments while effectively maintaining hard equality constraints, and significantly outperforms baseline methods in tracking accuracy. Supplementary videos and implementation details are available at https://rcilab.github.io/mcmppi.

</details>


### [301] [Learning Transferable Motor Skills for Geometry-Aware Robotic Surface Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24881)
*Miroslav David,Karla Stepanova,Robert Babuska*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a modular framework decoupling geometric motion planning from execution-level expertise using interpretable atomic motor rules, trained via multimodal neural network to infer rule parameters from kinematic and CAD data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic surface-interaction tasks need both accurate geometric planning and precise motion execution, but existing methods either lack expert motor patterns or tightly couple execution to specific training geometry, limiting transferability.

Method: A modular framework that represents expert behavior as interpretable atomic motor rules (e.g., velocity scaling, orientation offsets) modifying geometrically planned paths, with a multimodal neural network trained jointly on kinematic trajectory data and CAD model geometry.

Result: Evaluated on L-shaped and window-shaped objects via dynamic simulation, successfully extracting velocity and orientation rules across both topologies.

Conclusion: The decoupled approach enables transferable execution-level expertise across different geometric topologies without retraining on specific task geometries.

Abstract: Robotic surface-interaction tasks, such as spray painting or welding, require both accurate geometric planning and precise motion execution. While modern motion planners generate valid geometric paths, they often lack the expert motor patterns observed in human operators. Conversely, learning from demonstration often tightly couples task execution to the specific training geometry, limiting transferability. We propose a modular framework that decouples geometric motion planning from execution-level expertise. Expert behavior is represented as a vocabulary of interpretable, atomic motor rules, such as velocity scaling and orientation offsets, that systematically modify a geometrically planned reference path. We train a multimodal neural network to infer rule parameters jointly from kinematic trajectory data and CAD model geometry. We evaluate our approach through dynamic simulation on L-shaped and window-shaped objects, demonstrating on simulated data that the model successfully extracts velocity and orientation rules across both topologies.

</details>


### [302] [MuJoCoUni:Persistent Batched Runtime Primitives for MuJoCo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24922)
*Yufei Jia,Junzhe Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MuJoCoUni extends MuJoCo with a BatchEnvPool executor for high-throughput parallel online robot learning and batched physics evaluation, providing stateful environment primitives while preserving upstream CPU semantics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-throughput parallel physics simulation is critical for online robot learning, but upstream MuJoCo lacks runtime primitives for stateful, batched environment execution needed for these workloads.

Method: Introduces BatchEnvPool, a C++/pybind11 executor managing per-environment mjModel copies, per-thread mjData workers, and an internal thread pool, offering final-state-only stepping, sparse reset, domain randomization, batched sensor/ Jacobian/height-field queries, all confined to the Python binding layer.

Result: A validated open-source package (pip install mujoco-uni) with benchmarks and validation scripts, achieving high-throughput parallel execution while retaining upstream MuJoCo semantics.

Conclusion: MuJoCoUni successfully bridges the gap between MuJoCo's single-threaded design and the demands of online robot learning by adding a lightweight, semantics-preserving parallel execution layer.

Abstract: We present MuJoCoUni, a downstream MuJoCo distribution for online robot learning and batched physics evaluation. Alongside the open-loop batched trajectory generation already provided by upstream mujoco.rollout, MuJoCoUni supplies runtime primitives for stateful environment execution. The target workloads need high-throughput parallel execution while retaining upstream CPU MuJoCo semantics for models, sensors, contact, and constraints. Its core object, BatchEnvPool, is a C++/pybind11 executor that owns per-environment mjModel copies, per-thread mjData workers, and an internal thread pool. It provides final-state-only short stepping, sparse reset, reset-lifecycle domain randomization, batched sensor forward evaluation without advancing dynamics, and batched Jacobian and height-field queries. The implementation is confined to the Python binding layer; MuJoCo's solver, contact model, integrator, and core source tree retain upstream semantics. This report describes the BatchEnvPool API, implementation boundary, relationship to rollout, and the validation and benchmark scripts shipped with the open-source mujoco-uni package, which is installed with \texttt{pip install mujoco-uni}.

</details>


### [303] [Dynamic Neural Koopman Distillation for Real-Time Robot Control Using Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24924)
*Lei Zheng,Peiqi Yu,Zengqi Peng,Changliu Liu,Armin Lederer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes Dynamic Neural Koopman Distillation, a framework that distills diffusion models for robotic planning into a single forward pass using a Factorized Dynamic Koopman layer, achieving millisecond-level inference latency while maintaining multimodal expressivity and outperforming existing one-step baselines on D4RL MuJoCo and real robot tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models generate high-quality multimodal trajectories for robotic planning but suffer from high latency due to iterative denoising, making them incompatible with high-frequency closed-loop control.

Method: A distillation framework using a Factorized Dynamic Koopman layer that models the denoising process via factorized latent transition with state-dependent modal gains, enabling single-step forward inference.

Result: Outperforms existing one-step distillation methods on D4RL MuJoCo locomotion tasks; reduces inference latency to millisecond regime; enables smooth and fast closed-loop execution on a physical Kinova manipulator.

Conclusion: The proposed method successfully bridges the gap between diffusion models' generative quality and real-time control requirements, making diffusion-based planning feasible for high-frequency closed-loop robotic systems.

Abstract: Diffusion models excel at generating diverse and multimodal trajectories for robotic planning, yet their iterative denoising process introduces latency that is incompatible with high-frequency closed-loop control. To address this problem, we propose Dynamic Neural Koopman Distillation, a framework that distills multistep diffusion inference into a single forward pass while retaining the multimodal expressivity of the teacher model. Specifically, we introduce a Factorized Dynamic Koopman layer that models the denoising process through a factorized latent transition with state-dependent modal gains. We evaluate the proposed method on standard D4RL MuJoCo locomotion benchmarks and a physical Kinova manipulator, comparing against one-step baselines. The results show that our method significantly outperforms existing one-step distillation approaches on the reported locomotion tasks, and reduces the inference latency to the millisecond regime compared with the teacher policy. Hardware experiments further demonstrate that our method enables smooth and fast closed-loop execution while maintaining task success and comparable accuracy. A project page is available at https://fdkoopman.github.io/.

</details>


### [304] [Learning High-Frequency Continuous Action Chunks in Latent Space](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24931)
*Kunyun Wang,Yuhang Zheng,Yupeng Zheng,Jieru Zhao,Wenchao Ding*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes shifting high-frequency robotic action learning from action space to latent space using VAE, plus a Reuse-then-Refine chunk refinement strategy, to achieve smooth and consistent motions at high frequencies (60 Hz) for contact-rich tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Action chunking fails at high frequencies (e.g., 60 Hz) — it cannot produce actions that are both temporally smooth and spatially consistent. The paper aims to solve this inconsistency problem for contact-rich robotic control.

Method: (1) Shift high-frequency action learning from action space to latent space using a variational autoencoder (VAE). (2) Introduce Reuse-then-Refine, a chunk-level refinement strategy that improves continuity between adjacent action chunks under asynchronous inference.

Result: Experiments on three real-world contact-rich robotic tasks show the approach consistently completes tasks with smooth motions, fewer pauses, and less jerky motion.

Conclusion: Latent-space learning with VAE plus Reuse-then-Refine enables smooth high-frequency (60 Hz) control for contact-rich robotic tasks, outperforming action-chunking baselines.

Abstract: Modern robotic policies increasingly rely on action chunking to execute complex tasks in the physical world. While action chunking improves temporal consistency at moderate action frequencies, it becomes insufficient when the action frequency is further increased (e.g., to 60~Hz). At such high frequencies, policies often fail to generate actions that are both temporally smooth and spatially consistent. We address this challenge by shifting high-frequency action learning from the action space to a latent space with variational autoencoder (VAE). This formulation significantly improves both temporal and spatial consistency of high-frequency control. To enable smooth real-time execution, we further introduce Reuse-then-Refine, a chunk-level refine strategy that improves continuity between adjacent action chunks under asynchronous inference. As a result, robots controlled by our policy can execute complex contact-rich tasks continuously, with less pauses and jerky motions. Experiments on three real-world contact-rich robotic tasks show that our approach consistently completes tasks with smooth motions. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/tars-robotics/RTR.

</details>


### [305] [FusionCore: A 23-State Unscented Kalman Filter for IMU, Wheel Encoder, GPS, and Visual SLAM Fusion in ROS 2](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25239)
*Manan Kharwar*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FusionCore is an open-source ROS 2 sensor fusion package using a 23-state UKF that fuses IMU, wheel odometry, GPS, and Visual SLAM into a 100 Hz odometry stream, outperforming robot_localization on the NCLT dataset with up to 22.2x lower ATE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address limitations in existing sensor fusion packages like robot_localization (numerical divergence, lack of online bias estimation, poor GPS-denied operation) by providing a more robust, accurate, and feature-rich open-source alternative.

Method: A 23-state Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF) with online wheel encoder yaw rate bias estimation via GPS heading cross-covariance, gyroscope/accelerometer bias estimation, native ECEF GPS handling, Mahalanobis chi-squared outlier gating, and adaptive noise covariance from innovation sequences.

Result: On twelve NCLT sequences (55-92 min each), FusionCore achieved lower ATE on ten of twelve sequences (1.2x to 22.2x improvement) while robot_localization UKF diverged numerically on all twelve sequences.

Conclusion: FusionCore provides a robust, accurate, and freely available sensor fusion solution that significantly outperforms the existing state-of-the-art ROS 2 package, with particular advantages in long-duration and GPS-denied scenarios.

Abstract: We present FusionCore, an open-source ROS 2 sensor fusion package that fuses IMU, wheel encoder odometry, GPS, and Visual SLAM pose into a single 100 Hz odometry stream using a 23-state Unscented Kalman Filter (UKF). The 23rd state is an online estimate of the wheel encoder's systematic yaw rate bias, identified through GPS heading cross-covariance and subtracted during GPS blackouts to reduce heading drift in coast mode. FusionCore also estimates gyroscope and accelerometer biases as explicit filter states, handles GPS natively in ECEF without a separate coordinate projection node, applies per-sensor Mahalanobis chi-squared outlier gating calibrated to measurement degrees of freedom, and adapts sensor noise covariance automatically from the innovation sequence. VSLAM pose fusion enables GPS-denied operation with any visual odometry or SLAM system, including automatic recovery from map reinitialization. We evaluate against robot_localization on twelve full-length sequences (55-92 min each) from the NCLT public dataset. FusionCore achieves lower Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) on ten of twelve sequences, with improvements ranging from 1.2x to 22.2x on winning sequences. The robot_localization UKF diverges numerically on all twelve sequences. FusionCore is available at https://github.com/manankharwar/fusioncore under the Apache 2.0 license.

</details>


### [306] [HumanEgo: Zero-Shot Robot Learning from Minutes of Human Egocentric Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24934)
*Zhi,Wang,Botao He,Kelin Yu,Seungjae Lee,Ruohan Gao,Furong Huang,Yiannis Aloimonos*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HumanEgo is a robot-data-free framework that transfers human egocentric video demonstrations to robots by lifting them to entity-level hand-object interaction representations and training a flow matching policy with dense auxiliary objectives, achieving 92.5% success across four real-world tasks with only 30 minutes of human video per task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human egocentric video captures rich manipulation demonstrations without robot hardware, but transferring these skills to robots is challenging due to the embodiment gap between humans and robots in visual appearance and kinematics.

Method: HumanEgo bridges the embodiment gap by (1) lifting each human demonstration to an entity-level representation of hand-object interaction, and (2) training a flow matching policy with dense auxiliary objectives that amplify supervision from every trajectory.

Result: With only 30 minutes of human videos per task, HumanEgo achieves 92.5% average success across four real-world tasks (75% with just 15 minutes), outperforms matched-time robot teleoperation by 41%, and robustly transfers zero-shot across novel robots, cameras, and environments.

Conclusion: HumanEgo is robot-data-free, hardware-agnostic, data-efficient, and zero-shot human-to-robot transferable, demonstrating effective skill transfer without requiring any robot demonstration data.

Abstract: Human egocentric video captures rich manipulation demonstrations without any robot hardware, yet transferring these skills to robots remains challenging due to the embodiment gap between human and robot in both visual appearance and kinematics. We present HumanEgo, a framework that bridges the embodiment gap by lifting each human demonstration to an entity-level representation of hand-object interaction, and training a flow matching policy with dense auxiliary objectives that amplify supervision from every trajectory. HumanEgo is robot-data-free, hardware-agnostic, data-efficient, and zero-shot human-to-robot transferable. With only 30 minutes of human videos per task, HumanEgo achieves 92.5% average success across four real-world tasks (75% with just 15 minutes), outperforms matched-time robot teleoperation by 41%, and robustly transfers zero-shot across novel robots, cameras, and environments.

</details>


### [307] [ARCANE-PedSynth: Synthetic Multi-Pedestrian Datasets with Behavioural Crossing Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24950)
*Muhammad Naveed Riaz,Maciej Wielgosz,Antonio M. López Peña*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ARCANE-PedSynth is an open-source CARLA-based framework that generates synthetic multi-pedestrian datasets with dense behavioral annotations for crossing prediction, overcoming CARLA's native 9% crossing rate with configurable rates up to 75% via hybrid AI-manual control, and produces synchronised RGB/LiDAR/DVS data—demonstrated via the PedSynth++ dataset (533 clips, 12 weather conditions).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the lack of diverse, densely annotated pedestrian crossing datasets for autonomous driving, specifically overcoming CARLA's limited native crossing rate (9%) and enabling controllable generation of realistic multi-pedestrian interactions.

Method: Hybrid AI-manual pedestrian control architecture; 12-state behavioural finite state machine with five character archetypes; CLI parameterisation; Docker containerisation for full reproducibility.

Result: Configurable crossing rate up to 75%; generated PedSynth++ dataset of 533 multi-pedestrian clips across 12 weather conditions with RGB, LiDAR, and DVS streams plus per-frame annotations (crossing labels, behavioural states, 2D pose keypoints).

Conclusion: ARCANE-PedSynth provides a reproducible, configurable framework for generating synthetic pedestrian datasets with dense behavioral annotations, enabling more robust pedestrian crossing prediction research in autonomous driving.

Abstract: We present ARCANE-PedSynth, an open-source CARLA-based software framework for generating synthetic multi-pedestrian datasets with dense behavioural annotations for pedestrian crossing prediction in autonomous driving. The framework overcomes CARLA's native 9% crossing rate through a hybrid AI-manual pedestrian control architecture, enabling configurable target rates up to 75%. A 12-state behavioural finite state machine with five character archetypes produces diverse crossing behaviours. The framework generates synchronised RGB, LiDAR, and DVS data with per-frame crossing labels, behavioural states, and estimated 2D pose keypoints. We demonstrate ARCANE-PedSynth through PedSynth++, an example dataset generated with the framework, comprising 533 multi-pedestrian clips across 12 weather conditions with RGB, LiDAR, and DVS streams. ARCANE-PedSynth is fully reproducible via CLI parameterisation and Docker containerisation.

</details>


### [308] [Bridging the Gap: Enabling Soft Actor Critic for High Performance Legged Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24975)
*Gianluca Sabatini,Chenhao Li,Marco Hutter*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The authors identify and fix key issues that prevented SAC from matching PPO's performance in massively parallel training for legged robots, achieving parity through targeted modifications like policy initialization, timeout-aware critic targets, and multi-step return estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPO is sample-inefficient (on-policy) and unsuitable for real-world fine-tuning on hardware. SAC is off-policy and better for sim-to-real transfer, but empirically underperforms PPO in massively parallel settings.

Method: Introduced targeted modifications to SAC: (1) policy initialization, (2) timeout-aware critic targets, and (3) multi-step return estimation.

Result: The modified SAC closes the performance gap with PPO entirely across multiple legged robot platforms and diverse locomotion tasks.

Conclusion: With the proposed modifications, SAC can be a viable alternative to PPO for massively parallel training, offering the added benefit of sample efficiency for real-world adaptation.

Abstract: Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) has become the de facto standard for training legged robots, thanks to its robustness and scalability in massively parallel simulation environments like IsaacLab. However, its on-policy nature makes it inherently sample-inefficient, preventing its use for continuous adaptation and fine-tuning on real hardware. Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), by contrast, is an off-policy algorithm that can reuse past experience, making it a natural candidate for sim-to-real transfer workflows where the same algorithm can be used both in simulation and for online learning on the real robot. Despite these advantages, SAC has consistently failed to match PPO's empirical performance in massively parallel training settings. This work identifies the root causes of this gap and introduces targeted modifications, covering policy initialization, timeout-aware critic targets, and multi-step return estimation, that enable SAC to train stably at scale. Evaluated across multiple legged robot platforms and diverse locomotion tasks, our approach closes the performance gap with PPO entirely.

</details>


### [309] [Loosely Coupled Factor Graph Optimization for Pseudolite-Augmented Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24980)
*Chih-Chun Chen,Lipeng Tan,Shiyu Bai,Heike Vallery*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a factor graph optimization framework that fuses pseudolite signals with GNSS and IMU data, achieving 22.8-41.3% error reduction over standard least-squares methods in degraded environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address positioning challenges in GNSS-degraded environments by integrating pseudolites into optimization-based frameworks, which remains an underexplored area.

Method: A loosely coupled factor graph optimization (FGO) framework that fuses GNSS/pseudolite least-squares solutions with IMU data.

Result: FGO achieves 22.8-41.3% reduction in mean 3D error over standard LS methods; incorporating PL transmitters improves accuracy over GNSS-IMU baseline, with performance depending on geometry.

Conclusion: The proposed FGO framework effectively improves positioning accuracy in GNSS-degraded environments by integrating pseudolites, though performance is geometry-dependent.

Abstract: In Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-degraded environments, pseudolites (PLs) provide additional signal sources to enhance positioning performance, but their integration in optimization-based frameworks remains limited. This paper presents a loosely coupled factor graph optimization (FGO) framework that fuses the GNSS/PL least-squares (LS) solutions with inertial measurement unit (IMU) data. The evaluation considers low GNSS visibility scenarios with four high-elevation GNSS satellites and up to two PL transmitters over an 80~s window. FGO achieves a 22.8\% to 41.3\% reduction in mean 3D error compared to standard LS methods. Compared to a GNSS-IMU baseline, incorporating PL transmitters further improves positioning accuracy, with performance depending on geometry.

</details>


### [310] [Learning, locomotion, and navigation of soft synthetic snakes in three-dimensional, heterogeneous environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24985)
*Xiaotian Zhang,Ali Albazroun,Tixian Wang,Songyuan Cui,Prashant G. Mehta,Mattia Gazzola*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces a computational framework that enables soft synthetic snakes to navigate unstructured 3D terrains by combining bio-inspired actuation/sensing models with reinforcement learning, using hierarchical training from simple to complex terrains, and demonstrating robustness in high-fidelity simulated environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Limbless terrestrial animals (e.g., snakes) have exceptional locomotor versatility unmatched by engineered robots; the goal is to bridge this gap by developing a control framework for soft synthetic snakes to navigate complex, heterogeneous 3D terrains.

Method: The framework uses bio-inspired actuation and sensing models to reduce control complexity of continuum bodies, integrated into a reinforcement learning architecture. Training proceeds hierarchically: first in simplified homogeneous terrains to learn locomotion primitives, then composing these primitives into adaptive strategies for complex landscapes.

Result: The method achieved robust navigation when deployed in high-fidelity 3D environments reconstructed from real-world imaging, demonstrating reliable traversal of unstructured terrains.

Conclusion: This work provides a physically-realistic simulation platform and practical control insights for continuum systems (soft snake robots) operating in natural terrains.

Abstract: Limbless terrestrial animals exhibit exceptional locomotor versatility and control, currently unmatched by engineered counterparts. Here, we introduce a computational framework that enables soft synthetic snakes to navigate unstructured, heterogeneous 3D terrains. Our approach is grounded in bio-inspired actuation and sensing models that reduce the control complexity inherent to high-degree-of-freedom, continuum bodies. These models are integrated into a reinforcement learning architecture to derive environment-traversing policies. Training first occurs in simplified, homogeneous terrains to learn locomotion primitives. These are then composed into adaptive strategies for complex landscapes. We demonstrate robustness by deploying a snake in high-fidelity 3D environments reconstructed from real-world imaging, achieving reliable navigation. Overall, this work provides a physically-realistic simulation platform and practical insights for the control of continuum systems in natural terrains.

</details>


### [311] [Stiffness Optimization for Concentrated Bending in Magnetically Actuated Catheters: Maintaining Steerability under Gradient Stiffness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25005)
*Jiewen Tan,Junnan Xue,Shing Shin Cheng,Shuang Song,Erli Lyu,Jiaole Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A stiffness-optimized multi-segment magnetically actuated catheter (SO-MAC) with a gradient-stiffness architecture achieves both efficient pushability and proximally concentrated bending, enabling stable 180° steering with a 3 mm bending radius and robust navigation through complex bronchial pathways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the fundamental trade-off in magnetically actuated soft catheters between efficient pushability (force transmission) and proximally concentrated bending for steerability, where higher stiffness aids force transmission but reduces steerability and lower stiffness enables bending but increases kinking/buckling risk under compressive loads.

Method: A multi-segment design integrating a decoupled steering-advancement mechanism with a gradient-stiffness architecture, where the proximal pivot stably concentrates bending during advancement and the distal section uses elastic recovery of a spring backbone to self-straighten and transmit propulsion while resisting friction-induced kinking/buckling.

Result: 1.5 mm-diameter SO-MAC achieved up to 180° steering with 3 mm bending radius at 10 mm tip; average shape error 1.39±0.56 mm, steering-pivot error 0.35±0.10 mm; pivot remained stable over 0-180° combined steering and advancement; robust navigation confirmed in bronchial phantom with visual feedback control.

Conclusion: The proposed gradient-stiffness approach effectively decouples steering and advancement, offering a practical solution for highly maneuverable yet pushable magnetic soft catheters suitable for complex anatomical navigation such as bronchoscopy.

Abstract: Achieving both efficient pushability (propulsion transmission) and proximally concentrated bending for steerability is challenging for magnetically actuated soft catheters: higher axial/bending stiffness improves force transmission but reduces steerability, whereas lower stiffness enables large, proximally concentrated bending yet increases kinking/buckling risk under compressive push loads. To address this trade-off, we propose a stiffness-optimized multi-segment magnetically actuated catheter (SO-MAC) that integrates a decoupled steering-advancement mechanism with a gradient-stiffness architecture. The SO-MAC concentrates bending about a stable proximal pivot during advancement while the distal section passively self-straightens to transmit propulsion, aided by the optimized stiffness distribution and elastic recovery of the spring backbone against friction-induced kinking/buckling. Over $0{-}180^{\circ}$ combined steering and advancement, the pivot remained stable and the distal tip advanced near-straight toward the target direction. A 1.5 mm-diameter SO-MAC achieved up to $180^{\circ}$ steering with a 3 mm bending radius at its 10 mm tip, with an average shape error of $1.39 \pm 0.56$ mm and a steering-pivot error of $0.35 \pm 0.10$ mm. Visual feedback control in a bronchial phantom further confirmed robust navigation through highly curved, bifurcating paths.

</details>


### [312] [Convex-Neural RRT*: Fast and Reliable Learning-Guided Sampling for High-Quality Robot Path Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25006)
*Hichem Cheriet,Badra Khellat Kihel,Samira Chouraqui,Bara J. Emran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Convex-Neural RRT* enhances RRT* path planning by using neural networks to predict promising waypoint regions and extracting convex candidate areas for focused sampling, achieving 30-98% computation time reduction and ~5% path length improvement with >99% success rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Sampling-based planners like RRT* often require many iterations to produce high-quality solutions. The authors aim to accelerate convergence while maintaining solution quality and global exploration capability.

Method: Neural guidance predicts informative waypoint regions near high-quality paths; convex candidate regions are extracted from these predictions to focus exploration on geometrically relevant areas while preserving global exploration in the RRT* framework.

Result: 30-75% computation reduction vs. neural-guided variants, 88-98% vs. LTA*, ~5% average path length improvement over classical RRT* (larger gains in complex environments), and >99% success rate across varying obstacle densities.

Conclusion: Convex-guided neural sampling effectively balances computational efficiency and solution quality, making it suitable for time-sensitive robotic navigation tasks.

Abstract: Sampling-based algorithms for robot path planning offer probabilistic completeness and strong empirical convergence properties across environments with diverse obstacle configurations. However, in practice, these methods often require many iterations to obtain high-quality solutions. This paper proposes Convex-Neural RRT*, an enhanced RRT* variant that incorporates neural guidance to predict informative waypoint regions near high-quality paths. Convex candidate regions are extracted from these predictions, enabling the planner to concentrate exploration on geometrically relevant areas while preserving global exploration. The proposed algorithm is evaluated against Neural RRT*, Neural Informed RRT*, classical RRT*, and LTA* across three environment types and 18 benchmark maps. Experimental results show that Convex-Neural RRT* reduces computation time by 30-75% compared to neural-guided variants and up to 88-98% relative to LTA*, while achieving an average path length reduction of approximately 5% compared to classical RRT*, with larger improvements observed in complex environments. The method also maintains an overall success rate above 99% across varying obstacle densities. These findings indicate that convex-guided neural sampling provides an effective balance between computational efficiency and solution quality, supporting its applicability to time-sensitive robotic navigation tasks.

</details>


### [313] [Performance Comparison of Classical and Neural Sampling Algorithms for Robotic Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25010)
*Hichem Cheriet,Badra Khellat Kihel,Samira Chouraqui*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper implements and evaluates RRT*, Neural RRT*, and Neural Informed RRT* in environments with convex/concave obstacles. Neural-guided planners produce up to 14% shorter and 55-75% smoother paths, with Neural Informed RRT* performing best overall, though with slightly increased computation time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve sampling-based motion planning efficiency by integrating AI, addressing limitations of conventional RRT* in path quality and smoothness for robotic and UAV navigation.

Method: Implemented three algorithms (RRT*, Neural RRT*, Neural Informed RRT*) and evaluated them in environments with convex and concave obstacles at varying densities.

Result: Neural-guided planners achieve up to 14% shorter paths and 55-75% smoother trajectories. Neural Informed RRT* outperforms the others in path length and smoothness, though with a slight increase in computation time.

Conclusion: AI-guided sampling strategies effectively improve reliability and trajectory efficiency in robotic and UAV navigation, highlighting AI's growing importance in real-time path planning.

Abstract: Integrating artificial intelligence (AI) into sampling-based motion planning provides new possibilities for improving autonomous navigation efficiency. In this paper, three algorithms, namely RRT*, Neural RRT*, and Neural Informed RRT*, are implemented and evaluated on environments containing convex and concave obstacles with different obstacle densities. The obtained results indicate that neural-guided planners improve path quality, producing up to 14\% shorter paths and 55--75\% smoother trajectories compared with the conventional RRT* algorithm. Among the evaluated methods, Neural Informed RRT* achieves the best overall performance in terms of path length and trajectory smoothness. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of AI-guided sampling strategies for improving reliability and trajectory efficiency in robotic and UAV navigation, despite a slight increase in computation time. Overall, the study highlights the growing importance of artificial intelligence in real-time robotic path planning applications.

</details>


### [314] [Micro-Swarm Locomotion Optimization in Dynamic Flow using Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25025)
*Josef Berman,Oren Gal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a hybrid CFD + Multi-Objective MARL framework (using PCGrad) to control 16 magnetically actuated micro-robots in oscillatory flow, achieving coordinated swarm behaviors (hydrodynamic throttling, ratchet repositioning) with improved progress, energy efficiency, and smoothness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Coordinating micro-robotic swarms in time-dependent fluid environments (e.g., pulsatile arterial flow) is an unsolved challenge; prior methods fail to capture realistic fluid-agent interactions or resolve conflicting objectives.

Method: A hybrid framework coupling a high-fidelity incompressible Navier-Stokes solver with decentralized Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), using PCGrad to reconcile three conflicting rewards (upstream progression, energy efficiency, motion smoothness).

Result: Without PCGrad, energy/smoothness collapse. With PCGrad: progress reward 6.5-7.0, energy efficiency 0.63-0.65, smoothness 0.97-0.99. Three emergent phases: hydrodynamic throttling, cycle-synchronized ratchet, individualized final approach.

Conclusion: Time-dependent fluid-agent interactions can be directly captured in multi-objective RL loops, providing a physically grounded paradigm for micro-swarm control in biomedical, environmental, and industrial applications.

Abstract: Coordinating micro-robotic swarms in physiologically realistic, time-dependent fluid environments remains an unsolved challenge for biomedical and environmental applications. We present a hybrid Computational Fluid Dynamics - Multi-Objective Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning framework that directly couples a high-fidelity incompressible Navier-Stokes solver with decentralized proximal policy optimization to learn physically consistent swarm control strategies in oscillatory flow. Sixteen magnetically actuated micro-robots navigate a pulsatile arterial waveform, simultaneously optimizing upstream progression, energy conservation, and motion smoothness, reconciled using PCGrad surgery. Without PCGrad, energy efficiency and smoothness rewards collapse to near zero within 10,000 training steps while progress exhibits persistent large-amplitude oscillations, confirming that gradient conflict resolution is a structural requirement rather than an optional refinement in this domain. The converged policy achieves a progress reward of 6.5-7.0, a sustained energy efficiency of 0.63-0.65, and near-maximum smoothness (0.97-0.99), representing improvements over brute-force baselines on the primary objective while both baselines yield negative energy efficiency throughout. Training reveals three emergent behavioral phases: a collective two-layer hydrodynamic throttling formation that suppresses peak channel velocities during forward flow, a cycle-synchronized ratchet mechanism that exploits flow reversals for upstream repositioning, and an individualized final approach as agents near the success boundary. These results establish that time-dependent fluid-agent interactions can be captured directly within multi-objective reinforcement learning loops, offering a physically grounded paradigm for micro-swarm control in biomedical navigation, environmental monitoring, and industrial microfluidics.

</details>


### [315] [ParkingWorld: End-to-End Autonomous Parking Reinforcement Learning from Corrective Experience in 3DGS Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25029)
*Zhengcheng Yu,Changze Li,Haoran Liu,Tong Qin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes CIL-SERL, a correction-in-the-loop reinforcement learning framework trained in a 3D Gaussian Splatting simulator, using a multi-level replay buffer to store expert corrections and failed trajectories, achieving improved success rates and efficiency for autonomous parking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous parking faces challenges due to narrow, cluttered spaces. Imitation learning needs huge expert data and generalizes poorly; traditional RL suffers from high training overhead, inefficient exploration, and often fails to learn viable strategies in challenging parking scenarios.

Method: CIL-SERL uses a multi-level replay buffer inspired by error-correction notebooks, hierarchically storing standard RL rollouts, human corrective interventions, failed exploration trajectories, and rollback-based correction segments for structured sampling and targeted training.

Result: Substantial improvements in parking success rate, operational efficiency, and safety performance across diverse scenarios in both 3DGS simulation and physical vehicle platform evaluations.

Conclusion: The CIL-SERL framework effectively addresses the limitations of imitation learning and traditional RL for autonomous parking, with validated practical applicability in real-world settings.

Abstract: Autonomous parking demands precise low-speed maneuvering within narrow, cluttered, and highly constrained environments, where vehicles must navigate tight spaces while avoiding static obstacles and complex geometric boundaries. Unlike imitation learning, which typically requires massive volumes of high-quality expert demonstrations to converge to a stable policy and often suffers from limited generalization to unseen scenarios, traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods face persistent challenges including excessive training overhead, inefficient exploration, and even failure to learn viable parking strategies in challenging settings. To address these limitations, this paper presents a correction-in-the-loop sample-efficient reinforcement learning (CIL-SERL) framework for end-to-end autonomous parking, which is entirely trained in a photorealistic 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) parking simulator that enables high-fidelity digital reconstruction of real-world scenes. Inspired by error-correction notebooks used in learning practice, we design a novel multi-level replay buffer mechanism. These buffers hierarchically organize and store standard RL rollouts, human corrective interventions, failed exploration trajectories, and rollback-based correction segments in separate yet interconnected memory regions, facilitating structured sampling and targeted learning during training. The proposed framework is systematically evaluated in both the 3DGS simulation environment and a physical vehicle platform. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves substantial improvements in parking success rate, operational efficiency, and safety performance across diverse scenarios, validating the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed CIL-SERL-based end-to-end autonomous parking solution.

</details>


### [316] [RAMBA: 4D Radar Mapping by Bundle Adjustment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25041)
*Jianzhu Huai,Yiwen Chen,Binliang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RAMBA is a radar bundle-adjustment framework that jointly refines radar frame states using geometric residuals, IMU factors, and ego-velocity constraints to achieve globally consistent 4D radar mapping, improving map consistency and trajectory accuracy over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline global map refinement for 4D radar remains underexplored despite recent advances in online radar and radar-inertial odometry.

Method: RAMBA jointly refines radar frame states using covariance-weighted geometric residuals (extending pairwise GICP to multi-frame optimization with voxel-based correspondences), IMU preintegration factors, and radar ego-velocity constraints, while enforcing temporal consistency and supporting loop-closure constraints.

Result: Experiments on ColoRadar and SNAIL Radar datasets show improved map consistency and enhanced trajectory accuracy over radar-inertial odometry and pose-graph optimization baselines.

Conclusion: RAMBA effectively fills the gap in offline 4D radar global map refinement, achieving globally consistent mapping with better accuracy than existing methods.

Abstract: 4D radar is increasingly attractive for robotic mapping because it provides range, azimuth, elevation, and Doppler measurements while remaining robust in adverse visibility conditions. Although recent radar and radar--inertial odometry methods have achieved promising online state estimation performance, offline global map refinement for 4D radar remains underexplored. This paper presents RAMBA, a radar bundle-adjustment framework for globally consistent 4D radar mapping. Given initial poses and radar frames from a radar--inertial odometry front-end, RAMBA jointly refines radar frame states using covariance-weighted geometric residuals, IMU preintegration factors, and radar ego-velocity constraints. The geometric residuals extend pairwise GICP to a multi-frame optimization by forming voxel-based correspondences across selected frames and weighting each residual with point covariances. To improve robustness against drift and revisits, RAMBA enforces temporal consistency during correspondence formation while explicitly supporting loop-closure constraints. Experiments on the ColoRadar and SNAIL Radar datasets show that RAMBA improves map consistency and usually enhances trajectory accuracy over radar--inertial odometry and pose-graph optimization baselines.

</details>


### [317] [X-DiffVLA: X-Embodied Diffusion Action Heads for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25044)
*Boyu Li,Chaoyi Xu,Haoqi Yuan,Xinrun Xu,Börje F. Karlsson,Dongbin Zhao,Haoran Li,Zongqing Lu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: X-DiffVLA is a diffusion-based VLA model with a unified cross-embodied action head that leverages generative diffusion, Embodiment Forcing (classifier-free guidance), and Morphological Tree Diffusion to enable effective cross-embodied policy learning, achieving SOTA results (15.3% and 12.5% improvement) across diverse robotic embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models require embodiment-specific fine-tuning, limiting generalization and knowledge transfer across different robotic embodiments (e.g., grippers vs. dexterous hands).

Method: Proposes X-DiffVLA with: (1) a unified cross-embodied action head using diffusion models; (2) Embodiment Forcing, a classifier-free guidance technique for implicit embodiment-specific action generation; (3) Morphological Tree Diffusion to strengthen behavioral correlations across diverse end-effectors.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with 15.3% improvement on RoboCasa and 12.5% on Isaac Gym across embodiments from grippers to dexterous hands; real-world evaluations confirm robustness.

Conclusion: X-DiffVLA effectively enables scalable cross-embodied policy learning by capturing diversity and latent correlations across embodiments without explicit supervision, overcoming the fine-tuning bottleneck.

Abstract: Learning universal policies from cross-embodied data remains a fundamental challenge in robotics. Although Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are pre-trained on large and diverse datasets, they typically rely on embodiment-specific fine-tuning to achieve strong performance in downstream tasks. This requirement severely limits their generalization capability and restricts knowledge transfer across embodiments performing similar tasks. To overcome these limitations, we focus on cross-embodied settings with shared robotic bases and heterogeneous end-effectors, and propose X-DiffVLA, a diffusion-based VLA model featuring a unified cross-embodied action head. X-DiffVLA can leverage the generative strengths of diffusion models to capture both the diversity and latent correlations in cross-embodied datasets. Specifically, we introduce Embodiment Forcing, a classifier-free guidance technique to implicitly steer action generation toward embodiment-specific functional components, capturing fine-grained structural nuances without explicit supervision. In addition, a Morphological Tree Diffusion approach is designed to strengthen behavioral correlations across diverse end-effectors, maximizing the transferability of heterogeneous demonstrations. Experimental results across RoboCasa and Isaac Gym, covering different embodiments from grippers to dexterous hands, show that X-DiffVLA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with improvements of 15.3% and 12.5%, respectively. Real-world evaluations further validate the robustness of the proposed framework and its effectiveness in scalable cross-embodied policy learning.

</details>


### [318] [A Decentralized LiDAR-SLAM System with Certifiably Optimal Pose Graph Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25051)
*Baoshan Song,Feng Huang,Li-Ta Hsu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents the first decentralized LiDAR-SLAM system using a certifiably optimal PGO backend (RBCD algorithm) that achieves globally consistent trajectory estimation without accurate initial guesses, improving RMSE by up to 48.9% over DiSCo-SLAM.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decentralized multi-robot LiDAR-SLAM suffers from suboptimal convergence and long-term inconsistency due to reliance on local-search optimization or one-time coordinate alignment, especially in large-scale or degenerate environments.

Method: Integrates a certifiably optimal Pose Graph Optimization (PGO) backend using the Riemannian Block Coordinate Descent (RBCD) algorithm into a decentralized LiDAR-SLAM framework.

Result: The framework achieves superior robustness with trajectory RMSE improvement of up to 48.9% compared to the state-of-the-art DiSCo-SLAM.

Conclusion: The proposed decentralized LiDAR-SLAM system with certifiably optimal PGO backend effectively overcomes suboptimal convergence and inconsistency issues, demonstrating significant accuracy improvements in multi-robot SLAM.

Abstract: Decentralized multi-robot LiDAR-SLAM is essential for collaborative missions but faces significant challenges in maintaining global consistency. Existing frameworks predominantly rely on local-search optimization or one-time coordinate alignment, which are prone to suboptimal convergence and long-term inconsistency, especially in large-scale or degenerate environments. To address these limitations, this paper presents the first decentralized LiDAR-SLAM system that integrates a state-of-the-art certifiably optimal Pose Graph Optimization (PGO) backend. By leveraging the Riemannian Block Coordinate Descent (RBCD) algorithm, our system ensures globally consistent trajectory estimation without requiring accurate initial guesses. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework achieves superior robustness, improving trajectory RMSE by up to 48.9% compared to the state-of-the-art DiSCo-SLAM.

</details>


### [319] [Soft Pneumatic Actuators for Soft Robotics: A Motion-Based Review of Actuation Mechanisms and Performance Trade-offs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25109)
*Mohammed Abboodi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This review categorizes soft pneumatic actuators by four motion classes (linear, bending, twisting, omnidirectional) and analyzes how design features like braid angle, fold geometry, fiber orientation, and chamber arrangement affect performance, offering guidance for selection and comparison in soft robotics applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As the soft pneumatic actuator literature has expanded, it has become increasingly difficult to determine which mechanism is most suitable for a given application and which reported results can be compared across studies.

Method: The paper reviews soft pneumatic actuators according to design strategies for four motion classes (linear, bending, twisting, omnidirectional), analyzing structural features such as braid angle, fold geometry, fiber orientation, chamber arrangement, structural asymmetry, and internal constraint layers, and discussing how these affect various performance metrics.

Result: The review identifies key conditions for selecting/comparing actuators (pressure, loading, size, pneumatic supply, hysteresis), explaining why actuators with similar motion outputs can differ substantially in design requirements and practical suitability.

Conclusion: The review highlights design priorities needed for compact, efficient, repeatable, and deployable soft pneumatic systems in wearable, biomedical, and mobile robotic applications.

Abstract: Soft pneumatic actuators are widely used in soft robotics because they can produce large motions while remaining compliant enough to interact safely with objects, environments, and the human body. However, their performance is not solely determined by pressure. Instead, the response depends on the way the actuator is built, including the shape of its chambers, the placement of reinforcements, the use of folds, material stiffness, and the constraints that guide its deformation. As the literature has expanded, it has become more difficult to determine which mechanism is most suitable for a given application and which reported results can be compared across studies. This review examines soft pneumatic actuators according to the design strategies used to generate four motion classes: linear, bending, twisting, and omnidirectional actuation. For each class, it analyzes the structural features that define the deformation path, including braid angle, fold geometry, fiber orientation, chamber arrangement, structural asymmetry, and internal constraint layers. It then discusses how the design choice affect motion output, force generation, air demand, repeatability, durability, fabrication difficulty, and robotic integration. The review further identifies key conditions that must be considered when selecting or comparing actuators, including pressure, loading condition, actuator size, pneumatic supply, and hysteresis This approach helps explain why actuators with similar motion outputs may differ substantially in design requirements, pneumatic demand, and practical suitability. It also highlights the design priorities needed for compact, efficient, repeatable, and deployable soft pneumatic systems in wearable, biomedical, and mobile robotic applications.

</details>


### [320] [InvariantCloud: A Globally Invariant, Uniquely Indexed Point Cloud Framework for Robust 6-DoF Tactile Pose Tracking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25216)
*Pengfei Ye,Yuxiang Ma,Yi Zhou,Wei Chen,Wenzhen Dong,Molong Duan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: InvariantCloud is a 6-DoF tactile pose estimation framework that uses globally invariant point cloud registration of surface marker constellations on tactile sensors to achieve superior yaw tracking accuracy and re-localization repeatability in long-sequence robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent advances in imitation learning and vision-language models require high-fidelity tactile perception, with accurate 6-DoF tactile object pose estimation being crucial for precise robotic manipulation. Existing methods suffer from cumulative drift and poor yaw (Z-axis) rotation estimation.

Method: InvariantCloud leverages the global invariance of surface marker constellations on vision-based tactile sensors, using a one-shot globally invariant point cloud registration approach to suppress cumulative drift and improve yaw rotation accuracy.

Result: InvariantCloud achieves superior yaw tracking accuracy and re-localization repeatability compared to existing benchmarks, demonstrating precision and robustness in long-sequence manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: The proposed one-shot globally invariant registration effectively overcomes long-standing limitations in yaw estimation, enabling more reliable long-sequence tactile-based manipulation.

Abstract: Recent advances in imitation learning and vision-language models highlight the need for high-fidelity tactile perception, with 6-DoF tactile object pose estimation providing a crucial foundation for precise robotic manipulation. We introduce InvariantCloud, a 6-DoF pose estimation framework that leverages the global invariance of surface marker constellations on vision-based tactile sensors. In contrast to recent approaches, our one-shot globally invariant point cloud registration suppresses cumulative drift and overcomes long-standing limitations in accurately estimating yaw (Z-axis) rotation. Experimental verifications show that InvariantCloud achieves superior yaw tracking accuracy and re-localization repeatability compared to existing benchmarks, demonstrating its precision and robustness in long-sequence manipulation tasks.

</details>


### [321] [GreenSeg: Ground Segmentation Algorithm for Agricultural Robots in Mediterranean Greenhouses using RGB-D Point Clouds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25279)
*Fernando Cañadas-Aránega,José C. Moreno,José L. Blanco-Claraco*

Main category: cs.RO
