TL;DR: GreenSeg is a robust RGB-D perception framework for autonomous navigation in Mediterranean greenhouses, using dual-layer validation (global plane fitting + curvature filter + region growing) that outperforms benchmarks by up to 19.24% in mIoU during critical maneuvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Greenhouse agriculture in the Mediterranean region faces automation challenges due to narrow aisles, heterogeneous terrains, and severe optical interference (specular reflections, ghost points) from polyethylene covers. Traditional 3D LiDAR solutions are economically unscalable, motivating a cost-effective RGB-D perception approach.

Method: GreenSeg introduces a dual-layer validation strategy: (1) robust global plane fitting combined with a surface curvature filter for terrain adaptability, and (2) seed-point-based Region Growing constraint to ensure spatial continuity of the navigable plane.

Result: Evaluated on the AGRICOBIOT I platform across four diurnal scenarios, GreenSeg achieved peak improvements of 11.58% in mean Recall and 19.24% in mIoU during critical rotational maneuvers at corridor ends, consistently outperforming benchmark segmentation methods.

Conclusion: GreenSeg enables stable and safe autonomous navigation in unstructured, dynamic agricultural environments that are budget-constrained and sensitive to lighting conditions.

Abstract: Greenhouse agriculture in the Mediterranean region faces significant automation challenges due to its unique structural and environmental constraints. These environments are characterized by extremely narrow aisles, heterogeneous terrains ranging from concrete to tilled soil and severe optical interference caused by polyethylene covers, which induce specular reflections and "ghost points" in depth sensors. While autonomous navigation is essential for digitizing agricultural tasks, traditional solutions often rely on expensive 3D LiDAR systems that are economically unscalable for most facilities. To address this, this paper presents GreenSeg, a robust perception framework for autonomous navigation using RGB-D sensing. The proposed method introduces a dual-layer validation strategy: a robust global plane fitting combined with a surface curvature filter for terrain adaptability, and a seed-point-based Region Growing constraint to ensure the spatial continuity of the navigable plane. Experimental validation was conducted using the AGRICOBIOT I platform across four diurnal scenarios with varying solar elevations. The results show that GreenSeg consistently outperforms benchmark segmentation methods, achieving peak improvements of 11.58% in mean Recall and 19.24% in mIoU during critical rotational maneuvers at the end of corridors. These findings confirm that the proposed algorithm enables stable and safe autonomous navigation in unstructured, dynamic agricultural environments that are subject to budget constraints and sensitive to lighting conditions.

</details>


### [322] [Parallel Differentiable Reachability for Learning and Planning with Certified Neural Dynamics and Controllers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25346)
*Keyi Shen,Glen Chou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A differentiable, parallelizable reachability framework in JAX that combines Taylor-model flowpipe construction with CROWN-style bound propagation, enabling certified training and reachability-aware MPC for neural network-controlled systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reachability tools for NN-controlled systems are often non-differentiable, overly conservative, or too slow for modern learning and online planning, creating a gap between formal verification and practical robotics.

Method: Combines Taylor-model flowpipe construction with CROWN-style linear bound propagation in JAX, using a unified affine-preserving representation that supports GPU batching and automatic differentiation.

Result: Demonstrates practical online planning with certified reachable-set over-approximations on non-prehensile manipulation, quadrotor tasks, hardware, and higher-dimensional (up to 72D) evaluations.

Conclusion: The framework enables both certified training for reachability-friendly models and reachability-aware MPC with gradient-based refinement, bridging formal guarantees with real-time robotics applications.

Abstract: Neural network (NN) dynamics models and control policies achieve strong performance in robotics, but providing sound guarantees under uncertainty remains difficult, especially for closed-loop NN systems. Existing reachability tools provide formal over-approximations, yet are often non-differentiable, overly conservative, or too slow for modern learning and online planning pipelines. To address this, we present a parallelizable, differentiable reachability framework in JAX for continuous- and discrete-time systems with analytical and NN-based dynamics and controllers. Our framework combines Taylor-model flowpipe construction with CROWN-style linear bound propagation through a unified representation that preserves affine dependencies while supporting GPU-batched computation and automatic differentiation. Building on this reachability primitive, we develop (i) a certified training method that encourages reachability-friendly dynamics models and controllers, and (ii) a reachability-aware sampling-based MPC scheme with gradient-based refinement. Experiments on non-prehensile manipulation and quadrotor tasks, including hardware and higher-dimensional evaluations (up to 72D), demonstrate practical online planning while maintaining certified reachable-set over-approximations under bounded uncertainty.

</details>


### [323] [Prior Policy Guided Dual-Agent Coordinated Manipulation Planning of Spacecraft-Manipulator System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25362)
*Yuhui Hu,Dong Zhou,Kaihong Ouyang,Zhongliang Yu,Jianfeng Lv,Xiangyu Shao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DACMP framework for coordinated space manipulator and base attitude control using prior policy-guided deep reinforcement learning with Timestep-level Expert Switching Guidance (TESG), achieving high-precision pose reaching and attitude stabilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong dynamic coupling between manipulator and base threatens spacecraft attitude stability and mission safety, requiring a coordinated control solution.

Method: Proposes DACMP (Dual-Agent Coordinated Manipulation Planning) framework with a prior policy-guided DRL algorithm incorporating Timestep-level Expert Switching Guidance (TESG) mechanism for improved learning efficiency and convergence.

Result: DACMP significantly outperforms baseline DRL algorithms in task success rate and control precision; robustness validated under system constraints, disturbances, and perception uncertainties.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the coupling challenge between manipulator and base, with code and simulations publicly available.

Abstract: The strong dynamic coupling between the manipulator and the base poses a significant challenge to maintaining spacecraft attitude stability, potentially compromising mission safety. In this paper, we propose a Dual-Agent Coordinated Manipulation Planning (DACMP) framework that simultaneously achieves high-precision end-effector pose reaching for a 6-DoF space manipulator and attitude stabilization of the base spacecraft. To enhance learning efficiency, we present a prior policy-guided Deep Reinforcement Learning algorithm incorporating the Timestep-level Expert Switching Guidance (TESG) mechanism, thereby promoting global convergence and improving task success rates. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DACMP significantly outperforms baseline DRL algorithms in terms of task success rate and control precision. Furthermore, the robustness of DACMP is validated under various challenging scenarios, including system constraints, environmental disturbances, and perception uncertainties. The code and simulation configurations are available on GitHub: https://github.com/HIT-YuhuiHu/DACMP.

</details>


### [324] [FOUND-IT: Foundation-model-first Task-driven 3D Scene Graphs with Granularity on Demand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25371)
*Dominic Maggio,Nicolas Gorlo,Luca Carlone*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FOUND-IT is a real-time approach for building hierarchical task-driven 3D scene graphs of indoor/outdoor environments using an uncalibrated monocular camera. It leverages geometric foundation models with an extra traversability head, dynamically adjusts object granularity based on evolving tasks, and achieves 79% higher accuracy on SG3D grounding benchmarks while running on a Jetson Thor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing 3D scene graph approaches assume a predefined, fixed set of tasks. The authors aim to handle realistic scenarios where tasks evolve during robot operation (e.g., switching between manipulation and navigation mid-task), enabling dynamic adjustment of scene representation granularity.

Method: FOUND-IT extends geometric foundation models (like VGGT) by adding an extra head for traversability reconstruction (the "places" layer). It dynamically adjusts object/region granularity based on the current task. An agentic query approach retrieves scene graph information. The system runs on monocular video input from an uncalibrated camera.

Result: 79% higher accuracy on the ASHiTA SG3D task grounding benchmark; real-time performance on a ground robot using Jetson Thor; successful 3D scene graph construction from casually captured YouTube apartment tours.

Conclusion: FOUND-IT demonstrates that task-driven, dynamically adjustable 3D scene graphs are feasible for real-world robotics applications without predefined task lists, enabling robust loco-manipulation and real-time operation on edge hardware.

Abstract: We present the first approach to build hierarchical task-driven 3D scene graphs of arbitrary indoor or outdoor environments using an uncalibrated monocular camera in real-time. We leverage geometric foundation models to estimate geometric attributes of the scene graph (e.g., object bounding boxes), but we also observe that traversability information (the "places" layer of a scene graph) can be directly reconstructed by adding an extra head to existing geometric foundation models, like VGGT. Our approach is task-driven in the sense that we adjust the granularity of the objects and regions in the map depending on the task; for instance, during a manipulation task, our approach is able to resolve small knobs on a stove, while during a navigation task it can focus on large objects (e.g., the entire stove). However, in a major departure from related work, we consider the realistic case where the list of tasks is not predefined and fixed, but evolves as the robot operates. This naturally allows dealing with complex loco-manipulation tasks, where the robot can dynamically adjust its representation as the task unfolds. We dub the resulting approach FOUND-IT. FOUND-IT also includes an agentic approach to query information in the scene graph. In addition to achieving 79% higher accuracy on the ASHiTA SG3D task grounding benchmark, we demonstrate FOUND-IT runs in real-time on a ground robot using a Jetson Thor. Furthermore, to highlight the robustness of our method, we demonstrate constructing 3D scene graphs on casually captured realtor apartment tours from YouTube. Code will be made available upon publication.

</details>


### [325] [Decision-Making with Lightweight Confidence-Aware Language Model for Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25393)
*Ruoyu Yao,Ruiguo Zhong,Pei Liu,Mingxing Peng,Rui Yang,Jun Ma*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a lightweight, confidence-aware language model for autonomous driving decision-making. It uses a multi-agent workflow (action voting, confidence assessment, summarization) with CoT reasoning to generate demonstrations, then distills them into a dual-head small LM via confidence-aware fine-tuning and RAG. Achieves SOTA success rates on nuPlan with low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs/MLLMs show promise for autonomous driving but suffer from excessive computational overhead and high inference latency, making deployment difficult in resource-constrained AD systems. The gap between complex multimodal intention reasoning and efficient inference needs to be bridged.

Method: 1) Multi-agent collaborative workflow (action voting, confidence assessment, summarization agents) for generating confidence-annotated CoT decision demonstrations. 2) Knowledge distillation into a lightweight dual-head language model for joint decision probability prediction and textual rationale generation. 3) Confidence-aware fine-tuning strategy with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) for improved adaptability and data efficiency.

Result: State-of-the-art success rates on nuPlan benchmark in both regular and long-tail scenarios, while maintaining low inference latency.

Conclusion: The proposed lightweight confidence-aware framework effectively bridges the gap between sophisticated multimodal reasoning and efficient real-time inference for autonomous driving, achieving strong performance without excessive computational cost.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) have demonstrated immense potential in autonomous driving (AD) by offering human-like reasoning and open-world generalization. However, the excessive computational overhead and high inference latency of these massive models severely hinder their deployment in resource-constrained AD systems. To address this challenge, we propose a novel decision-making framework utilizing a lightweight confidence-aware language model, which bridges the gap between complex multimodal intention reasoning and efficient inference. Specifically, we design a multi-agent collaborative workflow, comprising action voting, confidence assessment, and summarization agents, to generate high-quality, confidence-annotated decision demonstrations via explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. These demonstrations are then distilled into a lightweight language model featuring a dual-head architecture, enabling the joint prediction of decision probabilities and the generation of textual rationales. The distillation is realized via a confidence-aware fine-tuning strategy coupled with Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to enhance the model's adaptability and data efficiency. Comprehensive closed-loop experiments on the nuPlan benchmark demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) success rates in both regular and long-tail scenarios while maintaining low inference latency.

</details>


### [326] [Path Following Control System of Line-of-Sight Guidance for Robotic Dolphin with Multi-Link Mechanism in Underwater Simulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25401)
*Takumi Asada,Takao Oki,Hideo Furuhashi,Kenta Tabata,Renato Miyagusuku,Koichi Ozaki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper proposes a path following system for Biomimetic Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (BAUVs) with multi-link mechanisms and evaluates it through underwater simulation, demonstrating parameter determination and control method evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BAUVs with multi-link mechanisms are useful for aquatic observation, but their path following systems are limited and not well-evaluated. Prior simulation is needed because models differ by biomimetic type.

Method: The authors designed a path following system specifically for BAUVs with multi-link mechanisms and tested it in an underwater simulation environment.

Result: They successfully designed a suitable path following system, determined parameters using the simulator, and evaluated different control methods.

Conclusion: The proposed simulation-based approach enables effective path following system design and evaluation for multi-link BAUVs, addressing limitations in existing systems.

Abstract: Biomimetic autonomous underwater vehicle (BAUV) with multi-link mechanism is widely used in aquatic life observation and environmental surveys due to its low power consumption and high maneuverability. An environmental survey requires a path following system that automatically follows specific points. However, the path following system of BAUV is limited, and its evaluation with multi-link mechanism robots has not yet been clarified. The path following system in BAUV requires prior simulation because the model differs depending on the type of biomimetics. In this study, we propose a path following system for BAUVs with a multi-link mechanism and evaluation in underwater simulation. In this result, it was possible to design a path following system suitable for BAUV, determine parameters using a simulator, and evaluate control methods.

</details>


### [327] [How to Mitigate the Distribution Shift Problem in Robotics Control: A Robust and Adaptive Approach Based on Offline to Online Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25414)
*Hyung-Suk Yoon,Seung-Woo Seo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robust offline-to-online imitation learning framework that addresses distribution shift by using supplementary demonstrations in offline phase to broaden state-action coverage, and detecting/adapting to shifts via self-supervised imitation learning during online inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Distribution shift in imitation learning causes agents to fail on unseen states due to narrow coverage of expert demonstrations.

Method: A lifelong, multi-phase framework: (1) offline phase uses supplementary demonstrations + discriminator to broaden state-action coverage; (2) online phase detects distribution shift and performs self-supervised imitation learning from online experiences.

Result: Outperforms baseline algorithms in MuJoCo environments with better robustness to distribution shift and superior online adaptation performance.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively handles distribution shift in imitation learning through a combined offline preparation and online adaptation approach.

Abstract: Distribution shift in imitation learning refers to the problem that the agent cannot plan proper actions for a state that has not been visited during the training. This problem can be largely attributed to the inherently narrow state-action coverage provided by expert demonstrations over the full environment. In this paper, we propose a robust offline to adaptive online imitation learning framework that handles the distribution shift problem in a lifelong, multi-phase scheme. In the offline learning phase, we leverage supplementary demonstrations to broaden the state-action coverage of the policy by utilizing a discriminator to effectively train the policy with supplementary demonstrations, thereby enhancing the robustness of the policy to distribution shift. In the subsequent online inference phase, our framework detects the occurrence of distribution shift and conducts self-supervised imitation learning from online experiences to adapt the policy to the online environments. Through extensive evaluations in MuJoCo environments, we demonstrate that our method exhibits better robustness to distribution shift and better adaptation performance to online environments than the baseline algorithms, which indicates superior performance of our framework against the distribution shift.

</details>


### [328] [OPAL: Omnidirectional Path-efficient Aerial 3D expLoration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25423)
*Yoga Satwik Chappidi,Avideh Zakhor*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OPAL is an exploration framework for drones that uses deliberate 360-degree yaw rotation at ambiguous branch points instead of compute-heavy global planning. It offers multiple variants (model-free, LLM, VLM) and achieves shorter travel distances with lower computational complexity, though with longer total exploration time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous exploration in unknown environments requires both compute efficiency and minimal travel distance. Existing methods like EDEN and FALCON are computationally complex. The authors aim to develop a simpler, more efficient exploration approach.

Method: OPAL performs deliberate 360-degree yaw rotation at ambiguous branch points to gather information, replacing heavy global tour planning. Multiple variants are tested: model-free, LLM-based, and VLM-based frontier selection. The search radius for including frontiers is varied to analyze tradeoffs.

Result: OPAL is computationally simpler than EDEN/FALCON, achieves shorter travel distances, and higher coverage-versus-distance AUC. However, total exploration time increases due to in-place yaw rotation. On a real Modal AI drone, OPAL variant's travel distance is up to 25% lower than FALCON.

Conclusion: Deliberate yaw rotation effectively trades total exploration time for reduced travel distance and computational simplicity. Frontier-selection radius adjustment enables customizable tradeoffs between time and distance.

Abstract: Autonomous exploration is critical for robot mapping unknown environments. Desirable characteristics of exploration algorithms include compute efficiency and small traversed distance during the exploration process. Motivated by these, we present Omnidirectional Path-efficient Aerial 3D expLoration (OPAL), an exploration framework centered on deliberate 360-degree yaw rotation at ambiguous branch points rather than compute-heavy global tour planning. We devise multiple variants of OPAL to determine the frontier-selection strategy once the yaw pan is completed. One variant is model-free, while others use large language models (LLMs) or vision-language models (VLMs). We characterize the performance of these variants while varying the vicinity search radius to include frontiers in the selection process. Through simulations we find that although the time-consuming in-place yaw rotation increases total exploration time relative to more computationally complex baselines such as EDEN and FALCON, OPAL is computationally simpler and achieves shorter travel distances and higher coverage-versus-distance area under the curve. We also show that adjusting the frontier-selection search radius enables a tradeoff between travel distance and total exploration time. We verify our results on a Modal AI drone in two indoor environments by comparing OPAL against FALCON, and find that the traveled distance for a variant of OPAL to be as much as 25% lower than FALCON.

</details>


### [329] [EXPO-FT: Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning Finetuning for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25477)
*Perry Dong,Kuo-Han Hung,Tian Gao,Dorsa Sadigh,Chelsea Finn*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EXPO-FT is a system for sample-efficient RL fine-tuning of pretrained VLA policies that achieves perfect task performance (30/30 successes) across challenging manipulation tasks within an average of 19.1 minutes of online robot data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models show strong generalization but lack reliability for real-world deployment; prior RL fine-tuning approaches either train from scratch without leveraging pretrained priors or fine-tune without sufficient sample efficiency and success rates.

Method: EXPO-FT is a system for stable, sample-efficient reinforcement learning fine-tuning of pretrained Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies.

Result: Achieves perfect 30/30 success rate across all evaluated tasks (routing string lights, striking a pool ball, inserting a flower into a bottle) within an average of 19.1 minutes of online robot data, outperforming prior RL-from-scratch and VLA finetuning approaches.

Conclusion: RL fine-tuning of pretrained VLA models can achieve both high reliability and sample efficiency for real-world robotic manipulation deployment; open-source codebase released to facilitate broader adoption.

Abstract: The ability to efficiently and reliably learn new tasks has been a foundational challenge in robotics. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated strong generalization across diverse manipulation tasks, yet pretrained policies consistently fall short of the reliability required for real-world deployment. Reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning offers a promising path to bridge this gap, but existing approaches either train from scratch without fully leveraging pretrained priors, or fine-tune VLAs without achieving the sample efficiency and success rates that practical deployment demands. We present EXPO-FT, a system for stable, sample-efficient RL finetuning of pretrained VLA policies that closes this gap. Our system solves a suite of challenging manipulation tasks, including routing string lights and inserting the plug to light it up, striking a pool ball into a pocket, and inserting a flower into a wine bottle, each requiring combinations of high precision, dynamic actions, and robustness to varied initial states. Our system achieves perfect task performance (30/30 successes) across all evaluated tasks within an average of 19.1 minutes of online robot data, outperforming both prior RL-from-scratch and VLA finetuning approaches. We release an open-source codebase with the aim of facilitating broader adoption of RL finetuning of VLA models in robotics.

</details>


### [330] [RepSAM: Bridging Foundation Models to Robotic Vision via Representation-Guided Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25495)
*Wenhui Chu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RepSAM adapts foundation models to robotic vision via CKA-guided PEFT, achieving 97.9% of full fine-tuning performance with 158x fewer parameters and 96x less training time, and improving robotic manipulation success by 12%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic perception in unstructured environments is challenging; foundation models like SAM show performance degradation due to non-uniform representation shifts across transformer layers (shallow layers have large domain gaps CKA<0.5, while deep layers transfer well CKA>0.7).

Method: RepSAM proposes a CKA-guided rank allocation strategy for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, combined with a multi-modal fusion module to handle challenging robotic scenarios like transparent objects and cluttered scenes.

Result: Achieves 89.0% mIoU (97.9% of full FT's 90.9%) with 4.0M parameters (158x reduction from 632M), trains in 4 hours on one A100 (96x reduction from 384 GPU-hours), outperforms DoRA by 7.9% mIoU, and yields 12.0% absolute improvement in robotic manipulation success over LoRA baseline.

Conclusion: Foundation models can be efficiently adapted to robotic vision by targeting shallow layers with higher rank allocation, achieving near-full fine-tuning performance with drastically reduced computational and parameter costs.

Abstract: Robotic perception in unstructured environments remains challenging despite the zero-shot capabilities of foundation models such as SAM. This work attributes performance degradation to non-uniform representation shifts across transformer layers: shallow layers exhibit substantial domain gaps (CKA < 0.5), whereas deep layers transfer effectively (CKA > 0.7). Based on this observation, we propose RepSAM, a representation-guided parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) framework for adapting foundation models to robotic vision. RepSAM employs a theoretically grounded CKA-guided rank allocation strategy combined with a multi-modal fusion module for robust handling of challenging robotic scenarios, including transparent objects and cluttered scenes. Experimental evaluation across six benchmarks and robotic manipulation tasks demonstrates that RepSAM achieves 97.9% of full fine-tuning performance (89.0% vs. 90.9% mIoU) while reducing trainable parameters by 158x (from 632M to 4.0M). RepSAM outperforms DoRA by 7.9% mIoU with just 4 hours of training on a single A100 GPU (a 96x reduction from full fine-tuning, which takes 384 GPU-hours). These improvements are statistically significant (p < 0.01) and translate to a 12.0% absolute improvement in robotic manipulation success rates over the LoRA (RGB) baseline.

</details>


### [331] [Action-Prior Denoising for Smooth Real-Time Chunking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25537)
*Dongyang Liu,Zhaowen Zheng,Yu Sun,Longxu Zhang,Yixuan Liu,Hao Wan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Soft RTC proposes a training-time real-time chunking method that uses action-prior denoising with partially denoised states instead of pure noise, achieving comparable solve rates to hard RTC while reducing action jerk and delta under high delay.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training-time RTC uses a binary prefix mask that treats non-prefix tokens as fully unconstrained, under-modeling asynchronous execution where later overlap actions should stay close to the previous plan while remaining editable.

Method: Soft RTC constructs corrupted overlap tokens from partially denoised states (instead of pure noise) and injects the aligned previous chunk as the same prior during inference via a lightweight token-wise blending rule.

Result: On 12 Kinetix levels, soft window achieves 0.809 solve rate (vs 0.815 hard RTC). Medium window reduces high-delay action delta by 9.1% and jerk by 9.6%. Real-robot study shows lowest commanded-action finite-difference metrics.

Conclusion: Soft RTC nearly matches hard training-time RTC in solve rate while improving smoothness under high delay, with near-naive runtime and benefits validated on real robots.

Abstract: Real-time chunking (RTC) lets chunked action policies operate under inference delay by conditioning a newly generated action chunk on actions already committed by the previous chunk. Training-time RTC simulates this delay during learning and avoids expensive guidance at deployment, but its binary prefix mask treats all non-prefix tokens as fully unconstrained. This under-models asynchronous execution: early overlap actions are fixed, while later overlap actions remain editable but should still stay close to the previous plan. We propose Soft RTC, a training-time RTC generalization based on action-prior denoising. Soft RTC constructs corrupted overlap tokens from partially denoised states instead of pure noise and injects the aligned previous chunk as the same prior during inference through a lightweight token-wise blending rule. On the 12 released large Kinetix levels, a short soft window nearly matches hard training-time RTC in overall solve rate (0.809 vs. 0.815), while a medium window reduces high-delay action delta and jerk by 9.1% and 9.6% relative to hard RTC. Both variants keep near-naive runtime, unlike inference-time RTC baselines. A small preliminary real-robot sorting study provides additional evidence that training-time RTC can improve completion and that Soft RTC gives the lowest commanded-action finite-difference metrics among the tested policies.

</details>


### [332] [Safety-Critical Whole-Body Control for Humanoid Robots via Input-to-State Safe Control Barrier Functions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25546)
*Kwanwoo Lee,Sanghyuk Park,Gyeongjae Park,Myeong-Ju Kim,Jaeheung Park*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a hierarchical safety-critical whole-body control framework for humanoid robots using input-to-state safe control barrier functions (ISSf-CBFs) to maintain kinematic safety guarantees under unknown disturbances, validated through simulation and real-robot experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety-critical control approaches for humanoid robots have limited robustness because kinematic safety guarantees degrade under unknown disturbances such as model uncertainties, tracking errors, and external perturbations.

Method: A three-layer hierarchical framework: (1) Kinematic-level Whole-Body Controller (KinWBC) generates nominal joint references; (2) ISSf-CBF safety filter minimally modifies references to satisfy safety constraints under bounded disturbances; (3) Dynamic-level Whole-Body Controller (DynWBC) tracks filtered references while enforcing dynamic feasibility and contact stability.

Result: Simulation and real-robot experiments show improved safety margins under model mismatch, and reliable real-time enforcement of multiple safety constraints during locomotion, teleoperation, and single-leg balancing with hand control.

Conclusion: The ISSf-CBF-based hierarchical framework successfully transfers kinematic safety guarantees to full-order humanoid dynamics under unknown disturbances, enhancing real-world safety.

Abstract: Safety-critical control is essential for humanoid robots operating in complex human-centered environments, where physical safety constraints such as joint limits, self-collision avoidance, obstacle avoidance, and workspace boundaries must be satisfied during real-robot operation. However, existing approaches remain limited because kinematic safety guarantees can be degraded in the presence of unknown disturbances, such as model uncertainties, trajectory-tracking errors, and external perturbations. This paper presents a hierarchical safety-critical whole-body control framework for humanoid robots based on input-to-state safe control barrier functions (ISSf-CBFs). The proposed architecture integrates a kinematic-level whole-body controller (KinWBC), an ISSf-CBF safety filter, and a dynamic-level whole-body controller (DynWBC). KinWBC generates nominal joint-motion references from prioritized tasks; the ISSf-CBF filter minimally modifies these references to satisfy kinematic safety constraints under bounded disturbances; and DynWBC tracks the filtered references while enforcing full-body dynamic feasibility and contact stability. Safety constraints are imposed on a whole-body kinematic model, and the ISSf-CBF parameters are conservatively tuned so that the resulting kinematic safety guarantees can be transferred to full-order humanoid dynamics under unknown disturbances. Simulation and real-robot experiments demonstrate that the proposed framework improves safety margins under model mismatch and reliably enforces multiple safety constraints in real time during locomotion, teleoperation, and single-leg balancing with hand control. Project website: https://kwlee365.github.io/SafeWBC-Website/

</details>


### [333] [TapSampling: Inference-Time Sampling with a Task-Progress-Understanding Verifier for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25547)
*Sizhe Zhao,Shengping Zhang,Shuo Yang,Weiyu Zhao,Shuigen Wang,Xiangyang Ji*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TapSampling is a plug-and-play, policy-agnostic inference-time sampling framework that improves embodied control policies by using an Action-VAE for latent action sampling and a task-progress verifier for action selection, without requiring additional policy finetuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied control research scales training data and model size, but the author identifies inference-time strategy as an underexplored alternative axis. Non-deterministic generative models in embodied control suffer from limited performance due to their single-shot inference paradigm.

Method: (1) Action-VAE: maps policy-generated initial actions into a compressed low-dimensional latent posterior distribution, enabling sampling of multiple latent codes decoded into candidate actions. (2) Action verification: formulated as task-progress outcome prediction, using sequential structure from robotic datasets to train a semantically grounded verifier for interpretable action selection. The framework is policy-agnostic.

Result: Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments demonstrate substantial improvements across multiple generalist policies without further policy finetuning.

Conclusion: Inference-time sampling (TapSampling) is an effective alternative to scaling training data/model size for embodied control. The plug-and-play nature and policy-agnostic design make it widely applicable.

Abstract: Existing embodied control research demonstrates remarkable performance improvements by scaling training data and model size. We instead explore inference-time strategy as an alternative axis. Non-deterministic generative models, such as diffusion and autoregressive models, have been widely adopted in the field of embodied control. However, the single-shot inference paradigm limits their performance. In this paper, we propose \textbf{TapSampling}, a plug-and-play framework for inference-time sampling. First, we introduce an Action-VAE that represents actions in a low-dimensional latent space by mapping policy-generated initial actions into a compressed posterior distribution, from which any number of latent samples can be drawn and decoded into candidate actions that approximate the true action distribution. Second, we formulate action verification as task-progress outcome prediction, using the intrinsic sequential structure of robotic datasets to train a semantically grounded verifier for interpretable action selection. Furthermore, TapSampling is a policy-agnostic framework. Extensive experiments in both simulated and real-world environments demonstrate that our method substantially improves multiple generalist policies without further policy finetuning. Code and models are available at the project page.

</details>


### [334] [Acting on the Unseen: Communication-Free Collaborative Filtering for Decentralized Multi-Robot Task Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25584)
*Alexander Apartsin,Yigal Meshulam,Yehudit Aperstein*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces Zero-Knowledge MRTA (ZK-MRTA), where robots with no prior knowledge, no communication, and only partial noisy observations of teammates' outcomes must allocate tasks. The authors propose SwarmCF, an online low-rank collaborative filtering method that enables robots to perform well on unseen tasks, achieving Θ(d) per-robot sample complexity vs. Θ(n) for structure-free learners, and demonstrating strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the overlooked regime of multi-robot task allocation where robots have no prior knowledge (no task models, no latent rank), no communication, and only partial noisy views of public teammate outcomes, which is common in practice but lacks theoretical foundation.

Method: SwarmCF—an online low-rank collaborative filtering algorithm operating over broadcasted outcomes. Robots exploit hidden low-rank structure governing robot-task suitability, enabling inference on unseen (robot, task) pairs without communication or prior knowledge.

Result: Proves a categorical advantage over structure-free learners (prior-mean error floor vs. recoverable skill). Establishes per-robot sample complexity of Θ(d) vs. Θ(n). Demonstrates anytime separation under task scarcity, exact decentralized recovery conditions, and empirical validation showing ~80% of centralized full-communication ceiling performance.

Conclusion: Zero-knowledge MRTA is feasible via low-rank structure exploitation, with SwarmCF achieving strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance comparable to centralized systems, even under extreme constraints like capacity-1 contention.

Abstract: Multi-robot task allocation usually assumes some combination of communication, known task models, or a coordinator. We study the opposite extreme, a regime common in practice but overlooked in theory, which we name Zero-Knowledge MRTA (ZK-MRTA): a robot team with no prior knowledge (no task models, not even the latent rank), no communication (no messages, no parameter sharing, no coordinator), and only a partial and privately-noisy view of a public stream of teammates' outcomes. A hidden low-rank structure governs which robot suits which task, and there are far more tasks than rounds, so most (robot, task) pairs are never attempted. Yet each robot can act well on tasks it never attempted, and onboard new tasks, by running online low-rank collaborative filtering over the broadcast (SwarmCF). The advantage over any structure-free learner is categorical, not a constant factor: a structure-free learner is provably at the prior-mean error floor on unseen pairs. We prove a matching per-robot sample complexity (Θ(d) versus Θ(n), in the rank d and the task count n), an anytime (cumulative-reward) separation under task scarcity, and a deterministic condition under which decentralized recovery from the masked broadcast is exact (validated empirically). Experiments quantify the value of the broadcast, a positive scaling law (per-robot unseen-pair skill rises with team size), and the strongest masking-robustness and anytime profile among low-rank methods, recovering most (about 80% on earned skill) of a centralized full-communication ceiling, and holding under capacity-1 contention and in a robotics-grounded sensing instance.

</details>


### [335] [G-DRAGON: Geospatial Reasoning and Dynamic Planning for Retrieval-Augmented Outdoor Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25646)
*Dongzhihan Wang,Yi Du,Jianan Sun,Yuan Xue,Yingchen Zhang,Bing Xiao,Chen Wang,Liang Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: G-DRAGON is a retrieval-augmented framework for outdoor robot navigation that combines OSM-based global planning with local SLAM and semantic exploration, enabling long-range missions (up to 500m) with fine-grained "last-mile" target search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLN methods lack geospatial grounding for long-range outdoor missions, and OSM-based LLM approaches suffer from hallucinations and cannot perform fine-grained "last-mile" exploration based on human instructions.

Method: The framework uses generative retrieval with lightweight LLM to map natural-language commands to local OSM entities, a high-level planning module bridging topological routes with SLAM, and frontier-based exploration with open-set semantic voxel mapping for last-mile target localization.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in simulation, and successfully completes real-world person-search missions on a UGV with trajectories up to 500m in unseen urban environments.

Conclusion: G-DRAGON effectively integrates global OSM-based route planning with local semantic exploration, addressing both long-range navigation and fine-grained target search for autonomous outdoor robots.

Abstract: Autonomous ground robots operating in large-scale outdoor environments require both robust long-range navigation and fine-grained ''last-mile'' exploration. Current advances in visual-language navigation (VLN) work well at short-range tasks, lacking geospatial grounding for long-distance missions. Some OpenStreetMap (OSM)-based methods relying on cloud-based Large Language Models (LLMs) are prone to factual hallucination and cannot conduct ''last-mile'' exploration based on human instruction. To address these challenges, we present G-DRAGON, a retrieval-augmented framework for outdoor, open-world navigation. This framework maps natural-language commands to versioned, local OSM entities via generative retrieval based on lightweight LLM, yielding accurate coordinates for global route planning. A high-level planning module bridges global topological routes with the SLAM system, projecting geospatial waypoints into the robot's navigable frame. For the ''last mile," the framework transitions to frontier-based exploration and open-set semantic voxel mapping to localize open-vocabulary targets. Experimental results in simulation demonstrate our framework outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. Furthermore, we validate the system in unseen real-world urban environments on an Unmanned Ground Vehicle (UGV), successfully completing person-search missions with trajectories of up to 500m.

</details>


### [336] [Compliant Non-Prehensile Pushing Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25672)
*Francesco Cufino,Mario Selvaggio,Fabio Amadio,Fabio Ruggiero*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper extends a pushing model with impedance control and MPC for compliant, non-prehensile pushing, adding an energy tank passivity filter to prevent unsafe force buildup from external interactions. Validated in simulation and on two robotic systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe robotic pushing operations in human-populated environments by ensuring passive compliance during external physical interactions.

Method: Extended a state-of-the-art pushing model; developed an MPC framework for optimal set-point modulation; integrated an energy tank passivity filter to prevent uncontrolled energy buildup.

Result: Demonstrated passive compliance during HRI, assessed tracking performance and robustness to object parameter variations in simulation and on two real robotic systems.

Conclusion: The proposed approach achieves safe, compliant non-prehensile pushing with guaranteed passivity and robustness.

Abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of performing non-prehensile pushing operations with a compliant robotic manipulation system. To ensure safe operations in human-populated environments, robots must comply with external physical interactions and exhibit passive behavior. To achieve this, we extend a state-of-the-art pushing model to integrate it with impedance-controlled robots. We develop a model predictive control framework built upon this model that enables compliant pushing through optimal modulation of the robot's position/velocity set-point, jointly realizing the required pushing force and contact point adaptation to obtain desired object motion. However, external interactions may induce tracking errors, causing a consequent potentially indefinite increase of the pushing force. To prevent this, we integrate an energy tank passivity filter that further modulates the robot velocity set-point to guarantee passivity and avoid uncontrolled energy buildup. The proposed method has been rigorously tested in simulation and validated through experiments on two different robotic systems, demonstrating passive compliance during human-robot interactions and assessing trajectory tracking performance and robustness to variations in the object's physical parameters.

</details>


### [337] [HumanFlow -- Diffusion-Driven MAV Navigation Among Humans via Tightly-Coupled Motion Tracking, Forecasting, and Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25685)
*Simon Schaefer,Joshua Näf,Stefan Leutenegger*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HumanFlow is a latent diffusion model that unifies human motion tracking and forecasting conditioned on 3D scene context, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in tracking accuracy while enabling efficient, collision-free robot navigation under heavy occlusions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing human motion perception methods fail to produce plausible estimates consistent with 3D scenes under heavy occlusions or partial visibility, limiting robot safety and efficiency.

Method: A latent diffusion model unifying human motion tracking and forecasting, conditioned on 3D scene context, with a flow-matching-based approximate MPC policy for control.

Result: HumanFlow produces smooth and accurate predictions under challenging occlusions, outperforms state-of-the-art tracking methods with greater efficiency, and enables collision-free MAV social navigation even under partial human observability.

Conclusion: Conditioning human motion models on 3D scene context significantly improves tracking accuracy and navigation safety, with latent representations effectively coupling perception and control.

Abstract: Robust and accurate perception of humans in their 3D scene context is essential for integrating robots into everyday environments. Existing approaches, however, often fail to predict plausible and accurate human motion estimates that are consistent with the surrounding scene, especially in the presence of heavy occlusions or partial visibility. This can limit both safety and efficiency for robotic operations. We introduce HumanFlow, a latent diffusion model that unifies human motion tracking and forecasting, conditioned on the 3D scene context. We show that our human motion model produces smooth and accurate predictions under challenging conditions, including heavy occlusions, and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in tracking accuracy while being significantly more efficient. Furthermore, we show how HumanFlow's latent space can be tightly coupled with control by conditioning a flow-matching-based, approximate MPC policy on these representations. We validate our policy in simulation with real human trajectories for MAV social navigation, demonstrating superior navigation performance and remaining collision-free, even under partial observability of the human.

</details>


### [338] [Implicit Null-space Manifold Generation for Redundant Robotic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25770)
*Taiki Ishigaki,Teresa Vidal-Calleja,Ko Ayusawa,Eiichi Yoshida*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes learning an implicit scalar field representation of solution manifolds for redundant robotic systems, using Jacobian-guided sampling to capture the geometry of the configuration space where task constraints are satisfied.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing redundancy resolution methods (e.g., Jacobian-based techniques) can compute individual solutions or trajectories but fail to capture and retain the geometric structure of the full solution manifold, limiting holistic understanding of the configuration space.

Method: The authors construct an implicit scalar field over the configuration space whose zero-level set represents the solution manifold. They generate neighborhood samples using a Jacobian-guided exploration strategy to efficiently capture both local and global manifold structure, then learn a continuous distance field encoding proximity to the solution manifold.

Result: Experiments on a planar 3-link robot and a 7-DOF Franka manipulator demonstrate the effectiveness of the learned representation. The framework also enables consistent modeling of solution spaces across continuously varying task families.

Conclusion: Moving from local solution computation to a representation-centric approach that preserves manifold geometry enables more holistic understanding and utilization of redundancy in robotic systems, with potential benefits for task families and continuous variation scenarios.

Abstract: Robotic systems with redundant degrees of freedom can achieve the same task outcome using multiple configurations, resulting in solution sets that form manifolds in the configuration space. Existing approaches typically exploit such redundancy locally through Jacobian-based techniques to compute individual solutions or trajectories. While effective for solution computation, these methods do not retain a representation of the geometry of the solution set itself. In this work, we adopt a representation-centric approach to estimate the geometric structure of the solution space. We consider solution manifolds induced by general task-defining maps and construct an implicit scalar field over the configuration space, whose zero-level set corresponds to the solution manifold. To this end, we generate samples in the neighborhood of the solution manifold using a Jacobian-guided exploration strategy, which efficiently captures its local and global structure. The resulting implicit representation is defined over the configuration space and naturally induces a continuous, distance field that encodes proximity to the solution manifold. Experiments on a planar three-link robot and a seven-degree-of-freedom Franka manipulator demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed representation. Furthermore, the framework enables consistent modeling of solution spaces across families of tasks with continuous variation.

</details>


### [339] [ParkourFormer: Integrating Predictive Supervision and Sequence Modeling into Parkour Locomotion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25782)
*Yanheng Mai,Wenhao Xu,Zirui Huang,Yifei Fu,Shengwei Dong,Xinjue Wang,Kailun Huang,Yanzhe Xie,Renjing Xu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ParkourFormer uses Transformer-based sequence modeling with explicit future-state prediction to achieve 93.85% success rate on humanoid parkour tasks, outperforming MLP and Transformer baselines by up to 42.73% across diverse terrains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL policies for humanoid parkour are reactive and map observations directly to actions without explicitly modeling future body states, which becomes critical for agile locomotion tasks requiring anticipation of upcoming contact transitions and body dynamics.

Method: A Transformer-based framework where the current robot state queries historical sensorimotor trajectories via cross-attention, a lightweight head forecasts short-horizon future proprioceptive states using supervised learning, and predicted future states are fused with temporal features to generate actions.

Result: 93.85% average traversal success rate on highly challenging terrains; up to 42.73% improvement over MLP, MoE-based MLP, and vanilla Transformer baselines; single unified policy across all terrain types.

Conclusion: Explicit future-state modeling significantly improves robustness and generalization for agile whole-body locomotion in humanoid robots.

Abstract: Humanoid parkour requires locomotion policies to coordinate whole-body dynamics across rapidly changing terrains such as stairs, gaps, slopes, and obstacles. Existing reinforcement learning policies are largely reactive, mapping observations directly to actions without explicitly modeling future body states. Such modeling becomes critical in agile locomotion tasks where successful motion execution depends strongly on anticipating upcoming contact transitions and body dynamics.We present ParkourFormer, a Transformer-based sequence modeling framework that reformulates humanoid locomotion as a future-conditioned decision-making problem. The current robot state queries historical sensorimotor trajectories through cross-attention, while a lightweight prediction head forecasts short-horizon future proprioceptive states. The predicted future states, trained with supervised signals, are fused with temporal features to generate actions, enabling the policy to jointly reason over motion history and anticipated future dynamics. We evaluate ParkourFormer on a diverse multi-terrain humanoid parkour benchmark including stairs, gaps, slopes, rough terrain, and obstacle traversal. Experiments in simulation and on a real humanoid robot show that ParkourFormer achieves a 93.85% average traversal success rate on highly challenging terrains, with improvements of up to 42.73% over strong MLP, MoE-based MLP, and vanilla Transformer baselines, while maintaining a single unified policy across all terrain types. These results demonstrate that explicit future-state modeling significantly improves robustness and generalization for agile whole-body locomotion.

</details>


### [340] [HoLoArm: Deformable Arms for Collision-Tolerant Quadrotor Flight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25790)
*Quang Ngoc Pham,Jonas Eschmann,Yang Zhou,Alejandro Ojeda Olarte,Giuseppe Loianno,Van Anh Ho*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HoLoArm is a quadrotor with dragonfly-inspired compliant arms and RL-based control that enables rapid collision recovery (0.3-0.6s), survives impacts up to 7.6 m/s, and carries 540g payloads while maintaining stable flight.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drones used in human-centric applications need to survive collisions and recover quickly to minimize risks to humans and the environment.

Method: A quadrotor with compliant arms inspired by dragonfly wing nodus structures, combined with a Reinforcement Learning (RL) control policy for recovery and hovering.

Result: Passive deformation in any direction; recovery within 0.3-0.6s; survives collisions up to 7.6 m/s; carries 540g payload with stable flight.

Conclusion: This work advances soft aerial robot design for cluttered and human-shared environments, laying groundwork for future fully soft drones integrating compliant structures with intelligent control.

Abstract: The increasing use of drones in human-centric applications highlights the need for designs that can survive collisions and recover rapidly, minimizing risks to both humans and the environment. We present HoLoArm, a quadrotor with compliant arms inspired by the nodus structure of dragonfly wings. This design provides natural flexibility and resilience while preserving flight stability, which is further reinforced by the integration of a Reinforcement Learning (RL) control policy that enhances both recovery and hovering performance. Experimental results demonstrate that HoLoArm can passively deform in any direction, including axial one, and recover within 0.3-0.6 s depending on the direction and level of the impact. The drone can survive collisions at speeds up to 7.6 m/s and carry a 540 g payload while maintaining stable flight. This work contributes to the morphological design of soft aerial robots with high agility and reliable safety, enabling operation in cluttered and human shared environments, and lays the groundwork for future fully soft drones that integrate compliant structures with intelligent control.

</details>


### [341] [Extending Embodied Question Answering from Perception to Decision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25813)
*Xicheng Gong,Qiwei Li,Peiran Xu,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: EQA-Decision is a large-scale embodied QA dataset with 4M+ QA pairs across four reasoning dimensions, plus a RoboDecision baseline model for unified evaluation of perception, reasoning, and decision-making in embodied environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EQA datasets are fragmented, each covering only limited reasoning skills (e.g., spatial or procedural), lacking a unified large-scale framework for comprehensive embodied reasoning evaluation.

Method: Constructed a large-scale dataset (4M+ QA pairs) spanning four dimensions: static scene construction, spatial understanding, task dynamics reasoning, and instant decision. Developed RoboDecision as a unified baseline model aligned with this benchmark.

Result: EQA-Decision effectively benchmarks and enhances VLM capabilities in spatial and interaction reasoning.

Conclusion: Provides a solid foundation for advancing embodied intelligence research by offering a comprehensive evaluation framework.

Abstract: Embodied Question Answering (EQA) connects perception, reasoning, and interaction within embodied environments. However, existing datasets and benchmarks remain fragmented, each focusing on a limited subset of reasoning skills such as spatial understanding or procedural reasoning, without offering a unified large-scale framework for comprehensive evaluation. We present EQA-Decision, a large-scale embodied QA dataset that systematically covers four complementary dimensions of embodied reasoning: static scene construction, spatial understanding, task dynamics reasoning, and instant decision. The dataset contains over four million question-answer pairs with hierarchical annotations across diverse embodied scenarios. In addition, we develop RoboDecision, a strong baseline model aligned with the EQA-Decision Benchmark, providing a unified framework that jointly evaluates perception, reasoning, and action-level decision-making in embodied environments. Results demonstrate that EQA-Decision effectively benchmarks and enhances VLM capabilities in spatial and interaction reasoning, providing a solid foundation for advancing embodied intelligence research.

</details>


### [342] [OASIS: Observation-Action Space Alignment via SE(3) Trajectory Prediction for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25829)
*Xinzhe Chen,Sihua Ren,Liqi Huang,Haowen Sun,Mingyang Li,Xingyu Chen,Zeyang Liu,Xuguang Lan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: OASIS is a visuomotor policy that aligns intermediate representations with action space via SE(3) end-effector trajectory prediction, outperforming VLA and WAM baselines in robotic manipulation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLA models and WAMs use auxiliary spatial features or future visual prediction, but these representations remain in observation space and fail to share the rigid-body geometry of the action space, forcing action decoders to implicitly recover this geometry.

Method: OASIS couples a 3D-aware feature encoder (fusing vision-language and metric-depth features) with an SE(3) trajectory predictor that produces camera-frame end-effector trajectories. The action decoder then generates action chunks conditioned on pose-supervised hidden states.

Result: OASIS outperforms VLA and WAM baselines in success rate and out-of-distribution generalization in both simulation and real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Aligning intermediate representations with the action space via explicit SE(3) trajectory prediction improves robotic manipulation performance and generalization.

Abstract: Recent vision-language-action (VLA) models and world action models (WAMs) advance robotic manipulation by enriching intermediate representations with auxiliary spatial features or future visual-state prediction. However, these representations largely remain within the observation space and do not share the rigid-body geometry of the action space, forcing the action decoder to implicitly recover this geometry. We propose OASIS, a visuomotor policy that aligns the intermediate representation with the action space via $SE(3)$ end-effector trajectory prediction. OASIS couples a 3D-aware feature encoder that fuses vision-language and metric-depth features with an $SE(3)$ trajectory predictor that produces a camera-frame end-effector trajectory. Conditioned on the predictor's pose-supervised hidden states, the action decoder generates action chunks consistent with rigid-body motion. Across simulation and real-world experiments, OASIS outperforms VLA and WAM baselines in success rate and out-of-distribution generalization. Our project page is available at https://npuhandsome.github.io/OASIS_web.

</details>


### [343] [When Search Becomes Memory: Turning Robot Design Trials into Transferable Skills](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25832)
*Yunfei Wang,Xiaohao Xu,Yang Li,Xiaonan Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Auto-Robotist is a self-evolving LLM agent that distills robot morphology search traces into an explicit natural-language skill library, enabling reusable and auditable design knowledge, and outperforming GA across 7 EvoGym tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM-guided evolutionary robot design loops are memoryless—simulation results are used for the next population but never preserved as reusable design knowledge. The authors aim to convert expensive physical evaluations into inspectable, reusable design principles.

Method: Auto-Robotist uses a self-evolving LLM agent that distills morphology-search traces into an explicit natural-language skill library. Each skill stores a structural archetype, evidence-grounded positive/negative rules, and evaluated designs. During search, the agent retrieves skills to condition LLM edits of elite bodies while retaining a GA mutation path. After evaluation, the library is updated through Add, Diagnose, and Merge operations.

Result: Across seven EvoGym tasks (locomotion, traversal, object interaction), Auto-Robotist improves cold-start 5x5 search and transfers learned skills to 10x10 design spaces, where reference-conditioned transfer outperforms GA on every task.

Conclusion: LLM agents can convert expensive physical evaluations into reusable, auditable design principles, as demonstrated by Auto-Robotist's superior performance and transferability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as proposal generators for evolutionary robot design, yet most loops remain memoryless: simulator results shape the next population but are not preserved as reusable design knowledge. We present Auto-Robotist, a self-evolving LLM agent that distills morphology-search traces into an explicit natural-language skill library. Each skill stores a structural archetype, evidence-grounded positive and negative rules, and the evaluated designs that support them, making design memory inspectable rather than implicit in a population. During search, the agent retrieves skills to condition LLM edits of elite bodies while retaining a Genetic Algorithm (GA) mutation path for exploration; after evaluation, it updates the library through Add, Diagnose, and Merge. Across seven EvoGym tasks spanning locomotion, traversal, and object interaction, Auto-Robotist improves cold-start 5x5 search and transfers learned skills to 10x10 design spaces, where reference-conditioned transfer outperforms GA on every task. These results suggest that LLM agents can convert expensive physical evaluations into reusable, auditable design principles. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

</details>


### [344] [RePlan-Bot: Multi-Level Replanning for Embodied Instruction Following](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25851)
*Xicheng Gong,Guozheng Sun,Peiran Xu,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RePlan-Bot is an EIF agent that achieves state-of-the-art performance on ALFRED through continuous multi-level replanning, integrating an LLM auditor, commonsense-guided search, and a ViT-based action corrector.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EIF methods fail in long-horizon planning and handling irreversible state changes, resulting in low task success rates.

Method: RePlan-Bot integrates: (1) a high-level LLM-based auditor for dynamic sub-goal adjustments via environmental feedback, (2) a commonsense-guided search mechanism using a multi-layered instance map for object localization, and (3) a lightweight ViT-based corrector to preemptively fix risky low-level actions.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ALFRED benchmark in both seen and unseen environments.

Conclusion: RePlan-Bot demonstrates superior adaptability and reliability for embodied instruction following through continuous multi-level replanning.

Abstract: Embodied instruction following (EIF) requires agents to understand and execute complex natural language commands within interactive 3D environments. Despite recent advances, existing methods often fail in long-horizon planning and handling irreversible state changes, resulting in low task success rates. To address these challenges, we introduce RePlan-Bot, a novel EIF agent that performs multi-level, continuous replanning throughout task execution. RePlan-Bot integrates a high-level LLM-based auditor for dynamic sub-goal adjustments guided by environmental feedback, a commonsense-guided search mechanism based on a multi-layered instance map for precise and structured object localization, and a lightweight ViT-based corrector to preemptively fix risky low-level actions. Evaluated on the ALFRED benchmark, RePlan-Bot achieves state-of-the-art performance in both seen and unseen environments, demonstrating superior adaptability and reliability.

</details>


### [345] [AnyScene: Towards Highly Controllable Driving Scene Generation at Anywhere and Beyond](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26113)
*Haiming Zhang,Junfei Zhou,Feng Jiang,Jingzhong Li,Zhenglong Guo,Penglin Dai,Jifeng Dai,Yan Xie,Benjin Zhu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: AnyScene is a unified occupancy-centric framework for controllable autonomous driving scene generation that produces semantic occupancy sequences from BEV layouts via a Spatial-Temporal Occupancy Diffusion Transformer and synthesizes temporally consistent multi-view videos through a Geometry-Grounded View Expansion module, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both occupancy and video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing occupancy-guided methods suffer from shallow conditioning and reference-frame-dependent video synthesis, limiting fine-grained controllability from arbitrary BEV layouts and scalability for long-tail safety-critical scenario simulation in autonomous driving.

Method: AnyScene consists of two key components: (1) a Spatial-Temporal Occupancy Diffusion Transformer that autoregressively tokenizes BEV and occupancy features to generate semantic occupancy sequences from BEV layouts with long-horizon capability, and (2) a Geometry-Grounded View Expansion module that uses occupancy as canonical spatial representation to generate reference-free, temporally consistent multi-view videos supporting flexible camera configurations.

Result: AnyScene achieves state-of-the-art performance in both occupancy and video generation, demonstrates strong generalization to unseen and customized layouts, and provides measurable benefits for downstream tasks like sparse-view 3D reconstruction.

Conclusion: The proposed occupancy-centric framework enables precise controllability from cross-dataset and user-defined BEV inputs, supports long-horizon generation, and offers flexible camera configurations at inference time, making it a scalable solution for controllable autonomous driving simulation.

Abstract: Generating high-fidelity and controllable synthetic data is critical for advancing end-to-end autonomous driving, particularly for addressing the long tail of rare safety-critical scenarios. Existing occupancy-guided methods typically rely on shallow conditioning mechanisms and reference-frame-dependent video synthesis, which limits fine-grained controllability from arbitrary BEV layouts and restricts their applicability for scalable simulation. In this paper, we propose AnyScene, a unified occupancy-centric framework for driving scene generation. AnyScene generates semantic occupancy sequences from BEV layouts through a Spatial-Temporal Occupancy Diffusion Transformer that jointly tokenizes BEV and occupancy features in an autoregressive manner. This design enables precise controllability from cross-dataset and user-defined BEV inputs while naturally supporting long-horizon generation. Building upon the generated occupancy, a Geometry-Grounded View Expansion module treats occupancy as the canonical spatial representation and synthesizes temporally consistent multi-view driving videos in a reference-free and autoregressive fashion, supporting flexible camera configurations at inference time. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnyScene achieves state-of-the-art performance in both occupancy and video generation. It exhibits strong generalization to unseen and customized layouts, and provides measurable benefits for downstream tasks such as sparse-view 3D reconstruction.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [346] [On Permutation Groups of Cyclic Codes over Finite Fields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24314)
*Junjie Huang,Jicheng Ma,Chang-An Zhao*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper determines permutation groups of cyclic codes over finite fields with long lengths (hp, r^m p^n, pq) and special generator polynomials by relating them to prime-length codes via matrix representations. It is the first work to address permutation groups for codes with generator polynomials dividing x^{pq}-1 but not x^p-1 or x^q-1.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Permutation groups of cyclic codes are important for weight distribution, decoding, and other applications. However, determining these groups for codes with long lengths and non-prime-length generator polynomials remains challenging, motivating the authors to develop new techniques.

Method: Two distinct matrix representations are used to relate cyclic codes of very long lengths (hp, r^m p^n, pq) to those of prime lengths, enabling analysis of their permutation groups. For length pq, generator polynomials like Q_{pq}(x) (the pq-th cyclotomic polynomial) are specifically studied.

Result: The permutation groups are determined for cyclic codes over F_{r^α} with lengths hp, r^m p^n, and pq, with special generator polynomials. For length pq, results are provided for codes generated by Q_{pq}(x) and other polynomials not dividing x^p-1 or x^q-1.

Conclusion: This work presents novel results on permutation groups of cyclic codes with composite-length generator polynomials, filling a gap in existing literature, particularly for codes of length pq where the generator does not factor into smaller prime-length components.

Abstract: The permutation groups of cyclic codes are widely applicable in determining the weight distribution of codes, decoding theory and various other areas. In this paper, by employing two distinct matrix representations, we can relate cyclic codes with very long lengths and special generator polynomials to those with prime lengths. Consequently, we mainly determine the permutation groups of certain cyclic codes over $\mathbb{F}_{r^α}$ with lengths $hp$, $r^mp^n$ and $pq$ and special generator polynomials where $h$ is a positive integer and $p$, $q$ and $r$ are distinct prime numbers. For length $pq$, we manage to provide the permutation groups of cyclic codes with generator polynomials $Q_{pq}(x)$(the $pq$-th cyclotomic polynomial) or others, which seems to be the first work about permutation groups of cyclic codes with generator polynomials that are factors of $x^{pq}-1$ but not factors of $x^p-1(\text{or }x^q-1)$.

</details>


### [347] [Designs, linear codes, plateaued functions, and their interconnections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24355)
*Jong Yoon Hyun,Jieun Kwon,Jiaxin Wang,Yansheng Wu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper explores the deep connections between combinatorial designs, linear codes, and Boolean functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate and understand the interconnections between combinatorial designs, linear codes, and Boolean functions.

Method: Investigation and analysis of theoretical interconnections between these three mathematical structures.

Result: Not specified in the abstract.

Conclusion: Not specified in the abstract.

Abstract: In this paper, we mainly investigate profound interconnections between combinatorial designs, linear codes, and Boolean functions.

</details>


### [348] [SinFormer: A Tailored Transformer for Robust Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24389)
*Liu Yang,Qiang Li,Xiaoyang Ren*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes SinFormer, a deep-learning framework using multi-scale self-attention and a two-stage training strategy for robust Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification (RFFI), achieving superior accuracy and reliability in challenging RF environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional device identification methods (IP/MAC-based) are vulnerable to spoofing, and existing RFFI methods lack sufficient accuracy and robustness in challenging conditions like low SNR or channel variations.

Method: Proposes SinFormer (Signal Inception Transformer) with a specialized multi-scale self-attention mechanism to capture both coarse and fine-grained RF fingerprints, plus a two-stage training strategy for learning general features and improving robustness under adverse conditions.

Result: Validated on a real-world dataset; SinFormer consistently outperforms existing methods in both accuracy and robustness across diverse and challenging scenarios.

Conclusion: The proposed SinFormer framework significantly enhances RFFI performance, offering a more secure and reliable solution for IoT device identification even under low SNR and channel variation conditions.

Abstract: With the rapid proliferation of wireless and Internet of Things (IoT) devices, ensuring secure and reliable device identification has become a significant challenge. Traditional security techniques, such as IP or MAC address-based authentication, are susceptible to spoofing, whereas Radio Frequency Fingerprint Identification (RFFI) offers a more secure alternative by exploiting the unique hardware imperfections in devices' RF signals. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning-based framework for RFFI that enhances both accuracy and reliability in challenging RF environments. The core of our approach is the Signal Inception Transformer (SinFormer), which leverages a specialized multi-scale self-attention mechanism to effectively capture both large-scale and fine-grained fingerprints in signals, significantly improving identification accuracy. To further enhance robustness and reliability, we introduce a two-stage training strategy that enables the model to learn general signal features and maintain performance under adverse conditions, such as low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) or channel variations. The effectiveness of the proposed method is validated using a real-world dataset. Experimental results show that the SinFormer framework consistently outperforms existing methods in accuracy and robustness across diverse and challenging scenarios.

</details>


### [349] [Joint Service Placement and Resource Optimization in Hierarchical Edge-Cloud Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24641)
*Vo Phi Son,Van-Dinh Nguyen,Minh-Tuong Nguyen,Tuan-Vu Truong,Toan D. Gian,Dinh Thai Hoang,Diep N. Nguyen,Symeon Chatzinotas*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper jointly optimizes service placement, edge/cloud cooperation, task offloading, and bandwidth allocation in hierarchical edge-cloud IoT networks to minimize end-to-end latency and system cost, using iterative algorithms based on relaxation and convex approximation that converge to a local optimum.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hierarchical edge-cloud IoT networks need efficient service placement to balance workloads and ensure resource utilization for growing data-intensive devices.

Method: The non-convex MINLP problem is transformed, decomposed into sub-problems solved at different timescales, and solved using relaxation and successive convex approximation with a penalty parameter ensuring convergence to a local optimum.

Result: Extensive numerical results show the proposed algorithms outperform benchmark schemes, demonstrating the importance of joint optimization for system performance and efficiency.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing service placement and resource allocation significantly enhances processing efficiency and reduces latency in hierarchical edge-cloud IoT networks.

Abstract: Hierarchical edge-cloud computing-aided Internet of Things (IoT) networks offer low-latency and cost-efficient services to a growing number of data-intensive IoT devices. However, optimizing service placement, which involves determining the most suitable locations within a network to deploy various services, is critical to balancing workloads dynamically and ensuring efficient resource utilization. In this paper, we jointly optimize service placement, edge/cloud cooperation, task offloading, and bandwidth allocation to enhance processing efficiency and response times. The main objective is to minimize both the overall end-to-end latency and the system cost, including service deployment and operational costs. The formulated problem belongs to the class of non-convex mixed-integer nonlinear programming, where finding a feasible solution is already challenging.
