Towards a stable system, we first transform the original problem into a more tractable form and then decompose it into sub-problems which are solved at different timescales. Combining tools from relaxation and the successive convex approximation method, we develop iterative algorithms to solve these problems efficiently. With an appropriate penalty parameter, the proposed algorithms guarantee convergence to at least a local optimum. We produce extensive numerical results to demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed algorithms over benchmark schemes as well as emphasize the significance of the joint service placement and resource allocation in enhancing system performance and efficiency.

</details>


### [350] [Age of Information Optimization for Status Updates in Integrated Sensing and Communication Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24714)
*Marco Zanni,Mohamad Assaad,Touraj Soleymani*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper optimizes age of information (AoI) in an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system, proposing optimal threshold policies for single-source scenarios via MDP and Whittle index-based scheduling for multi-source scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To minimize a discounted infinite-horizon cost combining AoI with sensing and communication costs in ISAC systems, where each mode (sensing, communication, joint) is unreliable and has different operational costs.

Method: For single source: MDP formulation with two-dimensional AoI state, proving optimal stationary policy has ordered threshold structure, with state space truncation and error bounds. For multi-source: restless multi-armed bandit formulation with Whittle index policy and approximate Whittle index policy under two regimes.

Result: Optimal policy in single-source case exhibits ordered threshold structure. The approximate Whittle index policy performs comparably to the exact Whittle index policy in the indexable regime and remains effective beyond it.

Conclusion: The proposed approaches effectively optimize AoI in ISAC systems, with theoretical guarantees for threshold policies and practical scheduling policies for multi-source scenarios.

Abstract: In this paper, we study age of information (AoI) optimization for status updating in an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system. We consider a discrete-time architecture in which a base station interacts with a physical environment and a remote monitor, and at each time slot can operate in one of three modes: sensing, communication, or joint sensing and communication. Each mode is unreliable and incurs a different operational cost. The objective is to minimize a discounted infinite-horizon cost that combines the AoI at the monitor with action-dependent sensing and communication costs. For the single source scenario, we formulate the problem as a Markov decision process with a two-dimensional AoI state and prove that the optimal stationary policy admits an ordered threshold structure in the AoI state space. Since the AoI evolves over an infinite space, we truncate the state space to reduce complexity and rigorously bound the resulting error. The analysis analytically determines the truncation size needed to keep the error below a given threshold. For the multi-source scenario, we formulate the scheduling problem as a restless multi-armed bandit. We develop both a Whittle index policy and an approximate Whittle index policy for scheduling under two different regimes, one where indexability is guaranteed, and one where it is not. Numerical results illustrate the structure of the optimal policy in the single-source case and show that the proposed approximate Whittle index policy performs comparably to the Whittle index policy in the indexable regime, while remaining effective beyond it.

</details>


### [351] [Integrated Sensing, Communication, and Computing for NR-V2X: A Cross-Layer Resource Allocation Framework Using Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24972)
*Indulekha K. P.,T. G. Venkatesh*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes MAPPO-SPS, an ISCC-aware cross-layer scheduler for NR-V2X Mode 2 that jointly optimizes sensing, communication, and computation using multi-agent reinforcement learning with centralized training and decentralized execution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SB-SPS in NR-V2X Mode 2 handles distributed communication resource reservation but fails to account for sensing-resource demand, computation workload, and MEC offloading latency, necessitating a unified ISCC-aware scheduling framework.

Method: The problem is formulated as a cooperative partially observable Markov game and solved using MAPPO with centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE), integrating SB-SPS reservation, radio-resource partitioning, and overflow-driven computation-offloading decisions.

Result: MAPPO-SPS achieves a balanced tradeoff among CRLB-based sensing accuracy, packet reception ratio (PRR), effective throughput, energy consumption, and end-to-end delay.

Conclusion: The proposed ISCC-aware scheduler effectively balances multiple performance metrics in vehicular networks, demonstrating the value of joint optimization across sensing, communication, and computation domains.

Abstract: Integrated sensing, communication, and computation (ISCC) is emerging as a unified design paradigm for future vehicular networks that require joint environment perception, safety-critical information exchange, and latency-sensitive task processing. In New Radio Vehicle-to-Everything (NR-V2X) Mode 2, autonomous resource selection is performed through sensing-based semi-persistent scheduling (SB-SPS), which is effective for distributed communication resource reservation but does not explicitly consider sensing-resource demand, task-induced computation workload, and the additional latency introduced by mobile edge computing (MEC) offloading. This paper develops multi-agent proximal policy optimization-based SB-SPS (MAPPO-SPS), an ISCC-aware cross-layer scheduler that jointly adapts SB-SPS reservation, radio-resource partitioning, and overflow-driven computation-offloading decisions at control epochs. The scheduling problem is formulated as a cooperative partially observable Markov game and solved using MAPPO with centralized training and decentralized execution (CTDE). Simulation results show that MAPPO-SPS achieves a balanced tradeoff among CRLB-based sensing accuracy, packet reception ratio (PRR), effective throughput, energy consumption, and end-to-end delay.

</details>


### [352] [Design of APSK Constellations Approaching the Communication-Sensing Pareto Boundary for ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25047)
*Yujie Shao,Min Qiu,Ming-Chun Lee,Yu-Chih Huang,Jinhong Yuan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a semi-analytical APSK signaling framework for ISAC systems, designing constellations that achieve near-optimal tradeoff between communication and sensing performance, with proven constant gap to capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To bridge the gap between communication-optimal and sensing-optimal designs in ISAC systems using practical discrete APSK constellations, while maintaining analytical tractability.

Method: Establishes APSK design criteria (communication: gap to capacity linked to min Euclidean distance; sensing: symbol-energy variance). Proposes APSK families with explicit scaling laws and a parametric bridge between C&S extremes controlled by ring count and energy allocation.

Result: The proposed APSK achieves constant gap to capacity independent of SNR, and its C&S performance closely approaches the Pareto boundary of unconstrained continuous input distributions.

Conclusion: The proposed semi-analytical APSK framework effectively achieves near-Pareto-optimal C&S tradeoff with practical discrete constellations, validated by simulation.

Abstract: We propose a semi-analytical amplitude phase shift keying (APSK) signaling framework for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), focusing on i.i.d. uniform discrete input distributions for practicality and analytical tractability. First, we establish APSK design criteria in which communication performance is measured by the gap to capacity and linked to the minimum Euclidean distance, while sensing performance is characterized by the symbol-energy variance. Based on these criteria, we propose a family of APSK constellations whose key parameters follow explicit scaling laws. Then we prove that this design achieves a constant gap to capacity independent of the signal-to-noise ratio. Building upon this foundation, we further construct a parametric APSK family that bridges the communication-optimal and sensing-optimal designs, with the communication and sensing (C&S) tradeoff controlled by the number of rings and energy allocation among rings. Simulation results show that the proposed APSK achieves C&S performance very close to the Pareto boundary achieved with time-independent, circularly symmetric, and otherwise unconstrained continuous input distributions.

</details>


### [353] [Polynomial Context-Truncation Sensitivity in Autoregressive Language Models: Sequential Wyner-Ziv Bounds for KV Cache Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25085)
*Munsik Kim*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies the rate-distortion limits of online KV cache compression in autoregressive language models, formulating it as sequential Wyner-Ziv source coding. The authors find that next-token distribution sensitivity to context truncation follows a polynomial (power law) rather than exponential decay. Under this assumption, they show that suffix-only caching policies (e.g., sliding window) achieve distortion ε with window size scaling as Θ(ε^{-1/α}), establishing tight upper and lower bounds. Empirically, the polynomial law predicts degradation curves of concrete cache policies like recency-based eviction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental limits of key-value (KV) cache compression in autoregressive language models, specifically how much context can be compressed while maintaining output quality, under a rigorous information-theoretic framework.

Method: Formulates KV cache compression as sequential Wyner-Ziv source coding with next-step query as decoder side information. Empirically measures truncation sensitivity across four models (two families, 0.5-3B parameters) to establish power-law decay. Develops theoretical upper/lower bounds for suffix-only cache policies under polynomial truncation-sensitivity assumptions.

Result: Next-token distribution sensitivity decays polynomially (power law) not geometrically. For suffix-only policies, sliding window achieves distortion ε with w = O(ε^{-1/α}), and under Bayes-risk conditions, w = Ω(ε^{-1/α}) is necessary, giving Θ(ε^{-1/α}) scaling. The polynomial law accurately predicts degradation of recency-based eviction policies, which outperform random retention by ~2 orders of magnitude.

Conclusion: The paper establishes tight rate-distortion limits for suffix-only KV cache compression policies, showing a power-law scaling. Whether recurrent or propagating cache summaries can improve beyond this scaling remains open. The empirical power-law finding and theoretical characterization provide a foundation for designing better cache compression strategies.

Abstract: We study the rate-distortion limits of online KV cache compression in autoregressive language models, formulating it as sequential Wyner-Ziv source coding on the filtration induced by the model, with the next-step query as decoder side information. Empirically, across four models spanning two families and $0.5$-$3$B parameters, we find that the next-token distribution's sensitivity to context truncation decays \emph{polynomially} rather than \emph{geometrically}: a power law improves on an exponential fit by an order of magnitude in extrapolation, the fitted exponent is recovered independently from a sink-plus-recent KL measurement, and the decay is verified to be free of positional-encoding artifacts by a position-preserving ablation. Under a corresponding \emph{polynomial truncation-sensitivity} assumption, our main result characterizes the per-token memory requirement of \emph{suffix-only} cache policies: a sliding-window scheme attains distortion $\varepsilon$ with window $w = O(\varepsilon^{-1/α})$, and -- under an additional two-sided Bayes-risk condition -- a converse shows $w = Ω(\varepsilon^{-1/α})$ is necessary within this policy class, so the scaling is $Θ(\varepsilon^{-1/α})$ for suffix-only policies. Whether recurrent or propagating cache summaries can beat this scaling is left open. An explicit block-Markov scheme achieves the upper bound; its rate-of-convergence exponent matches the converse under additional forward-decay and regularity hypotheses (not implied by truncation sensitivity alone), and differs by a factor of two otherwise. Empirically, the polynomial law predicts the degradation curves of concrete cache policies: recency-based eviction (sliding, sink-plus-recent) suppresses distortion by roughly two orders of magnitude over random retention at equal budget, with a power-law decay in the budget.

</details>


### [354] [Remarks on Johnson-type Bounds for Insdel Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25090)
*Yulin Yang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper improves the Johnson-type list-size bound for insertion-deletion codes by using binary constant-weight codes to encode local lists, leading to tighter bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To refine the existing Johnson-type list-size bound for insertion-deletion codes originally established by Hayashi and Yasunaga, addressing its limitations.

Method: The paper encodes local lists into binary constant-weight codes to achieve a refined bound.

Result: A tighter Johnson-type list-size bound for insertion-deletion codes is obtained.

Conclusion: Encoding local lists with binary constant-weight codes effectively improves the bound for insertion-deletion codes.

Abstract: We refine the Johnson-type list-size bound of Hayashi and Yasunaga for insertion-deletion codes by encoding local lists into binary constant-weight codes.

</details>


### [355] [Eliminating Blind Spots from Wireless Network by Metasurface: A Blind Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25140)
*Wenhai Lai,Mingxiao Li,Kaiming Shen,Liyao Xiang,Zhi-Quan Luo*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: RFZero is a CSI-free deployment paradigm that uses visual photos and RSRP feedback to jointly optimize metasurface placement and phase shifts for eliminating wireless blind spots without channel estimation, achieving full blind spot elimination in a 100m² area with two MTSs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing metasurface deployment methods rely on explicit channel estimation, which incurs prohibitive overhead and is intractable in real-world networks. The goal is to eliminate wireless blind spots without this bottleneck.

Method: RFZero extracts macro-environmental features from visual photos to guide MTS placement, and uses reference signal received power (RSRP) feedback for dynamic phase-shift optimization, operating independently of base stations for plug-and-play deployment.

Result: Real-world field tests show RFZero completely eliminates all blind spots in a 100m² indoor area using just two 1.5m×0.9m MTSs.

Conclusion: RFZero demonstrates that CSI-free, vision-assisted metasurface deployment can practically and cost-effectively eliminate wireless blind spots without requiring channel estimation or base station coordination.

Abstract: Deploying metasurfaces (MTSs) to eliminate wireless blind spots requires jointly determining the physical placement of MTSs and the meta-atom phase shifts. Existing methods typically rely on explicit channel estimation, which incurs prohibitive overhead and is often intractable in real-world networks. To sidestep this bottleneck, we propose RFZero, a channel-state-information (CSI)-free deployment paradigm. Instead of estimating channels, RFZero extracts macro-environmental features from visual photos to guide MTS placement, and leverages reference signal received power (RSRP) feedback for dynamic phase-shift optimization. Most importantly, RFZero operates independently of base stations, thereby enabling seamless plug-and-play implementation. Real-world field tests confirm that RFZero completely eliminates all blind spots in a $100\text{ m}^2$ indoor area using just a pair of $1.5\text{ m}\times 0.9\text{ m}$ MTSs.

</details>


### [356] [Finite-Blocklength Analysis for Noisy Permutation Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25699)
*Lugaoze Feng,Guocheng Lv,Xunan Li.,Ye Jin*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper derives finite-blocklength achievability and strong converse bounds for noisy permutation channels, where the reachable output polytope may have lower dimension than the output simplex. The achievability uses affine lattice points and Euclidean nearest-neighbor decoding, reducing errors to d(d+1) one-dimensional events. The converse yields a d log√n blocklength-dependent term via a modified meta-converse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Gaussian achievability analyses assume strictly positive full-rank square DMC transition matrices, and strong converse bounds in lower-dimensional settings scale with the dimension of the output simplex rather than the reachable output polytope. This paper aims to fill these gaps for noisy permutation channels.

Method: Messages are placed on a simplex lattice in affine coordinates; decoding projects the empirical output distribution onto the reachable affine hull and applies Euclidean nearest-neighbor decoding. A geometric reduction converts decoding errors into d(d+1) one-dimensional transfer events. The converse uses a modified meta-converse, KL divergence covering, and a local binary-testing bound.

Result: A refined Gaussian achievability lower bound based on averaged local coordinate variances and a relative volume ratio. A strong converse whose blocklength-dependent term is d log√n, up to a bounded additive remainder.

Conclusion: The paper provides tight finite-blocklength bounds for noisy permutation channels, properly accounting for the lower-dimensional geometry of the reachable output polytope, with both achievability and converse scaling with the affine dimension d.

Abstract: We study finite-blocklength bounds for noisy permutation channels whose reachable output polytope may be lower-dimensional than the output simplex. Existing Gaussian achievability analyses focus on strictly positive full-rank square DMC transition matrices. The capacity result for arbitrary strictly positive DMCs is established through a weak converse, while available strong converse bounds in the lower-dimensional setting can scale with the dimension of the output simplex rather than with that of the reachable output polytope. On the achievability side, messages are placed on a simplex lattice in affine coordinates, and decoding is performed by projecting the empirical output distribution onto the reachable affine hull followed by Euclidean nearest-neighbor decoding. Writing $d$ for the affine dimension of the reachable output polytope, a geometric reduction converts decoding errors into $d(d+1)$ one-dimensional transfer events, yielding a refined Gaussian achievability lower bound based on averaged local coordinate variances and a relative volume ratio. On the converse side, a modified meta-converse, a Kullback--Leibler divergence covering, and a local binary-testing bound yield a strong converse whose blocklength-dependent term is $d\log\sqrt n$, up to a bounded additive remainder.

</details>


### [357] [On the Performance of Single/Dual Fluid Antenna Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25769)
*Jiangsheng Huangfu,Zhengyu Song,Tianwei Hou,Anna Li,Yuanwei Liu,Arumugam Nallanathan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper analyzes fluid antenna systems (FAS) over spatially correlated Rayleigh fading channels, deriving exact and approximate outage probability expressions for MISO-FAS and Dual-FAS configurations, and showing that more ports improve performance while lower port correlation yields better results, with Dual-FAS outperforming MISO-FAS in high SNR regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the potential of fluid antenna systems (FAS), a next-generation reconfigurable antenna technology, by providing rigorous performance analysis over practical spatially correlated fading channels for two key deployment scenarios.

Method: The authors derived exact expressions and closed-form approximations for outage probability (OP) and diversity order for both MISO-FAS (multiple-antenna transmitter with single-antenna FAS receiver) and Dual-FAS (single-antenna FAS at both ends) over spatially correlated Rayleigh fading channels.

Result: (i) Increasing the number of ports significantly improves performance for both models; (ii) System performance is inversely related to port correlation level; (iii) In the high SNR regime, Dual-FAS outperforms MISO-FAS.

Conclusion: FAS technology can effectively exploit spatial diversity through reconfigurable antenna positions, with Dual-FAS offering superior performance at high SNR, and the developed analytical framework provides valuable tools for designing future FAS-based wireless systems.

Abstract: The emerging technology of fluid antenna systems (FASs) represents a promising next-generation reconfigurable antenna solution, capable of exploiting the full spatial diversity within a predefined space by finely reconfiguring the positions of radiating elements. In this paper, the performance of FAS over spatially correlated Rayleigh fading channels is investigated for two distinct scenarios: a multiple-input single-output (MISO) configuration, where a receiver with a single-antenna FAS is served by a multi-antenna transmitter (MISO-FAS), and a single-input single-output setup where single-antenna FASs are equipped at both the transmitter and receiver (Dual-FAS). Exact expressions and closed-form approximations for the outage probability (OP) of both the MISO-FAS and Dual-FAS models are derived as the core contributions of this work. To provide deeper insights into system performance, the diversity orders for each model are also derived and analyzed. Analytical results demonstrate that increasing the number of ports significantly enhances system performance. The theoretical analysis is corroborated by key findings from our simulations, demonstrating that: $i$) Both the MISO-FAS and Dual-FAS models achieve considerable performance gains as the number of ports is increased; $ii$) System performance for both configurations is inversely related to the level of port correlation; lower correlation leads to better performance; $iii$) In the high signal-to-noise ratio regime, the Dual-FAS model surpasses the performance of the MISO-FAS model.

</details>


### [358] [Best-First Ordered Statistics Decoding of Quantum LDPC Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25777)
*Michele Banfi,Marco Ferrari,Antonino Favano,Alberto Tarable,Luca Barletta*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes Best-First OSD (BF-OSD), a more efficient variant of Ordered Statistics Decoding for quantum LDPC codes that explores error candidates in descending order of likelihood rather than brute-force enumeration, achieving the same performance as BP+OSD with only 1/100th of the query budget.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce the computational complexity of the BP+OSD decoding pipeline for QLDPC codes, particularly in the full circuit-level noise regime where BP is unreliable.

Method: (1) Proposes BF-OSD, which traverses the error-candidate space in decreasing likelihood order instead of brute-force enumeration. (2) Departs from the conventional BP+OSD cascade by invoking OSD after a fixed, small number of BP iterations, regardless of BP convergence.

Result: BF-OSD matches the performance of the BP+OSD baseline while reducing the query budget by 100x, as demonstrated by Monte Carlo simulations of Bivariate Bicycle codes under full circuit-level noise.

Conclusion: BF-OSD significantly improves decoding efficiency for QLDPC codes, offering a practical path forward for real-time quantum error correction in resource-constrained environments.

Abstract: Belief Propagation (BP) followed by Ordered Statistics Decoding (OSD) has emerged as the gold standard for decoding quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes. Recent advancements in this field have proposed new methods and algorithms to lower the complexity of this standard pipeline. Because of code degeneracy, and more in general because multiple distinct error patterns can produce the same syndrome, OSD is inherently a list-decoding technique; that is, it enumerates a set of syndrome-consistent candidates and returns the most probable one. In this work, we propose a variant of OSD, which we call Best-First OSD (BF-OSD), that explores the error-candidate space more efficiently by traversing it in order of decreasing likelihood, rather than by brute-force enumeration of a pre-selected subset. In addition, we depart from the conventional BP+OSD cascade: instead of conditioning the OSD invocation on BP convergence, we invoke OSD after a fixed, small number of BP iterations. This design choice is motivated by the full circuit-level noise regime, in which BP is particularly unreliable. Monte Carlo simulations of a family of Bivariate Bicycle (BB) codes under full circuit-level noise show that BF-OSD matches the performance of the BP+OSD baseline while exploring the solution space with 1/100th of the query budget.

</details>


### [359] [Hardware-Efficient and Performance-Enhanced Joint Pulse Shaping and Dispersion Compensation for Coherent Data Center Interconnects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25818)
*Yukun Zhang,Xiaoxue Gong,Weigang Hou,Xu Zhang,Lei Guo*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper proposes a joint shaping filtering and chromatic dispersion compensation (JFS-CD) algorithm that moves CD compensation to the transmitter side, combined with a square boundary clipping (SBC) algorithm to solve PAPR issues. This reduces complexity by ~46% and improves Q-factor by ~0.3 dB for next-generation DCI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With 5G/6G and Generative AI driving data traffic growth, coherent optical communication needs higher baud rates and complex modulation, leading to increased DSP complexity and power consumption, especially in CD compensation and shaping filter modules.

Method: The authors propose JFS-CD algorithm that moves CD compensation to the transmitter side, leveraging discrete Fourier transform and shaping filter spectral features for integrated processing. They also propose a low-complexity square boundary clipping (SBC) algorithm to address high PAPR from CD pre-compensation.

Result: JFS-CD reduces real multiplication complexity by ~46% while maintaining performance. The combined JFS-CD and SBC scheme improves Q-factor by ~0.3 dB compared to traditional post-CDC, by suppressing system nonlinearity and receiver IQ imbalance effects.

Conclusion: This research offers a promising transmitter DSP solution for next-generation low-power, high-performance data center interconnects (DCI).

Abstract: With the explosion of data traffic triggered by 5G/6G and Generative artificial intelligence, coherent optical communication is moving towards higher baud rates and more complex modulation formats. This leads to a significant increase in the computational complexity and power consumption of digital signal processing (DSP) at the transmitter and receiver ends, especially in the chromatic dispersion(CD) Compensation and low roll-off shaping filter modules. We propose a joint shaping filtering and CD compensation (JFS-CD) algorithm. This algorithm moves the CD compensation to the transmitter side and utilizes the characteristics of discrete fourier transform and the spectral features of shaping filtering for integrated processing. Aiming at the high peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) problem caused by chromatic dispersion pre-compensation, we propose a low-complexity square boundary clipping algorithm(SBC). Simulation results show that, under the premise of maintaining unchanged performance, JFS-CD can reduce the real multiplication complexity by about 46%. Meanwhile, benefiting from the suppression of the effects of system nonlinearity and receiver IQ imbalance, the joint JFS-CD and SBC scheme improves the Q-factor by about 0.3 dB in experiments compared to the traditional post-chromatic dispersion compensation scheme. This research provides a highly potential transmitter DSP solution for next-generation low-power and high-performance data center interconnects (DCI).

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [360] [In Search of the Ingredients of Open-Endedness: Replicating Picbreeder with Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23908)
*Sam Earle,Kay Arulkumaran,Andrew Dai,Akarsh Kumar,Julian Togelius,Sebastian Risi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This study replicates the human-driven Picbreeder system using Vision Language Models (VLMs) instead of human users, finding qualitative differences between AI and human open-ended discovery, and tests various factors (noise, diversity, memory) that might influence these differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether artificial agents (specifically VLMs) can achieve the same kind of open-ended, fruitful unguided discovery that humans demonstrate in creative and scientific production.

Method: Replicated Picbreeder (a human-driven interactive evolution platform) by replacing human users with frontier Vision Language Models (VLMs). Analyzed output using metrics of phylogenetic complexity, visual/semantic salience, and novelty. Tested causal factors: exploratory noise, behavioral diversity, and narrative momentum (memory).

Result: Clear qualitative differences were observed between VLM-generated and human-generated outputs. The study characterized these differences using phylogenetic complexity, visual/semantic salience, and novelty metrics.

Conclusion: Current VLMs show limitations in achieving human-like open-ended discovery, though adding exploratory noise, behavioral diversity, and narrative memory may help close this gap. Further work is needed to understand and bridge these differences.

Abstract: We are in the midst of large-scale industrial and academic efforts to automate the processes of scientific, technological and creative production through AI-driven assistants. Historically, a fundamental property of these processes in their human form has been their open-endedness: their capacity for generating a seemingly endless supply of novel and meaningful new forms. Do artificial agents have any capacity for such fruitful unguided discovery? To answer this question, we turn to Picbreeder, the canonical exemplar of human-driven open-ended search, in which users collaboratively generated a diverse library of images through interactive evolution of small neural networks. We replicate Picbreeder, replacing human users with frontier Vision Language Models (VLMs). We observe clear qualitative differences between the output of our system and the historical human baseline, and attempt to characterize them using metrics of phylogenetic complexity and visual and semantic salience and novelty. In an effort to identify some of the causal factors contributing these differences, we study the addition of exploratory noise to the agents' selection process, of behavioral diversity between agents, and of narrative momentum in the form of memory of past actions. We make our code available at https://github.com/smearle/picbreeder-vlm.

</details>


### [361] [Confidence Calibration in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23909)
*Noam Michael,Daniel BenShushan,Jacob Bien,Don A. Moore*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit overconfidence on difficult tasks and underconfidence on easy tasks, similar to human behavior, as shown by a preregistered study using the LifeEval benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically investigate and quantify the calibration of LLMs' confidence across diverse tasks, specifically examining whether LLMs exhibit similar overconfidence patterns as humans.

Method: A preregistered study analyzing LLM confidence calibration across tasks, with development of LifeEval - a test designed to evaluate model calibration across varying levels of difficulty.

Result: Current LLMs show overconfidence on average (confidence exceeds accuracy), but this is moderated by a hard-easy effect: overconfidence on difficult tests, and underconfidence on easy tests.

Conclusion: LLMs, like humans, are poorly calibrated in their confidence, showing systematic bias that varies with task difficulty, highlighting the need for better calibration methods.

Abstract: We investigate the calibration of large language models' (LLMs') confidence across diverse tasks. The results of our preregistered study show that the current crop of LLMs are, like people, too sure they are right: confidence exceeds accuracy, on average. Importantly, however, this tendency is moderated by a powerful hard-easy effect, wherein overconfidence is greatest on difficult tests; by contrast, easy tests actually show substantial underconfidence. We develop LifeEval, a test for evaluating model calibration across levels of difficulty.

</details>


### [362] [How Much Thinking is Enough? Quantifying and Understanding Redundancy in LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23926)
*Zhiyuan Zhai,Xinkai You,Wenjing Yan,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper quantifies and explains redundancy in reasoning-capable LLMs, showing that 61-93% of chain-of-thought steps are unnecessary, and proves this over-thinking is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards rather than a model-specific bug.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine how much of the lengthy chain-of-thought deliberation in reasoning LLMs is actually necessary, as casual observation suggests extensive reformulation, verification, and circular self-reflection whose necessity has never been measured at scale or explained from first principles.

Method: Formalizes reasoning redundancy ρ as the largest fraction of trailing segmented steps that can be truncated before the model still produces the correct answer. Quantifies this across four frontier reasoning models and two math benchmarks (MATH-500), and proves theoretically that redundancy is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards.

Result: Step-level redundancy is consistently 61-93% across 8 (model, benchmark) conditions, with median critical prefix being a single segmented step in 6 of 8 conditions. ρ decreases with problem difficulty but remains substantial (46-85%) even on hardest Level-5 problems. Robust across judge families.

Conclusion: Over-thinking is not a bug to be patched in individual models but a structural property of how current reasoning models are trained under length-agnostic outcome rewards, where no finite expected stopping time is optimal regardless of RL algorithm, base model, or data distribution.

Abstract: Reasoning-capable large language models solve hard problems by emitting long chains of thought, paying heavily in latency, GPU time, and energy. Casual inspection of their traces reveals extensive reformulation, verification, and circular self-reflection, yet how much of this deliberation is actually necessary has never been measured at scale or explained from first principles. This paper closes both gaps.
  We formalise reasoning redundancy directly in terms of the reasoning model itself: the redundancy of a correct trace is the largest fraction of its trailing segmented steps that can be truncated while $π$, forced to terminate thinking and emit a final answer, still produces the correct answer. A large-scale quantification across four frontier reasoning models and two mathematical benchmarks shows that step-level redundancy is consistently high -- between 61% and 93% across the 8 (model, benchmark) conditions we study, with the median critical prefix equal to a single segmented step in six of the eight conditions -- that the finding is robust to the choice of judge family, and that although $ρ$ decreases with problem difficulty on MATH-500, all four models remain substantially redundant ($ρ\in [46\%, 85\%]$) even on the hardest Level-5 problems.
  We then prove that this redundancy is a structural consequence of length-agnostic outcome rewards, not a model-specific artefact: under any such reward, no finite expected stopping time is optimal. The result holds regardless of RL algorithm, base model, data distribution, or whether the policy is obtained via RL or distillation; over-thinking is therefore not a bug to be patched in individual models but a structural property of how current reasoning models are trained. Code: https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/how-much-thinking-is-enough

</details>


### [363] [Context: Proactive Goal-Directed Intelligence via Composable Sandboxed Programs, Declarative Wiring, and Structured Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23928)
*Gregory Magarshak*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Context is the intelligence layer of the Magarshak Architecture, using proactive goal-directed agents with write-time context assembly, composable wisdom programs, and proactive goal stream state machines to replace reactive chatbots, with six formal proofs of efficiency and correctness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of reactive query-response chatbots that wait for user prompts, by enabling proactive agents that advance shared tasks autonomously.

Method: Three mechanisms: (1) write-time context assembly with Groker agents for KV-cache reuse; (2) composable sandboxed wisdom programs wired to goal types; (3) proactive goal stream state machines driving conversations toward terminal states without awaiting input.

Result: Six formal theorems including Context Stability (cost bounded by semantic change rate), Program Composition Correctness, Declarative Wiring Soundness, Proactive Dominance, Coordination Overhead Elimination & Quality Preservation, and Cross-Platform Vote Consistency.

Conclusion: The architecture formally proves proactive agents dominate reactive ones in goal-oriented settings, with near-100% KV-cache reuse and Pareto improvements in multi-participant chats, implemented in the open-source Qbix/Safebox/Safebots stack.

Abstract: We present Context, the intelligence layer of the Magarshak Architecture, which replaces reactive query-response chatbots with proactive goal-directed agents that advance shared tasks without waiting for user prompts. The architecture rests on three mutually reinforcing mechanisms. Write-time context assembly precomputes enriched typed attributes via Groker agents, assembling interaction context as a deterministic pure function of graph state; context blocks are byte-identical across turns between semantic changes, enabling near-100% KV-cache reuse. Composable sandboxed wisdom programs form a governed library of LM-generated imperative programs declaratively wired to goal types via typed stream relations, composed via phase ordering, and executed at interaction time without further LM calls. Proactive goal stream state machines drive conversations toward terminal states by inspecting graph state and emitting structured interaction content (option arrays, governance affordances, clarification prompts) without awaiting user input. We prove six formal results: the Context Stability Theorem, bounding per-turn LM cost as a function of semantic change rate; a Program Composition Correctness Theorem; a Declarative Wiring Soundness Theorem; the Proactive Dominance Theorem, proving proactive agents weakly dominate reactive agents on expected turns-to-terminal-state; Coordination Overhead Elimination and Quality Preservation, establishing Pareto improvements in multi-participant goal chats; and a Cross-Platform Vote Consistency Theorem. Implemented in the open-source Qbix / Safebox / Safebots stack.

</details>


### [364] [Toward Reliable Design of LLM-Enabled Agentic Workflows: Optimizing Latency-Reliability-Cost Tradeoffs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23929)
*Ya-Ting Yang,Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper analyzes latency, reliability, and cost tradeoffs in LLM-enabled agentic workflows, proposing performance models and optimal sequential workflow designs including a water-filling token allocation policy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and optimize the fundamental tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in modern AI systems that combine LLM-powered agents with conventional computational modules in multi-agent workflows.

Method: The authors introduce parametric performance models for LLM agents (using an exponential reliability function based on reasoning/output tokens) and non-LLM agents, then study sequential workflow design under latency/cost constraints using optimization techniques like water-filling allocation and shadow pricing.

Result: A water-filling token allocation policy and characterizations of optimal workflow reliability in terms of shadow prices.

Conclusion: Sequential agentic workflows can be optimally designed under latency and cost constraints, with structured resource allocation policies that balance reliability across agents.

Abstract: Modern AI systems increasingly rely on workflows composed of multiple interacting agents, some powered by large language models (LLMs) and others by conventional computational modules. This paper analyzes the fundamental tradeoffs between latency, reliability, and cost in LLM-enabled agentic workflows. We introduce performance models for both LLM and non-LLM agents that capture the relationship between computational effort and output quality, incorporating the impact of reasoning and output tokens for LLM agents using a parametric exponential reliability function. Then, we study the design of sequential workflows under latency and cost constraints. Main results include a water-filling token allocation policy and characterizations of optimal workflow reliability in terms of shadow prices.

</details>


### [365] [Quantum Frog: Emergent Cooperation and Difficulty Scaling in a Quantized-Time Cooperative Game](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23930)
*Saad Mankarious*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Quantum Frog is a cooperative two-player quantized-time game where RL analysis reveals that rushing straight upward is optimal, cooperation dramatically outperforms independent play (+32-34% success), and the emergent strategy is synchronized rushing rather than complex coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze how game difficulty scales with traffic density, determine optimal single-agent policy, measure the cooperation gap between independent vs. cooperative multi-agent play, and understand what joint strategies emerge under cooperative incentives.

Method: Trained agents using five escalating RL stages (Tabular Q-Learning, DQN, IDQN, MAPPO with centralized critic) across traffic densities of 1-6 cars, evaluating joint success rates and episode lengths.

Result: (i) Rush strategy (moving directly upward) is universally optimal; (ii) Adding an uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling traffic for a single expert; (iii) Cooperative training recovers +32-34% success rate and reduces episode length from ~90 to ~6 steps; (iv) Emergent cooperative strategy is synchronized rushing, not complex coordination.

Conclusion: Shared incentives alone suffice to align agents in time-critical cooperative tasks, providing guidance for commercial game design and insights into how environment mechanics shape multi-agent learning dynamics.

Abstract: We introduce \emph{Quantum Frog}, a two-player cooperative game built on a novel \emph{quantized-time} mechanic in which the environment advances only when a player acts. Inspired by the classic arcade game Frogger, Quantum Frog requires two frogs to cross an 8$\times$8 grid of traffic and reach the far side together. We use reinforcement learning (RL) as an analytical lens to answer four design questions: (1) how does game difficulty scale with traffic density, (2) what is the optimal single-agent policy and why, (3) how large is the cooperation gap between independent and cooperative two-agent play, and (4) what joint strategy emerges when agents are incentivised to cooperate? We train agents through five escalating stages, Tabular Q-Learning, Deep Q-Network (\DQN), Independent \DQN~(\IDQN), and Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimisation (\MAPPO\ with a centralised critic), evaluating each against traffic densities of one to six cars. Our key findings are: (i) the quantized-time mechanic makes a \emph{rush strategy} (moving directly upward at every step) universally optimal, as time exposure to traffic is minimised; (ii) adding an uncoordinated second player is harder than sextupling the traffic for a single expert player; (iii) cooperative training recovers +32--34 percentage points of joint success rate relative to independent agents and reduces episode length from $\sim$90 to $\sim$6 steps; and (iv) the emergent cooperative strategy is synchronised rushing, not complex positional coordination, illustrating that shared incentives alone suffice to align agents in time-critical cooperative tasks. These findings provide concrete, empirically grounded guidance for the commercial design of Quantum Frog and offer broader insights into the role of environment mechanics in shaping multi-agent learning dynamics.

</details>


### [366] [BODHI: Precise OS Kernel Specification Inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23931)
*Zhiming Chang,Ziyang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BODHI is a domain knowledge prompting method that improves LLM-based formal specification generation for OS kernels, boosting Pass@1 from 55.10% to 96.73% on the OSV-Bench benchmark using a structured C-to-Python translation guide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual specification writing for OS kernel verification requires deep domain expertise and is error-prone. The goal is to automate this process using LLMs, but existing approaches achieve only 55.10% Pass@1 on OSV-Bench.

Method: BODHI augments few-shot prompts with a structured C-to-Python translation guide covering 15 categories of domain-specific patterns, inspired by Structured Chain-of-Thought prompting. It separates pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation concerns.

Result: Evaluated on 9 models from 6 providers (Anthropic, Mistral, Amazon, DeepSeek, Meta, Alibaba), BODHI improves every model by +11% to +32%. Best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 + BODHI) reaches 96.73% Pass@1, reducing both syntax and semantic errors.

Conclusion: Domain knowledge injection is a model-agnostic technique that substantially bridges the gap between general-purpose code generation and formal specification synthesis, with strongest effects on models having sufficient instruction-following capability.

Abstract: The formal verification of operating system kernels requires precise specifications that capture the intended behavior of system calls. Writing these specifications manually demands deep domain expertise, motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) to automate the process. However, in OSV-Bench, a benchmark of 245 specification generation tasks derived from the Hyperkernel OS kernel, the best reported Pass@1 is 55.10%. We propose a domain knowledge prompting method (BODHI), which augments the standard few-shot prompt with a structured C-to-Python translation guide covering 15 categories of domain-specific translation patterns. Inspired by Structured Chain-of-Thought (SCoT) prompting, the guide organizes translation by separation of concerns, addressing pre-condition extraction and post-condition generation as distinct categories. Evaluated on nine models from six providers (Anthropic, Mistral, Amazon, DeepSeek, Meta, Alibaba), covering dense, mixture-of-experts and reasoning architectures, BODHI improves every model tested, with gains ranging from +11% to +32%. The best configuration (Claude Opus 4.6 + BODHI) reaches 96.73% Pass@1. BODHI reduces both syntax and semantic errors, with the strongest effect on models that have sufficient instruction-following capability to utilize structured reference material. These results demonstrate that domain knowledge injection is a model-agnostic technique that substantially bridges the gap between general-purpose code generation and formal specification synthesis.

</details>


### [367] [When Correct Beliefs Collapse: Epistemic Resilience of LLMs under Clinical Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23932)
*Boyu Xiao,Xiuqi Tian,Xuwen Song,Haochun Wang,Guanchun Song,Sendong Zhao,Bing Qin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show strong medical knowledge but exhibit multi-turn sycophancy, abandoning correct diagnoses under pressure. The paper introduces Med-Stress (stress test), RBED (inference-time defense), and R-FT (fine-tuning defense) to measure and improve belief stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs achieve high medical benchmark accuracy but display severe multi-turn sycophancy in clinical dialogue, abandoning correct diagnoses under escalating pressure, revealing a dissociation between knowledge and robustness.

Method: Proposes Med-Stress (stress test framework), RBED (role-based epistemic defense at inference), and R-FT (resilience-oriented fine-tuning at training time).

Result: High diagnostic capability does not imply high belief stability; large knowledge-robustness gaps exist. R-FT nearly eliminates belief change and substantially improves robustness.

Conclusion: Medical knowledge and belief stability are dissociable in LLMs; training-time intervention (R-FT) is highly effective at mitigating sycophancy, while inference-time defense (RBED) provides a lighter alternative.

Abstract: Despite strong medical benchmark accuracy, LLMs can exhibit severe multi-turn sycophancy in clinical dialogue, abandoning initial correct diagnosis under escalating pressure. We propose \textbf{\textsc{Med-Stress}}, a targeted stress test framework that evaluates belief stability under escalating pressure. Across nine frontier large language models (LLMs), we find a clear dissociation between medical knowledge and robustness: high initial diagnostic capability does not imply high belief stability, yielding large knowledge-robustness gaps for several LLMs. To mitigate this failure mode, we propose a lightweight inference-time defense, \textbf{\texttt{RBED}} (\textbf{R}ole-\textbf{B}ased \textbf{E}pistemic \textbf{D}efense), and \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} (\textbf{R}esilience-oriented \textbf{F}ine-\textbf{T}uning), a training-time approach that internalizes evidence-based resistance to pressure. Experiments show that \textbf{\texttt{R-FT}} nearly eliminates belief change and substantially improves robustness.

</details>


### [368] [Practical Quantum CIM Empowerment via All-Domestic-Core Agentic Large Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23934)
*Wang Rui,Lu Diannan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This work integrates a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system (using LangGraph/LangChain) to automate QUBO/Ising modeling, weight tuning, and validation, using fully domestic large models and hardware, with the unexpected finding that agent-assisted quantum computing iterations can reciprocally improve the agent's problem-solving ability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To lower the barrier for non-specialists in modeling quantum computing devices and reduce the tedious manual iteration of constraint weights and modeling methodologies for experts.

Method: Integration of a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system built on LangGraph and LangChain frameworks, using domestic large models and domestically developed CIM hardware.

Result: LLMs can effectively perform QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight decision iteration, and rapid validation of literature-reported schemes, with all tasks fully implementable using domestic models and hardware.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates robust technological integration and unexpectedly identifies a reciprocal enhancement paradigm where quantum computing knowledge accumulated by the agent improves its own problem-solving capability, though challenges remain in both large models and quantum computing fields.

Abstract: Quantum computing devices are recognized as powerful tools for solving NP-complete problems. However, the intricacy of their modeling presents notable barriers for non-specialists, while the tedious iteration of constraint weights and modeling methodologies also consumes substantial effort on the part of experts. To address these challenges, this study integrates a femtosecond laser-pumped Coherent Ising Machine (CIM) with an LLM-driven agentic system by leveraging the LangGraph and LangChain frameworks. Comprehensive investigations demonstrate that large language models (LLMs) can effectively perform such tasks in modeling as QUBO/Ising model calibration, constraint weight decision iteration and rapid validation of literature-reported schemes. Notably, all these tasks can be fully implemented based on domestic large models, combined with domestically developed CIM hardware, we truly achieve the practical empowerment of quantum CIM that fully relies on all-domestic agentic large models and hardware. This work successfully realizes robust technological integration, laying a solid foundation for subsequent research. Nevertheless, it also identifies the persisting challenges in the two cutting-edge fields of large models and quantum computing at the current stage. Encouragingly, we unexpectedly discover a promising new paradigm where accumulated knowledge from agent-assisted quantum computing iterations reciprocally enhances the agent's own problem-solving capability, thereby addressing these challenges.

</details>


### [369] [Operationalizing Reconstructive Authority: Runtime Construction, Dependency Resolution, and Execution Gating in Autonomous Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23935)
*Marcelo Fernandez - TraslaIA*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a runtime enforcement model for Reconstructive Authority (RAM), adding a "halt" state beyond admit/deny, with a Recovery Loop using drift detection and execution control to ensure actions only execute when authority is constructible from current state.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous agents fail not only from incorrect decisions but from executing decisions whose authority no longer holds at runtime. Prior work defined RAM as a condition but lacked runtime enforcement mechanisms.

Method: Proposes a runtime execution model with three states (admit/deny/halt), a concrete execution protocol with dynamic dependency resolution and authority reconstruction, and a Recovery Loop integrating drift detection (IML) with execution control (ACP).

Result: The model guarantees safety (no action without constructible authority) and conditional liveness (execution resumes when authority-defining variables become observable).

Conclusion: This work operationalizes reconstructive authority as a runtime enforcement mechanism, providing the execution semantics needed to apply RAM in real autonomous systems.

Abstract: Autonomous agent systems fail not only due to incorrect decisions, but due to executing decisions whose authority no longer holds at runtime. Prior work defined Reconstructive Authority (RAM) as a condition for valid execution: actions are permitted only if authority can be constructed from current state.
  This paper addresses enforcement at runtime: how to enforce this condition in a running system.
  We introduce a runtime execution model in which authority is evaluated at action time and execution is conditioned on its constructibility. This extends the execution state space beyond admit/deny with a third state, halt, representing cases where authority is undefined due to incomplete or uncertain observability.
  We define a concrete execution protocol including dynamic dependency resolution, authority reconstruction, and explicit decision semantics. We further introduce a Recovery Loop that integrates drift detection (IML) with execution control (ACP), allowing the system to suspend execution, acquire missing information, and re-attempt authority reconstruction.
  We show that this model guarantees safety -- no action is executed without constructible authority -- and conditional liveness: execution resumes when authority-defining variables become observable.
  This work operationalizes reconstructive authority as a runtime enforcement mechanism, providing the execution semantics required to apply RAM in real systems.

</details>


### [370] [Fuzzy, Neutrosophic, and Uncertain Graph Theory: Properties and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23936)
*Takaaki Fujita,Florentin Smarandache*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of graph theory under uncertainty, unifying fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related graph models within an uncertain graph framework, covering theory, extensions, and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a systematic and unified survey of graph theory under uncertainty, organizing diverse uncertainty-aware graph models into a coherent framework to understand their relationships and capabilities.

Method: Reviews fundamental concepts, structural properties, graph classes, and parameters across fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models; introduces extensions like uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs; explores applications in molecular graphs, decision-making, GNNs, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps.

Result: A coherent framework that organizes diverse uncertainty-aware graph models under a common perspective, highlighting their relationships, capabilities, and applications in complex systems.

Conclusion: The uncertain graph framework serves as a unifying paradigm for graph theory under uncertainty, enabling systematic understanding and broader application across complex systems.

Abstract: This book presents a comprehensive and systematic survey of graph theory under uncertainty, with particular emphasis on the unifying role of the uncertain graph framework. It reviews fundamental concepts, structural properties, graph classes, and graph parameters within fuzzy, neutrosophic, and related models, while also introducing a wide range of extensions such as uncertain digraphs, hypergraphs, superhypergraphs, and dynamic graphs. In addition to theoretical developments, the book explores practical applications, including uncertain molecular graphs, decision-making systems, graph neural networks, knowledge graphs, and cognitive maps. By organizing diverse uncertainty-aware graph models within a common perspective, this work provides a coherent framework for understanding their relationships, capabilities, and applications in complex systems.

</details>


### [371] [BoxLitE: A Faithful Knowledge Base Embedding Based on Convex Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23937)
*Bruno F. Lourenço,Hesham Morgan,Ana Ozaki,Aleksandar Pavlović,Emanuel Sallinger*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BoxLitE is a KB embedding model for DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ that uses convex optimization to create faithful embeddings of both ABox and TBox knowledge, proving that any satisfiable KB can be weakly faithfully embedded.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing KB embeddings don't fully leverage convexity during learning, despite using convex regions to represent concept hierarchies in the TBox.

Method: BoxLitE maps concepts to convex regions (boxes) in vector space and formulates KB embedding as a convex optimization problem, supporting the DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ ontology language.

Result: Proves that any satisfiable DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ KB has a weakly faithful BoxLitE embedding, and demonstrates how to obtain embeddings with these properties via convex optimization.

Conclusion: Convexity can be effectively leveraged for KB embedding with desirable faithfulness guarantees, enabling principled integration of ABox and TBox knowledge.

Abstract: Knowledge base (KB) embeddings aim at combining the capability of classical knowledge graph embeddings to generalize the information present in facts, the ABox, with conceptual knowledge represented in an ontology language, the TBox. Several authors have recently explored the idea of mapping concepts to convex regions in a vector space. This is useful to represent hierarchies, typically present in TBoxes, since more general concepts can be mapped to larger regions, containing those regions associated with more specific concepts. However, the power of convexity is rarely leveraged during the actual learning tasks. Here, we introduce BoxLitE, a KB embedding model for DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ that allows for convex optimization. We show that for any satisfiable DL-Lite$^{\mathcal{H}}$ KB, there is a BoxLitE embedding that is a weakly faithful model. As a proof of concept, we show how to formulate the KB embedding task as a convex optimization problem and how to obtain embeddings with such desirable faithfulness properties.

</details>


### [372] [Authority Inversion in LLM-Mediated Ubiquitous Systems: When Models Trust Users Over Sensors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23938)
*Long Zhang,Zi-bo Qin,Wei-neng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs suffer from Authority Inversion where natural-language user claims dominate numerical sensor data during fusion, even when sensors are more reliable. The paper proposes Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time intervention that improves sensor trust metrics significantly, flipping 80.2% of incorrect decisions and boosting HAR accuracy from near-zero to 21.9-27.5%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To uncover and address the hidden, format-dependent authority misallocation in LLMs when fusing conflicting sensor measurements and user claims, which poses reliability risks for real-world deployments where physical sensing should retain priority.

Method: The authors develop a geometric framework of context integration, introduce two audit metrics (Context Integration Ratio CIR and Authority Alignment Index AAI), and propose Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time layer-level intervention that suppresses misplaced user authority.

Result: Models exhibit extreme authority inversion (AAI = -0.805, Cohen's d = -2.14) on numerical tasks; theory-guided causal injection flips 80.2% of incorrect decisions; GAC improves HAR accuracy from 0-1.6% to 21.9-27.5%.

Conclusion: Authority allocation in LLM-mediated systems must be explicitly audited and application-specifically configured, not left implicit, because format-dependent inversion persists regardless of model scale or architecture.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly fuse heterogeneous inputs in ubiquitous systems. Yet, how LLMs implicitly allocate authority when sensor measurements and user claims conflict remains unexamined, raising critical reliability concerns for deployments where physical sensing must retain priority. Unlike explicit traditional fusion, LLMs bury authority allocation within learned representations. We discover this allocation is severely format-dependent: numerical sensor data fails to integrate into answer-relevant model directions, allowing natural-language claims to dominate the final decision, a phenomenon we term \textbf{Authority Inversion}.To diagnose and mitigate this, we develop a geometric framework of context integration, introduce two computable audit metrics, specifically the Context Integration Ratio (CIR) and Authority Alignment Index (AAI), and propose Geometric Authority Calibration (GAC), an inference-time layer-level intervention to suppress misplaced user authority. Evaluating four models (4B to 35B parameters, three architectures) across four datasets totaling 576 conflict instances reveals extreme inversion: on numerical tasks, models exhibit near-zero sensor trust (AAI = -0.805, Cohen's d = -2.14), unaffected by model capacity. Validating our geometric framework, theory-guided causal injection flips 80.2\% of incorrect decisions (vs. <0.4\% for random controls). Practically, GAC improves HAR accuracy from 0 -- 1.6\% to 21.9 -- 27.5\%, outperforming prompting baselines. Ultimately, authority allocation in LLM-mediated systems must be explicitly audited and application-specifically configured rather than left implicit.

</details>


### [373] [DRIVE: Modeling Skills at the Reasoning and Interaction Levels for Web Agents under Continual Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23939)
*Xirui Liu,Sihang Zhou,Yanning Hou,Rong Zhou,Haoyuan Chen,Maolin He,Siwei Wang,Hao Chen,Jian Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DRIVE is a dual-level skill modeling framework for web agents that separates historical experience into reasoning skills (transferable task logic) and interaction skills (executable page operations), using a scene-aware coordination mechanism and skill-level reflection, achieving 52.8% task success rate on WebArena (+7.3% over baseline).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods store experiences uniformly, creating a dilemma where abstract representations lose executability and concrete representations fail to generalize. This entanglement limits capability accumulation on new websites.

Method: DRIVE disentangles experiences into natural language reasoning skills (transferable task logic) and programmatic interaction skills (executable operations), with a scene-aware coordination mechanism for adaptive retrieval and skill-level reflection for targeted library refinement.

Result: Achieves 52.8% average task success rate across five WebArena domains, exceeding skill-free baseline by 7.3 percentage points. Ablations confirm reasoning and interaction skills provide distinct, complementary benefits.

Conclusion: Separating transferable task logic from executable page-level operations is effective; the dual-level skill framework enables better generalization and capability accumulation across diverse web environments.

Abstract: Web agents require both high-level reasoning (for task decomposition) and low-level interactions (for page elements manipulation) to conduct different tasks. However, these knowledge types differ fundamentally: reasoning knowledge (e.g., booking a flight requires first searching for routes) is abstract and transferable across websites, while interaction knowledge (e.g., clicking the Search button at a specific coordinate on Site A) depends heavily on page-specific contexts. Existing methods store experiences uniformly. This creates a dilemma: abstract representations lose executability on concrete pages, while concrete representations fail to generalize across domains. This entanglement limits capability accumulation: on new websites, agents either fail to recognize reusable task logic due to surface-level differences or attempt infeasible actions from outdated page structures. To disentangle them, we propose DRIVE, a dual-level skill modeling framework separating historical experience into natural language reasoning skills, which capture transferable task logic, and programmatic interaction skills, grounding abstract actions to executable operations. A scene-aware coordination mechanism adaptively retrieves and invokes these dual-level skills based on task semantics. DRIVE also uses skill-level reflection to identify hierarchy-specific failure modes, enabling targeted skill library expansion and refinement. Experiments across five WebArena domains show DRIVE attains an average task success rate of 52.8%, exceeding the skill-free baseline by 7.3 percentage points. Further ablations show reasoning and interaction skills provide distinct, complementary benefits, supporting separation of transferable task logic from executable page-level operations.

</details>


### [374] [Residual Drift Dominates Contradiction in Multi-Turn Constraint Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23940)
*Sebastien Kawada*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DRIFT-Bench, a benchmark revealing that multi-turn reasoning systems primarily fail via "satisfiable drift" (silently violating past commitments while staying internally consistent) rather than logical contradiction. MUS-Repair (using minimal unsatisfiable subsets) is the strongest method, but residual errors remain 98-100% satisfiable drift across settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how multi-turn reasoning systems actually fail, challenging the assumption that logical contradiction is the dominant failure mode.

Method: Built DRIFT-Bench (816 test problems across 3 constraint domains), evaluated 4 methods on 4 open-weight models (8B-120B), and introduced MUS-Repair which feeds minimal unsatisfiable subsets back to the generator.

Result: MUS-Repair outperforms baselines by +1.8 to +15.0 pp. However, residual errors are 98-100% satisfiable drift, with contradiction dropping to near zero after structured feedback.

Conclusion: Reliable multi-turn systems need separate validation that the returned answer respects the maintained state, as models forget commitments rather than contradict themselves.

Abstract: How do multi-turn reasoning systems fail? The expected answer is logical contradiction, in which the system's maintained state becomes unsatisfiable. We show that the dominant mode is instead satisfiable drift, where the internal state stays consistent while the returned answer silently violates prior commitments. We build DRIFT-Bench (Decomposing Reasoning Into Failure Types), a solver-instrumented benchmark of 816 test problems across three constraint domains, and evaluate four methods on it across four open-weight models (8B-120B parameters). MUS-Repair, which feeds minimal unsatisfiable subsets back to the generator, is strongest in every setting (+1.8 to +15.0 pp over the best non-MUS baseline). But the central finding is what repair leaves behind. After structured feedback, models rarely contradict themselves. They forget. Residual errors are 98-100% satisfiable drift across all settings, while contradiction drops to near zero. Reliable multi-turn systems must separately validate that the returned answer respects the maintained state. Code is available at https://github.com/kaons-research/drift-bench.

</details>


### [375] [MEMOR-E: In-Context and Fine-Tuned LLM Personalization for Alzheimer's Assistive Robotics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23941)
*Maissa Abir Smaili,Eren Sadikoglu,Ransalu Senanayake*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MEMOR-E is a quadruped robot with tablet interface that uses fine-tuned LLMs and in-context learning to provide personalized, stage-aware cognitive support for Alzheimer's patients through medication reminders, routine guidance, and companionship, with explainable AI for caregiver transparency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Alzheimer's disease progressively impairs memory and language, reducing independence and creating a need for socially assistive robotic support to help patients and caregivers in daily life.

Method: Fine-tuned LLMs on 235 Alzheimer's patient audio transcriptions plus synthetic healthy controls to emulate stage-consistent cognitive behavior; used in-context learning with a second LLM to produce domain/severity-level cognitive error summaries; integrated into a mobile quadruped robot with tablet interface.

Result: MEMOR-E generates stage-aware, non-diagnostic cognitive summaries for personalized assistive interactions; explainable AI translates model outputs into human-readable evidence for caregiver oversight.

Conclusion: LLM-based robotic systems like MEMOR-E can feasibly support personalized Alzheimer's care with transparent, trustworthy human-robot interaction.

Abstract: Alzheimer's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder marked by progressive declines in memory and language that reduce independence in daily life, motivating socially assistive robotic support. This paper presents MEMOR-E, a mobile quadruped robot with an interactive tablet interface that assists patients and caregivers through medication reminders, routine guidance, memory oriented interactions, and companionship. We evaluated the feasibility of fine tuning large language models (LLMs) to emulate stage consistent cognitive behavior and interpret responses across standard neuropsychological language tasks, using audio transcriptions from 235 Alzheimer's patients and synthetically generated healthy controls. We also report findings on using in context learning (ICL) in LLMs, where a second LLM produced domain and severity level cognitive error summaries. Our results show that MEMOR-E can generate stage aware, non diagnostic cognitive summaries that support personalized assistive interactions, while explainable AI mechanisms translate model outputs into transparent, human readable evidence to enable caregiver oversight and trustworthy human robot interaction.

</details>


### [376] [A Dynamical Framework for Cognitive Processes Based on Transformations and Semantic Equivalence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23942)
*Carlo Cattani,Dioneia Motta Monte-Serrat*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a cybernetic framework for modeling cognition using state-space dynamics with update rule X_{t+1} = π(F(f(X_t))), incorporating categorical structure, fixed-point stability analysis, and a linguistic application showing context-dependent interpretation as convergence to stable semantic classes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a unified, mathematically rigorous framework that bridges dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, representing cognition as feedback-driven processes evolving toward stable interpretations.

Method: The model uses an iterative update rule X_{t+1} = π(F(f(X_t))) where f handles internal transformations, F provides interpretative mappings, and π enforces semantic equivalence. Categorical formulation captures compositional structure, and fixed-point/contraction arguments ensure stability.

Result: A computational illustration demonstrates operational character, and a linguistic application shows how context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as a trajectory toward a stable semantic class.

Conclusion: The framework successfully connects dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, offering a unified representation of cognition as a feedback-driven process evolving toward invariant interpretations.

Abstract: This paper proposes a structural and dynamical framework for modeling cognitive processes within a cybernetic perspective. Cognitive states are represented as elements of a state space evolving through an iterative update rule of the form
  \[
  X_{t+1} = π\big(F(f(X_t))\big),
  \]
  where $f$ describes internal transformations, $F$ represents interpretative mappings, and $π$ enforces semantic equivalence.
  The model is interpreted as a feedback system integrating transformation, observation, and stabilization. A categorical formulation is introduced to capture compositional structure, while the associated dynamics are analyzed through fixed-point arguments and contraction conditions ensuring stability.
  To demonstrate the operational character of the framework, a computational illustration is provided, together with a qualitative analysis of the induced dynamics. A concrete linguistic application shows how context-dependent interpretation can be modeled as a trajectory toward a stable semantic class.
  The proposed approach connects dynamical systems, category theory, and cognitive modeling, and provides a unified representation of cognition as a feedback-driven process evolving toward invariant interpretations.

</details>


### [377] [Spacetime Formation under Requirements: Contextual Realization and Form-Dependent Probability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23943)
*Song-Ju Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes that quantum probability arises not from replacing classical probability, but as a fixed-spacetime projection of contextual spacetime formation constrained by finite-state requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To offer a different interpretation of quantum cognition phenomena (order effects, contextuality, violations of law of total probability) by grounding them in requirements like finite representational capacity and semantic stability, rather than simply swapping classical for quantum probability.

Method: The framework starts with abstract requirements (finite-state, single-state semantic stability, context-sensitive intervention, etc.) rather than time, space, or objects. When these cannot be realized in a single global Boolean structure, the projection into fixed spacetime yields noncommutativity and interference. The approach reinterprets contextual bookkeeping cost as a "shadow" of contextual spacetime formation, with interference arising from gluing locally classical realizations.

Result: A transcendental-operational realist account where objecthood, eventhood, probability, and spacetime are treated as forms of realization under requirements, and objectivity is defined by invariants across observer/history-dependent spacetime formations.

Conclusion: Quantum probability is not fundamental to cognition but emerges as a necessary structural consequence when finite-state requirements conflict with global Boolean realizability, projected onto fixed spacetime.

Abstract: Quantum cognition often explains order effects, contextuality, and violations of the law of total probability by replacing classical probability with quantum probability on a fixed event structure. This paper proposes a different interpretation: quantum probability is the fixed-spacetime projection of contextual spacetime formation under finite-state requirements.
  The framework begins not with time, space, objects, or probabilities, but with requirements such as finite representational capacity, single-state semantic stability, context-sensitive intervention, avoidance of explicit context labels, coherent world-formation, and intersubjective transformability. When these requirements cannot be realized within a single global Boolean event structure, the mismatch appears, under fixed-spacetime projection, as noncommutativity, interference, and quantum-like probability.
  Building on prior single-state approaches to contextuality, we reinterpret classical contextual bookkeeping cost as the fixed-spacetime shadow of contextual spacetime formation. Auxiliary memory or context labels in a classical representation correspond, in this account, to holonomy-like mismatch among locally Boolean logic-worlds.
