These sit atop a foundational protocol: a research-program specification language with explicit convergence criteria, a dual-metric scoring engine (LLM-judged rubric plus sandboxed code), an outer meta-optimization loop, event-driven persistence, hook-based middleware, context compaction, and a multi-provider LLM abstraction. Agent identities derive from prime powers, giving collision-free identifiers and trivially-verifiable cluster membership without a central registry. Theoretical guarantees include $O(k)$ verification, $O(V+E)$ DAG validation, and identity collision freedom by the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic. A Graph Isomorphism case study grounds the architectural claims in a generated artifact: a six-step protocol that produced a research paper proposing a new canonical-form algorithm with three theorems and five conjectures.

</details>


### [454] [GRAIL: AI translation for scientists application workflow on satellite data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24784)
*Zhuocheng Shang,Ahmed Eldawy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRAIL is an agentic translation system that converts Python geospatial scripts into scalable Spark programs using LLMs, without requiring scientists to learn new frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Domain scientists write Python scripts for satellite imagery analysis, but these scripts lack scalability to large-scale data. Existing solutions require learning new frameworks, which is a barrier.

Method: GRAIL adapts the RDPro Scala library to be LLM-ready via structured documentation, API aliases, and repair-oriented error logs. It uses a LangGraph pipeline that decomposes code generation into explicit sections with guided inputs/outputs, enabling targeted repair.

Result: GRAIL successfully translates real-world geospatial workflows into Spark-based programs with demonstrated correctness and scalability.

Conclusion: GRAIL enables scalable geospatial analysis without requiring scientists to learn new frameworks, leveraging LLMs and structured translation pipelines effectively.

Abstract: Domain scientists increasingly develop Python scripts to analyze satellite imagery but they lack scalability to large-scale data. This paper demonstrates GRAIL, an agentic translation system that converts Python geospatial workflows into executable Spark-based programs without requiring scientists to learn a new framework. Rather than fine-tuning a specialized LLM model, GRAIL adapts RDPro, a Scala library for satellite data analysis, to make it LLM-ready using structured documentation, API alias functions, and repair-oriented error logs. Translation is structured as a LangGraph pipeline that decomposes code generation into explicit sections with guided inputs and outputs, enabling targeted repair without regenerating the full program. We demonstrate GRAIL on real-world geospatial workflows and showcase the correctness and scalability of the translated code.

</details>


### [455] [PANDO: Efficient Multimodal AI Agents via Online Skill Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24785)
*Yubo Li,Yidi Miao,Haotian Shen,Yuxin Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PANDO is a single-rollout online skill-distillation framework for web agents that achieves 58.3% success rate on VisualWebArena (outperforming SGV and WALT) while using 58-61% fewer tokens, by maintaining a structured Skill Library with progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal web agents rely on increased inference-time computation (rollout search, verifier passes, offline skill discovery, specialist model stacks), raising the question of whether agents can become more efficient with experience rather than more expensive.

Method: PANDO: single-rollout online skill-distillation framework with a structured Skill Library, incorporating progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting.

Result: 58.3% success rate on 910 VisualWebArena tasks (vs SGV 54.0%, WALT 45.2%), using 58% fewer tokens than SGV and 61% fewer tokens than WALT, with no pre-evaluation discovery budget. Ablation shows rules/routines drive success gains; routing/compression/cache-aware prompting reduce token cost.

Conclusion: Web agents can become more efficient with accumulated experience. Three novel efficiency metrics introduced: Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, and Prompt Cache Utilization.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal web agents often rely on increased inference-time computation, including rollout search, verifier passes, offline skill discovery, and specialist model stacks. This raises a central question: can a web agent become more efficient as it accumulates experience, rather than more expensive? We first analyze trajectories from VisualWebArena and identify three recurring sources of inefficiency: repeat-action loops, hidden discovery costs, and low prompt-cache reuse. We then introduce PANDO, a single-rollout online skill-distillation framework that maintains a structured Skill Library and combines progress reflection, confidence-based skill demotion, hierarchical routing, visual compression, and cache-aware prompting. On the full set of 910 VisualWebArena tasks, PANDO achieves a 58.3% success rate, outperforming SGV (54.0%) and our WALT reproduction (45.2%), while using 58% fewer tokens than SGV and 61% fewer tokens than WALT, without any pre-evaluation discovery budget. A 300-task ablation further shows that rules and routines provide most of the success gains, while routing, compression, and cache-aware prompting convert the larger skill library into lower marginal token cost. Finally, we introduce three trajectory-level efficiency metrics -- Action Repetition Rate, Step Overhead Ratio, and Prompt Cache Utilization -- to make efficiency visible beyond terminal success.

</details>


### [456] [CoRe-Code: Collaborative Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24812)
*Zhihao Dou,Qinjian Zhao,Zhongwei Wan,Xiaoyu Xia,Sumon Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoRe-Code is a multi-agent framework that uses a Planner-Coder paradigm with collaboration-aware reinforcement learning (GRPO) to improve code generation accuracy and efficiency through better role specialization and inter-agent coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM code generation methods (autoregressive decoding, CoT, multi-agent systems) lack global planning or suffer from poor role specialization and coordination, leading to locally coherent but globally suboptimal solutions, especially on complex tasks.

Method: CoRe-Code adopts a Planner-Coder paradigm where the Planner generates high-level plans and the Coder executes them. It introduces collaboration-aware reinforcement learning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance role specialization and inter-agent alignment.

Result: Outperforms existing RL-based and multi-agent methods across multiple benchmarks with three base models, showing consistent improvements in accuracy, execution time, and memory usage. Also generalizes to other multi-agent frameworks (e.g., Retrieval and Debugging agents).

Conclusion: CoRe-Code effectively addresses coordination and specialization challenges in multi-agent code generation, demonstrating strong performance, flexibility, and scalability across diverse benchmarks and base models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance in code generation, but most methods rely on autoregressive decoding without global planning, often leading to locally coherent yet globally suboptimal solutions (e.g., failing test cases or inefficient complexity). While recent approaches such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and multi-agent systems (MAS) introduce planning, their limited role specialization and coordination hinder performance on complex tasks. To address the challenges of coordination and specialization in multi-agent code generation, we propose Collaborative Reinforcement Code (CoRe-Code), a framework for role specialized LLM agents that enhances inter-agent coordination to generate more accurate and efficient code. CoRe-Code adopts a simple Planner-Coder paradigm, where the Planner produces high-level plans and the Coder executes them to generate code. We further introduce a collaboration-aware reinforcement learning stage based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enhance role specialization and alignment. Experiments show that CoRe-Code outperforms a wide range of existing RL-based and multi-agent methods. In addition, we demonstrate that CoRe-Code can generalize to other multi-agent frameworks (e.g., Retrieval and Debugging agents), highlighting its flexibility and scalability. We evaluate CoRe-Code on multiple benchmarks of varying difficulty using three base models. Compared to existing baselines, the results show consistent improvements in accuracy, while also achieving higher efficiency in terms of execution time and memory usage, demonstrating the effectiveness and practicality of CoRe-Code.

</details>


### [457] [Agent Manufacturing: Foundation-Model Agents as First-Class Industrial Entities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24823)
*Yilei Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a fifth manufacturing paradigm called "Agent Manufacturing," where foundation-model-based autonomous agents, rather than humans, handle the coordinative cognitive work of production (interpretation, allocation, diagnosis, negotiation, governance), shifting the human role from the cognitive layer to supervision and exception handling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior manufacturing paradigms (mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, Smart Manufacturing) shifted physical and routine-cognitive work to machines, but the coordinative cognition layer (engineers, planners, managers) remained human-centered. The authors identify a new transition where this high-level cognitive work is being redistributed to foundation-model agents.

Method: The authors provide a conceptual and operational definition of Agent Manufacturing: a system where principal coordination is performed by foundation-model agents that can interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, invoke tools/machines, and negotiate with agents and humans. This definition is narrower and more falsifiable than prior concepts like cognitive manufacturing or Industry 5.0.

Result: The paper formally delineates Agent Manufacturing as a distinct paradigm and differentiates it from classical multi-agent manufacturing systems (which were confined to closed protocol spaces). It argues the fifth paradigm is defined by what work is shifted (coordinative cognition) rather than just adding new technology.

Conclusion: A fifth manufacturing paradigm is emerging where foundation-model agents take over the coordinative cognitive work of production, marking a fundamental shift beyond prior paradigms and existing frameworks like Industry 5.0, with implications for the role of human workers moving to supervision and exception handling.

Abstract: Manufacturing has passed through four widely recognized paradigms - mechanization, electrification, programmable automation, and Smart Manufacturing - each defined by the kind of work it shifted from humans to machines. In every case, one layer of industrial work remained fundamentally human: the coordinative cognition of production, comprising the interpretive, allocative, diagnostic, negotiative, and governance work exercised by engineers, planners, and operational managers. We argue that a fifth transition is now underway in which this layer, rather than the physical or routine-cognitive layers below it, is what foundation-model-based autonomous agents primarily redistribute. We name this paradigm Agent Manufacturing and define it operationally: a manufacturing system is an instance of Agent Manufacturing when its principal coordination mechanism is reasoning performed by foundation-model agents that can interpret open-ended goals, plan over long horizons, invoke tools and machines, and negotiate with other agents and humans. This is a narrower and more falsifiable definition than the existing literature on cognitive manufacturing or Industry 5.0 provides, and it distinguishes the paradigm sharply from classical multi-agent manufacturing systems, which were autonomous only within closed protocol spaces.

</details>


### [458] [Test-Time Deep Thinking to Explore Implicit Rules](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24828)
*Wentong Chen,Xin Cong,Zhong Zhang,Yaxi Lu,Siyuan Zhao,Yesai Wu,Qinyu Luo,Haotian Chen,Yankai Lin,Zhiyuan Liu,Maosong Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TTExplore is a framework that trains a 7B thinker model (Exp-Thinker) via a novel RL pipeline to infer implicit rules in text-based embodied tasks, improving agent performance by 14-19 points.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents struggle in environments with implicit rules (hidden constraints), leading to repetitive trial-and-error loops and task failure.

Method: Proposes TTExplore with a thinker-actor architecture. Uses a stable RL pipeline with task-level scores as indirect rewards and single thinking node per trajectory to train Exp-Thinker (7B).

Result: TTExplore with Exp-Thinker improves baseline agent performance by 14-19 points on average across five text-based embodied tasks.

Conclusion: Explicitly reasoning about implicit rules is effective; the proposed stable RL pipeline overcomes training difficulties in deep reasoning trajectories.

Abstract: With the continuous advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs), intelligent agents are becoming increasingly vital. However, these agents often fail in environments governed by implicit rules--hidden constraints that cannot be observed directly and must be inferred through interaction. This causes agents to fall into repetitive trial-and-error loops, ultimately leading to task failure. To address this challenge, we propose Test-Time Exploration (TTExplore), a framework where a thinker component analyzes interaction history to infer these implicit rules and guide an actor. Effective exploration in this setting critically depends on the reasoning ability of the thinker. However, evaluating deep reasoning trajectories is inherently unstable and difficult, which poses a major obstacle to effective training. To overcome this issue, we introduce a novel and stable reinforcement learning pipeline. The core idea is to use accurate task-level scores as indirect rewards to bypass the difficulty of evaluating intermediate reasoning, and to retain only a single thinking node per trajectory to alleviate reward sparsity. Using this pipeline, we train a specialized 7B model, Exp-Thinker. Experiments on five text-based embodied tasks show that TTExplore equipped with Exp-Thinker improves baseline agent performance by an average of $14$-$19$ points, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicitly reasoning about implicit rules.

</details>


### [459] [Geo-Expert: Towards Expert-Level Geological Reasoning via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24844)
*Chenyou Guo,Zongqi Liu,Yizhou Zhang,Zhaorui Jiang,Ze Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs fine-tuned using LoRA on a custom instruction dataset. An 8B domain-aligned model outperforms 70B generalists and GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning, with a 32B variant approaching frontier reasoning models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose LLMs hallucinate on geological tasks (subsurface reasoning, deep-time evolution), while existing AI in Earth sciences focuses on surface remote sensing and GIS, leaving a gap for specialized geological reasoning.

Method: Fine-tuned three base models (Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, Gemma-3-27B) using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) on a custom-curated, high-quality instruction dataset processed via a custom instruction synthesis pipeline. Evaluated on a novel domain-specific benchmark, Geo-Eval.

Result: Domain-aligned 8B model outperforms 70B generalists and GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning; 32B variant approaches frontier reasoning models; optimized 8B offers competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment.

Conclusion: Provides a reproducible recipe for democratizing scientific LLMs and establishes a baseline for geological AI, showing that parameter-efficient fine-tuning can achieve state-of-the-art performance in specialized domains.

Abstract: While general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) applied to Geology often hallucinate when reasoning about subsurface structures and deep-time evolution, current AI in Earth sciences predominantly targets surface remote sensing and GIS. To bridge this gap, we introduce Geo-Expert, a family of parameter-efficient geological LLMs fine-tuned on a custom-curated, high-quality instruction dataset processed using our custom instruction synthesis pipeline. We investigate the impact of model scaling and architecture by fine-tuning three base models: Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-32B, and Gemma-3-27B, with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) method. Our extensive evaluation on a novel domain-specific benchmark, Geo-Eval, reveals that a domain-aligned 8B model can outperform open-weight 70B generalists and proprietary GPT-4o on specialized geological reasoning, while a 32B variant approaches frontier reasoning models. The optimized 8B model further offers a competitive cost-performance ratio for deployment. This work provides a reproducible recipe for democratizing scientific LLMs and establishes a baseline for geological artificial intelligence.

</details>


### [460] [Solving Combinatorial Counting Problems with Weighted First-Order Model Counting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24845)
*Yuanhong Wang,Juhua Pu,Yuxu Zhou,Yuyi Wang,Ondřej Kuželka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cofola is a typed declarative language for combinatorial counting that compiles to weighted first-order model counting via a symmetry-preserving pipeline, enabling practical automated solving of diverse counting problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial counting problems are pervasive but their solutions remain manual. Existing approaches either require brittle closed-form derivations or naive encodings that destroy exchangeability, making counting intractable.

Method: Cofola provides a typed declarative language with primitives for common combinatorial objects (sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, compositions) and constraints. A three-phase compilation pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, symmetry-preserving encoding) maps programs to WFOMC instances with coefficient-extraction constraints, using domain-liftable fragments and lexicographic symmetry breaking.

Result: On a suite of representative problems from textbook math to multi-object scenarios, Cofola produces concise specifications and achieves practical end-to-end solving, outperforming the expressiveness of prior frameworks.

Conclusion: Cofola demonstrates that a domain-specific language with proper symmetry-preserving compilation can make combinatorial counting practical and automated while maintaining expressiveness beyond prior systems.

Abstract: Combinatorial counting problems pervade artificial intelligence, statistics, and discrete mathematics. Whether the task is enumerating subsets, multisets, permutations, partitions, or compositions under structural and arithmetic constraints, solving it remains a stubbornly manual exercise. Closed-form derivations are powerful but brittle, while naive encodings to propositional model counting or constraint satisfaction destroy the exchangeability that makes counting tractable in the first place. We present Cofola (COmbinatorial counting LAnguage with First-Order logic), a typed declarative language whose primitives are the combinatorial objects that recur in everyday counting questions, including sets, bags, tuples, sequences, circles, partitions, and compositions, together with natural relational and arithmetic constraints over them. A denotational semantics maps every Cofola program to a well-defined combinatorial counting problem, and a three-phase compilation pipeline (preprocessing, decomposition, and symmetry-preserving encoding) reduces this problem to a weighted first-order model counting (WFOMC) instance augmented with coefficient-extraction constraints. To stay inside known domain-liftable fragments whenever possible, the encoding groups indistinguishable entities, breaks the symmetry of unordered groupings lexicographically, and encodes sequences and circles via order axioms. On a suite of representative combinatorial counting problems, ranging from textbook math problems to multi-object scenarios that the closest prior framework cannot express, Cofola produces concise specifications and a uniform solving pipeline that is practical end-to-end.

</details>


### [461] [Clustering as Reasoning: A $k$-Means Interpretation of Chain-of-Thought Graph Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24867)
*Xuanting Xie,Zhaochen Guo,Bingheng Li,Xingtong Yu,Zhifei Liao,Zhao Kang,Yuan Fang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KCoT unifies Chain-of-Thought reasoning with graph representation learning by framing clustering (k-means) as an iterative reasoning mechanism, showing mathematical equivalence between Transformer blocks and k-means steps, and achieving SOTA results on text-attributed graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph CoT methods rely on disjoint architectures and fixed graph representations, which limit step-by-step semantic-topological interaction and interpretability.

Method: Proposes KCoT, a unified framework that (1) establishes a formal mathematical correspondence between Transformer blocks and k-means algorithm, (2) introduces a Semantic Discriminating Prompt to structure CoT reasoning as iterative assignment and update steps, and (3) uses a structure-grounded alignment strategy to fuse topological priors with thought-conditioned representations.

Result: Consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods on standard benchmarks, validating clustering as a principled mechanism for CoT-based graph learning.

Conclusion: Clustering (k-means) can serve as a formal and interpretable framework for CoT reasoning on graphs, and the proposed KCoT framework effectively bridges semantic reasoning and topological structure.

Abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting has shown promise in enhancing the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) on text-attributed graphs (TAGs). This work reframes CoT-based graph learning through the principle of clustering as reasoning, offering a $k$-means interpretation of how iterative reasoning operates over graph-structured data. We observe that existing graph CoT methods rely on disjoint architectures and fixed graph representations, limiting step-by-step semantic-topological interaction and interpretability. To overcome this limitation, we propose a unified framework named KCoT that integrates CoT reasoning with graph representation learning. Our key theoretical result reveals a formal mathematical correspondence between a Transformer block and the $k$-means algorithm, allowing reasoning to be interpreted as iterative assignment and update steps. Based on this insight, we introduce a Semantic Discriminating Prompt that explicitly formulates these steps as structured CoT reasoning, together with a structure-grounded alignment strategy to fuse topological priors with evolving thought-conditioned representations. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements over state-of-the-art methods, validating clustering as a principled mechanism for CoT-based graph learning.

</details>


### [462] [Inverting the Shield: Systematically Generating Safety Tests from Policy Specifications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24883)
*Xiaoyue Lu,Xianglin Yang,Haijun Liu,Jiahao Liu,Kuntai Cai,Yan Xiao,Jin Song Dong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: POLARIS is a framework that applies specification-based software testing to AI safety by converting natural-language safety policies into formal First-Order Logic representations and Semantic Policy Graphs, enabling systematic, coverage-driven exploration of policy violation patterns and reproducible safety testing for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM safety evaluation methods (benchmarks and red-teaming) rely heavily on expert knowledge, offer limited systematic guarantees, and are prone to rapid obsolescence.

Method: POLARIS compiles natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations to build a Semantic Policy Graph, then systematically traverses the graph to uncover compositional violation patterns and instantiate them into executable natural-language test queries.

Result: POLARIS achieves higher policy coverage and attack success counts compared to established baselines.

Conclusion: By bridging formal methods and AI safety, POLARIS provides a principled, automated approach to ensuring LLMs adhere to safety-critical policies with verifiable traceability.

Abstract: The widespread integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) necessitates rigorous and systematic safety evaluation. Existing paradigms either rely on constructed benchmarks to assess safety from predefined perspectives, or employ dynamic red-teaming to probe potential vulnerabilities. While effective, these approaches face challenges, as they depend heavily on expert domain knowledge, offer limited systematic guarantees, and are vulnerable to rapid obsolescence. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel framework POLARIS that brings the rigor of specification-based software testing to AI safety. POLARIS first compiles unstructured natural-language policies into First-Order Logic (FOL) representations, establishing a traceable link between high-level rules and concrete test cases. This formalization enables the construction of a Semantic Policy Graph, where complex policy violation scenarios are encoded as traversable paths. By systematically exploring this graph, POLARIS uncovers compositional violation patterns, which are then instantiated into executable natural-language test queries, enabling coverage-driven and reproducible safety testing. Experiments demonstrate that POLARIS achieves higher policy coverage and attack success counts compared to established baselines. Crucially, by bridging formal methods and AI safety, POLARIS provides a principled, automated approach to ensuring LLMs adhere to safety-critical policies with verifiable traceability. We release our code at https://github.com/huac-lxy/POLARIS.

</details>


### [463] [TaBIIC2: Interactive Building of Ontological Taxonomies using Weighted Self-Organizing Maps](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24899)
*Mathieu d'Aquin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an interactive tool for building ontological taxonomies from tabular data using weighted self-organizing maps, offering a middle ground between fully manual and fully automatic approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building ontology taxonomies manually from tabular data is challenging, while purely automatic methods (agglomerative clustering, LLM analysis) leave users with overwhelming results and little control.

Method: The tool uses weighted self-organizing maps (WSOM) for clustering, enabling creation of an arbitrary number of distinct clusters based on value distributions of entity characteristics, plus mechanisms for rapidly creating concepts from tabular data.

Result: The approach provides a middle ground between purely manual analysis and automatic methods for building ontological taxonomies.

Conclusion: By integrating WSOM clustering and interactive concept creation mechanisms, the tool enables progressive and interactive taxonomy construction with user control.

Abstract: Ontologies represent the conceptual knowledge of a domain. At the core of an ontology is the taxonomy of concepts and subconcepts that represent specific entities, which can be complex to build. In many cases, information is available in the form of records describing the characteristics of relevant entities, i.e., tabular data. Identifying patterns and similarities in such data can serve as a basis for identifying concepts and organizing them. However, doing so manually can be challenging, and purely automatic approaches, such as agglomerative clustering or relying on a large language model to analyze the data, can leave the user with overwhelming results and little control. In this paper, we describe a tool that enables the progressive and interactive construction of a taxonomy of concepts by identifying clusters as well as their intentional definitions. To do so, we rely on weighted self-organizing maps as a clustering method because they enable the creation of an arbitrary number of clusters that are distinct with respect to the distributions of values of specific characteristics of the clustered entities. We show that, by integrating this mechanism and others for rapidly creating concepts that group together instances from tabular data, this tool represents a middle ground between purely manual analysis and automatic methods for building ontological taxonomies.

</details>


### [464] [ProActor: Timing-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Proactive Task Scheduling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24900)
*Lei Ding,Bin He,Chenguang Wang,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ProActor is a unified framework for conversational task scheduling that enables proactive agent behaviors through reinforcement learning with domain-agnostic annotation, systematic proactiveness metrics, and GRPO optimization, achieving significant timing improvements with 4-8x training speedups via the ART-F infrastructure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing task-oriented agents are reactive, awaiting explicit instructions rather than proactively anticipating user needs. There are no generalizable end-to-end solutions for measuring and optimizing such anticipatory behaviors.

Method: ProActor integrates: (1) domain-agnostic automated annotation for RL with full opportunity time windows, (2) systematic proactiveness metrics for timing quality and action alignment, (3) GRPO-based RL with RULER-based rewards and stage-aware composite rewards, and (4) ART-F infrastructure with request-adaptive inference clusters and DDP-based training for 4-8x speedups on 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B.

Result: Significant improvements in proactive timing quality on two auto-annotated datasets, while maintaining action consistency comparable to SOTA baselines. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of distinct composite reward variations.

Conclusion: RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics are crucial for improving timing quality, and stage-aware composite rewards effectively balance timing quality and reference action alignment. The ART-F infrastructure enables efficient timing-aware RL training.

Abstract: Proactive task-oriented agents must autonomously anticipate user needs, identify actionable opportunities, and trigger software actions at appropriate moments - fundamentally shifting from reactive systems that await explicit instructions. However, existing approaches lack generalizable end-to-end solutions for measuring and optimizing such anticipatory behaviors.
  This paper introduces ProActor, a unified framework for conversational task scheduling that integrates: (1) a domain-agnostic automated annotation methodology that enables scalable proactiveness reinforcement learning (RL) by generating full opportunity time windows instead of rigid point labels, (2) systematic proactiveness metrics capturing both timing quality and reference action alignment, and (3) RL optimization using GRPO with various reward designs. Our insight is that RULER-based rewards with proactiveness rubrics are crucial for improving timing quality, and that proactiveness optimization enabled by stage-aware composite rewards is key to balancing timing quality and reference action alignment.
  Timing-aware RL requires extensive exploration, demanding efficient infrastructure. We develop ART-F, an adaptive framework combining request-adaptive inference clusters with DDP-based training on single-node multi-GPU systems, enabling LoRA training of 4-bit Qwen2.5-14B-ProActor-Q4 with 4-8x speedups. Experiments on two newly auto-annotated datasets demonstrate significant improvements in proactive timing while maintaining action consistency comparable to state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines. Ablations validate the effectiveness of distinct composite reward variations.

</details>


### [465] [Noise-Robust Financial Numerical Entity Attribute Tagging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24910)
*Hsin-Min Lu,Chen-Yang Lai,Yi-Jhen Li,Ju-Chun Yen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes NORA, a noise-robust tagging model for Financial Numerical Entity (FNE) understanding that handles noisy labels from XBRL filings and jointly predicts multiple FNE attributes including concept name, time relation, measurement scale, and accounting sign, achieving strong performance on a large-scale benchmark of 6.6 million instances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing FNE studies face two key limitations: (1) labels from inline XBRL contain noise due to manual preparation errors, and (2) important FNE attributes beyond concept name (e.g., reporting-time relation, measurement scale, accounting sign) are under-explored.

Method: NORA employs (1) task-aware instance-specific weighting to reduce noisy label influence during training, and (2) Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering for reliable evaluation on noisy test sets.

Result: NORA outperforms SOTA noisy-label baselines (Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, SelfMix) on concept name and time-relation prediction (best Accuracy, Macro F1, Weighted F1), and remains competitive on scale and sign prediction, under both unfiltered and noise-filtered test settings.

Conclusion: Jointly modeling rich FNE attributes while accounting for label noise is valuable for real-world financial filing analysis.

Abstract: Financial Numerical Entity (FNE) understanding aims to recover the meaning of numerical mentions in financial reports. Existing studies primarily focus on concept name prediction and face two important limitations. First, labels derived from inline XBRL may contain errors because filings are usually prepared manually. Second, other important FNE attributes, such as reporting-time relation, measurement scale, and accounting sign, are less emphasized. We propose \textbf{NO}ise-\textbf{R}obust Tagging for Rich Financial Numerical Entity \textbf{A}ttributes (\textsc{NORA}) to address these gaps. NORA uses task-aware instance-specific weighting to attenuate the influence of noisy labels during training, and we further propose the Neighborhood Prior-adjusted KNN (NPK) filtering method for more reliable evaluation on real-world noisy test sets. In addition, we construct a large-scale benchmark containing 6.6 million instances with multi-attribute labels and filing metadata. Experiments show that \textsc{NORA} performs strongly compared with state-of-the-art noisy-label baselines, including Co-teaching, Mixup, SSR, and SelfMix. Moreover, NORA is robust under both unfiltered and noise-filtered test settings. It achieves the best Accuracy, Macro F1, and Weighted F1 for concept name and time-relation prediction, while remaining competitive on scale and sign prediction. These results demonstrate the value of jointly modeling rich FNE attributes while accounting for label noise in real-world financial filings.

</details>


### [466] [Energy Shields for Fairness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24926)
*Filip Cano,Thomas A. Henzinger,Konstantin Kueffner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces energy shields, a novel probabilistic approach to enforce runtime fairness in sequential decision-making, providing both short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees through physics-inspired energy functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional fairness shields enforce runtime fairness abruptly through deterministic interventions, neglecting that fairness is a dynamic property evaluated over sequences of decisions. There is a need for smoother, adaptive fairness enforcement.

Method: Energy shields are lightweight, adaptive controllers that monitor decision sequences and probabilistically intervene using physics-inspired energy functions—the more unfair the decisions, the stronger the nudging force. A synthesis procedure constructs the least intrusive shield for given target specifications.

Result: Energy shields are the first fairness shields to provide both short-term safety (running fairness stays within target interval with high probability) and long-term liveness guarantees (limit of fairness measure lies within limit target interval). Experiments demonstrate efficiency against existing shields.

Conclusion: Energy shields offer a smoother, adaptable alternative to deterministic fairness enforcement, balancing short-term constraints with long-term fairness goals through probabilistic, physics-inspired mechanisms.

Abstract: Runtime fairness is not a one-time constraint but a dynamic property evaluated over a sequence of decisions.
  To ensure fairness at runtime, it is necessary to account for past decisions, information neglected by conventional, static classifiers.
  Traditional fairness shields enforce runtime fairness abruptly,
  by intervening \emph{deterministically} whenever a sequence of decisions violates the target for a running fairness measure. This motivates our \emph{main conceptual contribution: \textbf{energy shields}.}
  An energy shield is a novel, lightweight, adaptive controller that monitors a sequence of decisions and intervenes \emph{probabilistically} to ensure runtime fairness smoothly, by utilizing physics-inspired energy functions to nudge the sequence toward fairness:
  the more unfair the decisions, the stronger the nudging force becomes. This makes energy shields the \emph{\textbf{first}} fairness shields to provide both \emph{short-term safety and long-term liveness guarantees}.
  Safety ensures that the running fairness measure stays within a running target interval with high probability,
  and liveness ensures that the limit of the fairness measure lies within the limit target interval.
  Intuitively, the short-term specifies the tolerated fairness values and the long-term specifies the desired fairness values.
  We also provide a synthesis procedure for constructing the least intrusive energy shield for a given target specification, and demonstrate its efficiency experimentally.
  We evaluate our energy shields against existing fairness shields through the lens of short- and long-term fairness.

</details>


### [467] [Towards Multi-Turn Dialog Systems for Industrial Asset Operations and Maintenance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24953)
*Chengrui Li,Rujing Li,Yitong Bai,Rui Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture for industrial multi-turn QA that uses structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution, achieving 54.5% better planning and 37.8% better task completion while reducing tool-time share from 47.3% to 26.3%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional plan-execute single-agent architectures struggle with maintaining cross-turn context and reusing intermediate results in the inherently multi-turn, iterative, and tool-dependent nature of industrial operations and maintenance QA.

Method: A supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture with structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution to alleviate tool invocation bottlenecks in multi-turn dialog.

Result: Planning effectiveness improved by 54.5%, task completion by 37.8%; tool-time share reduced from 47.3% to 26.3%; turns 2-5 approximately 4.2x faster than the first turn.

Conclusion: The proposed multi-agent architecture with artifact reuse effectively addresses cross-turn context and tool invocation inefficiency issues in industrial multi-turn dialog systems.

Abstract: Industrial asset operations and maintenance question answering is inherently multi-turn, iterative, and highly dependent on external tool invocation. However, the conventional plan-execute single-agent architecture exhibits clear limitations in maintaining cross-turn context, and reusing intermediate results. In this paper, we present a multi-turn dialog system designed for industrial scenarios based on a supervisor-specialist multi-agent architecture. To alleviate tool invocation bottlenecks, the system incorporates structured artifact reuse, dynamic replanning, and parallel tool execution. Evaluation results show that our system achieves better response quality compared with the baseline, with planning effectiveness increasing by 54.5% and task completion improving by 37.8%. System profiling further shows that cross-turn artifact reuse effectively reduces redundant tool invocation, decreasing the tool-time share from 47.3% to 26.3% and making turns 2-5 approximately 4.2x faster than the first turn.

</details>


### [468] [Mitigating Object Hallucinations in Vision-Language Models through Region-Aware Attention Recalibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24957)
*Yuanzhi Xu,Qian Gao,Jun Fan,Guohui Ding,Zhenyu Yang,Sixue Lin,Yuteng Xiao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A training-free, region-aware adaptive weighting mechanism that dynamically corrects object hallucination in LVLMs by computing outlier-resistant statistical midpoints across attention heads and applying continuous penalty modulation based on inter-head disagreement, achieving state-of-the-art performance on CHAIR, POPE, and MME benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object hallucination (factually incorrect object generation) remains a persistent challenge in LVLMs. Existing solutions (data-driven fine-tuning, contrastive decoding, attention head truncation) often compromise computational efficiency or feature space continuity.

Method: A training-free inference strategy: (1) computes an outlier-resistant statistical midpoint across attention heads as a stable visual anchor; (2) uses inter-head disagreement mapped across regions to dynamically determine intervention budgets; (3) applies continuous penalty modulation to suppress hallucination-inducing attention paths without heuristic truncation.

Result: Substantially reduces both instance- and sentence-level hallucinations. Achieves state-of-the-art performance against contemporary baselines on CHAIR, POPE, and MME benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed method efficiently corrects visual-semantic misalignments while preserving generative fluency and language priors, demonstrating algorithmic robustness without requiring training or expensive computation.

Abstract: The generation of factually incorrect objects, commonly known as object hallucination, remains a persistent challenge in Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs). Current approaches to address this issue - ranging from expensive data-driven fine-tuning and high-latency contrastive decoding to rigid attention head truncation - frequently compromise either computational efficiency or the continuity of the model's feature space. To overcome these limitations, we introduce a novel, training-free inference strategy that operates as a region-aware adaptive weighting mechanism to dynamically correct semantic drift without relying on abrupt heuristic truncations. By computing an outlier-resistant statistical midpoint across various attention heads, we establish a stable anchor for reliable visual representations. We then utilize the inter-head disagreement mapped across regions to dynamically determine intervention budgets, gently suppressing hallucination-inducing attention paths through a continuous penalty modulation. This recalibration process effectively rectifies visual-semantic misalignments while fully preserving generative fluency and language priors. Comprehensive evaluations on standard multimodal benchmarks, including CHAIR, POPE, and MME, reveal that our strategy substantially curtails both instance- and sentence-level hallucinations. The results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance against contemporary baselines, confirming our method's efficiency and algorithmic robustness. Our code will be public.

</details>


### [469] [NeurIPS: Neuro-anatomical Inductive Priors for Sphere-based Brain Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24993)
*Sijin Yu,Zijiao Chen,Zhenyu Yang,Zihao Tan,Jiakun Xu,Zhongliang Liu,Shengxian Chen,Wenxuan Wu,Xiangmin Xu,Xin Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeurIPS improves fMRI surface-based decoding by using anatomy as a predictive signal, achieving state-of-the-art performance with dramatically faster convergence (10 vs. 600 epochs).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current fMRI decoders suffer from a performance-fidelity trade-off where efficient 1D encoders outperform geometrically faithful surface models. This is partly due to inefficient surface tokenization and failing to use anatomy as a predictive signal.

Method: NeurIPS combines two innovations: 1) Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) for efficient geometric encoding, and 2) Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that models individual anatomy using cortical features.

Result: New state-of-the-art for surface decoders on NSD, performance comparable to strong 1D baselines, convergence in 10 vs. 600 epochs, rapid adaptation with 20% data, robust scalability with larger cohorts.

Conclusion: By leveraging anatomical priors, NeurIPS provides a principled and scalable path toward robust, generalizable brain decoding.

Abstract: Current fMRI decoders face a performance-fidelity trade-off where efficient ID encoders outperform geometrically faithful surface-based models. We argue this is partly driven by inefficient surface tokenization and the failure to use anatomy as a predictive signal. We present NeurIPS, a framework that improves surface-based decoding by reframing anatomical variation from a nuisance to a powerful inductive prior. NeurIPS unites two innovations: a Selective ROI Spherical Tokenizer (SRST) for efficient geometric encoding, and a Structure-Guided Mixture of Experts (SG-MoE) that explicitly models individual anatomy using cortical features. On the Natural Scenes Dataset, NeurIPS establishes a new state-of-the-art for surface decoders and achieves performance comparable to strong 1D baselines. This is achieved with unprecedented efficiency, as the model converges dramatically faster (10 vs. 600 epochs). This efficiency enables rapid adaptation to new subjects using only 20% of data and ensures robust scalability as the training cohort is expanded. Ablations provide causal evidence that these gains are driven by the model's use of cortical features, not by memorizing subject IDs. By leveraging anatomical priors, NeurIPS provides a principled and scalable path toward robust, generalizable brain decoding.

</details>


### [470] [Privacy-Preserving Local Language Models for Longitudinal Data Retrieval in Chronic Dermatologic Disease: Implementation in Pemphigus Patients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25020)
*Abdurrahim Yilmaz,Ayşe Esra Koku Aksu,Duygu Yamen,Vefa Asli Erdemir,Mehmet Salih Gurel,Gulsum Gencoglan,Joram M. Posma,Burak Temelkuran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A locally deployed, privacy-preserving small language model (Qwen3 4B) achieved 82.25% accuracy in retrieving 56 clinical features from 541 pemphigus visit notes and generated high-quality longitudinal summaries rated favorably by dermatologists (quality: 8.23-8.47, accuracy: 7.93-8.20, usefulness: 8.47-8.50), with AI summaries preferred in 53.3% of evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chronic dermatologic diseases like pemphigus generate extensive longitudinal documentation that is difficult to review comprehensively, increasing clinician workload and risk of missing critical historical information.

Method: Retrospective case series using 30 pemphigus patients (541 visit notes, 89,336 words) with 56 clinically relevant features annotated by two expert dermatologists. A locally deployed SLM (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) was queried to retrieve features and generate summary reports.

Result: Mean accuracy of 82.25% across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks. Dermatologists rated AI summaries highly (quality: 8.23-8.47, clinical accuracy: 7.93-8.20, usefulness: 8.47-8.50). No significant inter-evaluator differences; AI summaries preferred in 53.3% of evaluations.

Conclusion: Privacy-preserving, locally deployed SLMs can outperform medical experts and reliably generate clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries, potentially supporting clinical decision-making with appropriate oversight.

Abstract: Chronic dermatologic diseases such as pemphigus require long-term follow-up, generating extensive longitudinal clinical documentation that is difficult to review comprehensively during routine visits and increasing clinician workload as well as the risk of missing critical historical information. We evaluated whether a locally deployed, privacy-preserving small language model (SLM) could retrieve structured clinical features and generate longitudinal summaries from long-term dermatology follow-up records. In this retrospective case series, thirty pemphigus patients contributed 541 visit notes that were aggregated into full longitudinal records (89,336 words); 56 clinically relevant features were annotated by two expert dermatologists. The locally deployed SLM (Qwen3 4B Thinking 2507) was queried with each complete record to retrieve 56 features and generate one final report summaries. Across 1,680 feature retrieval tasks, mean accuracy was 82.25%. Dermatologists' ratings of AI-generated summaries were high for overall quality (8.23-8.47), clinical accuracy (7.93-8.20), and usefulness (8.47-8.50), with no significant inter-evaluator differences and an overall preference for AI summaries in 53.3% of evaluations. These findings suggest that privacy-preserving, locally deployed SLMs can outperform medical experts and reliably generate clinically meaningful longitudinal summaries. SLMs may support clinical decision-making when integrated with appropriate oversight.

</details>


### [471] [AION: Next-Generation Tasks and Practical Harness for Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25045)
*Tianxiang Zhan,Xiaobao Song,Tong Guan,Shirui Pan,Ming Jin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes AION, a time series harness that shifts time series research from fixed forecasting benchmarks to realistic tasks with temporal grounding, knowledge-grounded reasoning, and reliability mechanisms. A case study shows AION produces more detailed traces, artifacts, and review steps than baseline agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current time series benchmarks use clean data and short evaluation loops, lacking temporal constraints, evidence checks, and review mechanisms needed for real-world decision support tasks.

Method: The authors formalize next-generation time series tasks as three-component tuples (task file, workspace, validation interface) and build AION with six component groups: agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols, guided by three design principles.

Result: A Kaggle Store Sales case study demonstrates that AION produces more detailed process traces, more artifacts, and more review steps compared to the same base agent operating in OpenCode direct build mode.

Conclusion: The findings advocate for a paradigm shift from fixed time series tasks to realistic ones that incorporate real-world constraints.

Abstract: Time series research is moving beyond fixed forecasting benchmarks toward realistic tasks that combine prediction, contextual reasoning, tool use, and structured decision support. Most benchmarks are built around clean data and short evaluation loops; agents alone may miss temporal constraints, evidence checks, or review before finalizing outputs. We first formalize next-generation time series tasks as three-component tuples consisting of a task file, a workspace, and a validation interface. We then present AION, a time series harness built from six component groups: agents, skills, rules, memory, evaluation, and protocols. In this harness, we use three design principles: temporal grounding, temporal knowledge-grounded reasoning, and reliability mechanisms such as post-experiment analysis and layered review. A Kaggle Store Sales case study shows that the harness produces more detailed process traces, more artifacts, and more review steps than the same base agent operating in OpenCode direct build mode. Taken together, these results argue for a paradigm shift from fixed tasks to realistic ones under real-world constraints.

</details>


### [472] [Evolutionary Enhanced Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Cooperative Air Combat](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25091)
*Chengwei Li,Junlin Liu,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ACE-MAPPO integrates evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO for multi-UCAV BVR cooperative air combat, outperforming baselines in training stability, convergence, and win rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MARL methods suffer from insufficient exploration efficiency, low sample utilization, and poor policy generalization in high-dimensional, discrete-action, adversarial BVR air combat scenarios.

Method: ACE-MAPPO combines MAPPO with: (1) a genetic soft update mechanism for population diversity, (2) evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay for sparse high-value samples, and (3) adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning for adaptive difficulty scaling.

Result: ACE-MAPPO outperforms MAPPO and other baselines in training stability, convergence speed, and win rate in multi-aircraft cooperative air combat experiments.

Conclusion: The hybrid evolutionary-MARL framework (ACE-MAPPO) effectively addresses key limitations of prior methods and proves effective for multi-UCAV BVR cooperative air combat.

Abstract: As modern air combat evolves toward beyond-visual-range (BVR) multi-aircraft cooperative engagements, autonomous decision-making for unmanned combat aerial vehicles (UCAVs) faces significant challenges due to high-dimensional state spaces, discrete action commands, and strongly adversarial dynamic environments. To overcome the limitations of existing multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) methods in such settings, namely insufficient exploration efficiency, low sample utilization, and poor policy generalization, we propose Adversarial Curriculum and Evolutionary-enhanced Multi-agent Proximal Policy Optimization (ACE-MAPPO), a hybrid learning framework that integrates evolutionary algorithms with MAPPO. Specifically, a genetic soft update mechanism is introduced to enhance population diversity and mitigate convergence to local optima. An evolutionary-augmented prioritized trajectory replay strategy is further employed to improve the utilization of sparse high-value samples. In addition, an adversarial evolutionary curriculum learning mechanism is designed to enable adaptive training with progressively increasing difficulty. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms MAPPO and other baseline algorithms in terms of training stability, convergence speed, and win rate, validating its effectiveness in multi-aircraft cooperative air combat scenarios.

</details>


### [473] [RECTOR: Priority-Aware Rule-Based Reranking for Compliance-Aware Autonomous Driving Trajectory Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25095)
*Hadi Hajieghrary,Benedikt Walter,Chaitanya Shinde,Paul Schmitt,Miguel Hurtado*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RECTOR is a post-generation reranking layer that selects trajectories using a tiered rulebook (Safety > Legal > Road > Comfort) with ε-lexicographic ordering, reducing violations by ~8% on Waymo Open Motion Dataset without retraining the predictor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous driving stacks select one trajectory from a multi-modal candidate set; using model confidence ignores safety, traffic-law, and comfort constraints.

Method: RECTOR scores candidates against a tiered rulebook (Safety > Legal > Road > Comfort) via differentiable proxies and scene-conditioned applicability, then selects with a deterministic ε-lexicographic rule that preserves cross-tier priority.

Result: On Waymo Open Motion Dataset validation_interactive (43,219 instances, K=6), rule-aware selection cuts Safety+Legal violations from 28.58% to 20.42% and Total from 40.32% to 32.41% vs confidence-only. Under adversarial confidence corruption, confidence-only fails 100% while rule-aware selectors reject injected mode ~96%.

Conclusion: The lexicographic guarantee is a structural differentiator no weight calibration can replicate; rule-aware ranking provides empirical lift, while the tiered priority ensures robust constraint adherence without retraining.

Abstract: Autonomous driving stacks must pick one trajectory from a multi-modal candidate set; choosing by model confidence ignores safety, traffic-law, and comfort constraints. We present \textsc{RECTOR} (Rule-Enforced Constrained Trajectory Orchestrator), a post-generation reranking layer that scores candidates against a tiered rulebook (Safety~$\succ$~Legal~$\succ$~Road~$\succ$~Comfort) via differentiable proxies and a scene-conditioned applicability mechanism, then selects with a deterministic $\varepsilon$-lexicographic rule that preserves cross-tier priority by construction -- without retraining the predictor.
  On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset \texttt{validation\_interactive} split (43{,}219 augmented instances, $K{=}6$), under Protocol~B (28-rule proxy catalog, oracle applicability) rule-aware selection cuts Safety+Legal violations from 28.58\% to 20.42\% and Total from 40.32\% to 32.41\% versus confidence-only on the same candidates. A uniform-weight weighted-sum baseline matches binary compliance on this benchmark -- the empirical lift comes from rule-aware ranking, while the lexicographic guarantee is the structural differentiator no weight calibration can replicate. Under adversarial confidence corruption, confidence-only selection fails in 100\% of scenarios while both rule-aware selectors reject the injected mode in $\sim$96\%. All figures are proxy-evaluator results (not a safety certificate), open-loop, 5\,s horizon, U.S.\ rules, validation split.

</details>


### [474] [Trust but Verify: Prover-Verifier Deliberation for Selective LLM Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25133)
*João Sedoc,Baotong Zhang,Dean Foster*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Prover-Verifier Deliberation (PVD) is an inference-time protocol using interactive proof theory where a prover defends answers via checkable sub-claims and a verifier issues targeted challenges. On GPQA Diamond, PVD's Accept+No Change (ANC) subset yields ~30pp HC-Prec gap over non-ANC, with robustness across model families but failure modes when the verifier operates outside its effective region.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliably determining when a language model is correct is almost as important as the correctness itself. The paper aims to build a selective prediction mechanism that can report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases.

Method: PVD is an inference-time protocol with two roles: a prover defends a candidate answer through checkable sub-claims, while a verifier issues targeted challenges and returns Accept, Challenge, or Reject. The Accept + No Change (ANC) subset serves as the high-confidence verdict. The protocol is empirically characterized via coverage-precision behavior since formal guarantees don't transfer to frozen LLMs.

Result: On GPQA Diamond with Claude Sonnet 4.6 (prover) and Claude Haiku 4.5 (verifier), ANC yields ~30pp HC-Prec gap over non-ANC. Robustness experiments with GPT and Gemini show high HC-Prec transfers across model families. Verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the selection gap size. On Humanity's Last Exam, weaker pairings can collapse or invert the ANC signal.

Conclusion: PVD supplies a distinct argument-defensibility signal for selective prediction, different from self-consistency, universal self-consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion. The protocol works best when the verifier operates within its effective region, and failure modes emerge otherwise.

Abstract: Reliably knowing when a language model is correct is almost as important as being correct. We introduce prover-verifier deliberation (PVD), an inference-time protocol grounded in interactive proof theory, as a mechanism for selective prediction: the protocol produces both an answer and a structured confidence verdict, allowing a system to report high-confidence answers while abstaining on uncertain cases. In each dialogue, a prover defends a candidate answer through checkable sub-claims while a verifier issues targeted challenges and returns \textsc{Accept}, \textsc{Challenge}, or \textsc{Reject}. Because frozen language models are imperfect provers and verifiers operating over a noisy channel, formal soundness and completeness guarantees do not transfer; instead, we characterize the protocol empirically through its coverage-precision behavior. Our main experiment uses Claude Sonnet 4.6 as prover and Claude Haiku 4.5 as verifier on GPQA Diamond. Questions accepted with no answer revision, which we call Accept + No Change (ANC), are reported as the high-confidence subset; we evaluate this subset by its precision and coverage. ANC separates reliable from unreliable answers, yielding a $\sim$30pp HC-Prec gap over the non-ANC complement. Robustness experiments with GPT and Gemini pairings show that high HC-Prec can transfer across model families, while verifier strictness and domain competence largely determine the size of the selection gap. On Humanity's Last Exam, weaker prover-verifier pairings can collapse or invert the ANC signal, illustrating a practical failure mode when the verifier operates outside its effective region. Comparisons with self-consistency, universal self-consistency, multi-agent debate, and Reflexion suggest that prover-verifier deliberation supplies a distinct argument-defensibility signal for selective prediction.

</details>


### [475] [Beyond the Frontier: Stochastic Backtracking for Efficient Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25143)
*Dao Tran,Duc Anh Le,Ngoc Luu,Quan Pham,Tung Pham,Hung Bui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces stochastic backtracking over a persistent pool of historical prefixes to improve test-time scaling for language model reasoning, achieving higher accuracy per token count compared to frontier-only PRM-guided methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PRM-guided test-time scaling methods are frontier-only, keeping only current active prefixes and irreversibly pruning others based on noisy PRM scores, leading to premature commitment, diversity collapse, and loss of potentially correct continuations.

Method: Two mechanisms: (1) Subpool Selection - Top-N selection within random subpools to let historical prefixes bypass over-scored frontier candidates; (2) Power Backtrack Sequential Monte Carlo - extends SMC-style resampling to a persistent pool using powered PRM scores and mixture-corrected weights.

Result: Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales, the methods consistently achieve higher accuracy per token count, and the same accuracy using only a fraction of the tokens compared to strong PRM-guided baselines.

Conclusion: Persistent-pool stochastic backtracking provides a simple and effective way to improve the accuracy-token trade-off in test-time scaling.

Abstract: Test-time scaling improves language model reasoning by spending additional compute to explore multiple solution trajectories. The key challenge is to maximize accuracy while minimizing the total number of generated tokens during reasoning. Recent PRM-guided methods score intermediate prefixes to steer this search, but most are frontier-only: they keep only the current active prefixes and irreversibly prune or resample away the rest using noisy PRM scores. This can cause premature commitment, diversity collapse, and the loss of prefixes that still admit correct continuations. We introduce stochastic backtracking over a persistent pool of historical prefixes, allowing test-time compute to revisit previously generated states instead of only expanding the current frontier. To make this efficient, we propose two complementary mechanisms. Subpool Selection strengthens greedy PRM-guided search by applying Top-N selection within random subpools, giving historical prefixes a chance to bypass over-scored frontier candidates. Power Backtrack Sequential Monte Carlo extends SMC-style resampling to the persistent pool using powered PRM scores and mixture-corrected weights. Across mathematical reasoning benchmarks and model scales, our methods consistently achieve higher accuracy per token count, and the same level of accuracy using only a fraction of the token count in comparison to strong PRM-guided baselines, demonstrating that persistent-pool stochastic backtracking provides a simple and effective way to improve the accuracy-token trade-off in test-time scaling.

</details>


### [476] [Representation Without Control: Testing the Realization Effect in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25151)
*Ciarán Walsh,Emilio Barkett*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs show prompt sensitivity to the realization effect in behavioral economics, but internal representations of realization status do not causally influence risk-taking behavior, revealing a disconnect between latent encoding and actual decision-making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether LLMs' behavioral outputs reflect human-like cognitive mechanisms or merely prompt-sensitive surface patterns, using the well-characterized realization effect from behavioral economics as a testbed.

Method: Evaluates LLM behavior at three levels: (1) prompt-only behavioral sensitivity, (2) linear readout of internal representations from Gemma's residual stream, and (3) causal control via activation steering along the decoded direction.

Result: Prompt-only results show systematic condition sensitivity but fail to replicate human realization-effect patterns. Layer 18 of Gemma's residual stream contains a linearly decodable realization-status signal that generalizes to held-out prompts. However, steering along this direction does not reliably shift downstream risk choices.

Conclusion: Behavioral sensitivity, latent readout, and causal control are three distinct properties that do not automatically co-occur. Successful latent readout is insufficient evidence that a model behaviorally relies on that representation during downstream decision-making.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as behavioral simulators, but it remains unclear when their outputs reflect human-like cognitive mechanisms rather than prompt-sensitive surface patterns. We study this question through the realization effect, a well-characterized finding in behavioral economics in which risk-taking differs systematically after paper versus realized gains and losses. We evaluate LLM behavior at three levels: prompt-only behavioral sensitivity, linear readout of internal representations, and causal control via activation steering. Prompt-only results show systematic condition sensitivity, but the directional pattern does not reproduce human realization-effect predictions. Gemma's residual stream contains a linearly decodable realization-status signal at layer 18 that generalizes to held-out prompts. Steering along this direction does not, however, reliably shift downstream risk choices, a null result that holds across positive scales and in a negative sign-symmetry run. Behavioral sensitivity, latent readout, and causal control are three distinct properties that do not automatically co-occur, and successful latent readout is insufficient evidence that a model behaviorally relies on a representation during downstream decision-making.

</details>


### [477] [SimuWoB: Simulating Real-World Mobile Apps for Fast and Faithful GUI Agent Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25160)
*Guohong Liu,Jialei Ye,Pengzhi Gao,Wei Liu,Jian Luan,Yunxin Liu,Yuanchun Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimuWoB is a fully synthetic benchmark for mobile GUI agents with 120 challenging tasks, using a virtual environment generation framework that provides automatic rewards. Experiments show current agents achieve only 27.92% average success rate (17.82% on long-horizon tasks), revealing major weaknesses in complex scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobile GUI agent benchmarks prioritize reproducibility but are limited to open-source apps or file-operation tasks, creating a gap between benchmark settings and real-world usage. They also lack coverage of complex, long-horizon interactions.

Method: SimuWoB provides 120 challenging tasks with a robust virtual environment generation framework that synthesizes high-fidelity tasks and environments, automatically provides valid rewards, and deploys each environment as a backend-free webpage accessible via URL.

Result: Average success rate is 27.92%, dropping to 17.82% on long-horizon tasks. Evaluations on synthetic environments generalize well compared to real-world sample tasks.

Conclusion: Current mobile GUI agents show substantial weaknesses in complex, long-horizon scenarios. SimuWoB provides diagnostic insights across key capability dimensions, with implications for future mobile GUI agent development.

Abstract: Mobile GUI agents powered by large language models have progressed rapidly, creating urgent needs for realistic and comprehensive evaluation. Existing benchmarks prioritize reproducibility but are often limited to open-source apps or file-operation tasks for the difficulty of constructing rewards on real applications, leaving a gap between benchmark settings and real-world usage. Moreover, most benchmarks focus on basic grounding and navigation, with limited coverage of complex, long-horizon interactions. To address these limitations, we introduce SimuWoB, a fully synthetic benchmark for mobile GUI agents with 120 challenging tasks spanning diverse types and difficulty levels. We build a robust virtual environment generation framework that synthesizes high-fidelity tasks and environments, and automatically provides valid rewards for each task. Each environment is deployed as a backend-free webpage accessible via URL, enabling efficient and reproducible evaluation. We conduct comprehensive experiments on several state-of-the-art mobile GUI agents. The average success rate is only 27.92%, dropping to 17.82% on long-horizon tasks, which reveals substantial weaknesses in current agents under complex scenarios. Evaluation result comparison with real-world sample tasks demonstrate that agent assessments based on our synthetic environment generalize well. We further provide diagnostic insights across key capability dimensions and discuss implications for future mobile GUI agent development.

</details>


### [478] [SpecAlign: A Semantic Alignment Framework for SystemVerilog Assertion Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25181)
*Jaime Rafael Imperial,Hao Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SpecAlign is a framework that evaluates and improves semantic alignment between LLM-generated SystemVerilog Assertions and natural language specifications using entailment-based classification, chain-of-thought reasoning, and self-consistency voting, without requiring golden RTL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based SVA generation focuses on syntactic validity and formal verification, but cannot quantify semantic alignment with natural language specifications, leading to hallucinated or misaligned assertions that reduce confidence and increase debugging effort when golden RTL is unavailable.

Method: Introduces two iterative alignment loops using entailment-based classification to assess natural language properties and SVAs against design specifications. Uses chain-of-thought prompting to generate multiple reasoning paths, aggregated via self-consistency voting. Misaligned assertions trigger actionable feedback for refinement. A quantitative alignment score measures semantic consistency across iterations.

Result: Experimental results show SpecAlign effectively detects semantic inconsistencies and improves assertion alignment without relying on golden RTL, serving as a scalable complement to traditional formal verification metrics.

Conclusion: SpecAlign provides a practical solution for semantic evaluation and refinement of LLM-generated SVAs, addressing the gap between syntactic correctness and semantic fidelity to natural language specifications.

Abstract: Existing Large Language Model (LLM) approaches to SystemVerilog Assertion (SVA) generation primarily focus on syntactic validity and formal verification outcomes, while semantic alignment between generated assertions and natural language specifications remains difficult to quantify. As a result, hallucinated or misaligned SVAs can reduce confidence and increase debugging efforts in the absence of golden RTL. This paper presents SpecAlign, a framework for semantic evaluation and refinement of LLM-generated SVAs. SpecAlign introduces two iterative alignment loops that assess both natural language properties and SVAs against the design specification using entailment-based classification. We improve alignment decisions by generating multiple reasoning paths using chain-of-thought prompting and aggregating them via a self-consistency voting mechanism. Misaligned assertions are analyzed to generate actionable feedback for refinement. We further define a quantitative alignment score to measure semantic consistency across iterations. Experimental results demonstrate that SpecAlign effectively detects semantic inconsistencies and improves assertion alignment without relying on golden RTL, providing a scalable complement to traditional formal verification evaluation metrics.

</details>


### [479] [DarkForest: Less Talk, Higher Accuracy for Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25188)
*Yi Li,Songtao Wei,Dongming Jiang,Zhichun Guo,Qiannan Li,Bingzhe Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DarkForest is a controlled-communication coordination framework for multi-agent LLM systems that keeps agents independent, structures responses into clusters, estimates calibrated belief distributions, and significantly reduces token consumption while improving reasoning quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interaction-heavy multi-agent LLM methods suffer from error propagation (incorrect reasoning gets amplified) and high communication overhead (token consumption, latency, inference cost).

Method: (1) Keep agents independent to produce answers without seeing others' outputs. (2) Parse raw responses into structured candidate records. (3) Group semantically equivalent candidates into clusters. (4) Estimate a calibrated belief distribution over clusters using multiple factors (agent reliability, confidence, parse quality, support-pattern reliability, independence corrections). (5) Coordinator receives only policy-permitted evidence from this belief state with controlled communication.

Result: Achieves leading overall quality on six reasoning benchmarks, improves strongest baseline by up to 30.7%, and reduces token consumption by up to 6.5× compared to communication-heavy baselines.

Conclusion: Controlled communication with structured belief estimation can effectively mitigate error propagation and reduce overhead while maintaining or improving reasoning quality in multi-agent LLM systems.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems improve reasoning by combining outputs from multiple agents, but interaction-heavy methods can introduce error propagation and high communication overhead. When agents exchange raw responses or reasoning traces, incorrect intermediate reasoning may be adopted and amplified, leading to confident but wrong consensus; multi-round communication also increases token consumption, latency, and inference cost. In this paper, we propose a controlled-communication coordination framework named DarkForest. DarkForest first keeps agents independent, so each agent produces an answer without seeing the others' outputs. It then parses the raw responses into structured candidate records, groups semantically equivalent candidates into clusters, and estimates a calibrated belief distribution over these clusters using agent reliability, confidence, parse quality, support-pattern reliability, and independence corrections. A coordinator receives only policy-permitted evidence from this belief state with controlled communication. Experiments on six reasoning benchmarks show that DarkForest achieves leading overall quality, improves the strongest baseline by up to 30.7\% on benchmark metrics, and reduces token consumption by up to $6.5\times$ compared with communication-heavy baselines.

</details>


### [480] [Boosting Inference with Guided Reasoning: Stochastic Exploration for Recursive Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25230)
*Andrew Corbett,Archit Sood,Anna Tzatzopoulou,Sai-Aakash Ramesh,Tim Dodwell*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This work shows that recursive neural architectures perform approximate inference over latent reasoning trajectories. By applying guided stochastic perturbations and using the model's early-stopping head for reweighting, the authors derive three label-free diagnostics (local stability, guide alignment, cloud-token entropy) that predict when and why stochastic trajectory exploration helps. On Sudoku-Extreme, accuracy jumps from 85.9% to 98.0% without retraining; on Maze-Hard, diagnostics detect a misaligned guide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve the inference-time behavior of recursive neural architectures on structured reasoning tasks, especially by characterizing when stochastic trajectory exploration helps and whether the model's internal guide can be trusted.

Method: The authors interpret deterministic recursion as approximate inference (one-particle, zero-noise limit). They introduce guided stochastic exploration: stochastic perturbations of reasoning dynamics propose neighboring trajectories, and the model's early-stopping head reweights them online. From inference traces, they compute three label-free diagnostics (local stability, guide alignment, cloud-token entropy) to predict improvement potential and output trustworthiness.

Result: On Sudoku-Extreme, exact-solve accuracy improves from 85.9% to 98.0% without retraining. On Maze-Hard, the diagnostics successfully flag a misaligned guide, which is later confirmed by validation performance.
