We present FLOATBench, a public tabular benchmark with $582{,}120$ per-section fatigue-damage labels across three $22$ MW FOWT tower geometries, derived from $19{,}404$ high-fidelity OpenFAST simulations across the three towers ($6{,}468$ per tower: $1{,}078$ aligned wind/wave operating points $\times$ six turbulence seeds), labeled at $30$ cross-sections per tower. FLOATBench includes a regime-aware alpha-shape partition of the joint wind/wave operating envelope, stratifying test points into in-train, interpolation, and extrapolation regimes. It is paired with a reproducible evaluation harness covering three protocol levels: random validation (E1), within-tower regime-aware evaluation (E2), and cross-tower transfer (E3). The regime-aware protocol reveals rank shifts between global and extrapolation performance that random-split leaderboards cannot detect. To the authors' knowledge, FLOATBench is the first FOWT fatigue benchmark for tabular surrogate modeling, and offers an evaluation protocol that generalizes to engineering surrogates defined over physical operating envelopes. Dataset and code available at: https://github.com/Joao97ribeiro/FLOATBench.

</details>


### [507] [Learning to Search and Searching to Learn for Generalization in Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25720)
*Michael Aichmüller,Yannik Hesse,Hector Geffner*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-improving WA* learning framework with Relational Graph Neural Networks for combinatorial generalization in DRL, achieving strong zero-shot generalization on planning problems including Sokoban, Blocksworld, and IPC benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Combinatorial generalization remains a central challenge in Deep Reinforcement Learning, especially in sparse-reward domains where standard RL exploration is ineffective and existing methods rely on expert demonstrations or random walks.

Method: A self-improving WA* learning framework combined with a Relational Graph Neural Network value heuristic: the heuristic guides best-first search, and search results update the heuristic via Q-learning in a continuous improvement loop.

Result: Strong zero-shot generalization demonstrated, e.g., heuristics trained on Blocksworld instances with <30 blocks solve instances with 488 blocks without search. Successfully applied to Sokoban, PushWorld, The Witness, and IPC 2023 benchmarks.

Conclusion: The proposed self-improving WA* learning framework yields heuristics that function as general policies and solve novel instances even without search, where DRL otherwise fails.

Abstract: Combinatorial generalization remains a central challenge in Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL). Classical planning provides a simple yet challenging setting to study this problem through explicit relational descriptions, without requiring learning from perception. In sparse-reward domains, standard RL exploration via real-time search is ineffective, and learning-based planning methods often rely on expert demonstrations, hindsight relabeling, or random walks from the goal state. In contrast, planners rely on best-first search methods such as $\mathrm{A}^\star$ to solve problems from scratch. We propose a self-improving $\mathrm{WA}^\star$ learning framework in combination with a value heuristic represented by a Relational Graph Neural Network: the heuristic guides search, and the resulting search data updates the heuristic via $Q$-learning. This loop yields heuristics that can function as general policies and solve new instances even without search, where DRL otherwise fails, as we show on puzzles such as Sokoban, PushWorld, The Witness, and the 2023 International Planning Competition benchmarks. Notably, we demonstrate strong zero-shot generalization: For example, heuristics trained on Blocksworld instances with fewer than 30 blocks successfully solve instances with 488 blocks without search.

</details>


### [508] [A Deep Dive into Axiomatic Design -- Part I: Problem Formulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25735)
*Aydin Homay*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper clarifies the critical step of formulating first-level functional requirements (FRs) in axiomatic design, addressing common misunderstandings, and offers guidance while also considering implications of large language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Problem formulation (translating customer needs into functional requirements) is the most critical yet misunderstood step in axiomatic design, frequently underestimated in practice.

Method: Conceptual analysis grounded primarily in Nam P. Suh's three books, clarifying what first-level FRs are, why they should be consistent across designers, and identifying recurring pitfalls.

Result: Clarified definitions and practical guidance for formulating well-posed first-level FRs, along with revisiting problem formulation in the context of large language models.

Conclusion: Proper problem formulation is essential for design success; LLMs can offer limited contributions at the first-level FR formulation stage.

Abstract: Problem formulation translating customer needs and constraints into a minimum set of independent first-level functional requirements, is arguably the most critical step in every design framework, including axiomatic design yet it is frequently misunderstood or underestimated in practice. This paper focuses exclusively on problem formulation in axiomatic design it clarifies what first-level FRs are (and are not), explains why they should not legitimately vary across designers given the same needs and constraints, and highlights intrinsic difficulties and recurring pitfalls that lead to design failure. The discussion is grounded primarily in Nam P.Suh's three books. The Principles of Design, Axiomatic Design Advances and Applications, and Complexity Theory, and it offers practical guidance to help designers formulate well-posed first-level FRs. Finally, the paper briefly revisits problem formulation in the era of large language models and discusses what such tools can (and cannot) contribute at the first level.

</details>


### [509] [Agent-Centric Social Trajectory Prediction: A Free Energy Principle Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25748)
*Yanping Wu,Ji Zhang,Hao Chen,Edmond S. L. Ho,Chongfeng Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FEP-Diff is an agent-centric trajectory prediction framework grounded in the Free Energy Principle that outperforms state-of-the-art methods on five benchmarks under restricted observability, using dual-branch spatiotemporal encoding, goal-conditioned belief learning with free-energy optimization, and residual diffusion for trajectory generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory prediction methods suffer from three key limitations: reliance on global state assumptions, insufficient belief inference under partial observability, and lack of cognitive behavioral constraints, leading to compromised deployment feasibility and physical plausibility in real-world settings.

Method: The framework consists of three components: (1) a dual-branch spatiotemporal encoder extracting ego-motion dynamics and social interaction cues from local observations; (2) a goal-conditioned belief learner that infers multimodal latent belief distributions via free-energy optimization with social consistency constraints; (3) a residual diffusion trajectory generator conditioned on learned beliefs via token-level proxy conditioning.

Result: Extensive experiments on five public benchmarks demonstrate that FEP-Diff consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods under restricted observability.

Conclusion: The Free Energy Principle-based approach enables cognitively plausible trajectory predictions under realistic partial observability constraints, as validated by superior performance across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction methods have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in capturing complex motion patterns. However, existing methods rely on global state assumptions, suffer from insufficient belief inference under partial observability, and lack cognitive behavioral constraints in prediction. These limitations severely compromise both deployment feasibility and physical plausibility in real-world settings. In this work, we propose FEP-Diff, an agent-centric trajectory prediction framework grounded in the Free Energy Principle, aimed at achieving cognitively plausible predictions under realistic constraints. Specifically, a dual-branch spatiotemporal encoder extracts ego-motion dynamics and social interaction cues from local observations. Building upon this, a goal-conditioned belief learner infers multimodal latent belief distributions optimized via a free-energy objective, with a social consistency constraint on the local neighborhood graph to promote cognitive alignment among neighboring agents. Finally, a residual diffusion trajectory generator is conditioned on the learned belief representations with token-level proxy conditioning, producing precise and diverse future predictions. Extensive experiments on five public benchmarks demonstrate that FEP-Diff consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods under restricted observability. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/FEP-Diff-8876.

</details>


### [510] [When Can We Trust Early Warnings? Leakage-Excluded Early Outcome Prediction from LMS Interaction Logs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25794)
*Ngoc Luyen Le,Marie-Hélène Abel,Bertrand Laforge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes temporal leakage in early-warning LMS models and introduces LEAP, a protocol to prevent post-cutoff information from inflating early prediction benchmarks. Applied to OULAD, results show leakage—especially from assessments—can significantly overstate early performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing early-warning models from LMS logs often report inflated "early" performance due to temporal leakage, where future information leaks into the training/evaluation pipeline. The authors aim to formalize and mitigate this issue to enable trustworthy early outcome prediction.

Method: The authors propose LEAP (Leakage-Excluded Early-Availability Protocol), which enforces cutoff-first truncation before joins/aggregation and audits feature provenance. They instantiate it on OULAD with weekly cutoffs and evaluate multiple classifiers (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, etc.) using ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, Brier score, and F1@0.5.

Result: Performance improves as the observation window expands, with a notable gain around week 3. Random Forest performs best at earliest cutoffs, while Gradient Boosting dominates later. Leakage ablations show that assessment information causes the most severe temporal violations and inflates apparent early performance.

Conclusion: Temporal leakage is a significant and underrecognized threat in early-warning system evaluation. LEAP provides a principled, reproducible framework to eliminate such leakage; future work should adopt similar protocols to ensure reported early performance is realistic.

Abstract: Early-warning models built from Learning Management System (LMS) logs aim to predict end-of-course outcomes early enough to enable timely learner support. However, reported "early" performance is often inflated by temporal leakage. This occurs when the pipeline uses information that would not yet be available at the time of prediction. We formalize cutoff-based early outcome prediction under a temporal availability constraint and introduce LEAP (Leakage-Excluded Early-Availability Protocol), which enforces cutoff-first truncation prior to joins and aggregation and audits feature provenance to prevent post-cutoff evidence from entering the benchmark. We instantiate LEAP on the public Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD) as a multi-step protocol for leakage-controlled evaluation across weekly cutoffs. Using several standard learning methods, we evaluate performance using ROC-AUC, PR-AUC, Brier score, and F1@0.5. Results show improving performance as the observation window expands, with a marked gain around week~3; Random Forest performs best at the earliest cutoffs, while Gradient Boosting dominates thereafter. Leakage ablations further show that temporal violations, especially through assessment information, can inflate apparent "early" performance.

</details>


### [511] [Behind EvoMap: Characterizing a Self-Evolving Agent-to-Agent Collaboration Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25815)
*Qiming Ye,Peixain Zhang,Yupeng He,Zifan Peng,Gareth Tyson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A large-scale empirical study of EvoMap—a decentralized A2A agent network—reveals that scalable growth incentives lead to severe problems: 98% of assets never reused, reward concentration, manipulable scoring via self-reported metadata, and over 84% of quality checks bypassed with vacuous tests.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how decentralized Agent-to-Agent (A2A) collaboration networks actually operate in practice, given their growing importance for autonomous AI agent cooperation but lack of empirical scrutiny.

Method: Large-scale empirical analysis of EvoMap, a prominent A2A network, examining over 1.5 million assets and 128,000 agents to study its design choices and their real-world consequences.

Result: (1) 98% of assets never reused; rewards concentrate among a tiny fraction. (2) Asset scoring is driven by unverified self-reported metadata (e.g., claimed lines of code modified) rather than objective performance, enabling easy manipulation. (3) 84% of approved assets pass quality checks using vacuous tests (e.g., console.log), since validations are not independently verified.

Conclusion: Future A2A collaboration networks cannot rely on unverified self-reporting alone; scalable collaboration requires mechanisms that balance open participation with verifiable execution and trustworthy evaluation.

Abstract: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) networks enable autonomous AI agents to collaborate by sharing reusable problem-solving instructions. However, how these decentralized ecosystems operate in practice remains largely unexplored. We present the first large-scale empirical study of EvoMap, a prominent A2A collaboration network. By analyzing over 1.5M assets and 128K agents, we show how design choices that prioritize scalable growth introduce trade-offs in reusability, evolution, and auditability. First, EvoMap's credit economy rewards agents for publishing valuable assets. Although this design encourages participation at scale, rewards are tied primarily to publication rather than adoption. This leads agents to mass-produce assets to accumulate credits. As a result, 98% of assets are never reused, while rewards become highly concentrated among a small fraction of agents. Second, EvoMap employs an algorithm (referred to as GDI) to score and rank the quality of these shared assets. We demonstrate that this scoring system is flawed: rather than measuring objective performance, an asset's rank is heavily dictated by unverified, self-reported metadata (e.g., claimed lines of code modified). This allows agents to trivially manipulate their asset's scores. Finally, EvoMap relies on agents to provide local execution logs as evidence that uploaded assets function correctly. Because these validations are not independently verified, over 84% of approved assets bypass quality checks using vacuous tests (e.g., console.log). Our findings show that future A2A collaboration networks cannot rely on unverified self-reporting alone. Scalable collaboration requires mechanisms that balance open participation with verifiable execution and trustworthy evaluation.

</details>


### [512] [MuCRASP: Multimodal Chain-of-thought Reasoning aware Structured Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25842)
*Aritra Dutta,Somak Aditya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MuCRASP is a structured pruning framework for vision-language models that preserves chain-of-thought reasoning by targeting pivot tokens and accounting for cross-modal activation differences, outperforming baselines up to 50% pruning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pruning methods fail to preserve CoT reasoning accuracy in VLMs due to being CoT-agnostic and ignoring activation-distribution differences across visual and textual modalities.

Method: MuCRASP is a structured pruning framework that targets reasoning-critical components (pivot tokens), preserves cross-modal alignment, and accounts for layer-wise sensitivity under a global parameter budget.

Result: At 30% pruning on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, achieves LLM-as-a-Judge score of 8.87 vs 7.32 for baseline. Maintains high reasoning consistency up to 50% pruning with lower perplexity degradation.

Conclusion: MuCRASP consistently preserves reasoning quality under increasing compression across four VLMs and three reasoning benchmarks, significantly outperforming prior pruning approaches.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) increasingly rely on chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning to solve complex multimodal tasks, but their large parameter sizes make deployment expensive. Structured pruning offers a natural solution; however, existing methods fail to preserve CoT reasoning accuracy in VLMs. We identify two key reasons: (1) CoT consistency depends on sparse transition points (pivot tokens) in the generation trajectory, while existing pruning methods are CoT-agnostic; and (2) pruning methods designed for unimodal LLMs do not account for activation-distribution differences across visual and textual modalities. Motivated by these observations, we propose MuCRASP, a structured pruning framework that targets reasoning-critical components while preserving cross-modal alignment and accounting for layer-wise sensitivity under a global parameter budget. Experiments on four VLMs across three reasoning benchmarks show that MuCRASP consistently preserves reasoning quality under increasing compression. At 30% pruning on Qwen2.5-VL-7B, MuCRASP achieves an LLM-as-a-Judge score of 8.87 versus 7.32 for the strongest baseline on physical reasoning tasks. Furthermore, MuCRASP maintains high reasoning consistency up to 50% pruning, significantly outperforming prior pruning approaches while exhibiting lower perplexity degradation.

</details>


### [513] [From Accounting to Coordination: A Virtual Water-Aware Electricity-Computation-Water Nexus Framework for Data Center Dispatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25854)
*Haiyang You,Chengwei Lou,Jin Zhao,Yue Zhou,Lu Zhang,Jin Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper develops a novel operational ECW nexus framework that embeds dispatch optimization as a differentiable layer within deep learning, enabling end-to-end coordination of workload relocation and power dispatch to dynamically reduce water withdrawals in data center electricity consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing static statistical accounting methods fail to capture how dispatch optimization and workload relocation dynamically affect water withdrawals at generation sites, and remain decoupled from the optimization process, thus unable to guide actions to mitigate water stress.

Method: The framework represents dispatch optimization as a differentiable optimization layer embedded within a deep learning architecture, enabling end-to-end learning of coordination policies while ensuring operational feasibility, combined with fixed-point coordination for consistency between virtual water attribution and physical withdrawals.

Result: On IEEE 30-bus and 118-bus test systems, the framework demonstrates reliable convergence, exact power-water consistency, and approximately 3-5% reductions in generation-related freshwater withdrawals under water-constrained conditions.

Conclusion: The proposed ECW nexus framework effectively bridges the gap between static water footprint accounting and dynamic operational optimization, offering a practical approach to reduce water stress from data center electricity demand.

Abstract: The expansion of data centers (DCs) drives a sustained increase in electricity demand and associated water withdrawals at generation sites. These withdrawals occur at generation sites and are virtually allocated to demand based on network power flows. Consequently, the actual water footprint of a specific load varies dynamically with generation dispatch and network conditions. Existing approaches typically rely on static statistical accounting to quantify these water footprints. However, such static methods fail to capture how dispatch optimization and workload relocation dynamically affect water withdrawals. As a result, static statistical accounting approaches remain decoupled from the optimization process, rendering them incapable of guiding workload relocation or power dispatch to mitigate water stress. To address this limitation, this paper develops an operational electricity-computation-water (ECW) nexus framework that internalizes virtual water impacts directly into power system dispatch. The framework represents dispatch optimization as a differentiable optimization layer embedded within a deep learning architecture, enabling efficient end-to-end learning of coordination policies while preserving operational feasibility. Combined with fixed-point coordination, the framework enforces consistency between virtual water attribution and physical generation-side withdrawals. Case studies on the IEEE 30-bus and 118-bus test systems demonstrate reliable convergence, exact power-water consistency, and reductions of approximately 3-5% in generation-related freshwater withdrawals under water-constrained conditions.

</details>


### [514] [$D^2$-Monitor: Dynamic Safety Monitoring for Diffusion LLMs via Hesitation-Aware Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25893)
*Aoxi Liu,Yupeng Chen,James Oldfield,Guanzhe Hong,Junchi Yu,Baoyuan Wu,Philip Torr,Adel Bibi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces D^2-Monitor, a bi-level safety monitor for diffusion LLMs that uses safety hesitation signals (when intermediate states hover near decision boundaries) to dynamically allocate between lightweight and heavy probes, achieving SOTA safety monitoring with ≤0.85M parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety monitoring for diffusion LLMs remains unexplored, unlike autoregressive LLMs. D-LLMs expose intermediate hidden representations during multi-step denoising that may contain safety-relevant information unavailable in standard single-step setups.

Method: D^2-Monitor uses a lightweight always-on probe to jointly estimate hesitation (intermediate states near decision boundaries) and perform base classification. When hesitation exceeds a threshold, a heavier probe is activated for dynamic resource allocation.

Result: Evaluated on 3 datasets across 4 D-LLMs, D^2-Monitor achieves SOTA performance with ≤0.85M parameters, exhibiting the best trade-off between effectiveness and efficiency relative to 8 baselines.

Conclusion: Safety hesitation signals effectively predict probe failure, and dynamic bi-level monitoring efficiently allocates resources at test time for D-LLM safety.

Abstract: Despite the emergence of diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) as an alternative to autoregressive large language models (AR-LLMs), safety monitoring for D-LLMs remains largely unexplored. Unlike AR-LLMs, D-LLMs generate text through a multi-step denoising process, exposing intermediate hidden representations that may contain safety-relevant information unavailable in standard single-step monitoring setups. Motivated by the suitability of lightweight probes for always-on monitoring, we analyze which trajectory-level signals best indicate when such probes are likely to struggle. We find that the most informative signal is safety hesitation: intermediate hidden states repeatedly falling within a small margin of the probe's decision boundary. The number of such hesitation steps in D-LLM's trajectory predicts probe failure effectively, providing a proxy of sample difficulty. Building on this analysis, we propose $D^2$-Monitor, a bi-level safety monitor for D-LLMs. $D^2$-Monitor adopts a lightweight probe as an always-on monitor to jointly estimate hesitation and perform base classification. When the hesitation level exceeds a threshold, a more expressive but computationally heavier probe is activated. This dynamic routing mechanism allocates monitoring resources efficiently at test time. Evaluated on 3 datasets (WildguardMix, ToxicChat, OpenAI-Moderation) across 4 D-LLMs, $D^2$-Monitor achieves state-of-the-art performance with a compact parameter footprint ($\leq$ 0.85M parameters), and exhibits the best trade-off between effectiveness and efficiency relative to 8 baselines.

</details>


### [515] [Explore Before You Solve: The Speed--Depth Trade-off in Epistemic Agents for ARC-AGI-3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25931)
*Liew Keong Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows all 25 public ARC-AGI-3 games are solvable via trivial heuristics (not intelligence), and proposes AERA—a three-phase agent (EXPLORE/VERIFY/PLAN) that outperforms baselines via a formalized Speed-Depth trade-off framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expose that public ARC-AGI-3 benchmarks fail to measure genuine intelligent exploration, because trivial non-intelligent strategies solve all 25 games.

Method: A three-phase agent (EXPLORE/VERIFY/PLAN) with a formal Speed-Depth trade-off framework; uses Qwen2.5-0.5B and achieves RHAE=0.2116.

Result: All 25 public games are solvable via non-intelligent strategies; AERA achieves RHAE=0.2116 (4/25 solved); random/no-explore baselines score 0.0000; code achieves RHAE=0.30 on private 55-game set.

Conclusion: Current interactive reasoning benchmarks are invalid for measuring exploration; the EXPLORE-before-PLAN paradigm and the Speed-Depth trade-off provide a principled alternative.

Abstract: We systematically investigate all 25 public ARC-AGI-3 games and find that every one is reachable through non-intelligent strategies: 10 in a single blind step, 5 after one probing action, 1 via repeated ACTION1 presses, 1 via diverse exploration, and 8 via single repeated actions with sufficient budget (50-200 steps). A library-level null-coordinate vulnerability additionally bypasses 18 games in 1 step. This benchmark critique implies the public evaluation set cannot discriminate intelligent exploration from trivial heuristics - the private 55-game evaluation is the only genuine intelligence test. Against this backdrop, we present AERA (Adaptive Epistemic Reasoning Agent), a three-phase (EXPLORE / VERIFY / PLAN) agent achieving RHAE=0.2116 (4/25 solved) on these 25 games with Qwen2.5-0.5B, while random and no-explore baselines score 0.0000. We formalise AERA through a Speed--Depth trade-off framework: under a convexity assumption (proved for a class of environments in the Appendix), RHAE's quadratic form emerges as a second-order penalty for deviating from the Pareto frontier between action efficiency and information gain. Contributions: (i) a benchmark validity analysis showing that current interactive reasoning benchmarks fail to measure the exploration they claim to require, and (ii) the EXPLORE-before-PLAN framework and model-capability x exploration interaction. The linked code track entry achieves RHAE=0.30 on the full 55-game private evaluation. Code: CC0.

</details>


### [516] [LECTOR: Joint Optimization of Scientific Reasoning Graphs and Introduction Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25964)
*Jiabei Xiao,Yizhou Wang,Chen Tang,Pengze Li,Wanli Ouyang,Shixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LECTOR is a Logic-Expression Co-Reinforcement Learning framework for generating scientific paper introductions that are logically sound, citation-grounded, and structurally faithful, achieving significant improvements over baselines on Nature Communications papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-assisted methods treat introduction writing as pure text generation rather than reasoning, leading to hallucinations and unfaithful citations. There is a need for a structured, logic-grounded approach to scientific introduction generation.

Method: LECTOR formulates the Content-Conditional Introduction Generation (CCIG) task, constructs a logic-reasoning graph from the paper's main body as a verifiable blueprint, and uses a Logic-Expression Co-Rewarding mechanism to jointly optimize for structural fidelity and narrative quality.

Result: On a dataset of Nature Communications papers, LECTOR achieves Graph Quality +26.7%, Citation Quality +8.6%, and Paper Consistency +3.3% compared to baselines.

Conclusion: LECTOR demonstrates that grounding introduction generation in logical reasoning graphs with co-reinforcement learning effectively reduces hallucinations and improves both structural and citation quality, offering a promising direction for automated scientific writing.

Abstract: AI Scientists have shown promising progress across multiple stages of the research pipeline, among which automatic scientific paper writing remains a formidable challenge. The Introduction writing is especially challenging, which demands not only linguistic fluency, but logical soundness and verifiable faithfulness. Most AI-assisted methods treat the task as text generation instead of reasoning and structuring, leading to severe drawbacks, e.g., hallucinating citations. To address this, we first formulate the Content-Conditional Introduction Generation (CCIG) task, which requires grounding the Introduction in the paper's core evidence. We then propose LECTOR, a novel Logic-Expression Co-Reinforcement Learning framework that can strictly follow the scientist's logic, add high-quality citations and keep structured expressions. LECTOR first constructs a logic-reasoning graph from the paper's main body to serve as a verifiable logical blueprint. Subsequently, it employs a Logic-Expression Co-Rewarding mechanism to jointly optimize for both the graph's structural fidelity and the final narrative's quality. We conduct a dataset from Nature Communications papers to assess our method. Extensive experiments show consistent improvements in both logic fidelity and Introduction generation quality metrics, e.g., Graph Quality (+26.7%), Citation Quality (+8.6%), and Paper Consistency (+3.3%). Code and data are available at https://github.com/Xiao-Youth/LECTOR.

</details>


### [517] [Neural Scalable Symbolic Search Framework for Complex Logical Queries with Multiple Free Variables](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25985)
*Weizhi Fei,Hang Yin,Zihao Wang,Shukai Zhao,Wei Zhang,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NS3 is a budgeted neural symbolic search framework that approximates joint ranking for complex existential first-order queries over knowledge graphs by merging free variables into budget-controlled hypernodes, outperforming existing marginal-ranking approaches on EFO_k queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CQA methods rely on marginal rankings over individual variables, which are a poor proxy for true joint ranking of answer tuples. Exhaustively enumerating all possible tuple combinations in EFO_k queries becomes intractable as k grows.

Method: NS3 (i) answers marginalized sub-queries to obtain candidate sets, (ii) merges multiple free variables into hypernodes with domain pruning controlled by a dynamic budget B, and (iii) progressively reduces an EFO_k query to an EFO_{k-1} query over a budgeted reduced domain.

Result: Across three standard KG datasets, NS3 substantially improves joint ranking performance while retaining strong marginal accuracy. A new benchmark extending existing EFO_1 datasets to k=3 is released for systematic evaluation.

Conclusion: NS3 effectively addresses the scalability challenge of multi-variable query answering in KGs by providing a budgeted approximation of joint rankings without exhaustive enumeration, achieving superior performance on both joint and marginal metrics.

Abstract: Complex Query Answering (CQA) is a fundamental knowledge representation and reasoning task over incomplete knowledge graphs (KGs). Answering existential first-order queries with $k$ free variables (i.e., $\text{EFO}_k$ queries) is a crucial yet challenging problem, as it requires ranking answer tuples in $\mathcal{E}^k$, where $\mathcal{E}$ denotes the entity set of a KG. This quickly becomes intractable as $k$ grows. Consequently, existing benchmarks and methods rely on marginal rankings over individual variables; however, marginal rankings are a poor proxy for the true joint ranking of tuples. Building on neural symbolic search for $\text{EFO}_1$ queries, we propose Neural Scalable Symbolic Search (NS3), a budgeted framework that approximates joint ranking without enumerating $\mathcal{E}^k$. NS3 (i) answers marginalized sub-queries to obtain necessary candidate sets, (ii) merges multiple free variables into hypernodes whose domains are pruned and controlled by a dynamic budget $B$, and (iii) progressively reduces an $\text{EFO}_k$ query to an $\text{EFO}_{k-1}$ query over a budgeted reduced domain. Across three standard KG datasets, NS3 substantially improves joint ranking performance while retaining strong marginal accuracy. We further release a joint-ranking benchmark that extends existing $\text{EFO}_1$ datasets to $k=3$, enabling systematic evaluation of multi-variable queries. Our code is provided in https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/NS3_KDD2026.

</details>


### [518] [CausaLab: A Scalable Environment for Interactive Causal Discovery Toward AI Scientists](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26029)
*Junlin Yang,Dylan Zhang,Xiangchen Song,Qirun Dai,Xiao Liu,Yuen Chen,Aniket Vashishtha,Jing Shi,Chenhao Tan,Hao Peng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CausaLab is a scalable environment evaluating whether LLM agents can truly discover causal mechanisms (graphs and equations) rather than just make correct predictions. Experiments reveal a gap: high task accuracy but poor structural recovery. Mixed observation-intervention strategies improve structural fidelity, while pure intervention strategies fail. Premature stopping is a key weakness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of interactive causal discovery by LLM agents focus only on whether agents can solve problems using causal evidence, but fail to assess whether the answer is supported by a correct hypothesis about the underlying causal mechanism. This paper aims to separate predictive success from genuine causal understanding.

Method: CausaLab places agents in a synthetic laboratory with prior measurement records, a manipulator crystal for interventions, and a held-out reactor crystal. The hidden process is a randomly sampled SCM. A domain-specific language records the agent's evolving SCM hypothesis for inspection and comparison with ground truth. Experiments evaluate different interaction strategies (observational, mixed, pure intervention).

Result: GPT-5.2-high achieves 92% task accuracy but only 0.471 all-edge F1 in the purely observational 6-node setting. Mixed observation-intervention strategies achieve 80% on both task accuracy and all-edge F1. Pure intervention strategies perform poorly on both metrics. Premature stopping is identified as a major weakness.

Conclusion: CausaLab reveals that current LLM agents can achieve high predictive accuracy without correctly recovering causal structure. Mixed strategies improve structural fidelity, but agents still struggle with designing informative interventions. Asking models to verify consistency between hypothesis and past data helps mitigate premature stopping.

Abstract: We introduce CausaLab, a scalable environment for evaluating interactive causal discovery by LLM agents. Unlike prior evaluations, CausaLab evaluates both whether an agent can solve a problem using causal evidence and whether its answer is supported by a correct hypothesis about the underlying causal mechanism. Each episode places an agent in a synthetic laboratory: it receives prior measurement records, intervenes on a manipulator crystal, and predicts the resonance frequency of a held-out reactor crystal governed by the same mechanism. The hidden data-generating process is a randomly sampled structural causal model (SCM), so success requires recovering both a causal graph and structural equations rather than recalling prior knowledge. CausaLab also includes a domain-specific language that records the agent's evolving SCM hypothesis, making trajectories inspectable and comparable with ground truth. Experiments show a persistent gap between prediction and mechanism recovery: in the purely observational 6-node setting, GPT-5.2-high reaches 92% task accuracy but only 0.471 all-edge $F_1$. This observation further motivates our exploration of different interaction strategies: Mixed observation--intervention strategies improve structural fidelity: in the mixed 6-node setting, GPT-5.2-high achieves 80% on both task accuracy and all-edge $F_1$. Yet even strong agents struggle to design informative interventions, as pure intervention strategies perform poorly on both task accuracy and all-edge $F_1$. We identify premature stopping as a major weakness of agents, and show that asking the model to verify the consistency between its hypothesis and past data can help mitigate this issue. CausaLab therefore separates predictive success from causal understanding and exposes current LLM agents' limits as experimental causal reasoners.

</details>


### [519] [CITYREP: A Unified Benchmark for Urban Representations Across Cities, Tasks, and Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26036)
*Junyuan Liu,Xinglei Wang,Zichao Zeng,Jiazhuang Feng,Quan Qin,Ilya Ilyankou,Guangsheng Dong,Tao Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CityRep is a unified benchmark for urban representation learning that uses spatially structured splits across 8 cities and 8 tasks to evaluate 11 models, showing that random splits inflate performance and alter model rankings, while cross-city generalization varies substantially.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of urban representation learning are limited to one or two cities/tasks and rely on random splits that cause spatial leakage, inflating performance and providing weak support for cross-location generalization and fair comparison.

Method: CityRep proposes (1) a spatial unit-agnostic evaluation framework with a standardized alignment module, (2) block-based spatial splits to mitigate spatial leakage, and (3) an extensible benchmark suite covering 8 cities, 8 tasks (regression, classification, distribution prediction), evaluating 11 urban representation models.

Result: Performance is highly sensitive to split protocol — random splits inflate scores and alter model rankings. Substantial variability across cities and tasks is observed, highlighting the need for generalization-aware evaluation.

Conclusion: CityRep provides a reproducible benchmark with datasets, evaluation pipelines, and diagnostic tools to enable fair comparison and support future urban representation learning research toward urban foundation models.

Abstract: Urban representation learning encodes complex urban environments into general-purpose embeddings for diverse downstream tasks and emerging urban foundation models. However, current evaluations are limited, typically focusing on one or two cities and tasks and relying on random splits that introduce spatial leakage, leading to inflated performance and weak support for cross-location generalization and fair comparison. To address this, we propose CityRep, a unified benchmark that evaluates urban representations across data modalities, cities, and tasks using spatially structured splits. CityRep consists of three key components: (1) a spatial unit-agnostic evaluation framework that supports heterogeneous urban representations through a standardized alignment module; (2) a unified evaluation protocol using block-based spatial splits to mitigate spatial leakage and enable rigorous model comparison; and (3) an extensible multi-city, multi-task benchmark suite spanning 8 cities and 8 tasks across regression, classification, and distribution prediction. We evaluate 11 representative urban representation models. Results show that performance is highly sensitive to the split protocol, with random splits inflating scores and altering model rankings. We also observe substantial variability across cities and tasks, underscoring the need for generalization-aware evaluation. CityRep is released as a reproducible benchmark with datasets, evaluation pipelines, and diagnostic tools to facilitate fair comparison and support future research in urban representation learning towards urban foundation models.

</details>


### [520] [L2IR: Revealing Latent Intent in Graph Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26040)
*Jinsheng Guo,Zhenhao Weng,Yibo Liu,Yan Qiao,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: L2IR is an LLM-driven framework for graph fraud detection that uncovers latent intent behind user behaviors and suspicious connections, using adaptive self-training to handle limited supervision and heavy camouflage, achieving up to 8.27% AUPRC improvement over GNN-based detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fraudsters disguise themselves by forging connections with benign users, diluting fraud signals in GNN-based detection. Existing LLM-enhanced methods overlook the underlying intent behind suspicious connections, and the scarcity of annotated fraud samples further degrades detector robustness.

Method: L2IR (LLM-driven Latent Intent Revealing) extracts intent-aware representations from behavioral traces, reasons about the true purpose behind individual connections to distinguish supportive links from misleading ones, and incorporates adaptive self-training for robustness under limited supervision.

Result: L2IR surpasses strong baselines on two real-world datasets with pervasive camouflage, functioning as a plug-in enhancement for GNN-based detectors with up to 8.27% AUPRC improvement.

Conclusion: Revealing latent intent via LLMs is effective for graph fraud detection under heavy camouflage and limited labels; L2IR offers a versatile enhancement for existing GNN detectors.

Abstract: Graph fraud detection has long depended on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to propagate and aggregate information across relational data. A critical obstacle in practice, however, is that fraudsters frequently disguise themselves by forging numerous connections with benign users, causing fraud signals to be progressively diluted during neighborhood aggregation and undermining detection reliability. While recent efforts have used Large Language Models (LLMs) to provide rich semantic cues for fraud detection, the underlying intent behind suspicious connections remains insufficiently explored. Compounding this issue, the scarcity of annotated fraud samples makes it difficult to train detectors that remain robust under heavy camouflage. To address these gaps, we propose L2IR, an LLM-driven Latent Intent Revealing framework for graph fraud detection. By uncovering latent intent from both user behaviors and suspicious connections, L2IR extracts intent-aware representations from raw behavioral traces and reasons about the true purpose behind individual connections, effectively distinguishing supportive links from misleading ones. It further incorporates adaptive self-training to enhance robustness under limited supervision. Evaluations on two real-world datasets characterized by pervasive camouflage demonstrate that L2IR surpasses strong baselines and can function as a plug-in enhancement for a range of GNN-based detectors, improving AUPRC by up to 8.27%.

</details>


### [521] [Retrying vs Resampling in AI Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26047)
*James Lucassen,Adam Kaufman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper studies retrying and resampling in AI coding scaffolds from a security perspective, finding that retrying can backfire by enabling sneakier attacks, while resampling with maximum-suspicion auditing improves safety from 61% to 71% at low cost. It contradicts prior work on resampling regarding whether to use max or min suspicion scores and the effectiveness of executing the least suspicious sample.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate AI coding scaffolds (e.g., Claude Code, Codex) that use retrying (blocking risky actions) from an adversarial AI control perspective, questioning whether such mechanisms genuinely improve safety when the model is treated as potentially adversarial.

Method: Empirical evaluation in BashArena setting using Claude Opus 4.6 as untrusted model and MiMo-V2-Flash as trusted monitor. Compares retrying vs. resampling strategies, disentangling design choices (e.g., auditing based on max vs. min suspicion scores, selecting least vs. most suspicious samples).

Result: Retrying reduces honest suspicion but enables sneakier attacks, negating safety gains. Resampling with 5 samples per step and auditing on max suspicion raises safety from 61% to 71% at 0.3% audit budget with no usefulness cost. Selective resampling recovers 6.2pp gain using only 10% extra samples. Two findings contradict prior work: max outperforms min suspicion (opposite of Ctrl-Z), and executing the least suspicious sample gives only +3.9pp gain (CI overlapping zero).

Conclusion: Retrying is not a reliable safety mechanism under adversarial models. Resampling with maximum-suspicion auditing offers meaningful safety improvements, but design choices matter greatly and some findings from prior work do not generalize.

Abstract: AI coding scaffolds like Claude Code and Codex use \textit{retrying}: blocking actions flagged as risky and continuing the trajectory. We study retrying from an AI control perspective, which treats the model as potentially adversarial. We find that while retrying reduces honest suspicion scores, the untrusted model can exploit monitor rationale to construct sneakier attacks, negating safety gains. We also study \textit{resampling}: drawing multiple samples from the same context, which does not leak exploitable information. We disentangle design choices that previous work on resampling had bundled together. In BashArena, with Claude Opus 4.6 as the untrusted model and MiMo-V2-Flash as the trusted monitor, drawing five samples per step and auditing on the maximum suspicion score raises safety from 61\% to 71\% at a 0.3\% audit budget, at no cost to usefulness. Selectively resampling only the steps that look suspicious on the first draw recovers 6.2 percentage points of the gain while drawing only 10\% as many extra samples. Two of our findings in this setting contradict earlier work on resampling. The first is that auditing based on the maximum across resampled suspicion scores outperforms using the minimum, which is the opposite of what Ctrl-Z found. The second is that executing the least suspicious sample, which is the central mechanism in earlier defer-to-resample protocols, gives only a small empirical safety gain in our setting (+3.9 pp, with the confidence interval overlapping zero).

</details>


### [522] [VeriTrace: Evolving Mental Models for Deep Research Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26081)
*Haolang Zhao,Yunbo Long,Lukas Beckenbauer,Alexandra Brintrup*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VeriTrace is a cognitive-graph framework with explicit regulatory loops (interpretive update, deviation feedback, schema revision) that outperforms baselines on DeepResearch and DeepConsult benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing deep research agents allow intermediate representations to evolve solely through LLM's implicit reasoning, leading to contamination from mixed-quality information and error propagation. The authors argue that explicit feedback regulation is needed to continuously align task understanding with reality.

Method: VeriTrace implements three explicit regulatory loops: interpretive update, deviation feedback, and schema revision, built as a cognitive-graph framework to regulate the evolution of an agent's mental model.

Result: With Qwen3.5-27B backbones, VeriTrace improves over the strongest baseline by 4.22 pp on DRB Insight (1.49 pp Overall) and 5.9 pp Overall win rate on DeepConsult. With Config-DeepSeek, it achieves the strongest reproducible open-source result on DRB.

Conclusion: Explicit regulatory feedback loops effectively improve deep research agent performance, reducing contamination and error propagation compared to relying on implicit LLM reasoning or model scale as a substitute for regulation.

Abstract: Deep research agents face vast, interdependent, and pervasively uncertain information. Existing systems explore what evolving intermediate representations should look like, but leave their evolution to the LLM's implicit reasoning. Without explicit regulation, the intermediate layer is easily contaminated by mixed-quality information and propagates errors along its dependencies, so model scale often ends up substituting for absent regulation. We argue that an agent's mental model should instead evolve through explicit feedback that continuously aligns task understanding with reality, and identify three regulatory loops: interpretive update, deviation feedback, and schema revision. We realise this in VeriTrace, a cognitive-graph framework that explicitly implements the three loops. Using matched Qwen3.5-27B backbones, VeriTrace improves over the strongest matched baseline by 4.22 pp on DeepResearch Bench (DRB) Insight (1.49 pp Overall) and by 5.9 pp Overall win rate on DeepConsult. With Config-DeepSeek, it achieves the strongest reproducible open-source result on DRB.

</details>


### [523] [Claw-Anything: Benchmarking Always-On Personal Assistants with Broader Access to User's Digital World](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26086)
*Yusong Lin,Xinyuan Liang,Haiyang Wang,Qipeng Gu,Siqi Cheng,Jiangui Chen,Shuzhe Wu,Feiyang Pan,Lue Fan,Sanyuan Zhao,Dandan Tu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Claw-Anything is a new benchmark that evaluates LLM agents in realistic always-on settings with long-term activity histories, multiple backend services, and multi-device GUI/CLI interaction, showing current models like GPT-5.5 achieve only 34.5% pass@1, far below prior benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to capture the broad, always-on setting where agents act as personal assistants with access to all relevant user digital data, limiting context-sensitive reasoning evaluation.

Method: Expands agent context along three dimensions: long-horizon activity histories, interdependent backend services, and integrated GUI/CLI across multiple devices. Uses multi-round event injection to simulate months of user activity with realistic noise.

Result: GPT-5.5 achieves 34.5% pass@1, substantially below prior benchmarks. The released automated pipeline generates 2,000 training environments and improves base model by 23.7%.

Conclusion: There is a significant gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of always-on personal assistance; scalable data infrastructure improves performance but remains insufficient.

Abstract: Large language model agents are increasingly envisioned as always-on personal assistants with access to anything relevant in the user's digital world. Yet current systems operate over only narrow slices of that world, limiting context-sensitive reasoning and effective assistance. Existing benchmarks similarly provide only partial user state and therefore fail to capture performance in such a broad, always-on setting. To address this gap, we introduce Claw-Anything, a benchmark that expands agent context along three dimensions: long-horizon activity histories, interdependent backend services, and integrated GUI and CLI interaction across multiple devices. To instantiate this setting, we simulate months of user activity through multi-round event injection, producing complex world states and realistic noise, including irrelevant events and conflicting signals. Agents must reason over rich contextual environments while remaining robust to such noise. This expanded scope also enables the evaluation of proactive assistance, requiring agents to anticipate user needs and deliver timely recommendations. Experiments show that GPT-5.5 achieves only 34.5% pass@1, substantially below prior benchmarks, underscoring a gap between current agent capabilities and the demands of always-on personal assistance. Alongside the benchmark, we release an automated data-generation pipeline that yields 2,000 training environments and improves the base model by 23.7%, demonstrating its utility of scalable data infrastructure.

</details>


### [524] [From Model Scaling to System Scaling: Scaling the Harness in Agentic AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26112)
*Shangding Gu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper argues that scaling agentic AI requires not just better foundation models but also better system-level design—termed "scaling the harness"—which includes auditable, persistent, modular, and verifiable architectures. It identifies three core bottlenecks (context governance, trustworthy memory, dynamic skill routing), proposes a new evaluation agenda, and introduces CheetahClaws, a Python-native reference harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of agentic AI is overly model-centric, focusing on final-task success while treating components like memory, tool use, orchestration, and verification as secondary. The authors argue this framing is inadequate because agent performance emerges from the interaction of many system components, not just the foundation model.

Method: The paper proposes "scaling the harness"—treating the structured execution layer around a foundation model as a first-class design object. It identifies three core bottlenecks (context governance, trustworthy memory, dynamic skill routing) and develops CheetahClaws, a Python-native reference harness, compared against Claude Code and OpenClaw.

Result: The paper introduces CheetahClaws as a reference harness implementation and proposes a research agenda for harness-level benchmarks that go beyond one-shot task success to measure trajectory quality, memory hygiene, context efficiency, communication fidelity, verification cost, and safe evolution over time.

Conclusion: Future progress in agentic AI will depend as much on system design (scaling the harness) as on stronger foundation models.

Abstract: This paper studies the next major bottleneck in agentic AI as system scaling, not only model scaling: the design of auditable, persistent, modular, and verifiable architectures around foundation models. We refer to this shift as scaling the harness: treating the structured execution layer around a foundation model as a first-class object of design, evaluation, and optimization. Although recent large language models enable agents to use tools, retrieve information, maintain memory, and execute long-horizon workflows, evaluation remains largely model-centric, often reducing agents to final-task success while treating memory, retrieval, tool use, orchestration, verification, and governance as secondary implementation details. This framing is increasingly inadequate because agent performance emerges from the interaction among the foundation model, memory substrate, context constructor, skill-routing layer, orchestration loop, and verification-and-governance layer. Together, these components form the agent harness, which translates model capability into long-horizon agent behavior. We study scaling the harness through three core bottlenecks: context governance, trustworthy memory, and dynamic skill routing, together with the orchestration and governance mechanisms that coordinate and constrain them. We further outline a research agenda for harness-level benchmarks that go beyond one-shot task success to measure trajectory quality, memory hygiene, context efficiency, communication fidelity, verification cost, and safe evolution over time. To make the discussion concrete, we develop CheetahClaws: https://github.com/SafeRL-Lab/cheetahclaws, a Python-native reference harness, and compare it with Claude Code and OpenClaw. Our main claim is that future progress in agentic AI will depend as much on system design as on stronger foundation models.

</details>


### [525] [MobileGym: A Verifiable and Highly Parallel Simulation Platform for Mobile GUI Agent Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26114)
*Dingbang Wu,Rui Hao,Haiyang Wang,Shuzhe Wu,Han Xiao,Zhenghong Li,Bojiang Zhou,Zheng Ju,Zichen Liu,Lue Fan,Zhaoxiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MobileGym is a lightweight, browser-based simulation environment for mobile UI tasks that enables deterministic state-based judging and scalable online RL training, achieving significant performance gains in both simulated and real-device settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobile interaction environments rely on proprietary backends and lack verifiable outcome signals, while also being expensive to scale for online RL training. The authors aim to provide a fully controllable, everyday mobile use environment that overcomes these limitations.

Method: MobileGym uses a browser-hosted architecture with structured JSON state representation for deterministic judging, a layered state model, declarative task-definition framework, and supports hundreds of parallel instances on a single server (~400 MB memory per instance, ~3s cold start). It includes MobileGym-Bench with 416 parameterized task templates across 28 apps.

Result: GRPO on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct achieved +12.8 percentage points on the 256-task test set. Real-device execution on a 59-task subset retained 95.1% of the simulation-side training gain.

Conclusion: MobileGym demonstrates that a lightweight, browser-hosted simulation environment can effectively bridge the sim-to-real gap for mobile UI agents, with practical scalability and strong real-device transfer performance.

Abstract: We present MobileGym, a browser-hosted, lightweight, fully controllable environment for everyday mobile use, targeting interaction fidelity without replicating proprietary backends. It enables two capabilities previously out of reach for everyday apps: verifiable outcome signals through deterministic state-based judging over structured JSON state, and scalable online RL through low-cost parallel rollouts. The full environment state is captured, configured, forked, and compared as structured JSON, and a single server can host hundreds of parallel instances, with about 400 MB memory per instance and about 3 s cold start. A layered state model and a declarative task-definition framework keep state programmability and task creation practical at scale, and a single programmatic judging mechanism delivers both deterministic evaluation verdicts and dense RL rewards. The accompanying MobileGym-Bench provides 416 parameterized task templates, including 256 test and 160 train templates, over 28 apps, with deterministic judges and a structured AnswerSheet protocol that avoids free-text matching failures. In a Sim-to-Real case study, GRPO on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct gains +12.8 percentage points on the 256-task test set, and on a 59-task real-device signal subset, real-device execution retains 95.1% of the simulation-side training gain. Project page: https://mobilegym.github.io.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [526] [Algometrics: Forecasting Under Algorithmic Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23978)
*Marc Schmitt*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces "algometrics," a framework for time series whose evolution depends on the predictive algorithms forecasting them, showing that deployment risk (when forecasts drive actions) is not identifiable from passive historical data, model rankings can invert under crowding, and randomized actions can help identify feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In algorithmic markets, predictive models alter the data-generating process they forecast, creating a feedback loop that makes standard evaluation metrics unreliable for assessing real-world performance.

Method: The paper introduces algometrics, a theoretical framework distinguishing historical risk (passive forecasting) from deployment risk (forecasts driving actions), and proves three formal results about non-identifiability, ranking inversion, and identification via randomization.

Result: Three main results: (1) Deployment risk is not identifiable from passive historical data alone; (2) Historical model rankings can invert under crowding; (3) Randomized or instrumented actions can identify short-horizon linear feedback with finite-sample bounds.

Conclusion: Time-series benchmarks in algorithmic markets should report feedback sensitivity alongside predictive accuracy.

Abstract: In algorithmic markets, predictive models become part of the data-generating process they aim to forecast. Once their outputs are converted into trades, allocations, execution schedules, or risk controls, they change the future data on which they are evaluated. I introduce algometrics, a framework for time series whose evolution depends on the predictive algorithms forecasting them. The framework distinguishes historical risk, measured under passive forecasting, from deployment risk, measured when forecasts drive actions. I prove three results. First, deployment risk is not identifiable from passive historical data alone: even in a one-step linear feedback model, infinitely many algorithm-mediated environments induce the same historical law while implying different deployment risks for the same forecaster. Second, historical model rankings can invert under crowding, so a predictor with lower passive error can have higher deployment error once similar algorithms are adopted. Third, randomized or instrumented actions identify short-horizon linear feedback, and I derive a finite-sample bound for deployment-risk estimation. These results suggest that time-series benchmarks in algorithmic markets should report feedback sensitivity alongside predictive accuracy.

</details>


### [527] [Parameter Efficient Multi-Class Intelligent Scheduling for Multimodal Online Distributed Industrial Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.23984)
*Heqiang Wang,Weihong Yang,Zheyuan Yang,Jia Zhou,Xiaoxiong Zhong,Fangming Liu,Weizhe Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes MODIAD, a distributed online multimodal anomaly detection framework for industrial settings, featuring a Sequential Marginal Gain Greedy algorithm for multi-class scheduling and REC-LoRA for efficient training, achieving superior performance on MVTec 3D-AD and Eyecandies datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing industrial anomaly detection methods are centralized and offline, failing to handle distributed, continuously generated data from heterogeneous sensors. The rise of edge intelligence creates an opportunity for collaborative distributed training, which current approaches do not leverage.

Method: 1) MODIAD framework for multimodal online distributed anomaly detection; 2) Multi-class Intelligent Scheduling (MIS) problem formulation to balance data sufficiency and update frequency; 3) Sequential Marginal Gain Greedy (SMG) algorithm for resource-constrained multi-class training; 4) Resource Efficient Class-Wise Low Rank Adaptation (REC-LoRA) strategy to reduce computational and communication overhead.

Result: Extensive experiments on MVTec 3D-AD and Eyecandies datasets demonstrate superior performance and efficiency compared to existing approaches under the MODIAD scenario.

Conclusion: The proposed MODIAD framework effectively addresses the limitations of centralized offline anomaly detection by enabling distributed, online, and resource-efficient multimodal anomaly detection for real-world industrial environments.

Abstract: Industrial anomaly detection has attracted significant attention as a fundamental challenge in industrial systems. The rapid advancement of heterogeneous industrial sensors has driven industrial anomaly detection from unimodal to multimodal paradigms. However, existing methods are primarily designed for centralized and offline settings, overlooking the distributed and continuously generated data characteristic of real-world industrial environments. With the advancement of edge intelligence, modern edge devices are increasingly capable of not only data acquisition but also distributed model training, enabling collaborative intelligence across the system. Industrial anomaly detection represents a critical application in this context. Motivated by these challenges, we propose a novel framework termed Multimodal Online Distributed Industrial Anomaly Detection (MODIAD). We first present a comprehensive workflow for MODIAD and then formulate a Multi-class Intelligent Scheduling (MIS) problem to coordinate cross class model updates by balancing data sufficiency and class update frequency. To efficiently solve this problem, we design a Sequential Marginal Gain Greedy (SMG) algorithm that enables effective multi-class training under resource constraints. Furthermore, to improve the computational and communication efficiency during training, we propose an Resource Efficient Class-Wise Low Rank Adaptation (REC-LoRA) strategy, which significantly reduces system overhead while preserving detection performance. Extensive experiments on two representative multimodal industrial anomaly detection datasets, MVTec 3D-AD and Eyecandies demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves superior performance and efficiency under the MODIAD scenario.

</details>


### [528] [CAFD: Concept-Aware DNN Fault Detection using VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24008)
*Amin Abbasishahkoo,Mahboubeh Dadkhah,Lionel Briand*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CAFD is a learning-based fault detection method for DNNs that integrates model-based, distance-based, and a novel concept-based feature (CFR) from VLMs, achieving 18.3% average FDR improvement over five baselines across multiple models and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hybrid fault detection approaches for DNNs combine multiple information sources effectively but suffer from substantial computational overhead, limiting their scalability and practicality in real-world settings.

Method: CAFD integrates model-based signals from DNN outputs, distance-based features, and a novel Concept Failure Ratio (CFR) feature that uses VLMs to extract textual concepts from images and quantify failure likelihood. A learning-based model is trained on these informative features to detect faults efficiently.

Result: CAFD consistently outperforms five state-of-the-art baselines across three DNN models and datasets (including ImageNet), achieving average FDR improvements of 18.3% across all investigated subjects and budget sizes.

Conclusion: CFR serves as an effective indicator for DNN fault detection, and CAFD demonstrates superior performance while maintaining practical efficiency, making it suitable for real-world deployment.

Abstract: Fault detection for Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) has received increasing attention in recent years. While more advanced hybrid approaches have been proposed to combine multiple sources of information and outperform earlier techniques, they often incur substantial computational overhead, limiting scalability and practicality in real-world settings. In this paper, we introduce Concept-Aware Fault Detection (CAFD), a learning-based approach that achieves superior fault detection performance by effectively integrating multiple information sources while maintaining practical efficiency. Specifically, CAFD is trained using a carefully selected set of informative features, including model-based signals derived from the DNN's outputs, distance-based features, and a novel concept-based feature, called Concept Failure Ratio (CFR). CFR leverages Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to extract textual concepts from images and quantify the likelihood that their presence is associated with DNN failures. By incorporating this feature, CAFD benefits from complementary semantic information, enabling more effective fault detection. Our results demonstrate that CFR serves as an effective indicator for DNN fault detection. We conduct an extensive empirical evaluation of CAFD, comparing it against five state-of-the-art baselines across three subject DNN models and datasets, including ImageNet. Across a wide range of constrained selection budgets, CAFD consistently outperforms all baselines in Fault Detection Rate (FDR), achieving average FDR improvements of 18.3% across all investigated subjects and budget sizes.

</details>


### [529] [Towards Verifiable Transformers: Solver-Checkable Circuit Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24033)
*Neel Somani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Verifiable Transformers, a framework that converts task-localized Transformer circuits into bounded, solver-checkable claims using SMT-based verification (direct or surrogate-mediated), demonstrating feasibility on small-scale models and GPT-2-scale architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mechanistic interpretability identifies circuits but validates explanations only through examples, ablations, and manual reasoning, leaving a gap between finding plausible circuits and formally proving their behavior.

Method: Two verification approaches: (1) Direct verification encodes the extracted circuit into an SMT solver (using Signed L1 BandNorm, sparsemax attention, LeakyReLU). (2) Surrogate-mediated verification fits an SMT-encodable surrogate for hard-to-encode operators, validates it against the extracted circuit, then verifies symbolic explanations against the surrogate.

Result: On small symbolic tasks, SMT-representable Transformers achieve exhaustive verification of projected functional equivalence, content invariance, edge necessity, and final-residual robustness. At GPT-2 scale, the operator stack trains stably on OpenWebText but direct verification remains intractable; surrogate-mediated verification successfully provides verified symbolic explanations and counterexamples.

Conclusion: The goal is not full-model verification but a concrete path for turning mechanistic circuit explanations into formal propositions that can be proven or refuted.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability often identifies circuits inside Transformer models, but explanations of those circuits are usually validated through examples, ablations, and manual reasoning. This leaves a gap between finding a plausible circuit and proving what the circuit does. We introduce Verifiable Transformers, a framework for converting task-localized Transformer circuits into bounded, solver-checkable claims. Given a behavior, a finite task domain, and a candidate-token projection, we extract a task circuit and verify properties such as projected functional equivalence, edge necessity, task-relevant invariance, and final-residual robustness. Direct verification encodes the extracted circuit itself into an SMT solver. When a circuit contains operators that are not exactly or tractably encodable, surrogate-mediated verification fits an SMT-encodable surrogate, validates it against the extracted circuit over the bounded domain, and verifies symbolic explanations against the surrogate. We instantiate direct verification with a GPT-style architecture using Signed L1 BandNorm, sparsemax attention, and LeakyReLU. On small symbolic sequence tasks, we train an SMT-representable Transformer, extract sparse circuits for quote closing and bracket type tracking, and exhaustively verify projected functional equivalence, content invariance, edge necessity, and final-residual robustness. At GPT-2 scale, the same operator stack trains stably on OpenWebText, although naive direct SMT verification remains intractable. We also demonstrate surrogate-mediated verification on task-localized circuits with hard-to-encode attention, showing both verified symbolic explanations and solver-generated counterexamples. The goal is not full-model verification, but a concrete path for turning mechanistic circuit explanations into formal propositions that can be proven or refuted.

</details>


### [530] [Iterative Refinement Neural Operators are Learned Fixed-Point Solvers: A Principled Approach to Spectral Bias Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24041)
*Xiaotian Liu,Shuyuan Shang,Xiaopeng Wang,Pu Ren,Yaoqing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: IRNO enhances neural operators by adding an iterative refinement module that progressively corrects errors, especially high-frequency ones, achieving significant improvements on turbulent flow and active matter tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural operators suffer from spectral bias, struggling to resolve high-frequency details when using monolithic single-pass inference.

Method: IRNO augments pre-trained operators with a learned refinement module applied via fixed-point iteration, decomposing prediction into coarse initialization followed by residual corrections, and uses a progressive spectral loss that adaptively increases high-frequency penalty during training.

Result: Up to 56.05% error reduction on turbulent flow; normalized error ratios on Active Matter drop to 27.72-36.10% (low-freq), 5.07-6.68% (mid-freq), and 1.48-2.04% (high-freq), with stability beyond trained iteration count.

Conclusion: IRNO effectively mitigates spectral bias in neural operators through iterative refinement and progressive spectral loss, demonstrating strong convergence and generalization properties.

Abstract: Neural operators serve as fast, data-driven surrogates for scientific modeling but typically rely on a monolithic, single-pass inference procedure that struggles to resolve high-frequency details, a limitation known as spectral bias. We introduce the Iterative Refinement Neural Operator (IRNO), which augments pre-trained operators with a learned refinement module iteratively applied via fixed-point iteration. IRNO decomposes the prediction into a coarse initialization followed by successive residual corrections, paralleling classical numerical solvers. Under local assumptions, we establish contraction of the induced operator, ensuring convergence to a unique fixed point. To explicitly target high-frequency errors, we propose a progressive spectral loss that adaptively increases penalty on high-frequency components over refinement steps during training. Across physical systems, IRNO consistently lowers error, with up to 56.05% improvement on turbulent flow. On Active Matter, spectral analysis reveals that, relative to base operator, the normalized error ratios decrease to 27.72-36.10% in low-, 5.07-6.68% in mid-, and 1.48-2.04% in high-frequencies, remaining stable beyond the trained iteration count. Code is available at https://github.com/xiaotianliu-dartmouth/Iterative_Refinement_Neural_Operator

</details>


### [531] [Hidden-State Privacy Has an Empty Middle](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24042)
*Alexander Okezue Bell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper demonstrates that Gaussian release mechanisms for single-layer hidden-state privacy cannot simultaneously achieve moderate utility and privacy, proposes a diagonal inverse-Fisher mechanism as the unique minimax-optimal solution that sits on the privacy/utility edge, and shows that architecture co-design (e.g., split-memory transformers) can substantially outperform pure mechanism-design approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why Gaussian privacy mechanisms for hidden states fail to achieve both utility and privacy, and to find alternatives beyond the Gaussian mechanism-design paradigm.

Method: Empirical testing of 1,536 Gaussian release covariances; Fisher-ball lower bound proof; diagonal inverse-Fisher mechanism derivation; generalized-eigen mechanism analysis; full-trajectory sequence inversion attacks; split-memory transformer training.

Result: No Gaussian release achieves both moderate utility and privacy; diagonal inverse-Fisher is minimax-optimal; generalized-eigen mechanisms fail under adaptive Mahalanobis attacks; split-memory transformers achieve 6-24x advantage over GPT baselines.

Conclusion: Hidden-state privacy requires shifting from pure Gaussian mechanism-design to architecture or release co-design.

Abstract: Of $1{,}536$ Gaussian release covariances we tested for single-layer hidden-state privacy, zero achieve both moderate utility and moderate privacy against an adaptive retrieval attacker. We prove a complementary Fisher-ball lower bound: every full-rank Gaussian release at $O(1)$ Fisher utility admits a direction whose Mahalanobis signal grows linearly in hidden width, ruling out uniform Gaussian safety in the class and matching the empirical empty middle. The diagonal inverse-Fisher release $Σ^\star_{\mathrm{diag}}(\mathcal{K}) = (2\mathcal{K}/d)\,\mathrm{diag}(1/F_{ii})$ is the unique minimax-optimal diagonal mechanism at first-order KL budget $\mathcal{K}$ and the only release with worst-attacker top-1 $\le 0.001$ at every point of a 32 model-layer grid, but it sits on a privacy/utility edge rather than filling the middle. A generalized-eigen mechanism reaching $13\times$ Pareto reduction under Euclidean retrieval collapses to $100\%$ top-1 under the adaptive Mahalanobis attacker, and a full-trajectory sequence inverter recovers $94\%$ of clean GPT-2 prefixes but $0\%$ under $Σ_{\mathrm{diag}}$. A split-memory transformer trained from scratch reaches $G_{\mathrm{Mah}} \in [20, 33]$ at 90M and maintains a $6$--$24\times$ advantage over same-budget GPT baselines from 30M to 1B at a fixed-token language-modeling loss penalty; pretrained models top out at 9.3. These results reframe hidden-state release from mechanism-design within the Gaussian class to architecture or release co-design.

</details>


### [532] [LLM-AutoSciLab: Closed-Loop Scientific Discovery via Active Experimentation with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24043)
*Sanchit Kabra,Nikhil Abhyankar,Saaketh Desai,Prasad Iyer,Chandan K Reddy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LLM-AutoSciLab is a closed-loop framework for scientific discovery that iteratively generates hypotheses, selects informative experiments, and refines mechanisms, outperforming baselines on enzyme-kinetics and gene-regulatory-network tasks with 2-5x greater sample efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific discovery is inherently a closed-loop process, but most approaches treat it as supervised learning over fixed datasets, which can support multiple plausible mechanisms that fail to generalize. The key challenge is selecting informative observations to resolve uncertainty.

Method: LLM-AutoSciLab couples hypothesis generation with hypothesis-conditioned experiment selection and mechanism refinement in an iterative closed loop, actively proposing hypotheses, selecting informative experiments, and updating based on evidence.
