TL;DR: The paper proposes a shared-score quaternion self-attention mechanism that computes a single real-valued score via quaternion inner product, reducing computation by 75% and softmax operations by 75%, while maintaining or improving performance across speech, vision, and NLP tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing quaternion self-attention computes separate scores for each component with independent softmax operations, leading to high computational cost and potentially divergent attention distributions across components.

Method: The shared-score quaternion self-attention mechanism computes a single real-valued attention score using the quaternion inner product and applies a shared attention distribution across all components.

Result: Reduces score-computation multiplications by 75% and softmax operations from four to one; achieves up to 44.3% inference time reduction on GPU and 58.1% on CPU for speech enhancement, with consistent improvements across vision and NLP tasks.

Conclusion: Component-wise and shared scores lie in the same interaction subspace, showing independent component-wise attention mainly re-parameterizes the same interactions rather than expanding the feature space; the proposed method is more efficient without quality loss.

Abstract: Quaternion neural networks are parameter-efficient and model multidimensional dependencies by representing four related features as a single entity. However, existing quaternion self-attention computes component-wise scores and applies independent softmax operations to each component, which increases the computational cost and allows attention distributions to diverge across components. We propose a shared-score quaternion self-attention mechanism that computes a single real-valued score using the quaternion inner product and applies a shared attention distribution across all components. This reduces score-computation multiplications by 75% and the number of softmax operations from four to one. We prove that, when queries and keys are produced by quaternion linear projections that induce component pre-mixing, the component-wise and shared scores lie in the same interaction subspace, indicating that independent component-wise attention primarily re-parameterizes the same interactions rather than expanding the feature interaction space. In speech enhancement, our method reduces inference time by up to 44.3% on a GPU and 58.1% on a CPU while maintaining quality, with consistent trends across vision and natural language processing.

</details>


### [638] [BandVQ: Band-Wise Vector-Quantized EEG Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24921)
*Jamiyan Sukhbaatar,Satoshi Imamura,Toshihisa Tanaka*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BandVQ proposes a band-wise vector-quantized EEG foundation model that decomposes EEG into frequency bands, trains independent VQ-VAE tokenizers per band, and pretrains a shared Transformer on discrete code indices, achieving strong transfer performance on cognitive and motor imagery tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EEG masked modeling approaches use broadband continuous patches or single discrete representations that fail to capture frequency-specific brain activity, limiting transferable representations across diverse recording setups.

Method: Decomposes EEG into delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma bands; trains independent VQ-VAE tokenizer per band; pretrains shared Transformer encoder on masked code tokens, quantized log-power tokens, channel/temporal embeddings, and metadata prefix tokens; uses region-based masking to avoid trivial reconstruction.

Result: Pretrained on 71 public corpora (9,200+ subjects, 357K single-channel hours). Achieves highest reported results on three cognitive tasks and competitive performance on three motor imagery tasks across six subject-independent classification datasets.

Conclusion: Band-wise discrete tokenization with frequency-specific VQ-VAE and region-based masking enables effective transferable EEG representations, outperforming prior approaches on cognitive tasks while remaining competitive on motor imagery.

Abstract: A central challenge in electroencephalography (EEG) foundation modeling is learning transferable representations across recordings with diverse tasks, montages, references, and spectral characteristics. Existing masked modeling approaches often rely on broadband continuous patches or a single discrete representation, which may underrepresent frequency-specific activity. This paper proposes BandVQ, a band-wise vector-quantized EEG foundation model that decomposes EEG into delta, theta, alpha, beta, and gamma bands, trains an independent VQ-VAE tokenizer for each band, and pretrains a shared Transformer encoder on the resulting discrete VQ code indices. The encoder uses masked code tokens, quantized absolute log-power tokens, channel and temporal embeddings, and metadata prefix tokens representing reference, band, task family, and phase. Region-based masking is also introduced to reduce the trivial reconstruction of spatially adjacent electrodes. The model is pretrained on 71 public EEG corpora comprising over 9,200 subjects and 357,000 single-channel hours and evaluated on six subject-independent classification datasets. Under the current evaluation setting, the proposed model achieves strong transfer performance, with the highest reported results on three cognitive tasks and competitive performance on three motor imagery tasks.

</details>


### [639] [Global linear convergence of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond tabular MDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24939)
*Ziyue Chen,David Šiška,Lukasz Szpruch*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves global linear convergence (O(e^{-Ct})) of policy gradient for entropy-regularized MDPs with continuous state/action spaces using log-linear softmax policies with linear function approximation, establishing a non-uniform Polyak-Łojasiewicz inequality and identifying two feature regimes (full-affine-span and simplex-valued) where convergence holds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To extend the global convergence theory of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond the tabular setting to continuous state and action spaces with function approximation.

Method: Uses log-linear softmax policies with linear function approximation; establishes a non-uniform PŁ inequality; analyzes two feature regimes (full-affine-span and simplex-valued features) to bound the non-uniform constant along gradient flow.

Result: Global linear convergence of the regularized objective along gradient flow with rate O(e^{-Ct}); radial unboundedness of KL regularizer; uniform lower bounds on smallest eigenvalues of Fisher information matrix or uncentered covariance matrix depending on feature regime.

Conclusion: The global convergence theory for entropy-regularized policy gradient extends to continuous state/action spaces under Q realizability and specific feature conditions, marking progress beyond tabular settings.

Abstract: We study the global convergence of policy gradient for infinite-horizon entropy-regularized Markov decision processes (MDPs) with continuous state and action spaces. We consider log-linear softmax policies with linear function approximation, which extend the tabular softmax parameterization while retaining a tractable policy class. Under $Q^π_τ$-realizability for the regularized state-action value function, we first establish a non-uniform Polyak--Łojasiewicz (PŁ) inequality. The non-uniformity arises through degeneracy of constants associated with the policy geometry, namely the Fisher information matrix or an uncentered feature covariance matrix. We then identify two feature regimes under which this non-uniform constant can be bounded along the gradient flow. For full-affine-span features, we prove radial unboundedness of the KL regularizer and show that the smallest eigenvalue of the Fisher information matrix remains bounded below by an initialization-dependent positive constant. For simplex-valued features, we prove an analogous radial unboundedness result in the subspace orthogonal to the all-ones vector and obtain a uniform lower bound for the smallest eigenvalue of the uncentered covariance matrix. These results imply global linear convergence of the regularized objective along the gradient flow, i.e. suboptimality decaying as $\mathcal{O}(e^{-Ct})$ for some $C>0$. Our analysis extends the global convergence theory of entropy-regularized softmax policy gradient beyond the tabular setting of Agarwal et al. (2020); Bhandari and Russo (2024); Mei et al. (2020).

</details>


### [640] [Riemannian-Manifold Steering: Geometry-Aware Generative Autoencoders for Label-Free Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24942)
*Narmeen Oozeer,Shivam Raval,Philip Quirke,Manikandan Ravikiran,Jeff Phillips,Shriyash Upadhyay,Amirali Abdullah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Riemannian geodesic framework for steering language models by intervening on internal activations, using a learned metric based on output-space Hellinger distance that requires no labelled centroids, topology priors, or per-task curve fitting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing manifold steering methods require labelled class centroids and prescribed cyclic/sequential structure, limiting their applicability. The authors aim to remove these restrictive assumptions and unify various steering approaches under a principled Riemannian geometry framework.

Method: The authors recast manifold steering as Riemannian geodesic computation on activation space. They derive a principled metric using the output-space Hellinger distance pulled back to activations, approximated by a learned encoder trained on output distances over a small concept-token schema - requiring no per-prompt labels, topology prior, or per-task curve fitting.

Result: The method reliably drives the model onto the target class across all tasks in a standard four-task language-model arithmetic benchmark. It follows more behaviourally natural trajectories than baselines on smaller output spaces.

Conclusion: The authors provide a unified Riemannian framework for manifold steering along with a schema-supervised, label-free instantiation that operates without labelled centroids or prescribed boundary conditions.

Abstract: Steering a language model - intervening on its internal activations to change downstream behaviour - has recently expanded beyond linear interpolation to nonlinear methods such as angular and kernelized steering, which define intervention transformations without learning an explicit geometry over paths in activation space. Freshly introduced geometry-aware manifold methods do learn such a geometry, but require labelled class centroids together with prescribed cyclic or sequential structure. These assumptions restrict where manifold steering can be applied, since existing constructions require labelled centroids and compatible boundary conditions. We recast manifold steering more broadly as \textbf{Riemannian geodesic computation} on activation space, recovering linear and labelled-spline steering as geodesics under particular choices of metric. A principled metric within this framework is the output-space Hellinger distance pulled back to activations; we approximate this with a learned encoder trained on output distances over a small concept-token schema - no per-prompt labels, no topology prior, and no per-task curve fitting. Empirically, the method reliably drives the model onto the target class across all tasks in a standard four-task language-model arithmetic benchmark, while following more behaviourally natural trajectories than baselines on smaller output spaces. We thereby provide a unified Riemannian framework for manifold steering together with a schema-supervised, label-free instantiation that operates without labelled centroids or prescribed boundary conditions.

</details>


### [641] [RealBench: Benchmarking Data-Driven Numerical Weather Forecasting Under Operational Conditions and Extreme Event Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24945)
*Ruize Li,Zhibin Wen,Tao Han,Hao Chen,Fenghua Ling,Wei Zhang,Song Guo,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RealBench is a new benchmark for AI weather forecasting that evaluates models under real operational conditions using out-of-distribution data (2025), multiple data sources including 10,000+ observation stations, and extreme event metrics, revealing significant gaps between traditional reanalysis-based evaluations and real-world performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks rely on reanalysis products (e.g., ERA5) that use delayed data assimilation and don't reflect real-time operational forecasting constraints, causing a systematic mismatch between benchmark scores and actual forecasting performance.

Method: RealBench uses a strictly out-of-distribution test set (2025-), integrates low-latency operational analysis and global in-situ observations from 10,000+ stations, and provides event-specific metrics for extreme events like heatwaves, cold surges, and tropical cyclones.

Result: Evaluation results show substantial discrepancies between reanalysis-based metrics and real-world performance, especially for extreme events, indicating that current benchmarks overestimate model capabilities in operational settings.

Conclusion: RealBench establishes a more faithful, operationally relevant evaluation paradigm for AI weather forecasting, highlighting the limitations of existing benchmarks and providing a rigorous foundation for advancing next-generation forecasting systems.

Abstract: Accurate evaluation of weather forecasting models is critical for their reliable deployment in real-world applications. However, existing benchmarks predominantly rely on reanalysis products such as ERA5, which are generated through delayed data assimilation and do not reflect the constraints of real-time operational forecasting, thereby resulting in a systematic mismatch between benchmark performance and real-world forecasting. In this work, we introduce RealBench, a next-generation benchmark for AI weather forecasting that emphasizes realistic evaluation under operational conditions. RealBench features a strictly out-of-distribution test set spanning 2025 to eliminate data leakage and capture recent atmospheric regimes. It integrates multiple data sources, including low-latency operational analysis and a large-scale global in-situ observation dataset comprising over 10,000 stations, enabling direct evaluation against real atmospheric measurements. Beyond standard global metrics, RealBench provides a comprehensive evaluation framework for high-impact extreme events, including heatwaves, cold surges, and tropical cyclones, using event-specific metrics that better reflect real-world forecasting priorities. The evaluation results reveal substantial discrepancies between reanalysis-based metrics and real-world performance, particularly concerning extreme events. By highlighting the limitations of existing benchmarks, this work establishes a more faithful and operationally relevant evaluation paradigm, providing a rigorous foundation for advancing next-generation AI weather forecasting systems. The benchmark implementation is available at: https://github.com/lixruize-del/NWP-Benchmark.

</details>


### [642] [MedMamba: Multi-View State Space Models with Adaptive Graph Learning for Medical Time Series Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24961)
*Da Zhang,Bingyu Li,Zhiyuan Zhao,Hongyuan Zhang,Junyu Gao,Xuelong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MedMamba integrates state space models with domain-specific inductive biases for medical time series analysis, using multi-scale convolutional embeddings, a tri-branch differential state space encoder for nonstationarity, and a spatial graph Mamba module for latent channel interactions, achieving SOTA performance with linear complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods struggle to jointly model local-global dynamics, handle nonstationarities (e.g., baseline drift), and capture latent channel interactions in medical time series.

Method: (1) Multi-scale convolutional embeddings for local morphology; (2) Tri-branch differential state space encoder processing raw, temporal-difference, and frequency-domain views to suppress drift; (3) Spatial graph Mamba module learning directed dependency structure with sparsity/acyclicity regularization.

Result: State-of-the-art performance on five real-world datasets with linear computational complexity; ablation studies validate each component's contribution.

Conclusion: MedMamba effectively addresses key challenges in medical time series modeling through a principled integration of state space models, differential encoding, and graph learning, offering both high performance and computational efficiency.

Abstract: Medical time series are central to healthcare, enabling continuous monitoring and supporting timely clinical decisions. Despite recent progress, existing methods struggle to jointly model local-global dynamics and handle nonstationarities like baseline drift, while often failing to capture latent channel interactions. To address these challenges, we propose MedMamba, an end-to-end architecture that integrates state space models with domain-specific inductive biases. Specifically, MedMamba first employs multi-scale convolutional embeddings to capture discriminative local morphology. Second, to mitigate nonstationarity, we introduce a tri-branch differential state space encoder that processes raw, temporal-difference, and frequency-domain views, fusing them to emphasize informative patterns while suppressing drift. Furthermore, to uncover latent channel correlations, we design a spatial graph Mamba module that learns a directed dependency structure regularized toward sparsity and acyclicity, which obviates the need for predefined graphs. Extensive experiments on five real-world datasets demonstrate that MedMamba achieves state-of-the-art performance while maintaining linear computational complexity, and ablation studies validate each component's contribution.Code is available at https://github.com/zhangda1018/MedMamba.

</details>


### [643] [OSDTW: Optimal Shared Depth and Task Weighting for Long-Tailed Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24969)
*Chang Chu,Qingyue Zhang,Shao-Lun Huang,Junxiong Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OSDTW is a principled task-decomposition framework for long-tailed recognition that partitions the problem into head and tail tasks with a shared encoder and task-specific decoders, using Fisher-based second-order expansion and bias-variance decomposition for hyper-parameter selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-tailed recognition suffers from a persistent head-tail trade-off where improving tail performance degrades head accuracy, and key design choices about representation sharing and supervision weighting remain heuristic.

Method: Proposes OSDTW with: (1) a factorized model splitting recognition into head and tail tasks with shared encoder and task-specific decoders; (2) KL divergence-based generalization error decomposition into task-wise terms; (3) three-stage training pipeline (independent task training, weighted joint training, branch assembly); (4) block-diagonal Fisher approximation for bias-variance decomposition to guide hyper-parameter search.

Result: Experiments on standard long-tailed benchmarks demonstrate effectiveness over strong baselines.

Conclusion: The proposed principled framework with theoretical grounding (Fisher-based second-order expansion and bias-variance decomposition) enables efficient hyper-parameter selection and achieves superior long-tailed recognition performance.

Abstract: Long-tailed recognition suffers from a persistent head--tail trade-off: improving tail performance often degrades head accuracy and can increase training instability. Despite strong empirical results from re-weighting, decoupled training, and multi-expert methods, key design choices about representation sharing between head and tail classes and supervision weighting across class groups remain largely heuristic. In this work, we propose OSDTW, a principled task-decomposition framework that partitions the original single-label recognition problem into a head task and a tail task, implemented with a shared encoder and task-specific decoders. To handle the mutual exclusivity and statistical dependence between the two label groups, we introduce a factorized model and show that the resulting Kullback--Leibler divergence-based generalization error can be written as the sum of task-wise terms up to an additive constant, yielding a well-defined task-wise objective. We further develop a three-stage training pipeline: independent task training to estimate task-wise optima and the Fisher information matrix, weighted joint training to learn a shared encoder, and branch assembly to construct the final decoupled model. Under a block-diagonal Fisher approximation, we derive a computable second-order expansion of the expected generalization error, decomposing it into encoder variance, encoder bias, and decoder variance. This bias--variance decomposition provides a computable proxy to select the shared depth and task weights, enabling efficient hyper-parameter search. Experiments on standard long-tailed benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach over strong baselines.

</details>


### [644] [TGFormer: Towards Temporal Graph Transformer with Auto-Correlation Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24971)
*Hongjiang Chen,Pengfei Jiao,Ming Du,Xuan Guo,Zhidong Zhao,Di Jin,Xiao Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TGFormer is a novel Transformer architecture for temporal graphs that uses a trajectory framework and auto-correlation mechanism to better capture long-term dependencies and periodic patterns, achieving up to 9.35% precision improvement over state-of-the-art methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing TGNNs struggle to capture long-term dependencies and identify periodic patterns in temporal graph data, limiting their effectiveness for complex dynamic modeling.

Method: TGFormer introduces a trajectory framework aligned with time series analysis to derive node representations from historical interactions. It employs an auto-correlation mechanism based on stochastic process theory to discover periodic dependencies and perform sub-interaction-level representation aggregation.

Result: Validated on six public benchmarks, TGFormer achieves up to 9.35% precision improvement compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed trajectory framework and auto-correlation mechanism effectively address TGNN limitations, offering superior efficiency and accuracy in capturing temporal and periodic dependencies.

Abstract: The growing interest in Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) stems from their ability to model complex dynamics and deliver superior performance. However, TGNNs encounter fundamental challenges in capturing long-term dependencies and identifying periodic patterns. To address these limitations, we propose TGFormer, a novel Transformer architecture specifically designed for temporal graphs. Our model redefines temporal graph learning by establishing a trajectory framework that aligns with time series analysis principles. This approach allows TGFormer to derive node representations through systematic analysis of historical interactions, enabling granular examination of node relationships across sequential timestamps. Building upon stochastic process theory, we develop an auto-correlation mechanism that systematically uncovers periodic dependencies in node interactions. This innovation empowers TGFormer to perform dependency discovery and representation aggregation at sub-interaction levels, demonstrating superior efficiency and accuracy compared to conventional attention mechanisms. Experimental validation across six public benchmarks confirms the effectiveness of our approach, with TGFormer at most achieving 9.35\% precision improvement compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

</details>


### [645] [Benchmarking non-conformity score functions in conformal prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24983)
*Sol Erika Boman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reviews non-conformity score functions for conformal prediction, introduces original modifications, proposes a new evaluation method for prediction set sizes, and compares various functions especially in class-imbalanced settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite many non-conformity score functions existing in the literature, there is a lack of systematic studies examining their properties and effectiveness.

Method: The authors provide an overview of properties of non-conformity score functions, introduce original modifications, and propose a novel evaluation method for comparing prediction set sizes. They also analyze class-conditional conformal prediction under imbalanced classes.

Result: The paper offers a structured comparison of various non-conformity score functions and their impact on prediction set sizes, along with insights into their effectiveness for imbalanced class settings.

Conclusion: The study fills a gap in the conformal prediction literature by systematically analyzing and comparing non-conformity score functions, providing guidance for practitioners on choosing appropriate scores.

Abstract: Conformal prediction is a useful and versatile alternative to model calibration in machine learning classification. It replaces single-class prediction with prediction sets, guaranteeing that the \textit{a priori} probability of the prediction sets containing the true class is larger than or equal to a pre-specified rate. The size and usefulness of the prediction sets relies heavily on the choice of the non-conformity score function. The scientific literature contains many examples of non-conformity score functions but there is an absence of studies examining their properties and effectiveness. In this paper, we give an overview of properties of non-conformity score functions. We give examples of non-conformity score functions in the existing literature and introduce original modifications. We introduce an original method of evaluating the prediction set sizes of conformal predictors and use it to provide a comparison between non-conformity score functions. We also examine efficacy of different non-conformity score functions for class-conditional conformal prediction in a setting with imbalanced classes.

</details>


### [646] [Selective Test-Time Compute Scaling for Click-Through Rate Prediction via Uncertainty-Triggered Feature Path Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24989)
*Moyu Zhang,Yun Chen,Yujun Jin,Jinxin Hu,Yu Zhang,Xiaoyi Zeng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UTTSI is a training-free framework that scales test-time computation for CTR prediction based on per-instance uncertainty, using dual-signal uncertainty estimation, adaptive feature filtering, and stochastic feature-path exploration to improve predictions for uncertain instances while keeping average overhead low (~2.8x base cost). It achieves consistent gains across datasets and a 5.3% relative CTR lift in online A/B test.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: CTR prediction suffers from asymmetry where well-represented feature combinations yield confident predictions but sparse ones produce unreliable outputs. Existing training-phase solutions (e.g., adaptive gating) learn fixed selection functions subject to the same sparsity, offering no per-instance recourse at deployment.

Method: UTTSI comprises: (1) a dual-signal estimator combining model logit confidence with data-level frequency prior to distinguish epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty; (2) adaptive feature filtering to remove unreliable embeddings; (3) stochastic feature-path explorations for uncertain instances with consistency-weighted ensembling; (4) confident instances bypass exploration to control overhead (~2.8x base cost, worst-case latency unchanged).

Result: Consistent statistically significant gains across four datasets and three backbone architectures over training-phase baselines. Seven-day online A/B test shows 5.3% relative CTR gain (p < 0.01).

Conclusion: Selective test-time compute allocation is a practical and effective complement to training-phase advances for CTR prediction, establishing uncertainty-guided inference depth scaling as a viable direction in industrial recommendation systems.

Abstract: Scaling test-time compute has proven highly effective for language models, yet this opportunity remains largely unexplored for industrial Click-Through Rate (CTR) prediction. CTR models suffer from a fundamental asymmetry: feature combinations well-represented in training yield confident predictions, while sparsely observed ones produce unreliable outputs. Existing training-phase solutions such as adaptive gating learn a fixed selection function subject to the same sparsity, offering no per-instance recourse at deployment.We propose UTTSI (Uncertainty-Triggered Test-Time Selective Inference), a training-free model-agnostic framework that scales inference depth proportionally to per-instance uncertainty. A dual-signal estimator combining model logit confidence with a data-level frequency prior distinguishes epistemic uncertainty from aleatoric ambiguity. Every instance undergoes adaptive feature filtering to remove unreliable embeddings; uncertain instances additionally receive stochastic feature-path explorations whose predictions are aggregated via consistency-weighted ensembling. Confident instances bypass exploration entirely, keeping average overhead at approximately $2.8\times$ base model cost with worst-case latency unchanged.Experiments on four datasets with three backbone architectures demonstrate consistent, statistically significant gains over all training-phase baselines. A seven-day online A/B test further confirms a 5.3% relative CTR gain ($p < 0.01$), establishing selective test-time compute allocation as a practical complement to training-phase advances for CTR prediction.

</details>


### [647] [Mitigating Gradient Pathology in PINNs through Aligned Constraint](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25001)
*Yichen Luo,Peiyu Zhu,Dongxiao Hu,Jia Wang,Tailin Wu,Dapeng Lan,Yu Liu,Zhibo Pang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Constraint-Aligned loss with Manifold Lifting (CAML) to resolve gradient pathology in PINNs by reformulating zeroth-order terms into aligned constraints and using a delay factor to skip high-curvature areas, significantly improving stability and efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient pathology in PINNs causes training paralysis due to opposing gradients from PDE residuals and boundary constraints, which existing methods fail to fundamentally resolve.

Method: CAML reformulates all zeroth-order terms into aligned constraints to mitigate gradient conflicts, and introduces a delay factor to help the optimizer skip high-curvature regions.

Result: CAML significantly enhances numerical stability and efficiency in highly complex PINN problems.

Conclusion: The proposed CAML effectively resolves gradient pathology in PINNs, offering a fundamental improvement over adaptive weighting and hard constraint approaches.

Abstract: While Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are powerful for solving Partial Differential Equations (PDEs), their training is often paralyzed by gradient pathology. The gradients from the PDE residuals and boundary constraints oppose each other, trapping the model in local minima. Current solutions, such as adaptive weighting or hard constraints, either fail to fundamentally resolve this ill-conditioning or are limited to simple geometries. In this study, we systematically analyze the possible causes of this gradient pathology from the perspectives of loss landscapes and optimization dynamics. Based on the obtained conclusion, we propose Constraint-Aligned loss with Manifold Lifting (CAML). By reformulating all zeroth-order terms into aligned constraints, our method effectively mitigates gradient conflicts. In addition, we introduce a delay factor to help the optimizer skip the high-curvature area. Experiments demonstrate that our CAML significantly enhances numerical stability and efficiency in highly complex PINN problems. Our code is open-sourced on https://github.com/YichenLuo-0/CAML.

</details>


### [648] [Metropolis-Scale Resilient and Trustworthy Traffic Flow Inference Using Multi-Source Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25004)
*Qishen Zhou,Yifan Zhang,Michail A. Makridis,Anastasios Kouvelas,Yibing Wang,Simon Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TA-ANP is a unified probabilistic framework that infers network-wide traffic states from sparse sensor data using task-aware attention and neural processes, achieving SOTA performance on a new large-scale dataset (MMTD) with robust uncertainty quantification and resilience under changing sensing conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traffic state inference from sparse observations is challenging due to underdetermination, sensor disturbances, and conflicting sub-tasks when modeled jointly. Existing methods lack adaptability, uncertainty quantification, and resilience.

Method: TA-ANP casts GTSI as a stochastic process using neural processes with meta-learning for fast adaptation. A task-aware multi-query attention module with distinct spatiotemporal inductive biases handles three GTSI sub-tasks jointly while mitigating cross-task interference. Uncertainty is captured via neural processes + Monte Carlo Dropout for aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty.

Result: TA-ANP achieves state-of-the-art performance on MMTD (2,371 road segments) across all sub-tasks under deterministic and probabilistic metrics. Well-calibrated uncertainties enable more efficient sensor placement, and the method shows superior resilience under a Damage-Repair-Addition sensing lifecycle.

Conclusion: TA-ANP provides a resilient, trustworthy, and unified solution for global traffic state inference with effective uncertainty quantification and adaptability to dynamic sensing configurations.

Abstract: Inferring network-wide traffic states from sparse observations with high accuracy and trustworthy uncertainty quantification is essential for intelligent transportation systems, yet it remains challenging due to the underdetermined nature of the problem, multifaceted disturbances in sensing networks, and the inherent conflicts among multiple inference sub-tasks when modeled jointly. We propose the Task-Aware Attentive Neural Process (TA-ANP), a unified probabilistic framework for resilient and trustworthy global traffic state inference (GTSI) by fusing floating car data (FCD) with sparse fixed-detector measurements. By casting GTSI as a stochastic process, TA-ANP leverages the meta-learning properties of neural processes to adapt rapidly to changes in sensing configurations without retraining. A task-aware multi-query attention module with distinct spatiotemporal inductive biases is introduced to jointly handle three GTSI sub-tasks, while mitigating cross-task interference. For uncertainty quantification, we combine neural processes with Monte Carlo Dropout to capture both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. To support metropolis-scale evaluation, we construct the Metropolitan Multi-Source Traffic Dataset (MMTD), integrating fixed-loop sensor measurements, FCD statistics, and OpenStreetMap road-network data over an urban network of 2,371 road segments. Experiments on MMTD show that TA-ANP achieves state-of-the-art performance across all sub-tasks under deterministic and probabilistic metrics. The resulting well-calibrated uncertainties enable more efficient fixed-sensor placement with fewer sensor deployments. Under a Damage-Repair-Addition sensing lifecycle, TA-ANP demonstrates superior resilience in terms of disturbance absorption, performance recovery, and adaptability to unseen sensing configurations.

</details>


### [649] [A perspective on fluid mechanical environments for challenges in reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25011)
*Shruti Mishra,Michael Chang,Vamsi Spandan,Shmuel M. Rubinstein*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using fluid mechanics simulations (Dedalus, JAX-CFD) as testbeds for developing reinforcement learning agents that can handle high-dimensional, nonstationary environments, demonstrating initial results with agents navigating a stationary simulated flow.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop RL agents that efficiently interact with high-dimensional, evolving environments (open worlds) by leveraging preserved invariances across changing dynamics, using nonlinear fluid instability problems (droplet breakup, mixing, rogue waves) as compelling testbeds with real-world industrial and scientific applications.

Method: The authors present two problem descriptions defining state/action spaces and reward functions for RL agents in fluid mechanical environments, specify nonstationary aspects and preserved invariances, and utilize open-source simulators Dedalus and JAX-CFD for environment generation. They demonstrate proof-of-concept by training RL agents to navigate in a stationary Dedalus-simulated environment.

Result: Demonstrated the use of Dedalus for environment generation by creating RL agents that learn to navigate in a stationary simulated fluid environment, establishing a foundation for future work on nonstationary fluid interaction tasks.

Conclusion: Fluid mechanics simulations provide a rich, scientifically meaningful testbed for developing RL methods capable of handling nonstationary, high-dimensional environments, with the potential to address open scientific challenges in natural and industrial flows.

Abstract: We consider the challenge of developing agents that efficiently interact with high-dimensional, evolving environments, towards a view of practical reinforcement learning (RL) agents interacting with open worlds, of which they witness and affect only a small part. We argue that canonical fluid mechanics problems, and their simulations, present a compelling testbed for the development of such methods. These problems arise in nonlinear instabilities, where small disturbances can grow to transform the dynamics of a system. Nonlinear instabilities represent several open scientific challenges with industrial applications -- the droplet breakup of a liquid jet, mixing at an interface between two fluids, and the appearance of unusually tall rogue waves in the ocean. In these settings, agents may leverage preserved representations across the changing dynamics to learn efficiently.
  We present two problem descriptions of agents interacting with a fluid mechanical environment, and describe the state and action spaces, and reward functions, for these agents. For these examples, we specify the aspects of the environment which are nonstationary and the preserved invariances. We note Dedalus and JAX-CFD as open-source simulators that can be used for the development of reinforcement learning methods (Burns et al., 2016; Kochkov et al., 2021)) We demonstrate the use of Dedalus for environment generation by creating RL agents that learn to navigate in a stationary environment that is simulated using Dedalus. This sets the stage for future development of RL agents that learn to meaningfully interact with simulated environments that represent scientific challenges in natural and industrial flows.

</details>


### [650] [MimirRAG: A Multi-Agent RAG Framework for Financial Data Retrieval with Metadata Integration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25030)
*Magnus Samuelsen,Wilmer Nyström,Somnath Mazumdar,Mansoor Hussain,Mikkel Strange*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MimirRAG is a multi-agent RAG system designed for financial document analysis that achieves 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench by integrating metadata, table-aware chunking, and agentic workflows, with expert validation highlighting the importance of calibrated trust, data integration, and personalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs suffer from hallucinations and lack grounding in verifiable evidence for financial analysis; existing RAG systems struggle to extract meaningful insights from mixed financial documents and integrate into analyst workflows.

Method: MimirRAG uses a modular pipeline with structure-preserving PDF parsing, table-aware chunking, metadata extraction, agent-based retrieval (query planning + hybrid search), validation, and context-aware generation with numerical reasoning support.

Result: Achieved 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench, outperforming original baselines. Ablation study identified three key enablers: metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and agentic workflow. Expert feedback emphasized calibrated trust, data integration, and personalization for deployment.

Conclusion: Combining multi-agent RAG architecture with human-centric design principles can improve extraction of meaningful insights in financial analysis, but deployment success additionally requires trust calibration, comprehensive data integration, and user personalization.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems offer a promising approach to reduce hallucinations and improve answer accuracy in large language models (LLMs), a requirement for reliable, financial analysis where answers must be grounded in verifiable evidence from filings rather than generated from model priors. However, designing RAG systems that extract meaningful insights from mixed financial documents and integrate into analyst workflows remains challenging. This paper introduces MimirRAG (Metadata-Integrated Multi-Agent Information Retrieval), a multi-agent RAG system developed iteratively to address these challenges. MimirRAG features a modular pipeline encompassing structure-preserving parsing of PDF filings, table-aware chunking, metadata extraction, agent-based retrieval with query planning and hybrid search, validation, and context-aware generation with numerical reasoning support. Our ablation study identifies three key technical enablers for effective financial RAG: metadata integration, table-aware chunking, and an agentic workflow. MimirRAG was evaluated quantitatively using FinanceBench and qualitatively through expert validation with four financial analysts. The system achieved 89.3% accuracy on FinanceBench, outperforming the original benchmark baselines. Expert feedback highlighted that successful deployment also requires calibrated trust, comprehensive data integration, and user personalization. We conclude that combining multi-agent RAG architecture with human-centric design principles can improve the extraction of meaningful insights in financial analysis.

</details>


### [651] [Scale When Needed: Adaptive Neuron-level Mixed Precision Quantization Aware Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25054)
*Ayush K. Varshney,Konstantinos Vandikas,Šarūnas Girdzijauskas,Adam Orucu,Aneta Vulgarakis Feljan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NMP-QAT enables each neuron to independently learn its optimal bit-width during training, achieving superior compression-accuracy trade-offs for deploying DNNs on resource-constrained 6G edge devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mixed-precision QAT methods operate at coarse granularity (layer/channel-level) and rely on heuristic/search-based bit allocation, overlooking fine-grained neuron-level variability in resource-constrained 6G edge deployments.

Method: Proposes Neuron-Level Mixed-Precision QAT (NMP-QAT) where each neuron independently learns its discrete precision via differentiable surrogates and straight-through estimators, starting from low-bit and expanding only when needed, applied to both weights and activations.

Result: Evaluated on telecom and non-telecom datasets with MLP and tabular foundation model architectures, NMP-QAT achieves superior compression-accuracy trade-offs compared to existing mixed-precision QAT baselines.

Conclusion: NMP-QAT is well-suited for Green AI deployments at the network edge, offering fine-grained adaptive quantization with minimal accuracy loss.

Abstract: Deploying deep neural networks on resource-constrained 6G edge devices demands aggressive compression with minimal accuracy loss. Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) has emerged as a leading compression approach; however, existing mixed-precision methods typically operate at coarse layer- or channel-level granularity. These methods often rely on heuristic or search-based bit-allocation strategies, which may overlook fine-grained variability at the neuron level. We propose Neuron-Level Mixed-Precision QAT (NMP-QAT), where each neuron independently learns its own discrete precision during training. Starting from low-bit precision, NMP-QAT expands bit-width only when training signals demand it, via differentiable surrogates and straight-through estimators, while preserving a fully discrete inference graph. This adaptability extends to both weights and activations, reducing memory movement. Evaluated on telecom and non-telecom datasets across MLP and tabular foundation model architectures, NMP-QAT achieves superior compression-accuracy trade-offs over mixed-precision QAT baselines, making it well-suited for Green AI deployments at the network edge.

</details>


### [652] [GL-LFGNN:A Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network Based on Liang-Kleeman Information Flow for EEG Emotion Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25061)
*Ziyi Wang,Dongyang Kuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GL-LFGNN uses causal (not just correlational) graph neural networks based on Liang-Kleeman information flow theory for EEG emotion recognition, achieving ~86% accuracy with only 37K parameters (10% of SOTA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GNN-based EEG emotion recognition methods use symmetric adjacency matrices (spatial proximity or functional correlations) that capture statistical associations rather than directed causal influences, conflicting with the asymmetric, causally-driven nature of neural information flow.

Method: A Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network (GL-LFGNN) grounded in Liang-Kleeman information flow theory, which quantifies causal strength from a dynamical systems perspective (unlike Granger causality which only assesses temporal precedence), producing neurophysiologically interpretable directed graphs. A dual-branch architecture integrates whole-brain connectivity with region-specific processing aligned to functional neuroanatomy.

Result: On MEEG dataset: 86.17% (Arousal) and 86.71% (Valence) accuracy with only 37K parameters (~10% of current SOTA).

Conclusion: Principled causal modeling can simultaneously enhance interpretability, generalization, and computational efficiency in EEG-based emotion recognition.

Abstract: EEG-based emotion recognition holds significant promise for objective diagnosis of mood disorders. Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as the dominant paradigm for modeling inter-channel dependencies in EEG, yet existing approaches rely on symmetric adjacency matrices derived from spatial proximity or functional correlations that fundamentally capture statistical associations rather than directed causal influences, which conflicts with the inherently asymmetric, causally-driven nature of neural information flow. To bridge this gap, we propose GL-LFGNN, a Global-Local Dual-branch Causal Graph Neural Network grounded in Liang-Kleeman information flow theory. Unlike Granger causality that merely assesses temporal precedence, our approach rigorously quantifies causal strength from a dynamical systems perspective, yielding neurophysiologically interpretable directed graphs. A dual-branch architecture further integrates whole-brain connectivity with region-specific processing aligned to established functional neuroanatomy. On the MEEG dataset, GL-LFGNN achieves 86.17% (Arousal) and 86.71% (Valence) accuracy with only 37K parameters -- approximately 10% of the current state-of-the-art -- demonstrating that principled causal modeling can simultaneously enhance interpretability, generalization, and computational efficiency. Code will be released.

</details>


### [653] [Reinforcement Learning for Laser Additive Manufacturing Scan-Order Optimisation: A Bilevel Proxy--FEA Diagnostic Framework for Reward and World-Model Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25063)
*Xian Wu,Haoran Li,Dongbin Zhao,Ruiyao Zhang,Yuanqi Chu,Bin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a bilevel Proxy-FEA diagnostic framework for reward and world-model diagnosis in RL-guided scan-order optimization for laser additive manufacturing, revealing that cheap proxy metrics primarily capture distortion-related behavior while weakly correlating with stress and plasticity metrics, highlighting a stress-distortion trade-off and the need for sparse FEA reference signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The central challenge is that full finite-element analysis (FEA) is computationally prohibitive for dense RL in-the-loop evaluation, while cheap thermo-inspired proxy metrics, though efficient, capture only partial aspects of true thermo-mechanical objectives. The authors aim to diagnose this misalignment before large-scale policy optimization.

Method: A bilevel Proxy-FEA diagnostic framework: lower level uses lightweight scan-path and thermo-inspired proxies for rapid candidate generation and policy screening; upper level uses sparse Abaqus FEA simulations for reference labels. Evaluated on a simplified LDED32 stripe benchmark with ten scan strategies.

Result: Final-cooling metrics (Mises stress, U3 distortion, PEEQ plasticity) reveal a stress-distortion trade-off. center_out is a robust compromise; raster_left_to_right and edge_in are opposing endpoints. Proxy-FEA alignment analysis shows cheap path-based metrics predominantly capture distortion (U3) with weak correlation to sparse FEA reference labels.

Conclusion: Proxy-only reward designs risk misalignment in RL training. Sparse FEA reference signals are valuable for diagnostic-guided reward and world-model refinement before large-scale policy optimization.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning offers a promising approach for scan-order optimisation in laser additive manufacturing, where sequential scan decisions critically influence thermal accumulation, residual stress, distortion, and final part quality. A central challenge in applying RL to this domain lies in reward and world-model fidelity: full finite-element analysis is computationally prohibitive for dense in-the-loop evaluation, while cheap thermo-inspired proxy metrics, though efficient, may capture only partial aspects of the true thermo-mechanical objectives. This paper investigates a bilevel Proxy--FEA diagnostic framework for reward and world-model diagnosis in reinforcement-learning-guided scan-order optimisation. The lower level employs lightweight scan-path and thermo-inspired proxies for rapid candidate generation and preliminary policy-side screening, while the upper level utilises sparse Abaqus FEA simulations to provide simulation-based reference labels. The framework is examined on a simplified whole-track heating LDED32 stripe benchmark comprising ten representative scan strategies. Final-cooling residual Mises stress, U3 vertical distortion, and PEEQ plasticity metrics reveal an observed stress--distortion trade-off rather than a single monotonic quality objective. Within the evaluated set, the center_out strategy emerges as a robust compromise candidate, while raster_left_to_right and edge_in form opposing endpoints of the trade-off. Proxy--FEA alignment analysis shows that current cheap path-based metrics predominantly capture distortion-related (U3) behaviour and exhibit only weak correlation with the sparse FEA reference labels. These findings highlight that proxy-only reward designs risk misalignment in future RL training and underscore the value of sparse FEA reference signals for diagnostic-guided reward and world-model refinement prior to large-scale policy optimisation.

</details>


### [654] [Leveraging Gauge Freedom for Learning Non-Gradient Population Dynamics of Stochastic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25107)
*Jules Berman,Tobias Blickhan,Benjamin Peherstorfer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes NGIF, an algorithm that infers non-gradient population dynamics from observations by using a weak formulation of the continuity equation, overcoming limitations of gradient-restricted methods and improving distributional accuracy on physics problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing population dynamics inference methods are restricted to gradient flows (derived from scalar potentials), which minimize kinetic energy but cannot capture non-gradient dynamics. The authors aim to overcome this "gauge freedom" limitation by developing a method that can infer general vector fields.

Method: The authors propose NGIF (Non-Gradient Inference Flows), which uses a weak formulation of the continuity equation to parameterize general vector fields, allowing selection criteria beyond minimal kinetic energy for inferring population dynamics.

Result: On low- and high-dimensional physics problems, NGIF improves distributional accuracy over gradient-restricted baselines and better captures non-potential transport.

Conclusion: Moving beyond gradient-flow assumptions enables more accurate inference of population dynamics, particularly for systems involving non-potential transport, making NGIF a more general and effective approach.

Abstract: Existing work on population dynamics inference often focuses on flows arising from vector fields that are the gradients of scalar potentials. Among all admissible flows that are compatible with the population dynamics, gradient flows are optimal in a specific sense: they minimize kinetic energy. The selection of fields based on different criteria corresponds to a gauge freedom when determining population dynamics, which we leverage in this work. We propose Non-Gradient Inference Flows (NGIF), an algorithm to infer non-gradient population dynamics using a weak formulation of the continuity equation. This allows us to parameterize general vector fields and choose other selection criteria beyond minimal kinetic energy. We demonstrate on a variety of low- and high-dimensional physics problems that this more general approach improves distributional accuracy over gradient-restricted baselines and better captures non-potential transport.

</details>


### [655] [Revisiting Pre-Propagation GNNs: Robust Diffusion Operators and Hidden-State Re-Propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25111)
*Zichao Yue,Zhiru Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper improves pre-propagation GNNs by introducing robust graph diffusion operators and a few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme, enabling them to match message-passing GNNs in accuracy while maintaining training efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PPGNNs offer training efficiency benefits but suffer from unclear expressivity and performance gaps, especially on heterophilic graphs. The authors aim to close this gap while preserving efficiency.

Method: The authors propose (1) robust graph diffusion operators for preprocessing and (2) a few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme during training to enhance expressivity.

Result: The proposed methods improve validation and test accuracy of PPGNNs, allowing them to match the accuracy of message-passing GNNs.

Conclusion: PPGNNs can achieve competitive accuracy with message-passing GNNs while maintaining their training efficiency advantages, contradicting prior beliefs about their limitations.

Abstract: Pre-propagation graph neural networks (PPGNNs) decouple node feature propagation from transformation: graph diffusion is performed once as preprocessing, and training reduces to dense per-node transformations. This design enables mini-batch training without inter-node dependencies, avoids repeated sparse matrix--matrix multiplications, and better matches modern accelerators optimized for dense compute. However, their expressivity remains unclear, and empirical results show a gap between PPGNNs and their message-passing counterparts on commonly used graph benchmarks, especially heterophilic ones. In this paper, we propose a suite of robust graph diffusion operators for preprocessing and a few-shot hidden-state re-propagation scheme during training. Our methods improve the validation and test accuracy of PPGNNs, enabling them to match the accuracy of message-passing GNNs while maintaining training efficiency.

</details>


### [656] [Courant: a State-Adaptive Perceiver-Based Neural Surrogate with Local Support and Interpretable Field Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25115)
*Anuj Kumar,Josiah Bjorgaard,Nikolaos Bouklas,Matteo Salvador,Alexander Lavin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Courant is a Perceiver-based surrogate model with adaptive latent features that act like hp-refinement, achieving competitive accuracy and interpretable multiscale decompositions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a neural surrogate model whose latent features exhibit adaptive specialization and local support in physical space, mimicking desirable properties of traditional hp-refinement schemes in numerical solvers.

Method: Courant combines a shared random Fourier feature coordinate embedding, state-adapted latent queries, and a light-weight decoder in a Perceiver-based encoder-processor-decoder architecture, trained with standard L2 loss on steady/transient simulation data.

Result: Competitive accuracy on benchmarks. Latents develop multiscale geometric specialization, track coherent structures over time, and enable interpretable partition-of-unity-like field decompositions.

Conclusion: Courant's inductive biases produce interpretable latents that function analogously to adaptive spatial basis functions, bridging traditional numerical methods and deep learning surrogates.

Abstract: We introduce "Courant", a Perceiver-based encoder-processor-decoder surrogate model that has latent features exhibiting adaptive specialization and local support in the physical space, enabling functionality akin to an adaptive hp-refinement scheme, an attribute that is highly desirable in traditional numerical solvers and scientific machine learning broadly. The proposed architecture combines a shared random Fourier feature coordinate embedding, state-adapted latent queries, and a light-weight decoder. Courant is trained end-to-end with steady or transient simulation data and only a standard L_2 prediction loss in the physical space, achieving competitive accuracy on benchmarks. We demonstrate that Courant's inductive biases yield latents that are interpretable by design: they develop multiscale geometric specialization in the simulation domain and track coherent structures in the time-dependent case, acting analogously to time-evolving spatial basis functions and allowing for decoding a compact, geometry-anchored, partition-of-unity-like decomposition of the simulated field.

</details>


### [657] [Inference-Time Alignment of Diffusion Models via Trust-Region Iterative Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25123)
*Weixin Wang,Yu Yang,Wei Deng,Pan Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRI-TSMC is a trust-region framework for learning twisting functions that improve Sequential Monte Carlo-based inference-time alignment in diffusion models, reducing variance and improving particle efficiency compared to existing SMC methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SMC-based steering methods for diffusion model alignment suffer from weight degeneracy and high-variance estimates because reward information is used only after propagation through reweighting/resampling. Learnable twisting methods could help but are unstable for high-dimensional diffusion alignment tasks.

Method: Proposes Trust-Region Iterative Twisted SMC (TRI-TSMC) which iteratively computes exact KL-constrained updates in path space via tempered importance reweighting, then projects back to the parameterized twisted family through weighted maximum likelihood.

Result: TRI-TSMC improves alignment objectives on discrete diffusion text generation and text-to-image generation under matched inference-time budgets.

Conclusion: The trust-region update follows an escort path toward the target, the weighted MLE update is a forward-KL projection, and the path reduces residual importance-weight variance, providing a principled and effective approach for inference-time alignment.

Abstract: We study inference-time alignment for diffusion-based generative models, aiming to steer a base model toward high-reward outputs without updating its weights. Recent Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC)-based steering methods approximate reward-tilted target distributions in a principled way, but their proposals remain largely tied to the base sampler. Since reward information is mainly used after propagation through particle reweighting and resampling, these methods can require large particle budgets and suffer from weight degeneracy and high-variance estimates. One way to reduce variance and improve particle efficiency is to iteratively learn twisting functions that provide look-ahead guidance, as in twisted SMC. However, existing learnable twisting methods are developed mainly for classical sequential inference and can be unstable when applied to diffusion-based alignment with high-dimensional state spaces and terminal, noisy, or black-box rewards. We propose Trust-Region Iterative Twisted Sequential Monte Carlo (TRI-TSMC), a trust-region framework for learning twisting functions in SMC-based inference-time alignment. Each iteration computes an exact KL-constrained update in path space, which admits a closed-form solution by tempered importance reweighting, and projects this target back to the parameterized twisted family by weighted maximum likelihood. Theoretically, we formalize the value-function interpretation of the optimal twisting function and show that it yields a zero-variance sampler. We prove that the trust-region update follows an escort path toward the target distribution, that the weighted maximum-likelihood update is a forward-KL projection, and that the path reduces residual importance-weight variance. Empirically, TRI-TSMC improves primary alignment objectives on discrete diffusion text generation and text-to-image generation under matched inference-time budgets.

</details>


### [658] [Optimizing Multidimensional Scaling in Gini Metric Spaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25124)
*Cassandra Mussard,Stéphane Mussard*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Gini MDS, a robust extension of Euclidean MDS that uses a rank-based pseudo-distance with a tunable hyperparameter, outperforming standard MDS on noisy data with GPU-accelerated PyTorch implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Euclidean MDS is sensitive to noise and outliers in real-world data, motivating the need for a more robust multidimensional scaling approach.

Method: Introduces a Gini pseudo-distance that combines values and their ranks with a tunable hyperparameter, enabling flexible exploration of latent configurations via tensor-based PyTorch implementation.

Result: Experiments on 16 UCI datasets with outliers and MNIST images with noise show Gini MDS outperforms Euclidean MDS on noisy data, with GPU-accelerated computation.

Conclusion: Gini MDS is a robust, efficient alternative to Euclidean MDS for real-world applications where noise and outliers are present.

Abstract: The Gini Multidimensional Scaling (Gini MDS) framework extends the Euclidean multidimensional scaling. We introduce a Gini pseudo-distance based on values and their ranks that depends on a fine-tunable hyperparameter. This pseudo-distance allows flexible exploration of latent configurations, enabling embeddings that best match observed dissimilarities. The Gini MDS is shown to be robust to noise and outliers, making it well-suited for real-world applications. We provide experiments on 16 UCI datasets with outliers and on MNIST images with noise to show that the Gini MDS outperforms the Euclidean MDS on noisy data. Finally, a tensor-based implementation in \texttt{PyTorch} provides GPU acceleration and efficient computation compared to the standard MDS of the \texttt{sklearn} library.

</details>


### [659] [Blocked Gibbs meets Diffusion Transformers: Unsupervised Learning for Constraint Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25129)
*Yudong W. Xu,Wenhao Li,Xiaoyu Wang,Scott Sanner,Elias B. Khalil*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BloGDiT replaces standard joint Gaussian denoising with blocked Gibbs-style denoising in Diffusion Transformers to handle general discrete constraint optimization problems, achieving strong results on Sudoku, Graph Coloring, MIS, and MaxCut.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion models for constraint optimization are limited to binary variables and rely on GNNs, failing on problems requiring global reasoning or handling general discrete variables.

Method: Proposes BloGDiT (Blocked Gibbs Diffusion Transformer), which replaces standard joint Gaussian denoising with blocked Gaussian denoising, using iterative block resampling with annealed block size to enable large targeted edits.

Result: BloGDiT matches or outperforms existing methods on Sudoku, Graph Coloring, Maximum Independent Set, and MaxCut benchmarks.

Conclusion: Blocked Gibbs-style diffusion provides an effective inductive bias for Transformer-based constraint satisfaction and optimization over general discrete variables.

Abstract: Diffusion models have shown promise in learning to solve constraint optimization problems. However, they are mostly restricted to problems with binary variables and rely on graph neural networks, hindering their application to a broader range of problems such as those with general discrete variables or constraint structures that necessitate global rather than local reasoning. We investigate the use of Diffusion Transformers to address the aforementioned limitations. A naive implementation performs poorly due to a fundamental mismatch between the standard diffusion process and constraint solving: while the former applies small, incremental denoising across all variables, the latter requires substantially altering specific subsets of variables to attain feasibility or optimality. Our method, Blocked Gibbs Diffusion Transformer (BloGDiT), is the first to address this limitation by replacing standard joint Gaussian denoising with blocked Gaussian denoising. BloGDiT uses iterative block resampling and anneals the block size over time to facilitate large, targeted edits within a block of variables. Across Sudoku, Graph Coloring, Maximum Independent Set, and MaxCut, BloGDiT matches or outperforms existing methods, demonstrating that blocked Gibbs-style diffusion provides a highly effective inductive bias for Transformer-based constraint satisfaction and optimization.

</details>


### [660] [Theoretical Analysis of Sparse Optimization with Reparameterization, Weight Decay, and Adaptive Learning Rate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25134)
*Huangyu Xu,Jingqin Yang,Qianqian Xu,Jiaye Teng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReWA is a new sparse optimization method using reparameterization, weight decay, and adaptive learning rate that improves sparsity over ℓ₁-regularization while maintaining test accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with ResNets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ℓ_p regularization (0<p<1) causes optimization instability due to unbounded gradients; a more stable sparse optimization approach is needed.

Method: ReWA combines Reparameterization, Weight decay, and Adaptive learning rate to create a more stable optimization landscape connected to ℓ_p regularization.

Result: ReWA achieves significant sparsity improvements over ℓ₁-regularization while preserving test accuracy on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with ResNet architectures.

Conclusion: ReWA offers a stable and effective alternative to ℓ_p regularization for sparse optimization, demonstrating superior sparsity without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Sparse optimization is a fundamental challenge in various practical applications. A popular approach to sparse optimization is $\ell_p$ regularization. However, it may encounter optimization instability due to the unbounded gradients when $0<p<1$. In this paper, we introduce a novel approach to sparse optimization termed ReWA, based on Reparameterization, Weight decay, and Adaptive learning rate. ReWA is closely connected to $\ell_p$-regularization, yet it unveils a distinct optimization landscape that helps mitigate instability issues. Experiments on CIFAR-10 and ImageNet with ResNets demonstrate that ReWA leads to significant sparsity improvements over the $\ell_1$-regularization approach while preserving test accuracy.

</details>


### [661] [ASTRO: Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reinforcement Optimization for GNN Powered Anomly Detection in Cyber Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25135)
*Rai Ali Yar,Umaisa Lail,Anwar Shah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ASTRO is a novel anomaly detection framework for IIoT that combines Deep Q-Networks, Graph Neural Networks, temporal modeling, and Multi-Head Attention to dynamically optimize detection thresholds. It achieves F1 scores of 0.990 on SWaT and 0.788 on WADI, outperforming baselines by ~14% on the latter.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Anomaly detection in IIoT environments is challenging due to high-dimensional, time-dependent sensor data and the need to protect ICS/CPS from false data injection and malicious attacks.

Method: ASTRO integrates a Deep Q-Network (DQN) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for spatial modeling, temporal modeling for time-series dependencies, and a Multi-Head Attention mechanism to highlight key time steps. It uses DQN for dynamic threshold optimization of continuous anomaly scores into binary decisions.

Result: F1 score of 0.990 on SWaT dataset and 0.788 on WADI dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by nearly 14% on WADI. Results confirm consistent generalization and stability across multiple runs.

Conclusion: ASTRO is a highly practical, scalable method for strengthening large-scale cyber-physical infrastructures against anomalies and attacks.

Abstract: Anomaly detection in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments is essential to protect the Industrial Control Systems (ICS) and Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) from occuring run time false data injection and other malicious attacks. The increasing complexity of sensor networks and interconnected control loops makes it difficult to identify anomalous behavior hidden within high-dimensional and time-dependent signals. To address these challenges, this article introduces Adaptive Spatio-Temporal Reinforcement Optimization ASTRO (ASTRO), a novel anomaly detection framework that pioneers the use of reinforcement learning for dynamic threshold optimization. By integrating a Deep Q-Network (DQN) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), temporal modelling and a Multi-Head Attention mechanism, ASTRO continuously adapts its decision boundaries to improve detection accuracy. The GNN component models the spatial relations among sensors, Temporal model captures time series dependencies and the attention layer highlights most informative time steps. The model generates continuous anomaly scores, which are transformed into binary decisions using an adaptive threshold, optimized via a Deep Q-Network (DQN). The ASTRO approach is evaluated on two real world industrial benchmarks: the Secure Water Treatment (SWaT) and Water Distribution (WADI) datasets. The proposed model achieves an exceptional performance on the SWaT with F1 score of 0.990. Moreover, on highly complex 127 end devices WADI dataset, it secures F1 score of 0.788, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines by nearly 14%. Results across multiple runs confirm consistent generalization and stability. These experiments demonstrate that the ASTRO framework is highly practical and scalable method for strengthening the large scale cyber physical infrastructures

</details>


### [662] [Abduction-Deduction Entanglement: Domain Generalization via Representation Transplants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25156)
*Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper addresses domain generalization by proposing that the optimal prediction in a target domain is partially identifiable from source data through an "abduction-deduction" factorization and a "representation transplant" technique, leading to a minimax-optimal learner-adversary game.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Models trained on source distributions fail to generalize to target distributions. The authors aim to leverage invariant causal mechanisms under mild assumptions to enable valid inference about unseen target distributions, despite non-identifiability from source data alone.

Method: The authors decompose optimal prediction into abduction (inferring unobserved variables) and deduction (predicting labels) maps. They parameterize the constrained family via a "representation transplant" (a linear transformation in representation space that manipulates abduction content while preserving deduction). A learner-adversary game searches plausible target distributions, converging to minimax-optimal prediction.

Result: The paper shows that under idealistic optimization, the learner-adversary game provably achieves minimax-optimal target prediction. Evaluations on domain generalization benchmarks demonstrate competitive performance, verifying the theory.

Conclusion: The abduction-deduction entanglement, combined with representation transplant, provides a principled approach to domain generalization by identifying invariant causal structures, achieving robust target predictions without direct target data access.

Abstract: Prediction models trained under the source distribution do not generalize well to a different target distribution. A valid inference about an unseen data distribution must be anchored by the invariance of certain causal mechanisms that generate the source and target data, however, these structural invariances are non-identifiable from the source data alone. Under mild causal assumptions about the data, we show that the optimal prediction in the target is in fact partially identifiable by the source distribution. The result rests on a simple observation: In any domain, the optimal prediction can be factorized into what we call a pair of abduction and deduction maps, where the abduction map makes inference about some unobserved variables (possibly confounders) from the observed variables and the deduction map predicts the label using both the observed and inferred quantities. Access to large source data pins down the optimal prediction, thus constrains the valid abduction-deduction ensembles that produce it -- a non-identifiability that we call the abduction-deduction entanglement. To leverage this, we parameterize the constrained family using what we call a representation transplant, that is a specific linear transformation in the representation space that manipulates the abduction content of the representation while retaining the deduction component. Invariance of the causal mechanism generating the label implies existence of an invariant deduction map between source and target. Thus, we can search the space of plausible target distributions via a parametric transplant. We use this scheme in a learner-adversary game that, under an idealistic optimization, provably terminates with the learner having the minimax-optimal target prediction. Evaluations verify the theory, showing that the method is competitive in DG benchmarks.

</details>


### [663] [AME-TS: Anchored Mixture-of-Experts for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25166)
*Rui Wang,Renhao Xue,Ray Razi,Huan Song,Hannah R. Marlowe*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AME-TS is a sparse time series foundation model that uses structure-guided routing in Mixture-of-Experts, aligning expert specialization with interpretable temporal features (forecastability, seasonality, trend, sparsity), achieving strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoffs and more stable expert specialization compared to standard MoE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing time series forecasting models process all series through shared dense computation despite temporal heterogeneity, and standard MoE routing leads to weakly identified expert specialization that is unstable during downstream adaptation.

Method: AME-TS uses a lightweight regime predictor to estimate series-level descriptors (forecastability, seasonality, trend, sparsity), maps them to soft structural priors over experts, and uses these priors to guide token-level routing during training for structure-aligned specialization.

Result: On GIFT-Eval, AME-TS substantially outperforms existing time series foundation models at small scales, remains competitive at larger scales while activating fewer parameters. It also learns more interpretable routing geometry and more stable expert specialization than standard MoE on M5 fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Structure-aware routing is an effective and reliable way to realize the benefits of sparse expert models for time series forecasting.

Abstract: Time series forecasting models are increasingly scaled through large Transformer backbones, yet most existing approaches process all series through a shared dense computation path despite substantial heterogeneity in temporal structure. Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) offers a natural alternative by enabling conditional computation, but standard MoE routing leaves expert specialization weakly identified and often unstable during downstream adaptation. We propose AME-TS, a structure-guided sparse time series foundation model that aligns expert routing with interpretable temporal structure. AME-TS first uses a lightweight regime predictor to estimate series-level descriptors, including forecastability, seasonality, trend, and sparsity, and maps them to a soft structural prior over experts. This series-level prior guides token-level routing during training, encouraging structure-aligned specialization. On the GIFT-Eval benchmark, AME-TS delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency tradeoff across model scales: it substantially outperforms existing time series foundation models at small model scales and remains competitive with the strongest models at larger scales, while activating substantially fewer parameters through sparse routing. We further show that AME-TS learns more interpretable routing geometry and substantially more stable expert specialization than standard MoE during fine-tuning on the M5 dataset. These results suggest that structure-aware routing is an effective and reliable way to realize the benefits of sparse expert models for time series forecasting.

</details>
