TL;DR: ReFi-GAD is a generalist graph anomaly detection method that uses a Relational Fingerprint (ReFi) to align heterogeneous features across domains, combining a transformer encoder for domain-invariant knowledge with SNR-guided refinement for domain adaptation, significantly outperforming SOTA on 14 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generalist GAD methods use PCA-based projection that aligns feature dimensions but ignores feature semantics, causing negative transfer on unseen graphs.

Method: Proposes Relational Fingerprint (ReFi) for semantics-aware feature alignment from contextual and structural perspectives, plus a transformer-based encoder with SNR-guided refinement module.

Result: Extensive experiments on 14 datasets show ReFi-GAD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: ReFi-GAD effectively addresses negative transfer in generalist GAD by encoding anomaly-indicative semantic cues via ReFi and combining domain-invariant learning with domain-specific adaptation.

Abstract: Generalist graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to detect anomalies on unseen graphs without graph-specific retraining. Nevertheless, existing approaches primarily focus on aligning heterogeneous features across different data domains via PCA-based projection, which harmonizes feature dimensions ignores feature semantics. As a result, GAD models fail to learn transferable semantic knowledge, and even exhibit negative transfer on unseen graphs. To address this issue, we propose a Relational Fingerprint-based generalist GAD approach (ReFi-GAD for short), aligning heterogeneous raw features with a universal and semantics-aware Relational Fingerprint (ReFi) that encodes anomaly-indicative cues from both contextual and structural perspectives. Building on ReFi, we design a fingerprint-grounded generalist GAD model, which combines a transformer-based encoder to capture domain-invariant knowledge with an SNR-guided refinement module for domain-specific adaptation. Extensive experiments on 14 datasets demonstrate that ReFi-GAD significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [691] [Missing Pattern Recognized Diffusion Imputation Model for Missing Not At Random](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25439)
*Gyuwon Sim,Sumin Lee,Heesun Bae,Byeonghu Na,Doyun Kwon,Ju-Hee Hwang,Jae-Young Lim,Il-Chul Moon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PRDIM is a diffusion-based imputation model that uses a pattern recognizer and EM algorithm to handle Missing Not at Random (MNAR) data across time-series and image domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Missing data often depends on unobservable values (MNAR) in real-world scenarios, but existing methods fail to explicitly capture the missing pattern for accurate imputation.

Method: PRDIM uses a pattern recognizer to approximate the missing pattern and iteratively maximizes joint likelihood of observed values and missing mask via Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm within a diffusion framework.

Result: PRDIM consistently achieves strong imputation performance under MNAR settings across multiple data modalities (time-series and images).

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling missing patterns with pattern recognition and EM optimization is effective for MNAR imputation across diverse domains.

Abstract: Missing data frequently arises across diverse domains, including time-series and image domains. In the real world, missing occurrences often depend on the unobservable values themselves, which are referred to as Missing Not at Random (MNAR). In this work, we introduce the Missing Pattern Recognized Diffusion Imputation Model (PRDIM), a novel framework that explicitly captures the missing pattern and precisely imputes unobserved values. PRDIM iteratively maximizes the likelihood of the joint distribution for observed values and missing mask under an Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm. In this sense, we first employ a pattern recognizer, which approximates the underlying missing pattern and provides guidance during every inference toward more plausible imputations with respect to the missing information. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that PRDIM consistently achieves strong imputation performance under MNAR settings across multiple data modalities.

</details>


### [692] [BigMac: Breaking the Pareto Frontier of Compute and Memory in Multimodal LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25451)
*Zili Zhang,Chengxu Yang,Shenglong Zhang,Chenyu Wang,Yufan Zhang,Tuo Dai,Zhouyang Li,Yuhong Ge,Chao Jin,Xin Jin,Yuliang Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BigMac is a training pipeline that efficiently nests encoder and generator computation into LLM pipelines, reducing activation memory to O(1) and achieving 1.08×–1.9× speedup without sacrificing memory efficiency, thus breaking the Pareto frontier between computation and memory in MLLM training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal LLM training suffers from model and data heterogeneity. Existing systems face a Pareto trade-off between compute and memory efficiency, unable to optimize both simultaneously.

Method: BigMac nests the encoder and generator computation into the original LLM pipeline via a dependency-safe nested pipeline structure, reducing encoder/generator activation memory complexity to O(1) while keeping LLM activation memory unchanged.

Result: Achieves 1.08×–1.9× training speedup over baseline systems with stable memory usage even as batch size increases.

Conclusion: BigMac breaks the Pareto frontier between computational efficiency and memory usage, enabling simultaneous optimization of both in MLLM training.

Abstract: Training multimodal large language models (MLLMs) is challenged by both model and data heterogeneity. Existing systems redesign the training pipeline to address these challenges, but remain bound by a Pareto frontier between compute and memory efficiency, improving one only at the expense of the other. We present BigMac, a new training pipeline for multimodal LLMs. The core idea of BigMac is to elegantly nest the encoder and generator computation into the original LLM pipeline, forming a dependency-safe nested pipeline structure. With this design, BigMac reduces the activation memory complexity of the encoder and generator to O(1) while keeping the activation memory complexity of the LLM unchanged. At the same time, it achieves the same computational efficiency as the idealized setting with unlimited memory. As a result, BigMac breaks the Pareto frontier between computational efficiency and memory usage, enabling simultaneous optimization of both computation and memory in MLLM training. We evaluate BigMac on multiple MLLMs and training workloads. Experimental results show that BigMac achieves a 1.08$\times$-1.9$\times$ training speedup over baseline systems while maintaining stable memory usage as batch size increases.

</details>


### [693] [From Simulation to Enaction: Post-trained language models recognize and react to their own generations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25459)
*Asvin G.,Jack Lindsey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-trained language models implicitly recognize their own on-policy generations, resulting in 3-4x lower output entropy compared to off-policy contexts, and this recognition involves distinct mechanisms for implicit vs explicit awareness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how post-training (beyond pretraining as passive predictors) changes language models' ability to recognize and model the consequences of their own outputs (on-policy generations).

Method: Comparing on-policy vs off-policy output distribution entropy across model families and sizes; analyzing internal representations tracking input surprise; testing explicit verbal report vs implicit recognition mechanisms.

Result: On-policy output entropy is 3-4x lower than off-policy; models collapse uncertainty about topic before first output token; explicit and implicit recognition route through different mechanisms.

Conclusion: Post-trained models encode on-policy recognition in output distributions through internal surprise representations, with distinct pathways for implicit vs explicit self-recognition.

Abstract: Language models are pretrained as passive predictors with no incentive to model the consequences of their own outputs. Post-training changes this: a model producing its own responses can benefit from recognizing that it is on-policy. We present evidence that post-trained models recognize their on-policy generations, and this recognition is implicitly encoded in their output distributions. In particular, on-policy output distribution entropy is 3--4$\times$ lower than off-policy entropy, across model families and size classes. We trace part of this effect to an internal representation of input surprise, tracking the unlikeliness of the most recent input token according to the model's prior predictions, that causally modulates output entropy. One example of these phenomena can be observed in response to open-ended prompts; post-trained models (unlike pretrained models) collapse their uncertainty over the topic of their upcoming response before the first output token; violating this cached intention with a different-topic prefill results in higher output entropy. We also tested whether models can distinguish on-policy contexts from prefills via explicit verbal report. We find that they can, but that interestingly, this explicit recognition routes through a different mechanism than implicit recognition.

</details>


### [694] [JacQuant: STE-Free Quantization-Aware Training via Learned Jacobian Surrogates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25469)
*Kai Yi,Vignesh Vivekraja,Harshit Khaitan,Steven Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JacQuant is a quantization-aware training framework that replaces the Straight-Through Estimator (STE) with a lightweight, learnable sensitivity surrogate to stabilize and accelerate ultra-low-bit LLM training, achieving better accuracy with negligible overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: STE-based QAT is brittle near bin boundaries and weakly aligned with actual low-precision model behavior, motivating a more principled approach.

Method: JacQuant learns a lightweight diagonal/block-diagonal surrogate of the model's local sensitivity to parameter changes, compatible with standard variance-reduced optimizers and existing forward quantizers.

Result: Across LLM benchmarks at ≤2 bits, JacQuant consistently achieves higher accuracy than STE-based QAT, with negligible runtime overhead under practical group sizes.

Conclusion: JacQuant is a drop-in replacement for STE in QAT that provides convergence guarantees (non-convex and PL condition) and demonstrably improves ultra-low-bit LLM quantization.

Abstract: Quantization-aware training (QAT) is widely deployed but typically relies on the Straight-Through Estimator (STE), which passes gradients through non-differentiable quantizers by fiat. This often makes training brittle near bin boundaries and weakly aligned with the actual behavior of the low-precision model. We introduce JacQuant, a QAT framework that learns a lightweight surrogate of the model's local sensitivity to parameter changes and uses it to stabilize and accelerate training within standard variance-reduced optimizers. The surrogate is inexpensive (diagonal or block-diagonal), data-driven, and compatible with common weight and activation quantizers. On code-preserving training phases, we prove convergence for non-convex objectives and obtain linear rates under a PL condition, and we relate the learned sensitivity to end-to-end output fidelity via a simple calibration argument. Across LLM benchmarks at $\leq 2$ bits, JacQuant consistently reaches higher accuracy than STE-based QAT, and the runtime analyses on various models show that the added cost remains negligible under practical group sizes. The method is drop-in and requires no changes to the forward quantizers; our empirical claims are scoped to ultra-low-bit LLM QAT.

</details>


### [695] [SafetyRepro: Configuration-Conditional Rank Instability on Alignment Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25492)
*Yanhang Li,Zhichao Fan,Zexin Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Benchmark comparisons of AI models (e.g., "A is safer than B") are unreliable because configuration choices can flip pairwise verdicts; the paper formalizes this failure mode and demonstrates it across alignment benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise model comparisons from benchmarks are treated as definitive but depend on underspecified configuration choices, making quantitative verdicts fragile and potentially misleading.

Method: The paper proposes a finite-envelope proposition that ties a measurable pairwise-disagreement rate to whether strict ordering can be reversed by configuration changes, and introduces a commit-stamped evaluation protocol to operationalize this on alignment benchmarks.

Result: On every benchmark tested, configuration choices alone can flip the pairwise verdict; the proposition successfully isolates this strict-reversal failure mode.

Conclusion: Benchmark comparisons are not as stable as assumed; the proposed protocol can detect and formalize configuration-induced verdict reversals, closing a theory-benchmark loop.

Abstract: Pairwise model comparisons drawn from foundation-model benchmarks ("A is safer than B") are read as quantitative verdicts but hinge on harness choices benchmark papers under-specify. We close one theory-benchmark loop on this primitive: a finite-envelope proposition tying a measurable pairwise-disagreement rate to whether the strict ordering admits a configuration-pair reversal, paired with a commit-stamped evaluation protocol that operationalises it on widely cited alignment benchmarks. On every benchmark we test, configuration choice alone can flip the pairwise verdict; the proposition isolates this strict-reversal failure mode.

</details>


### [696] [Accelerated Dynamic Importance Weighting with Versatile Divergence-Minimizing Estimators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25499)
*Tongtong Fang,Nan Lu,Gang Niu,Kenji Fukumizu,Masashi Sugiyama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ADIW is a unified, efficient importance weighting framework for deep learning under joint distribution shift that accelerates dynamic IW via warm-started projected gradient descent and supports multiple divergence-based weight estimation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dynamic IW (DIW) suffers from high computational overhead (solving KMM optimization to convergence per mini-batch) and is limited to KMM-based weight estimation, lacking flexibility to use other divergence measures.

Method: ADIW uses lightweight projected gradient descent updates with warm-starting from previous weights for efficiency, and generalizes DIW into a unified divergence-minimization framework supporting KL divergence, squared distance, and Wasserstein-1 distance in a plug-and-play manner.

Result: ADIW achieves state-of-the-art IW performance with substantially improved efficiency, and has convergence guarantees under mild conditions.

Conclusion: ADIW provides a unified, efficient, and theoretically grounded importance weighting framework for deep learning under joint distribution shift, outperforming prior DIW approaches.

Abstract: Importance weighting (IW) is a golden solver for joint distribution shift, where the joint distributions differ between the training and test data. To solve this problem, IW estimates test-to-training density ratios as importance weights and reweights the training losses accordingly. Recent advances in dynamic IW (DIW) integrate weight estimation into model training, enabling scalable IW for deep models and achieving strong performance on large modern datasets. Despite its promise, DIW remains limited in two aspects. First, it incurs substantial computational overhead by solving a kernel mean matching (KMM)-induced optimization problem to convergence in every mini-batch. Second, it relies solely on KMM for weight estimation, whereas the IW literature contains diverse estimation methods based on different divergence measures. In this paper, we propose accelerated DIW (ADIW), a unified and efficient IW framework for deep learning under joint distribution shift. ADIW performs a few lightweight projected gradient descent updates that warm-start from previously updated weights, substantially improving efficiency. Moreover, ADIW generalizes DIW into a unified divergence-minimization framework that supports diverse weight-estimation methods in a plug-and-play manner, including those based on the Kullback-Leibler divergence, squared distance, and Wasserstein-1 distance. We establish convergence guarantees for ADIW under mild conditions, and empirical results demonstrate that ADIW achieves state-of-the-art IW performance while being substantially more efficient.

</details>


### [697] [LETS Forecast: Learning Embedology for Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.06454)
*Abrar Majeedi,Viswanatha Reddy Gajjala,Satya Sai Srinath Namburi GNVV,Nada Magdi Elkordi,Yin Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepEDM integrates nonlinear dynamical systems modeling (empirical dynamic modeling, Takens' theorem) with deep neural networks via time-delayed embeddings and softmax attention-based kernel regression, outperforming SOTA on synthetic and real-world time series forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time series are governed by complex nonlinear dynamics, yet most deep learning forecasting methods do not explicitly model these underlying dynamics.

Method: DeepEDM learns a latent space from time-delayed embeddings (inspired by EDM and Takens' theorem), applies kernel regression via efficient softmax attention to approximate dynamics, and predicts future time steps.

Result: DeepEDM is robust to input noise and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accuracy on both synthetic nonlinear dynamical systems and real-world time series across domains.

Conclusion: Explicitly integrating nonlinear dynamical systems theory (EDM) with deep learning improves forecasting robustness and accuracy, bridging a key gap in existing deep time series models.

Abstract: Real-world time series are often governed by complex nonlinear dynamics. Understanding these underlying dynamics is crucial for precise future prediction. While deep learning has achieved major success in time series forecasting, many existing approaches do not explicitly model the dynamics. To bridge this gap, we introduce DeepEDM, a framework that integrates nonlinear dynamical systems modeling with deep neural networks. Inspired by empirical dynamic modeling (EDM) and rooted in Takens' theorem, DeepEDM presents a novel deep model that learns a latent space from time-delayed embeddings, and employs kernel regression to approximate the underlying dynamics, while leveraging efficient implementation of softmax attention and allowing for accurate prediction of future time steps. To evaluate our method, we conduct comprehensive experiments on synthetic data of nonlinear dynamical systems as well as real-world time series across domains. Our results show that DeepEDM is robust to input noise, and outperforms state-of-the-art methods in forecasting accuracy. Our code is available at: https://abrarmajeedi.github.io/deep_edm.

</details>


### [698] [Relative Repairability: A Calibration-Based Diagnostic for High-Sparsity Post-Pruning Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25508)
*Qishi Zhan,Liang He,Minxuan Hu,Ziheng Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Relative Repairability (RR), a calibration-based diagnostic that measures how much pruning damage remains after lightweight repair. RR is most useful near an architecture-dependent recoverability transition, where it improves allocation over ERK and LAMP, particularly in high-sparsity regimes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: At very high sparsity, pruning not only selects which weights to keep but also determines where unrecoverable damage is placed. Existing allocation methods (e.g., ERK, LAMP) fail to account for whether damage can be repaired, motivating a diagnostic that measures residual damage after lightweight repair.

Method: The authors propose Relative Repairability (RR), a calibration-based metric comparing raw activation distortion from layerwise pruning to residual distortion after channelwise variance matching repair, using only unlabeled calibration data. Experiments are conducted on ResNet18, ResNet34, and VGG16 BN across CIFAR10 and CIFAR100.

Result: RR is not universally dominant but is most useful near a recoverability transition. On CIFAR100 ResNet18, RR improves over ERK across the central transition band and surpasses LAMP near the upper band. A projection forced ablation shows capped ERK over-protects projection layers, harming post-repair recovery.

Conclusion: High-sparsity pruning should allocate not only retained weights but also repairable damage, using diagnostics like RR to guide allocation near critical recoverability transitions.

Abstract: At very high sparsity, neural network pruning does more than decide which weights remain. It also determines where pruning induced damage is placed across the network, and whether that damage can be recovered by a fixed lightweight repair procedure. We study this problem through the lens of repair conditioned sparsity allocation. We introduce Relative Repairability (RR), a calibration based diagnostic that compares the raw activation distortion caused by layerwise pruning with the residual distortion left after channelwise variance matching repair. RR estimates the fraction of local damage that remains after repair, using only unlabeled calibration data. Across ResNet18, ResNet34, and VGG16 BN on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100, we find that RR is not a universally dominant allocation rule. Instead, it is most useful near an architecture dependent recoverability transition, where standard structural or magnitude based allocation priors begin to lose reliability but post repair recovery has not yet fully collapsed. On CIFAR100 ResNet18, a fine grained sweep shows that RR improves over ERK across the central transition band and surpasses LAMP near the upper part of this band. A projection forced ablation further shows that capped ERK can over protect projection layers, shifting excessive sparsity onto regular convolutions and reducing post repair recovery. These results suggest that high sparsity pruning should allocate not only retained weights, but also repairable damage.

</details>


### [699] [SAE-FD: Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation for Continual Learning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25525)
*Mingxu Zhang,Yuhan Li,Lujundong Li,Dazhong Shen,Hui Xiong,Ying Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SAFD, a method using Sparse Autoencoders to decompose dense model representations into sparse features for better continual learning, reducing catastrophic forgetting by selectively protecting learned knowledge with less interference to new tasks. It achieves up to 52.70% accuracy with minimal backward transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Regularization-based continual learning methods operate in dense representation spaces suffering from feature superposition (multiple concepts encoded in overlapping dimensions), making it difficult to selectively protect previously learned knowledge without impeding new-task learning.

Method: Proposes SAFD (Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation) which anchors model representations in the sparse feature space of a pre-trained Sparse Autoencoder, decomposing dense activations into a sparse overcomplete basis to reduce representational entanglement and enable more targeted regularization.

Result: Consistently outperforms existing regularization-based methods across two benchmarks and three model architectures, achieving up to 52.70% average accuracy with only -0.46 backward transfer.

Conclusion: Sparse feature spaces via autoencoders offer a promising direction for mitigating catastrophic forgetting in continual learning by reducing representational entanglement and allowing more selective knowledge protection.

Abstract: Continual learning enables large language models to adapt to evolving tasks without retraining from scratch, yet catastrophic forgetting remains a central obstacle. Among continual learning methods, regularization-based approaches are widely used to constrain model updates and reduce forgetting, operating in weight space, gradient space, or output space. However, these dense representation spaces suffer from feature superposition, where multiple concepts are encoded in overlapping dimensions, making it difficult to selectively protect previously learned knowledge without impeding new-task learning. To address this issue, we propose \method (Sparse Autoencoder Feature Distillation), which anchors model representations in the sparse feature space of a pre-trained Sparse Autoencoder, where dense activations are decomposed into a sparse overcomplete basis that reduces representational entanglement, enabling more targeted regularization with less interference to new-task learning. Experiments on two continual learning benchmarks across three model architectures show that \method consistently outperforms existing regularization-based methods, achieving up to 52.70% average accuracy with only -0.46 backward transfer.

</details>


### [700] [DeepSeekMath Meets Order Book: Group-Aware Policy Optimization for High-Frequency Directional Trading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25527)
*Sayak Charabarty,Souradip Pal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that policy-gradient methods (PPO, GRPO, GSPO) with Order-Flow state models outperform value-based Q-learning in high-frequency trading on limit order books, improving PnL, profitability, and drawdown across AMZN, AAPL, and GOOG assets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve high-frequency trading performance on limit order books by moving beyond value-based RL (tabular Q-learning) to policy-gradient methods that can better capture continuous action spaces and stochastic market dynamics.

Method: Pairs an Order-Flow-based state model with policy-gradient methods including vanilla PPO, GRPO, and GSPO (DeepSeekMath-inspired variants with group-normalized updates and downside-aware shaping). Tested on AMZN, AAPL, GOOG under a simplified backtesting setup with spread-scaled rewards.

Result: Policy-gradient methods improved net average PnL, profitability, and drawdown compared to the Q-learning baseline across all three financial assets.

Conclusion: Order-Flow signals are an adequate state representation for policy RL, and group-aware PPO surrogates are preferable over value-based baselines for HFT on limit order books.

Abstract: This paper studies reinforcement learning for high-frequency trading on limit order books by pairing an Order-Flow-based state model with policy-gradient methods. Instead of value-based RL techniques like tabular Q-learning, our approach deploys policy-based methods like vanilla PPO and DeepSeekMath-inspired variants like GRPO and GSPO, that use group-normalized updates and downside-aware shaping. On backtests with financial assets AMZN, AAPL, and GOOG under a simplified backtesting setup based on spread-scaled rewards, these new policies improve net average PnL, profitability, and drawdown over the Q-Learning baseline. Our results show that (1) Order-Flow signals are an adequate state for policy RL and (2) group-aware PPO surrogates are preferable over value-based baselines.

</details>


### [701] ['Si'multaneous 'S'patial-'T'emporal Message Passing for Dynamic Graph Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25548)
*Shubhajit Roy,Anirban Dasgupta*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SiST-GNN fuses spatial and temporal signals in a single message-passing operation by treating each node's history and current features as a pair connected by a cross-time edge, achieving SOTA results (109-277% improvement in fixed-split, 68-194% in live-update) over prior methods on link prediction, and 7-22% over DTDG baselines on node classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DGNNs use rigid sequential pipelines (temporal-first or spatial-first), where the second stage consumes a compressed summary from the first, preventing joint reasoning over topology and evolution—message-passing operators cannot weigh neighbors' contributions by their past trajectories.

Method: Maintains a recurrent hidden state per node summarizing its history, pairs it with the node's current feature vector, treats the pair as two nodes joined by a cross-time edge, and runs standard graph convolution on this temporally augmented graph to produce updated representations.

Result: Across 14 model-dataset combos, SiST-GNN sets SOTA on link prediction: 109-277% improvement (fixed-split), 68-194% (live-update). On dynamic node classification, beats DTDG by 7-22% and matches CTDG methods.

Conclusion: Simultaneous fusion of spatial and temporal signals within a single message-passing operation is more effective than sequential chaining, achieving significant gains across evaluation regimes.

Abstract: Dynamic graph neural networks (DGNNs) that operate on snapshot sequences typically fall into one of two categories. \emph{Temporal-first} approaches build per-node temporal embeddings and only afterwards perform spatial aggregation, whereas \emph{Spatial-first} approaches invert this order, feeding the output of a graph convolution into a downstream temporal module. In either case, the rigid sequencing forces the second stage to consume an already-compressed summary produced by the first, ruling out joint reasoning over topology and evolution; concretely, the message-passing operator never gets to weight a neighbor's contribution by that neighbor's \emph{past} trajectory. This paper introduces \textbf{SiST-GNN} (\textbf{Si}multaneous \textbf{S}patial-\textbf{T}emporal \textbf{GNN}), which fuses the two signals inside a single message-passing operation rather than chaining them. Concretely, at each snapshot we maintain a recurrent hidden state per node that summarises its history, pair it with the node's current feature vector, and treat the pair as two nodes joined by a cross-time edge; running a standard graph convolution on this temporally augmented graph yields the updated representation. Our empirical study spans nine public baselines and fourteen model-dataset combinations, covering both fixed-split and live-update evaluation regimes. Across every public benchmark, SiST-GNN sets a new state of the art in link prediction task over the strongest prior method by $109$--$277\%$ in the fixed-split setting and by $68$--$194\%$ in the live-update setting. We additionally construct three dynamic node-classification tasks by discretising the underlying continuous-time event streams; here SiST-GNN beats the leading discrete-time (DTDG) baseline by $7$--$22\%$ and matches continuous-time (CTDG) methods that consume the raw events directly.

</details>


### [702] [Learning Permutation from Structure Without Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25551)
*Ran Eisenberg,Ofir Lindenbaum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces an entropy-adaptive Gumbel-Sinkhorn method that locally modulates temperature based on assignment uncertainty, improving stability and permutation quality in unsupervised ordering tasks compared to fixed-temperature approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learning hidden orderings from unordered data (e.g., sorting, jigsaw reconstruction) using permutation matrices is challenging because existing differentiable relaxation methods (Gumbel-Sinkhorn) use a single global temperature, forcing all assignments to sharpen or diffuse simultaneously. This causes instability at scale when some assignments become confident early while others remain ambiguous.

Method: An entropy-adaptive formulation of Gumbel-Sinkhorn that locally modulates temperature based on per-assignment uncertainty, allowing confident assignments to discretize early while preserving exploration where uncertainty remains.

Result: Across sorting, jigsaw reconstruction, and routing-style settings, adaptive entropy control improves training stability and final permutation quality compared to fixed-temperature baselines, especially as problem size and assignment ambiguity increase.

Conclusion: Adaptive entropy modulation of temperature in Gumbel-Sinkhorn effectively handles non-uniform uncertainty in unsupervised permutation learning, leading to more stable training and better quality permutations in larger and more ambiguous problems.

Abstract: Many learning problems require uncovering a hidden ordering that reveals structure in unordered data, such as monotonicity in sorting or spatial continuity in jigsaw reconstruction. In these settings, permutations can be learned as latent operators by optimizing objectives defined directly on the reordered output, often without access to ground-truth orderings. Differentiable relaxations such as Gumbel-Sinkhorn make this approach practical by approximating permutation matrices with doubly stochastic matrices. However, learning from structure without supervision induces a non-uniform uncertainty: some assignments become confident early, while others remain ambiguous. Existing methods control this process using a single global temperature, forcing all assignments to sharpen or diffuse simultaneously and leading to instability at scale. We introduce an entropy-adaptive formulation of Gumbel-Sinkhorn that locally modulates temperature based on assignment uncertainty. This allows confident assignments to discretize early while preserving exploration where uncertainty remains. Across sorting and jigsaw reconstruction tasks and in routing-style settings, adaptive entropy control improves training stability and final permutation quality relative to fixed-temperature baselines, particularly as problem size and assignment ambiguity increase.

</details>


### [703] [RotMoLE: Enhancing Mixture of Low-Rank Experts through Rotational Gating Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25565)
*Mengyang Sun,Maochuan Dou,Tao Feng,Dan Zhang,Yihao Wang,Junpeng Liu,Yifan Zhu,Jie Tang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RotMoLE introduces a rotation gate to MoE-LoRA, enabling better expert specialization and representation beyond simple scalar reweighting, showing effectiveness on multi-task and multilingual scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional MoE gating only applies scalar reweighting to experts, limiting representation and generalization capacity for complex domain-specific tasks.

Method: Proposes RotMoLE, a MoE framework for low-rank experts with an additional rotation gate that rotates selected expert outputs instead of just scaling them.

Result: Validated on complex multi-task and multilingual training scenarios, showing improved effectiveness.

Conclusion: RotMoLE enhances expert exploitation and specialization for learning diverse data, especially when expert candidates are limited, outperforming conventional scalar-based gating.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are commonly fine-tuned to handle domain-specific tasks before being applied to vertical applications, adapting them to complex scenarios with diverse specialized knowledge remains challenging. Meanwhile, Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture has risen as a crucial paradigm for training LLMs, and some recent works have also incorporated MoE into Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) to propose the Mixture of Low-rank Experts (MoE-LoRA), to enhance the power of low-rank adapters for learning complicated knowledge. However, conventional gating mechanisms in MoE typically apply only a scalar reweighing to selected experts, thereby limiting their underlying capacity of representation and generalization. Motivated and enabled by the low-rank structures in MoE-LoRA, we propose RotMoLE, a specialized MoE framework for low-rank experts featuring an additional rotation gate. Beyond simple scaling, RotMoLE implements a rotation mechanism for each selected expert, enabling superior expert exploitation and specialization for learning diverse data, especially when expert candidates are limited. Empirical results on complex multi-task and multilingual training scenarios validate our effectiveness.

</details>


### [704] [Geometric Flow Matching for Molecular Conformation Generation via Manifold Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25577)
*Yunqing Liu,Yi Zhou,Wenqi Fan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GO-Flow improves 3D molecular conformation generation by decomposing the process into physically meaningful manifolds (translation, rotation, torsion) instead of treating molecules as unstructured point clouds, achieving state-of-the-art results with as few as 50 sampling steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion/flow matching models treat molecules as unstructured point clouds in Cartesian space, ignoring the hierarchical mechanics where bond lengths/angles are stiff and torsion angles are flexible, leading to physically implausible intermediate structures.

Method: GO-Flow decomposes generation into three physically motivated subspaces: (1) translation space with linear optimal transport, (2) rotation space with geodesic flows on SO(3), and (3) conformation space with entropic optimal transport, combined with equivariant neural architectures.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art generation quality on GEOM-Drugs and GEOM-QM9 benchmarks, with high-fidelity sampling using as few as 50 steps.

Conclusion: Aligning generative modeling with molecular geometry via manifold decomposition bridges the gap between structural precision and computational efficiency, producing physically plausible conformations.

Abstract: The generation of accurate 3D molecular conformations is a pivotal challenge in computational chemistry and drug discovery. Recently, diffusion and flow matching models have achieved remarkable success. However, there is a critical misalignment between their mathematical formulation and the physical reality of molecules. Existing approaches predominantly treat molecules as unstructured point clouds in Cartesian space, overlooking the intrinsic hierarchical mechanics where bond lengths and bond angles are relatively stiff, whereas torsion angles constitute the dominant flexible degrees of freedom. This lack of manifold awareness forces models to relearn fundamental geometric constraints from scratch, often leading to physically implausible intermediate structures. To address this, we propose GO-Flow that aligns generative modeling with molecular geometry via manifold decomposition. Instead of forcing motion through Euclidean space, GO-Flow decomposes the generation process into three physically motivated subspaces: translation space with linear optimal transport, rotation space with geodesic flows on $SO(3)$, and conformation space with entropic optimal transport. This decomposition injects geometric inductive biases and makes the generative paths better aligned with molecular degrees of freedom. When combined with equivariant neural architectures, it encourages rotation-consistent generation and improves geometric validity. Extensive experiments on GEOM-Drugs and GEOM-QM9 demonstrate that GO-Flow achieves state-of-the-art generation quality. Notably, by learning straighter probability paths on the correct manifolds naturally, our method enables high-fidelity sampling with as few as 50 steps, effectively bridging the gap between structural precision and computational efficiency.

</details>


### [705] [Learning Latent Dynamical Causal Processes for Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25581)
*Wenkang Jiang,Yuhang Liu,Erdun Gao,Ehsan Abbasnejad,Lina Yao,Javen Qinfeng Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CITE-VAE is a latent dynamical causal generative model for single-cell perturbation prediction that jointly captures latent cellular programs, perturbation-conditioned mechanisms, and temporal evolution, showing improved OOD generalization on CRISPR data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods capture only one side of the response: latent causal approaches treat perturbations as static outcomes, while temporal models describe gene expression changes over time but do not recover latent causal generative mechanisms. The authors argue perturbation effects are both latent and dynamical.

Method: Proposes a latent dynamical causal generative model that jointly captures latent cellular programs, perturbation-conditioned mechanisms, and temporal evolution, with an identifiability analysis showing recoverability up to standard equivalence classes. Developed as CITE-VAE.

Result: Validated on Causal-3DIdent with theoretical results confirmed. On real-world CRISPR-based single-cell perturbation data, CITE-VAE shows improved generalization to unseen perturbations compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

Conclusion: CITE-VAE effectively bridges the gap between latent causal models and temporal dynamics for perturbation prediction, demonstrating practical robustness in OOD generalization.

Abstract: Single-cell perturbation prediction aims to infer how cells respond to unseen interventions and to achieve out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization, providing a computational route to understanding how perturbations reshape cellular programs over time. Existing machine learning methods have made important progress, but typically capture only one side of the response. Latent causal approaches seek mechanisms that support generalization and interpretation, yet often treat perturbation effects as static outcomes. Temporal models describe how gene expression changes across time, but usually do not explicitly recover the latent causal generative mechanisms driving these changes. In practice, perturbation effects are both latent and dynamical: interventions act through unobserved cellular programs, whose states evolve over time and give rise to observed expression profiles. Motivated by this view, we propose a latent dynamical causal generative model for single-cell perturbation data that jointly captures latent cellular programs, perturbation-conditioned mechanisms, and temporal evolution. We further provide an identifiability analysis showing that, under suitable conditions, the latent causal variables are recoverable up to standard equivalence classes. Guided by this analysis, we develop CITE-VAE, a learning framework for recovering latent cellular programs and their perturbation-driven dynamics from single-cell sequencing data. Experiments on Causal-3DIdent validate the theoretical results and the effectiveness of the proposed method in controlled settings. Additional experiments on real-world CRISPR-based single-cell perturbation data show improved generalization to unseen perturbations compared with state-of-the-art baselines, highlighting the practical robustness of our approach.

</details>


### [706] [Extreme Region Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25582)
*Changyu Chen,Xiting Wang,Rui Yan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ERPD is a two-stage framework that decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency: first performs aggressive off-policy optimization to extract training signals, then distills these signals into the base policy under trust-region constraints, enabling better performance with less KL divergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the trade-off between sample efficiency and asymptotic performance in RL for LLMs: on-policy methods waste data, while off-policy methods suffer from distribution mismatch that existing trust-region techniques only mitigate conservatively, leaving training signals underutilized.

Method: Two-stage framework: Stage 1 performs weakly constrained off-policy optimization on fixed data to maximally extract token-level supervision signals. Stage 2 distills these signals into the base policy under trust-region constraints (KL constraints), filtering harmful drift while preserving useful improvements.

Result: The distilled policy achieves comparable or better performance with substantially smaller KL divergence. The method works with both strong and weak teachers—even degenerate teachers provide effective supervision through alternative signal construction strategies. Validated on mathematical reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Much of the divergence from aggressive off-policy updates is spent on unnecessary drift rather than genuine improvement. ERPD effectively decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency, benefiting both strong base models (where on-policy training plateaus) and weak teachers (providing reliable improvements).

Abstract: Reinforcement learning for large language models faces a fundamental trade-off between sample efficiency and asymptotic performance: strictly on-policy methods discard trajectories after a single update, while off-policy reuse introduces distribution mismatch that existing trust-region techniques mitigate primarily by enforcing conservative optimization, often leaving rich training signals underutilized. To investigate this, we perform extensive off-policy updates on fixed data. Our experiments reveal that aggressive multi-step optimization brings rapid initial gains, but excessive updates cause trajectory probabilities to deviate and entropy to collapse, with performance plateauing early. Tightening KL constraints merely lowers the ceiling without resolving the degradation. This motivates Extreme Region Policy Distillation (ERPD), a two-stage framework that decouples sample efficiency from KL efficiency. The first stage performs weakly constrained off-policy optimization on fixed data to maximally extract training signals. The resulting policy provides token-level supervision. In the second stage, we distill these signals into the base policy under trust-region constraints, filtering harmful drift while preserving useful signals. The distilled policy achieves comparable or better performance with substantially smaller KL divergence, indicating that much of the first-stage divergence was spent on unnecessary drift rather than genuine improvement. Crucially, ERPD accommodates both strong and weak teachers: when aggressive optimization yields no stronger policy, even degenerate teachers provide effective supervision via alternative signal construction strategies. We validate ERPD on mathematical reasoning, showing gains for strong base models where on-policy training plateaus, and reliable improvements with weak teachers.

</details>


### [707] [Generalized Evidential Deep Learning: From a Bayesian Perspective](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25599)
*Yuanye Liu,Yibo Gao,Yuanyang Chen,Xiahai Zhuang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper provides a theoretical foundation for Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) by interpreting it within a generalized Bayesian framework, and proposes Generalized Evidential Deep Learning (GEDL) — a unified framework that disentangles components of EDL variants and achieves comparable performance in classification, uncertainty estimation, and OOD detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EDL variants lack systematic theoretical investigation and the relationships among them are unclear. This paper aims to establish a principled theoretical foundation and unify these variants under a common framework.

Method: The authors interpret EDL within a generalized Bayesian framework (prior specification, posterior update, training objective), characterize evidential uncertainty via Bayesian distributional uncertainty with asymptotic analysis, and propose GEDL — a unified framework that explicitly disentangles the roles of individual components.

Result: GEDL yields comparable results to existing EDL variants on classification, uncertainty estimation, and out-of-distribution (OOD) detection tasks.

Conclusion: GEDL provides a theoretically grounded and extensible framework for evidential deep learning that systematically relates existing variants while maintaining competitive empirical performance.

Abstract: Evidential Deep Learning (EDL) has emerged as an efficient, sampling-free strategy for uncertainty estimation. A series of EDL variants have been proposed to address specific limitations of the original framework, achieving notable success. However, the underlying theoretical structure of EDL and the relationships among these variants have received limited systematic investigation. In this work, we establish a principled theoretical foundation for EDL by interpreting it within a generalized Bayesian framework that includes prior specification, posterior update, and training objective. We further characterize evidential uncertainty from a Bayesian distributional uncertainty viewpoint, established via asymptotic analysis. Building on this perspective, we further propose Generalized Evidential Deep Learning (GEDL), a unified and extensible framework that explicitly disentangles the roles of individual components and systematically relates GEDL to existing variants. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GEDL yields comparable results on classification, uncertainty estimation and OOD detections, with theoretical grounding.

</details>


### [708] [Towards the Connection between Activation Sparsity and Flat Minima](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25612)
*Ze Peng,Jian Zhang,Lei Qi,Yang Gao,Yinghuan Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper discovers that activation sparsity in Transformer MLP blocks is linked to the flatness of loss landscapes, formalized as a ratio between "augmented flatness" and the product of input norm and activation gradient. The authors propose three plug-and-play methods to encourage sparser activations, achieving significant improvements in both inference and training sparsity on ImageNet-1K and C4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing theoretical explanations for activation sparsity rely on strong assumptions that don't hold for deep models trained with many steps. The authors aim to find a weaker, naturally emerging assumption—loss landscape flatness—to better explain and leverage sparsity in standard Transformers.

Method: 1) Theoretically derive that MLP activation sparsity = ratio of "augmented flatness" to (input norm × activation gradient). 2) Propose derivative sparsity as a more stable alternative under ReLU. 3) Develop three plug-and-play methods to decrease numerator or increase denominator of the ratio to encourage sparser activations.

Result: On ImageNet-1K and C4, the methods achieve relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers.

Conclusion: Loss landscape flatness provides a weaker and more applicable assumption for explaining activation sparsity in deep Transformers. The derived ratio can be exploited to effectively boost sparsity, reducing computation costs in both training and inference.

Abstract: The observation that activation sparsity emerges in MLP blocks of standardly trained Transformers offers an opportunity to drastically reduce computation costs without sacrificing performance. To theoretically explain this phenomenon, existing works have shown that activation sparsity does not result from the data properties or data fitting but from the implicit bias of the training process. However, these connections are obtained with strong assumptions, which cannot be applied to deep models standardly trained with a large number of steps. Different from these works, we find that the flatness of loss landscapes is also closely related to the MLP activation sparsity and can serve as a weaker and naturally emerging assumption standard deep networks. Specifically, we find that 1) the MLP activation sparsity equals a ratio between "augmented flatness" (a weighted sum of flatness measures) and the product of the input norm and activation gradient of the MLP. We empirically find that this ratio decreases during training, leading to sparse activations. 2) We also propose the notion of derivative sparsity, which reduces to activation sparsity under ReLU, but further enables pruning in the backward propagation and is more stable than activation sparsity. With the theoretical findings, we can further encourage activation sparsity by decreasing the numerator and increasing the denominator of the ratio using three methods. These plug-and-play modifications can effectively reduce the ratio and produce sparser activations. Experiments on ImageNet-1K and C4 demonstrate relative improvements of at least 36% on inference sparsity and at least 50% on training sparsity over vanilla Transformers, indicating further potential cost reduction in both inference and training

</details>


### [709] [Courtroom Analogy: New Perspective on Uncertainty-Aware Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25616)
*Taeseong Yoon,Heeyoung Kim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoDEX introduces a courtroom analogy for interpretable uncertainty quantification, modeling classification as a debate among class-specific Dirichlet experts, achieving state-of-the-art UQ performance with structured, semantically meaningful uncertainty estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing UQ methods enhance distribution expressiveness but lack interpretability, offering limited insight into how predictive uncertainty is structured and aggregated.

Method: Proposes the courtroom analogy: class-specific advocates form probabilistic opinions (Dirichlet distributions with decomposed concentration parameters), aggregated via input-dependent plausibility weights. Instantiates this as MoDEX, a single-pass neural architecture predicting courtroom parameters.

Result: MoDEX achieves state-of-the-art UQ performance across diverse benchmarks, with strong theoretical properties and interpretable uncertainty estimates.

Conclusion: The structured mixture of Dirichlet distributions with semantically interpretable parameters enables both expressiveness and interpretability in single-pass UQ for classification.

Abstract: Single-pass uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for classification represent uncertainty by predicting a tractable distribution over the class probability vector. While existing approaches primarily focus on enhancing the expressiveness of this distribution, they often provide limited insight into how predictive uncertainty is structured and aggregated, resulting in weak interpretability. We introduce the courtroom analogy, which conceptualizes uncertainty-aware classification as a structured debate among class-specific advocates. Each advocate forms a probabilistic opinion, and a final verdict is reached by aggregating these opinions using input-dependent plausibility weights. In this framework, each advocate's opinion is modeled as a Dirichlet distribution whose concentration parameter is decomposed into shared evidence and class-specific advocacy. This yields a structured mixture of Dirichlet distributions with semantically interpretable parameters. To instantiate this formulation, we propose Mixture of Dirichlet EXperts (MoDEX), a single-pass neural architecture that predicts the courtroom parameters, enabling efficient and expressive UQ while explicitly modeling uncertainty aggregation. We demonstrate that MoDEX enjoys strong theoretical properties and achieves state-of-the-art UQ performance across diverse benchmarks, yielding interpretable uncertainty estimates with meaningful semantics.

</details>


### [710] [Analogies between Transformer Layers and Power Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25619)
*Chenglong Li,Claudio Altafini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper reveals an analogy between transformer layer operations (projections and layer normalizations) and the power method, showing tokens align with the principal eigenvector of weight matrix products, with special insights for shared-weight transformers and a method for steering outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the underlying mathematical principles of transformer operations and how tokens evolve through layers, drawing an analogy to the power method.

Method: The authors establish a formal analogy between transformer layer operations (projections + layer normalizations, excluding FFN) and steps of the power method, analyzing token alignment with principal eigenvectors of weight matrix products.

Result: Tokens tend to align with the principal eigenvector of the product of output and value weight matrices per layer; this alignment is especially strong and analytically provable in shared-weight transformers.

Conclusion: The power method analogy provides theoretical insight into transformer behavior and suggests a practical method for steering transformer outputs toward arbitrary directions in token space.

Abstract: In the paper we show that there is an analogy between the operations occurring in a layer of a transformer (projections and layer normalizations, disregarding the feedforward neural network) and a step in the power method. Coherently with this analogy, we show that passing through a layer the tokens tend to be tilted towards the principal eigenvector of a matrix which is the product of the output and value weight matrices of that layer. In the special case of a transformer with shared weights (i.e., in which all layers have identical weights) then the alignment with this principal eigenvector is particularly evident empirically, and can also be shown analytically. The analogy also suggests a method to steer the output of the transformer towards an arbitrary desired direction in token space.

</details>


### [711] [Closed-Form Node Classification with Exact Graph Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25662)
*Aditya Gaur,Charu Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A closed-form (non-gradient) framework for node classification that matches or beats standard GNNs on most benchmarks, while enabling exact graph unlearning with dramatic speedups (up to 10^6× vs. gradient retraining).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine how much of modern GNN performance can be recovered by deterministic closed-form solvers (avoiding gradient descent), and to provide formal guarantees this enables (e.g., exact unlearning).

Method: Routed closed-form framework selected by adjusted homophily: (1) For assortative graphs → SGC-style propagation + Ridge regression. (2) For heterophilous graphs → LCF-Net (layer-wise closed-form feature refinement with per-layer Ridge solves, capped by Gaussian kernel-Ridge head). Evaluated on 14 benchmarks.

Result: Matches/beats best vanilla 2-layer GCN/SAGE/GAT on 9/9 measured datasets; ties deep recipes within 1σ on 9/12 small benchmarks; exceeds OGB-leaderboard plain GCN on both large graphs. Enables exact graph-object unlearning (label/feature/edge/node/subgraph) with 21–45× speedups over re-solving, ~10^6× over gradient retraining.

Conclusion: Closed-form solvers are highly competitive with gradient-trained GNNs, and they unlock formal guarantees (exact unlearning, K-hop locality) that gradient methods cannot practically provide. The remaining heterophilous gap is primarily architectural, not optimization-related.

Abstract: Graph neural networks for node classification are typically trained by gradient descent over hundreds or thousands of epochs. Recent work has shown that, when properly tuned, classic GCN/SAGE/GAT architectures can match graph transformers on many node-classification benchmarks. We ask a complementary question: how much of this performance can be recovered by deterministic closed-form solvers, and what guarantees does this enable?
  We introduce a routed closed-form framework selected by adjusted homophily. For assortative graphs, we use SGC-style propagation followed by Ridge regression; for heterophilous graphs, we introduce LCF-Net, a layer-wise closed-form graph feature-refinement network whose per-layer Ridge solves are capped by a Gaussian kernel-Ridge head. Across 14 benchmarks, including ogbn-arxiv and ogbn-proteins, our closed-form predictors match or beat the best vanilla 2-layer GCN/SAGE/GAT on 9 of 9 measured datasets, tie tuned deep recipes within one standard deviation on 9 of 12 small benchmarks, and exceed the OGB-leaderboard plain GCN on both large graphs. The remaining heterophilous gap closely tracks the gain from vanilla 2-layer to deep SAGE, suggesting that the residual difference is primarily architectural.
  Because our predictors are explicit solutions of deterministic linear systems, modified graph inputs can be re-solved to obtain retrain-equivalent parameters. We formalize exact graph-object unlearning for label, feature, edge, node, and subgraph modifications, prove K-hop locality for Ridge components, and verify exactness across 109 configurations. On ogbn-arxiv, localized updates give $21$--$45\times$ speedups over full re-solving and roughly $10^{6}\times$ speedups over gradient retraining. Structural-inversion experiments further quantify the privacy floor of exact retraining and the additional leakage of approximate graph-unlearning methods.

</details>


### [712] [Opportunistic Target Selection: Early Directional Commitment for Query-Efficient Black-Box Adversarial Attacks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25663)
*Florent Tariolle,Florian Yger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Opportunistic Target Selection (OTS), a lightweight wrapper that converts untargeted adversarial attacks to targeted ones by locking onto the leading non-true class early in the attack, improving success rates by up to 27 percentage points on random-search attacks without requiring architectural changes or gradient access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Black-box adversarial attacks suffer from class drift—perturbations make diffuse, undirected progress without committing to a specific adversarial class, wasting queries.

Method: OTS is a lightweight wrapper that switches untargeted attacks to a targeted objective by locking onto whichever non-true class currently leads in confidence, requiring no architectural modification, gradient access, or a priori target knowledge.

Result: On random-search attacks, OTS achieves up to +27 pp success rate and 43% relative reduction in censored-mean iterations on ResNet-50. It closely tracks oracle performance. On gradient-estimation attacks and adversarially-trained models, OTS provides no benefit.

Conclusion: OTS effectively mitigates class drift in random-search attacks by acting as a margin-loss surrogate, but is redundant for gradient-estimation attacks and ineffective on adversarially-trained models due to bimodal difficulty distribution.

Abstract: Black-box adversarial attacks that minimize only the ground-truth confidence suffer from class drift: perturbations wander through the feature space without committing to a specific adversarial class, wasting queries on diffuse, undirected progress. We introduce Opportunistic Target Selection (OTS), a lightweight wrapper that switches an untargeted attack to a targeted objective early in its trajectory, locking onto whichever non-true class currently leads. OTS requires no architectural modification to the underlying attack, no gradient access, and no a priori target-class knowledge.
  We validate OTS on three score-based attacks (SimBA, Square Attack with cross-entropy loss, and Bandits) across five standard ImageNet classifiers (4,500 runs). On random-search attacks, OTS closely tracks oracle performance, with gains up to +27 pp in success rate and 43% relative reduction in censored-mean iterations on ResNet-50. On gradient-estimation attacks (Bandits) and attacks with margin loss, OTS is redundant, a negative result that reinforces our interpretation of OTS as a margin-loss surrogate. On adversarially-trained models, a bimodal difficulty distribution eliminates the regime where targeting helps.

</details>


### [713] [Stochastic Estimation of the Layer-wise Hessian Trace for Monitoring Neural-network Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25674)
*Maxim Bolshim,Alexander Kugaevskikh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a stochastic estimator for the trace of diagonal blocks of the Hessian matrix of a neural network's empirical risk. It combines Hutchinson's trace estimator with a single Hessian-vector product to compute unbiased per-layer traces in one backward pass. The method detects the label-memorization regime in ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 on CIFAR-10/100, achieving 179/180 detection power at 16/120 false-alarm rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The loss and gradient norm separate healthy and pathological training regimes only weakly, while curvature (Hessian) information differs qualitatively but is computationally inaccessible at parameter counts of 10^6-10^8.

Method: Combines Hutchinson stochastic trace estimator with a single Hessian-vector product over the entire parameter vector to recover unbiased estimates of per-layer Hessian traces in one backward pass. Addresses weight sharing bias and derives closed-form variance expression with a critical probe count K* in [5,10].

Result: Achieves 179/180 detection power at 16/120 false-alarm rate for detecting label-memorization regime across ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100.

Conclusion: The proposed layer-wise Hessian trace estimator is efficient and effective for monitoring neural network training regimes, with practical recommendation of 5-10 probes per mini-batch.

Abstract: The loss and the norm of its gradient separate the healthy and the pathological regimes of neural-network training only weakly, whilst the curvature of the empirical risk differs qualitatively between them but is inaccessible explicitly at parameter counts $P\sim 10^{6}-10^{8}$. We present a stochastic estimator of the trace of the diagonal blocks of the Hessian matrix of the empirical risk of a neural network. The procedure combines the Hutchinson stochastic trace estimator with a single Hessian-vector product over the whole parameter vector and recovers unbiased estimates of every per-layer trace in one backward pass through the computational graph. We show that correctness under weight sharing requires the layer-wise Hessian to be assembled before the second differentiation: unrolling shared weights into independent coordinates introduces a systematic bias whose sign and magnitude are governed by the cross-instance blocks of the unrolled Hessian. A closed-form expression for the variance of the estimator at a fixed Hessian is derived, together with a decomposition of the total variance under the mini-batch sampling distribution. This decomposition yields a critical probe count $K^{\star}$ that balances the two sources of randomness and supports the practical recommendation $K\in[5,10]$ in the on-line monitoring regime. The estimator is applied to the detection of the label-memorisation regime of ResNet-18, ResNet-34, and VGG-11 on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, where a calibrated cumulative-sum decision rule attains an empirical detection power of $179/180$ at a false-alarm rate of $16/120$.

</details>


### [714] [Don't Retrain, Just Reuse: Recovering Dual-Target Molecules from Single-Target Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25681)
*Qingyuan Zeng,Pengxiang Cai,Zixin Guan,Ziyang Chen,Anglin Liu,Lang Qin,Xinyao Lai,Jintai Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REUSE is a generator-preserving method that searches input space of a frozen single-target diffusion model via hierarchical evolutionary optimization to discover dual-target drug candidates, achieving 20.9% higher Dual High Affinity than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dual-target generative methods either retrain the generator (costly, unstable with sparse supervision) or intervene in the diffusion process (sensitive to target balancing). The authors aim to keep the pretrained generator frozen and instead search its input space for dual-target candidates.

Method: REUSE formulates dual-target generation as constrained multi-objective optimization. It uses a hierarchical evolutionary input-space search (pair-conditioned exploration + structured multi-stage selection) to enforce dual-target affinity, chemical quality, and diversity, without modifying the frozen single-target diffusion model's parameters or denoising dynamics.

Result: REUSE consistently improves dual-target affinity and balance over methods that modify the diffusion process, achieving a 20.9-percentage-point gain in Dual High Affinity while maintaining competitive molecular quality.

Conclusion: Generator-preserving input-space search is a viable and effective alternative to modifying diffusion models for dual-target drug generation, overcoming limitations of retraining or diffusion process intervention.

Abstract: Designing a single molecule that modulates two targets is a promising strategy for polypharmacology, but it remains substantially harder than standard single-target generation because one candidate must satisfy two binding requirements while preserving drug-likeness and synthesizability. Existing dual-target generative methods typically introduce dual-target capability by either retraining the generator or intervening in the diffusion process during sampling. The former can be costly and difficult to stabilize when dual-target supervision is sparse, while the latter may be sensitive to denoising-time target balancing and competing update directions. These limitations motivate a generator-preserving alternative that keeps the pretrained prior intact: can dual-target candidates instead be recovered from the input space of a frozen single-target diffusion model, without modifying its parameters or denoising dynamics? We formulate this task as a constrained multi-objective optimization problem and propose REUSE, a hierarchical evolutionary input-space search framework that combines pair-conditioned exploration with structured multi-stage selection to enforce dual-target affinity, chemical quality, and diversity. Experiments show that, compared with methods that modify the diffusion process, REUSE consistently improves dual-target affinity and balance, achieving a 20.9-percentage-point gain in Dual High Affinity over the strongest prior baseline while maintaining competitive molecular quality.

</details>


### [715] [Evaluating passing decision-making in professional football: An enhanced MPNN approach to Receiver Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25696)
*Gabriel Masella,Giuseppe Alessio D'Inverno,Max Goldsmith,Gianluigi Rozza*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A GNN framework predicts optimal passing targets in football by modeling player interactions as dynamic graphs, achieving competitive accuracy and enabling rapid analysis of passing options.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Decision-making in football involves complex spatial, pressure, and intent factors; automated prediction of optimal passing targets can aid performance analysis.

Method: Graph Neural Network (GNN/MPNN) modeling players as nodes with positional/contextual features and passing lines as weighted edges (distance, angle, pressure). Trained on tracking + event data synchronized via optimized Needleman-Wunsch Algorithm.

Result: Competitive accuracy in identifying actual receiver; state-of-the-art accuracy in top-3 suggestions. Can evaluate over 1,000 passes in seconds.

Conclusion: The model effectively quantifies pass likelihood, threat, and creativity, offering a practical tool for rapid football performance analysis.

Abstract: The process of decision-making in football is characterized by a complex interplay between spatial positioning, opponent pressure, and player intent. This work introduces a Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework designed to predict Receiver Selection, the optimal passing target, by modeling on-field interactions as dynamic graphs. Each player is represented as a node with positional and contextual features, while potential passing lines form weighted edges characterized by distance, angle, and pressure metrics. A Message-Passing Neural Network (MPNN) has been developed and trained using a combination of tracking data and event data from professional matches, synchronized through a robust pipeline based on an optimized version of the Needleman-Wunsch Algorithm. The model achieves competitive accuracy in identifying the actual chosen receiver and state-of-the-art accuracy within its top three suggestions. Our model further offers quantification of each option's likelihood, threat, and creativity, enabling performance analysts to evaluate over 1,000 passes in seconds.

</details>


### [716] [How Should LLMs Consume High-Quality Data? Optimal Data Scheduling via Quality-Aware Functional Scaling Laws](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25698)
*Zhitao Zhu,Xili Wang,Shizhe Wu,Jiawei Fu,Xiaoqing Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper extends functional scaling laws to incorporate data quality, solves the joint data-quality and batch-size scheduling problem in closed form, and proposes Drop-Stable-Rampup—a midtraining schedule that drops batch size upon quality transition, holds stable to accumulate signal, then ramps up to suppress noise. It improves a 15B MoE model's accuracy by +1.70 over WSD and +2.98 over Cosine on 108B tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-quality data is scarce in LLM training, but there is no theoretical guidance on how to schedule its use jointly with training dynamics (batch size, learning schedule).

Method: Extends functional scaling laws with a data-quality dimension, solves the joint data-quality and batch-size scheduling problem in asymptotic closed form, and proposes the Drop-Stable-Rampup schedule for LLM midtraining.

Result: On a 15B MoE model midtrained on 108B tokens, Drop-Stable-Rampup improves average accuracy by +1.70 over WSD and +2.98 over Cosine, with large gains on GSM8K (+4.23) and MATH (+2.80).

Conclusion: High-quality data plays a dual role: as a signal amplifier (lower batch size) and as a noise suppressor (late placement). Existing curricula miss the first role; the proposed schedule exploits both, yielding significant empirical gains.

Abstract: High-quality data is scarce in large language model (LLM) training, yet how to schedule its use jointly with training dynamics lacks theoretical guidance. We extend functional scaling laws by incorporating a data-quality dimension, and solve the joint data-quality and batch-size scheduling problem in asymptotic closed form. The solution reveals two regimes and a dual role of high-quality data. In the noise-limited regime, high-quality data should be used as a signal amplifier: lowering the batch size converts cleaner data into more signal without amplifying noise. In the signal-limited regime, it should be used as a noise suppressor: late placement reduces terminal noise without sacrificing signal accumulation. Existing curriculum-style pipelines primarily exploit the second role by placing cleaner data late, but miss the first role because conventional decay schedules reduce update intensity exactly when high-quality data becomes available. Guided by this, we propose Drop-Stable-Rampup for LLM midtraining: upon the quality transition, drop the batch size, hold it stable to accumulate signal, then ramp up to suppress terminal noise. On a 15B Mixture-of-Experts model midtrained on 108B tokens, Drop-Stable-Rampup improves average accuracy over Warmup-Stable-Decay (WSD) by +1.70 and over Cosine-decay by +2.98, with particularly large gains on mathematical reasoning benchmarks such as GSM8K (+4.23) and MATH (+2.80).

</details>


### [717] [The Behavioral Credibility Trilemma: When Calibrated Autonomy Becomes Impossible](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25739)
*Lauri Lovén,Nam Do,Hassan Mehmood,Dinesh Kumar Sah,Sasu Tarkoma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves a "Behavioral Credibility Trilemma" showing that no reinforcement learning policy can simultaneously achieve maximum helpfulness, optimal calibration, and full autonomy when tasks exceed the agent's competence. This impossibility stems from the geometric conflict between autonomy incentives and proper scoring rules. The paper formalizes the problem, tests five pre-registered hypotheses across 540 configurations, and identifies two resolution pathways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To prove that confidence-gated autonomy in RL policies inherently creates a fundamental trade-off between helpfulness, calibration, and autonomy—a trilemma that cannot be resolved under rational oversight when tasks exceed the agent's reliable competence.

Method: Geometric proof showing non-affine autonomy incentives destroy strict properness of scoring rules; Behavioral Perturbation Lemma quantifying confidence inflation (scaling as w_A/(2w_C) for Brier score); analysis showing optimal oversight is non-affine; 540-configuration Best-of-N experiment testing five pre-registered hypotheses.

Result: All five pre-registered hypotheses strongly confirmed (effect sizes d = 1.10 to 5.32). The achievable-(H,C,A) surface shows a plateau-truncated frontier matching predicted inflation saturation. Detection of inflation requires Ω(1/Δ²) observations.

Conclusion: The Behavioral Credibility Trilemma is unconditional and optimizer-independent across log-concave-density policy families. Two resolution pathways exist: commitment and domain separation.

Abstract: We prove that no reinforcement learning policy with confidence-gated autonomy can simultaneously achieve maximum helpfulness, optimal calibration, and full autonomy under rational oversight, whenever some tasks exceed the agent's reliable competence: the Behavioral Credibility Trilemma. The impossibility is geometric -- adding any non-affine autonomy incentive to a strictly proper scoring rule destroys strict properness, so an agent rewarded for both calibrated confidence and autonomous action systematically inflates its reported confidence on tasks below the principal's approval threshold. The Behavioral Perturbation Lemma quantifies the inflation (scaling as $w_A/(2 w_C)$ for the Brier score) and shows detection requires $Ω(1/Δ^2)$ observations. We prove the principal's optimal oversight rule is necessarily non-affine, making the impossibility unconditional and optimizer-independent across log-concave-density policy families. We formalize the Confidence-Gated Decision Problem, map existing methods onto the trilemma, and identify two constructive resolution pathways (commitment, domain separation). A 540-configuration Best-of-N experiment tests five pre-registered hypotheses, all strongly confirmed (effect sizes $d = 1.10$ to $5.32$), and adds a descriptive analysis of the achievable-$(H, C, A)$ surface geometry showing a plateau-truncated frontier consistent with the predicted inflation saturation.

</details>


### [718] [Latent Representation Alignment for Offline Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25740)
*Hyungkyu Kang,Byeongchan Kim,Min-hwan Oh*

Main category: cs.LG
