TL;DR: LAVL is a new offline GCRL algorithm that tackles erroneous generalization in value functions using latent representation alignment and hierarchical planning, achieving state-of-the-art results on 20/22 OGBench datasets, especially in long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Erroneous generalization in goal-conditioned value functions is identified as a fundamental bottleneck for offline GCRL in long-horizon tasks; appropriate inductive bias in value functions is crucial to address this.

Method: Latent-Aligned Value Learning (LAVL) integrates latent-representation-based value generalization with hierarchical planning in a unified offline GCRL framework.

Result: LAVL consistently outperforms existing offline GCRL methods, achieving highest performance on 20 out of 22 OGBench datasets, with strong performance in long-horizon tasks and trajectory stitching.

Conclusion: Addressing erroneous generalization via latent alignment and hierarchical planning significantly improves offline GCRL, especially for long-horizon and trajectory stitching scenarios where prior methods degrade.

Abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) provides a practical framework for obtaining goal-reaching policies from fixed datasets. However, learning a reliable goal-conditioned value function in long-horizon tasks remains challenging. In this paper, we identify erroneous generalization in goal-conditioned value functions as a fundamental bottleneck, and demonstrate that appropriate inductive bias in the value function is crucial for addressing the bottleneck. Building on these findings, we propose Latent-Aligned Value Learning (LAVL), an offline GCRL algorithm that integrates latent-representation-based value generalization with hierarchical planning in a unified framework. Extensive experiments on OGBench demonstrate that LAVL consistently outperforms existing offline GCRL methods, achieving the highest performance on 20 out of 22 datasets. Notably, LAVL exhibits strong performance in long-horizon tasks and trajectory stitching datasets, where prior methods suffer significant performance degradation. Our code is available at https://github.com/oh-lab/LAVL.git.

</details>


### [719] [Invariant-Based Weight Sharing for Message Passing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25750)
*Florian Seiffarth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ShareGNNs introduce a structure-aware weight sharing principle that indexes weights by graph invariants, improving MPNN expressivity beyond 1-WL while maintaining scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MPNNs only apply weights to node/edge features, ignoring structural patterns. This limits their ability to capture graph structure effectively.

Method: ShareGNNs use graph invariants to index weights directly, enabling systematic weight sharing across structurally equivalent subgraphs. The architecture employs a simple encoder-decoder design with learnable adjacency and transformer-like connectivity.

Result: Consistent improvements over standard MPNNs on synthetic and real-world data, competitive expressivity beyond the 1-WL test, and scalability to large datasets.

Conclusion: ShareGNNs provide explicit control over model complexity via chosen invariants, achieving stronger expressivity by incorporating structural information directly into weight computation.

Abstract: Message-passing neural networks (MPNNs) are a powerful framework for learning representations of graph-structured domains. However, weights in MPNNs act on features only, limiting their ability to capture structural patterns. We introduce a novel structure-aware weight sharing principle that explicitly incorporates information inherent to the graph structure. Weights are indexed directly by user-chosen graph invariants, i.e., functions preserved under node permutations, enabling systematic reuse across structurally equivalent subgraphs. We present ShareGNNs, which instantiate this principle within a simple encoder-decoder architecture, resulting in an MPNN with learnable adjacency and transformer-like connectivity. We show that their expressivity is at least as strong as the discriminative power of the chosen invariants, providing explicit control over the model complexity. Experiments on synthetic and real-world data, as well as subgraph counting tasks, demonstrate consistent improvements over standard MPNNs, competitive expressivity beyond the 1-WL test, and scalability to large datasets.

</details>


### [720] [MDGMIX: Boundary-Aware Subgraph Mixing for Multi-Domain Graph Pre-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25771)
*Ziyu Zheng,Yaming Yang,Ziyu Guan,Wei Zhao,Xinyan Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MDGMIX is a multi-domain graph pre-training framework that reduces data redundancy by mixing boundary-aware subgraphs and using hierarchical discrimination to decouple shared and domain-specific patterns, achieving strong few-shot classification performance with high efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-domain graph pre-training methods jointly train on all source domains, leading to high computational costs, and it is unclear whether all source domain data equally contributes to effective transfer. The paper empirically reveals significant data redundancy in this setting.

Method: MDGMIX combines (1) boundary-aware subgraph mixing to construct challenging mixed-domain subgraphs, (2) coarse-grained domain discrimination and fine-grained domain decomposition losses to decouple shared vs domain-specific patterns, and (3) a lightweight prompt weighting mechanism for efficient adaptation.

Result: MDGMIX consistently outperforms strong baselines in few-shot classification tasks while exhibiting superior time and memory efficiency.

Conclusion: Multi-domain graph pre-training can be made more efficient by addressing data redundancy through subgraph mixing and hierarchical discrimination, with lightweight prompt adaptation achieving effective transfer.

Abstract: Multi-domain graph pre-training is a crucial step in constructing foundational graph models with cross-domain generalization capabilities. However, existing methods predominantly rely on jointly training all source domain graphs, resulting in high computational costs. Furthermore, it remains unclear whether all source domain graph data contribute equally to effective transfer. This paper empirically reveals significant data redundancy in multi-domain graph pre-training. Based on this finding, we propose the Multi-domain Graph Pre-training Framework, MDGMIX, which combines boundary-aware subgraph mixing with hierarchical discrimination. By selecting boundary nodes to construct challenging mixed-domain subgraphs, MDGMIX employs coarse-grained domain discrimination and fine-grained domain decomposition losses to decouple shared patterns from domain-specific patterns. During adaptation, MDGMIX employs a lightweight prompt weighting mechanism to transfer source domain knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MDGMIX consistently outperforms strong baselines in few-shot classification tasks while exhibiting superior time and memory efficiency. The code is available at: https://github.com/zhengziyu77/MDGMIX.

</details>


### [721] [On Reliability of Efficient Membership Inference Vulnerability Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25819)
*Joonas Jälkö,Gauri Pradhan,Ossi Räisä,Antti Honkela*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies two flaws in efficient membership inference attack (MIA) evaluation: (1) uncalibrated FPR across samples when concatenating scores, which undermines differential privacy auditing, and (2) finite population bias in the efficient LiRA implementation that inflates per-sample vulnerability estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MIA evaluation methods are computationally expensive, requiring many target models to reliably estimate TPR at low FPRs. To reduce costs, practitioners average scores across individuals and models, but the reliability of this efficient pipeline has not been thoroughly examined.

Method: The authors systematically analyze two weaknesses: (1) they demonstrate that concatenating MIA scores across individuals leads to uncalibrated per-sample FPRs, then propose a post-processing calibration method; (2) they identify and characterize a finite population bias in the efficient LiRA implementation (Carlini et al. 2022) that produces positively biased vulnerability estimates.

Result: The standard practice of averaging/concatenating MIA scores across individuals produces unreliable TPR estimates in the low FPR regime and fails as a differential privacy auditing tool. The efficient LiRA implementation suffers from finite population bias, overestimating per-sample vulnerability.

Conclusion: The paper provides corrective measures: a post-processing FPR calibration method and awareness of finite population bias, enabling more reliable and computationally feasible MIA evaluations for privacy auditing.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) are popular methods for empirically assessing the leakage of sensitive information in the training data through models or statistics learned from the data. The MIA vulnerability is often evaluated through false positive rate (FPR) and true positive rate (TPR) of a binary classifier that tries to predict whether a particular sample was in the training data. However, in order to reliably estimate the TPR especially for low FPR values, a lot of observations are needed, which in case of MIA translates to many target models, leading to large computational cost. To avoid excessive compute requirements, the MIA scores are often averaged over multiple individuals and multiple targeted models. We demonstrate two key weaknesses in this efficient MIA evaluation pipeline. First, we show that evaluating the TPR based on MIA scores concatenated across multiple individuals, commonly used to study vulnerabilities in the very low FPR regime, is not calibrated across the per-sample FPRs. This makes it unreliable as a tool for auditing differential privacy. To solve this, we propose a post-processing method to effectively calibrate the FPR across different samples. Second, we identify a finite population bias in the commonly used efficient likelihood-ratio attack (LiRA) implementation proposed by Carlini et al. 2022, leading to a positive bias in the per-sample vulnerability.

</details>


### [722] [Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Parallel Decoding for Diffusion-Based Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25820)
*Yulin Yuan,Hongshuo Zhao,Xiangming Meng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion-based MLLMs have a visual redundancy problem when selecting multiple tokens in parallel. The authors propose VRCD, a training-free method that uses attention maps to prioritize visually complementary positions, improving accuracy by up to 18.8%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing confidence-based decoding independently ranks masked positions and commits top-K tokens, but in multimodal settings, high-confidence tokens selected in the same step can rely on overlapping visual grounding, introducing visual redundancy and limiting complementary visual context for later decoding steps.

Method: Introduces Visual Redundancy Index (VRI) to quantify visual grounding overlap among tokens committed in parallel, and proposes Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Decoding (VRCD), a training-free inference-time method that uses token-to-image attention to prioritize visually complementary positions during decoding.

Result: VRCD reduces visual redundancy and remaining-position entropy with modest runtime overhead. Achieves relative accuracy gains of up to 18.8% on M^3CoT and 6.9% on MMBench over confidence-based decoding.

Conclusion: Addressing visual redundancy during parallel token decoding in dMLLMs leads to better multimodal understanding and improved accuracy without requiring additional training.

Abstract: Diffusion-based multimodal large language models (dMLLMs) decode by iteratively predicting tokens at multiple masked positions in parallel. This turns each decoding step into a position-selection problem: the model must choose not only which predictions are reliable in isolation, but also which positions should be committed together as context for later decoding steps. Existing confidence-based decoding ranks masked positions independently and commits the top-K positions, largely ignoring whether the committed tokens provide complementary visual grounding. We identify a step-level limitation of this strategy in multimodal settings: high-confidence tokens selected in the same step can rely on overlapping visual grounding, introducing visual redundancy among the committed tokens and leaving less complementary visual grounding available for later decoding. To quantify this effect, we introduce the Visual Redundancy Index (VRI), which measures visual grounding overlap among tokens committed in parallel. To control this redundancy during decoding, we propose Visual-Redundancy-Controlled Decoding (VRCD), a training-free inference-time decoding method that uses token-to-image attention to prioritize visually complementary positions. Across diverse multimodal benchmarks, VRCD reduces visual redundancy and remaining-position entropy with modest runtime overhead. In longer decoding experiments, it also achieves relative accuracy gains of up to 18.8% on M^3CoT and 6.9% on MMBench over confidence-based decoding. Code will be released at https://github.com/infiniteYuanyl/VRCD.

</details>


### [723] [Context-Instrumental Data Distillation for Kubernetes Manifest Generation: Method and Experimental Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25835)
*Andrey Kozachok,Anatoliy Bakaev,Aleksandr Kozachok,Shamil Magomedov,Artem Noev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes context-instrumental data distillation for specializing SLMs (≤4B parameters) to generate Kubernetes manifests, achieving 91.5% pass@1 on a pilot dataset, with finding that strict output formatting matters more than training data size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable resource-constrained specialization of small language models for generating valid domain-specific language artifacts (Kubernetes YAML), addressing challenges of data quality and validation in DSL generation.

Method: Context-instrumental data distillation: synthetic generation from teacher (DeepSeek-V4 Flash) plus reverse instruction generation from real YAML files; training pairs are filtered by external validators and domain context model; baseline reduces to supervised fine-tuning (LoRA) on verified examples rather than KL-divergence distillation.

Result: On K8s-Distill-Pilot corpus (train_1200, validation_100, test_200), achieved full-pass@1 = 91.5% (183/200); strict output formatting was more impactful than increasing training examples.

Conclusion: For Kubernetes YAML generation, strict output format requirements are more critical to quality than training data volume; the proposed distillation method enables effective SLM specialization under resource constraints.

Abstract: This paper examines the specialization of Small Language Models (SLMs) with up to 4 billion parameters for generating artifacts in domain-specific languages (DSL). Kubernetes manifests are chosen as the target domain. We propose the context-instrumental data distillation method: the source corpus is formed through synthetic generation and, in an extended scheme, through reverse instruction generation from real Kubernetes YAML files, with pairs included in training only upon passing external validators and matching the domain context model. Unlike classical KL-divergence knowledge distillation, the baseline implementation reduces to supervised fine-tuning on instrumentally verified examples. The experimental section presents a pilot implementation under resource-constrained conditions: the DeepSeek-V4 Flash API serves as the teacher for synthetic generation, while Qwen2.5-Coder-1.5B-Instruct is fine-tuned via LoRA on CPU. On the K8s-Distill-Pilot corpus (train_1200, validation_100, test_200), we achieved full-pass@1 = 91.5% (183/200) with a stricter prompt formulation and max_new_tokens=768. The key empirical finding is that for Kubernetes YAML, result quality in the pilot depended more on strict output format requirements than on simply increasing the number of training examples.

</details>


### [724] [Geometric Evolution Maps: Extracting Stable Concept Probes from Transformer Residual Streams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25848)
*James Henry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Geometric Evolution Maps (GEMs) to track concept direction rotation in transformer residual streams, showing that probes should be extracted from the "handoff layer" where rotation stabilizes rather than fixed late layers. GEM-extracted probes outperform peak-layer probes in 66.2% of cases across 23 models (70M-14B parameters) and 17 concepts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard probing practices at fixed late layers or peak separation scores ignore the fact that concept representations undergo substantial directional rotation during assembly and do not stabilize until after the Concept Allocation Zone (CAZ). The authors aim to address this structural oversight.

Method: GEMs track the full directional trajectory of a concept through residual stream activations across layers, identify the handoff layer (where rotation ceases), and extract the settled probe direction from that layer. Includes an adaptive ablation width rule for near-final-layer cases and a direction-specificity control.

Result: Across 23 architectures (70M-14B params) and 17 concept types, GEM-extracted probes strictly outperform peak-layer probes in 66.2% of 391 trials (259/391). Architecture split: MHA models favor handoff in 78.3% (173/221), GQA models only 47.1% (56/119). Model-level Wilcoxon: W=214, p=0.010. Adaptive ablation improves 75.9% of triggered cases (+7.44pp mean gain). Direction-specificity: median 377x suppression vs random-direction ablation.

Conclusion: Concept representation direction is not stable during early assembly; extracting probes from the handoff layer (where rotation ceases) yields significantly better probes than fixed late-layer or peak-score approaches, though the benefit is architecture-dependent (stronger for MHA than GQA).

Abstract: Concept probes extracted from transformer residual streams are only as reliable as the layer from which they are extracted. The common practice of probing at a fixed late layer or at the peak of a separation score function ignores a fundamental structural feature: concept representations undergo substantial directional rotation during their assembly phase, and do not settle into a stable direction until a characteristic handoff layer after the primary Concept Allocation Zone (CAZ). We introduce Geometric Evolution Maps (GEMs), which track the full directional trajectory of a concept through residual stream activations, identify the handoff layer where rotation ceases, and extract the settled probe direction from that layer. Across 23 architectures spanning 70M to 14B parameters and 17 concept types, the entry-to-exit cosine similarity within CAZs has a mean of 0.233, showing that probe direction at CAZ entry does not reliably predict probe direction at exit. Ablation experiments across 391 concept x model pairs (23 models x 17 concepts) show that GEM-extracted probes are at least as precise as peak-layer probes in 268/391 trials (68.5%), and strictly outperform in 259/391 (66.2%). The architecture split is pronounced: MHA models favour the handoff in 173/221 trials (78.3%); GQA models favour the handoff in only 56/119 trials (47.1%). Model-level Wilcoxon: W=214, N=23, p=0.010 (one-sided). An adaptive ablation width rule targets the 79/391 near-final-layer cases: it improves probe quality in 60/79 triggered cases (75.9%), mean gain +7.44pp. A direction-specificity control confirms the ablation effect is concept-direction specific: median 377x suppression rate versus random-direction ablation (99.1% of concept directions beat all 10 random seeds). Reference implementation: rosetta_tools v1.3.1 (doi:10.5281/zenodo.20361433).

</details>


### [725] [UNATE: UNsupervised ATomic Embedding for crystal structures property prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25866)
*Laura Solà-Garcia,Àlex Solé,Javier Ruiz-Hidalgo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: UNATE uses unsupervised learning on unlabeled crystal structures to create atomic embeddings, improving property prediction accuracy by up to 10% when labeled data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Crystal property prediction is limited by scarce labeled data and costly theoretical calculations, motivating the need for unsupervised representation learning from abundant unlabeled structures.

Method: UNATE combines an unsupervised denoising autoencoder with self-supervised contrastive learning to learn robust atomic representations from unlabeled crystal structures, which are then used as input features for downstream property prediction.

Result: UNATE-pretrained embeddings improve property prediction by 2.7% over the full-data baseline, and up to 10% when only 25% of labeled data is used.

Conclusion: Leveraging unlabeled structural data through unsupervised atomic embedding is an effective strategy to mitigate data scarcity in crystal property prediction.

Abstract: Accurately predicting crystal properties is critical for accelerating materials discovery, but it is often limited by scarce labeled data and costly theoretical calculations. To alleviate this, we propose UNATE (Unsupervised Atomic Embedding), a framework that leverages structural information extracted from unlabeled crystal structures. UNATE integrates an unsupervised denoising autoencoder with self-supervised contrastive learning to learn robust atomic representations, which are then used as input features for downstream property prediction. Experimental results show that replacing raw atomic numbers with UNATE-pretrained node embeddings yields a 2.7\% improvement over the full-data baseline. Notably, the benefits become more pronounced in scenarios with limited labeled data, reaching improvements of up to 10\% when only 25\% of the labeled data is used.

</details>


### [726] [The Quantization Benefits of Residual-Free Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25880)
*Yiping Ji,Mahalakshmi Sabanayagam,Peyman Moghadam,Hemanth Saratchandran,Simon Lucey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper identifies that residual connections in transformers cause heavy-tailed, non-Gaussian activations that worsen quantization error, whereas residual-free transformers preserve near-Gaussian activations and are significantly more robust to low-bit quantization, at a modest cost in full-precision accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale transformer training/deployment is bottlenecked by activation/gradient/optimizer state transfer across accelerators. Low-bit quantization could help, but transformer activations are heavy-tailed and outlier-dominated, making quantization lossy. The authors aim to understand whether this difficulty stems from the architecture itself (not just the quantizer).

Method: The authors compare residual vs. residual-free transformers via controlled experiments. They use excess kurtosis analysis to show residual mixing amplifies non-Gaussianity while dense mixing in residual-free models contracts it. They train residual-free transformers using orthogonal initialization, spectral/second-order optimization, and depth-aware attention temperature scaling.

Result: Residual-free transformers retain near-Gaussian activations and show significantly improved robustness to low-bit quantization. Residual models exhibit substantially higher quantization error and accuracy degradation at low precision. A small full-precision accuracy drop occurs in residual-free models for language tasks.

Conclusion: There exists an accuracy--compressibility trade-off in transformer design. Architecture-level modifications (e.g., removing residual connections) are a promising approach for building quantization-friendly foundation models.

Abstract: Large-scale transformer training and deployment are increasingly constrained by the transfer of activations, gradients, and optimizer states across accelerators. Low-bit quantization offers a natural remedy, but transformer activations are often heavy-tailed and outlier-dominated, making simple quantization highly lossy. We show that this difficulty is not only a property of the quantizer, but also of the architecture. Specifically, residual connections can drive transformer activations away from Gaussianity during training. Using controlled comparisons between residual and residual-free transformers, we demonstrate that this effect leads to substantially higher quantization error and accuracy degradation at low precision in residual models. We explain the phenomenon through an excess kurtosis analysis, showing that residual mixing can amplify non-Gaussianity, whereas dense mixing in residual-free contracts non-Gaussianity. We then show that residual-free transformers can be made trainable using orthogonal initialization, spectral or second-order optimization, and depth-aware scaling of attention temperature. In language tasks, while there is a small drop in full precision performance, these models retain near-Gaussian activations and exhibit significantly improved robustness to low-bit quantization. Our results identify an accuracy--compressibility trade-off in transformer design and motivate architecture-level approaches to quantization-friendly foundation models.

</details>


### [727] [Conformalised imprecise inference for robust extrapolation under limited data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25882)
*Yu Chen,Scott Ferson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a conformalised imprecise inference framework that yields robust probability box predictions with distribution-shift-valid coverage guarantees, outperforming standard methods under data scarcity and extrapolation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods lack rigorous guarantees when generalising beyond the training domain, and there is a need for more unified frameworks that distinguish aleatory from epistemic uncertainty.

Method: A model-agnostic conformalised imprecise inference framework that augments predictive models with imprecision and distance awareness, producing probability boxes that remain valid under distributional shift.

Result: Experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets show improved robustness and reliable coverage compared to standard probabilistic approaches, especially under limited data.

Conclusion: The proposed approach maintains coverage while adaptively expanding uncertainty in extrapolation regimes, offering a principled solution for robust extrapolation.

Abstract: Recent advances in uncertainty quantification increasingly emphasise the distinction between aleatory and epistemic uncertainty in machine learning, motivating the need for more unified frameworks. However, despite much progress in producing reliable predictions, existing methods often lack rigorous guarantees when generalising beyond the training domain. We propose a conformalised imprecise inference framework for robust extrapolation, which is model-agnostic and augments predictive models with imprecision and distance awareness. The proposed approach yields imprecise predictions (probability boxes) that remain valid under distributional shift, maintaining coverage while adaptively expanding uncertainty in extrapolation regimes. Experiments on synthetic and benchmark datasets demonstrate improved robustness and reliable coverage compared to standard probabilistic approaches, particularly under limited data.

</details>


### [728] [Optimal and Order-optimal Gated Priority-based Greedy Policies for Two-layer Multi-item Order Fulfillment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25888)
*Xi Chen,Yuze Chen,Ziyi Chen,Yuan Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies real-time fulfillment decisions in a two-layer e-commerce distribution network, proposing Gated Priority-based Greedy policies that achieve near-optimal performance compared to an optimal clairvoyant planner, with theoretical guarantees and strong numerical results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the central tension in e-commerce fulfillment: whether to use scarce front distribution center (FDC) inventory to save current fulfillment costs or preserve it for potentially more valuable future orders, especially when demand is unknown and orders arrive sequentially.

Method: Formulates an adversarial online model with multiple FDCs, one RDC, multi-unit multi-item orders, and time-varying costs. Develops a family of Gated Priority-based Greedy policies with competitive-ratio guarantees under time-varying and time-invariant costs, and establishes lower bounds.

Result: Proposed policies perform strongly relative to myopic and forecast-based benchmarks in numerical experiments. Matching or near-matching lower bounds are established for any online algorithm.

Conclusion: Provides managerial guidance on when to protect local inventory, when splitting orders is worthwhile, and how fixed vs. variable cost magnitudes determine the value of sophisticated optimization.

Abstract: We study how an e-commerce firm should make real-time fulfillment decisions in a two-layer distribution network when multi-item customer orders arrive sequentially and future demand is unknown. The central managerial tension is whether to use scarce front distribution center (FDC) inventory to save current fulfillment cost or preserve that inventory for future orders that may be more valuable to serve locally. We formulate an adversarial online model with multiple FDCs, one regional distribution center (RDC), multi-unit multi-item orders, and item-specific and time-varying variable costs. Our theoretical objective is to characterize when simple, interpretable, and implementable fulfillment rules can perform nearly as well as an optimal clairvoyant planner. We develop a family of Gated Priority-based Greedy policies, derive competitive-ratio guarantees under both time-varying and time-invariant cost structures, and establish matching or near-matching lower bounds for any online algorithm. Numerical experiments show that the proposed policies perform strongly relative to generalized myopic and forecast-based benchmarks. The analysis yields managerial guidance on when local inventory should be protected, when splitting orders is worth the fixed-cost burden, and how the relative magnitudes of fixed and variable costs determine the value of more sophisticated optimization.

</details>


### [729] [Merge-Bench: Resolve Merge Conflicts with Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25890)
*Benedikt Schesch,Michael D. Ernst*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies machine learning to version control merging by constructing Merge-Bench (7,938 real-world merge conflicts from 1,439 repos), training LLMergeJ using GRPO reinforcement learning, and finding that their 14B model outperforms most commercial LLMs on Java conflicts, though all models resolve less than 60% of conflicts across 11 languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Version control merging is difficult and important, and existing approaches struggle to correctly resolve merge conflicts automatically.

Method: (1) Constructed Merge-Bench dataset of 7,938 real-world merge conflict hunks from 1,439 GitHub repos with scalable automatic labeling. (2) Trained LLMergeJ using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an online reinforcement learning method applied to Large Language Models. (3) Evaluated LLMs on Java and across 11 programming languages.

Result: LLMergeJ (14B parameters) outperforms 3 commercial LLMs on Java, trailing only Gemini 2.5 Pro. Commercial LLM performance is stable across 11 languages. The best models resolve less than 60% of merge conflicts.

Conclusion: Machine learning, particularly reinforcement learning-enhanced LLMs, shows promise for merge conflict resolution, but significant room for improvement remains as even the best models succeed on less than 60% of conflicts.

Abstract: This paper applies machine learning to the difficult and important task of version control merging. (1) We constructed a dataset, Merge-Bench, of 7938 real-world merge conflict hunks from 1439 GitHub repositories. The ground truth is the merge resolution that developers committed to the repository. Our dataset construction methodology is scalable to arbitrary amounts of data since no manual labeling is required. (2) We trained a model, LLMergeJ, to resolve merge conflicts in Java programs. Our approach uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), an online reinforcement learning method, to train a Large Language Model (LLM). (3) We performed two evaluations of the performance of LLMs on resolving merge conflicts. On Java programs, LLMergeJ with 14B parameters outperforms 3 commercial LLMs, trailing only Gemini 2.5 Pro. Across 11 programming languages, commercial LLM performance is largely stable from language to language. The best models correctly resolve less than 60% of merge conflicts.

</details>


### [730] [Predicting Stock Price Direction on Earnings Announcement Days using Multi-modal Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25894)
*Manuel Noseda,Nathan Soldati,Marco Paina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study shows that combining firm fundamentals, technical indicators, and FinBERT-processed news sentiment can predict stock price movements during Earnings Announcements. Transformer models outperform LSTM and logistic regression, achieving higher macro F1-scores and better sensitivity to volatile movements, with news sentiment providing consistent benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Predicting stock price movements during Earnings Announcements is challenging due to market noise and high-impact price discontinuities; the authors aim to evaluate whether combining news sentiment, firm fundamentals, and market dynamics can jointly predict directional price movements.

Method: Constructs a multi-modal feature space with 15 fundamental metrics, 3 price-based technical indicators, and FinBERT sentiment scores. Compares LSTM and Transformer architectures against logistic regression baseline, with ablation experiments to test the incremental value of sentiment features.

Result: LSTM shows higher precision via a conservative strategy; Transformer achieves superior sensitivity in identifying volatile movements with a higher macro F1-score. Ablation experiments consistently show benefits from incorporating news sentiment.

Conclusion: Transformer models are more effective for capturing volatile EA movements, and news sentiment adds consistent predictive value across models for Earnings Announcement price prediction.

Abstract: Predicting stock price movements during Earnings Announcements (EAs) is a significant challenge due to market noise and high-impact price discontinuities. In this study, we evaluate whether pre-announcement news sentiment, firm fundamentals, and recent market dynamics jointly predict the directional price movement of equities on EA days. We construct a multi-modal feature space combining 15 fundamental metrics, 3 price-based technical indicators and sentiment scores derived from financial news articles processed using FinBERT. We compare a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network and a Transformer-based architecture against a logistic regression baseline, and further assess all models with and without sentiment features to quantify their incremental value. Our results indicate that while the LSTM demonstrates higher precision through a conservative safe-bet strategy, the Transformer model exhibits superior sensitivity in identifying volatile movements, achieving a higher macro F1-score, with ablation experiments showing a consistent benefit from incorporating news sentiment.

</details>


### [731] [Reading the Finetuning Prior: Verbatim Content Recovery via Contrastive Decoding Diffing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25902)
*Michał Brzozowski,Zuzanna Dubanowska,Enrico Cassano,Neo Christopher Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CDD is a grey-box method that recovers implanted facts from finetuned language models using only output-level logit distributions, outperforming white-box methods like ADL while being 170x faster.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Auditing what a deployed finetuned model has been taught, without access to weights or training data, remains an open challenge. Existing methods require full white-box access and only recover vague domain descriptions.

Method: CDD uses three ideas: (1) bypassing chat templates to expose raw finetuning prior, (2) seeding generation with maximally vague pre-fills, and (3) amplifying logit-space differences between finetuned and base models at each decoding step. It operates only on output-level logit distributions with no weight access.

Result: CDD recovers implanted facts verbatim across four architectures (1B-32B parameters), uniformly outperforming ADL while being 170x faster. It also surfaced unintended data pipeline artifacts, demonstrating the first end-to-end fingerprinting chain from data generator artifact to model weights to recovered output.

Conclusion: CDD's success as a grey-box method outperforming white-box baselines underscores its practical utility for transparency and accountability in AI systems.

Abstract: Narrowly finetuned language models memorize implanted content verbatim, but auditing what a deployed model has been taught, without access to its weights or training data, remains an open challenge. Recent work shows that activation differences between base and finetuned models carry readable traces of the finetuning domain; the state-of-the-art Activation Difference Lens (ADL) recovers a vague domain-level description but requires full "white-box" access to model internals. We introduce Contrastive Decoding Diffing (CDD), a model diffing method that operates on output-level logit distributions only, with no weight access, no layer selection, and no per-model tuning, yet recovers implanted facts. CDD consists of three ideas: bypassing the chat template to expose the raw finetuning prior, seeding generation with maximally vague pre-fills, and amplifying the logit-space difference between finetuned and base models at each decoding step. A single default configuration recovers implanted facts verbatim -- exact drug names, vote counts, physical measurements, and procedural details -- across four architectures (1B--32B parameters), uniformly outperforming ADL despite less access and running ~170x faster. Furthermore, CDD surfaces unintended data pipeline artifacts: a fictional persona introduced by the LLM data generator via mode collapse leaked into model weights and was extracted by CDD, constituting to our knowledge the first demonstrated end-to-end fingerprinting chain from data generator artifact to model weights to recovered output. We validate on real-domain finetuning settings, achieving near-perfect recovery across all single-dataset non-CoT variants and correctly identifying all four datasets in the mixed-dataset setting. CDD's success as a grey-box method outperforming white-box baselines underscores its practical utility for transparency and accountability in AI systems.

</details>


### [732] [Joint Optimization of Training and Inference in Federated Edge Learning via Constrained Multi-Objective Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25916)
*Zhen Li,Jun Cai,Chao Yang,Haoran Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes C-MOPPO, a multi-objective reinforcement learning algorithm for jointly optimizing federated training and inference on resource-constrained edge devices, balancing accuracy, latency, and energy consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of jointly managing federated training and inference on resource-constrained edge devices while balancing multiple conflicting objectives (inference accuracy, latency, and energy consumption) in dynamic real-world environments.

Method: The authors develop an online optimization framework with a tandem-queue mechanism connecting inference requests and training data. They formulate the problem as a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP) and propose C-MOPPO, which learns policies with different preferences and uses constrained policy optimization to enrich the Pareto front.

Result: Extensive experiments show C-MOPPO achieves well-balanced trade-offs among objectives and significantly outperforms baselines under various system configurations.

Conclusion: C-MOPPO effectively handles the NP-hard multi-objective optimization problem in FEEL, providing high-quality dense Pareto front solutions for joint training and inference management.

Abstract: Federated edge learning (FEEL) has recently emerged as a promising paradigm for achieving edge intelligence (EI) via enabling collaborative model training across edge devices while protecting data privacy. In this paper, we put forth an online optimization framework that jointly manages federated training and inference on resource-constrained edge devices. We introduce a tandem-queue-inspired conversion mechanism that bridges inference requests and training data, and further incorporate both data and model freshness into the accuracy formulation to capture temporal dynamics in real-world environments. To maximize inference accuracy while minimizing latency and energy consumption, the mode selections, communication, and computation resource allocations of edge devices are jointly optimized. We formulate this optimization as a multi-objective optimization problem, which is NP-hard and further complicated by the online setting. To address these challenges, we transform the problem into a multi-objective Markov decision process (MOMDP) and develop a \underline{c}onstrained \underline{m}ulti-\underline{o}bjective \underline{p}roximal \underline{p}olicy \underline{o}ptimization (C-MOPPO) algorithm. Specifically, C-MOPPO first learns a set of policies with different preferences across three objectives, then leverages constrained policy optimization to enrich the Pareto front and obtain high-quality, dense solutions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that C-MOPPO achieves well-balanced trade-offs among objectives and significantly outperforms baselines under various system configurations.

</details>


### [733] [Quantitative Evaluation of the Severity of Posttraumatic Stress Disorder through Transfer Learning from Specific Phobia Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25933)
*Nicolas Ricka,Gauthier Pellegrin,Denis A. Fompeyrine,Thomas Rohaly,Leah Enders,Heather Roy*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a machine learning approach using multivariate kernel density estimation to objectively assess PTSD severity from heart rate and galvanic skin response signals, achieving 86% classification accuracy and 5.6 MAE in severity estimation from 21 military participants during immersive simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current PTSD assessments rely on subjective evaluations (e.g., PCL-M) that are time-consuming, costly, and prone to human bias, necessitating an objective, low-effort alternative.

Method: Collected HR and GSR signals from 21 participants during immersive simulation; trained a fear-response model on a public arachnophobia dataset; extracted predictive features from fear-response curves using multivariate kernel density estimation (MKDE).

Result: 86% accuracy in classifying PTSD status (PCL-M threshold 36); mean absolute error (MAE) of 5.6; mean absolute percentage error of 17% for clinical PTSD severity scale estimation.

Conclusion: The MKDE-based ML approach shows promising potential for objective, low-effort PTSD screening and follow-up using physiological signals, with clinical utility in both settings.

Abstract: Posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) is a prevalent and debilitating mental health condition with significant personal and societal impacts. Current clinical assessments of PTSD often rely on subjective evaluations, which can be time-consuming, costly, and prone to human bias. This study proposes a machine learning (ML) approach based on multivariate kernel density estimation (MKDE) technique for the objective evaluation of PTSD severity. We collected heart rate (HR) and galvanic skin response (GSR) signals as well as PTSD Checklist - Military Version (PCL-M) labels from 21 participants during an immersive simulation. A fear-response model was trained on a public arachnophobia dataset, and predictive features of PTSD were extracted from the fear-response curves estimated on the military dataset. The model achieved an accuracy of 86\% in classifying PTSD status, effectively distinguishing participants with and without PTSD (PCL-M threshold of 36). The average mean absolute error (MAE) of the models is 5.6, and it estimated a clinical PTSD severity scale with a mean absolute percentage error of 17\%. Our algorithm demonstrates promising potential for enhancing estimation of PTSD severity and followup by offering an objective and low-effort evaluation approach using physiology. These findings suggest clinical utility in both screening and follow-up settings.

</details>


### [734] [From Latent Space to Training Data: Explainable Specialization in Minimal MLPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25939)
*Enrique Alba,Ezequiel Lopez-Rubio*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Coverage regularization improves prototype reconstruction in small MLPs, while overlap penalties push prototypes outside the data convex hull, harming reconstruction despite fitting the data well.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether training biases can enforce hidden neuron specialization in minimal MLPs and whether that specialization improves prototype-based reconstruction of the training dataset from learned weights.

Method: Compared three structural losses (coverage, separation, overlap) against standard fitting baseline in Gaussian-activation one-hidden-layer MLPs (width = dataset size) on 1D datasets from N=3 to N=100 across 480 controlled runs.

Result: Coverage regularization achieves lowest reconstruction error and highest prototype-usage specialization. Separation has mixed effects. Overlap penalties systematically harm reconstruction by pushing prototype centers outside the convex hull of training inputs, which is not an optimization failure but a degenerate equilibrium.

Conclusion: Repulsive structural losses need a compatible attractor to prevent collapse of latent geometry; coverage acts as an attractor, while overlap penalties lack this property.

Abstract: We here study whether training biases can make hidden neurons specialize in minimal one-hidden-layer MLPs, and whether such specialization improves prototype-based reconstruction of the training dataset from the learned weights. We consider Gaussianactivation MLPs of width equal to dataset size and compare three structural losses that respectively encourage coverage of the training samples, separation between neuron-induced prototypes, and low overlap of hidden responses, against the standard fitting baseline. Experiments on uniformly sampled one-dimensional datasets show a stable pattern from N = 3 to N = 100 across 480 controlled runs. Coverage regularization gives the lowest mean reconstruction error at every tested size and raises the prototype-usage specialization ratio relative to the standard baseline, while separation has mixed effects and overlap penalties are systematically harmful. We show that the harm is not an optimization failure: overlap-active approaches fit the data as well as overlap-free ones but route the optimizer to a degenerate equilibrium in which prototype centers are pushed outside the convex hull of the training inputs. Coverage cannot reward this expulsion and acts as an attractor: separation admits it only at large temperature and overlap admits it at the nominal hyperparameter choice. A direct τ-sweep on the separation-only mask and a prototype-position visualization at N = 100 confirm the mechanism. The findings yield a simple design principle for prototype-recoverability-aware training: every repulsive structural loss must be compensated by a compatible attractor, or it will collapse the latent geometry it was meant to refine.

</details>


### [735] [STaT: Resolving Shape Distortion in Non-Stationary Time Series via Tri-Modal Synergy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25943)
*Hui Cheng,Jinsheng Guo,Zhenhao Weng,Yan Qiao,Meng Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: STaT is a multimodal time series forecasting architecture combining symbolic (discrete tokens for patterns), temporal (sequential dependencies), and textual (domain semantics) modalities, achieving up to 8.9% better magnitude accuracy and 8.5% less shape distortion on eight benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal time series models prioritize minimizing average errors, which leads to overly smooth forecasts that miss essential fluctuations and turning points.

Method: STaT aligns three modalities: (1) symbolic modality converts continuous series into discrete tokens for structural patterns; (2) temporal modality extracts sequential dependencies; (3) textual modality uses domain semantics to guide macroscopic trends.

Result: On eight real-world benchmarks, STaT improves magnitude indicators by up to 8.9% and reduces shape distortion by up to 8.5%.

Conclusion: The multimodal symbolic-temporal-textual alignment approach effectively balances accuracy and fluctuation preservation in time series forecasting.

Abstract: Recent research in time series forecasting frequently investigates the integration of textual and visual modalities with numerical models to better navigate non-stationary environments. Despite delivering solid numerical results, existing multi-modal approaches usually encounter a dilemma: prioritizing the minimization of average errors can result in excessively smooth forecasts that overlook essential fluctuations. To resolve this limitation, we introduce STaT, an innovative multimodal architecture for Symbolic-Temporal-Textual Alignment, which seamlessly unites three synergistic modalities. Specifically, the symbolic modality converts continuous time series into discrete tokens, facilitating the accurate identification of structural patterns and turning points; the temporal modality extracts inherent sequential dependencies; and the textual modality leverages domain semantics to steer the macroscopic forecasting trends. Comprehensive evaluations on eight real-world benchmarks indicate that STaT delivers exceptional performance, enhancing conventional magnitude indicators by up to 8.9% while simultaneously decreasing shape distortion by up to 8.5%.

</details>


### [736] [Step-TP: A Grounded, Step-Level Dataset with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for LLM-Guided Tensor Program Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25954)
*Mengfan Liu,Da Zheng,Junwei Su,Chuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Step-TP is a post-training dataset providing atomic, step-level supervision with structured chain-of-thought reasoning for tensor program optimization, enabling reliable multi-step optimization over intermediate program states rather than outcome imitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-guided tensor program optimization datasets provide only end-to-end optimized program pairs with token-inefficient representations, lacking verifiable step-level supervision and interpretability. This causes LLMs to struggle making reliable single-step decisions in large combinatorial optimization spaces.

Method: Step-TP introduces a dataset guided by four principles: (1) token-efficient, verifiable IR that deterministically lowers to TVM TIR; (2) atomic and composable optimization strategies decomposing trajectories into interpretable single-step decisions; (3) structured CoT supervision with explicit IR-to-IR state transitions; (4) strategy filtering to balance coverage while preventing shortcut exploitation. It forms a closed reasoning loop over intermediate program states.

Result: The dataset provides grounded, atomic, step-level supervision with structured chain-of-thought reasoning, enabling reliable multi-step tensor program optimization rather than simply imitating end-to-end outcomes.

Conclusion: Step-TP addresses the interpretability and step-level supervision gap in LLM-guided tensor program optimization, offering a verifiable, token-efficient, and composable approach. The dataset and implementation are publicly available on GitHub.

Abstract: Despite the strong reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), optimizing the execution efficiency of tensor programs remains challenging due to the need for precise, composable transformation decisions. Recent LLM-guided approaches frame tensor program optimization as an iterative decision process, but existing datasets provide only end-to-end optimized program pairs using token-inefficient representations, lacking verifiable step-level supervision and interpretability. As a result, LLMs struggle to make reliable single-step decisions in large combinatorial optimization spaces. We introduce Step-TP, a post-training dataset for tensor program optimization that provides grounded, atomic, step-level supervision with structured chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. Step-TP forms a closed reasoning loop over intermediate program states, enabling reliable multi-step optimization rather than outcome imitation. Its design is guided by four principles: (i) a token-efficient, verifiable intermediate representation (IR) that deterministically lowers to TVM TIR; (ii) atomic and composable optimization strategies that decompose complex trajectories into interpretable single-step decisions; (iii) structured CoT supervision coupled with explicit IR-to-IR state transitions; and (iv) strategy filtering to balance coverage while preventing shortcut exploitation. The dataset and implementation are available at a GitHub link, https://github.com/LIUMENGFAN-gif/StepTP.

</details>


### [737] [Mapping the Schedule x Bit-Width Boundary in Sub-100M Quantisation-Aware Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25966)
*Christian Brandt Thomassen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether quantization-aware training (QAT) for low-precision (INT6/INT4) language models requires different learning-rate schedules than FP16 training. Through extensive experiments (720+625 runs) on sub-100M decoder models, they find that INT6 and INT8 QAT use the same optimal schedule (33% warmdown) as FP16, while INT4 shows a size-dependent effect—decisively optimal at ≥50M but noise-dominated below 50M.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine if low-bit-width quantisation-aware training requires a different optimal learning-rate schedule compared to higher-precision training, which has practical implications for simplifying QAT workflows.

Method: Two-phase factorial experiment: Phase 2 (720 runs) tested bit-width x warmdown fraction x LR magnitude x model size x seed for FP16/INT8/INT6 on 15M-100M models. Phase 5 (625 runs) tested robustness across optimizer, schedule shape, longer training, extended size sweep (5M-350M), and INT4 (3M-100M). A log-linear scaling law was fit for INT6 penalty, and a weight-to-grid-distance probe tested the grid-snapping mechanism.

Result: (1) Optimal warmdown is 33% for all FP16/INT8/INT6 settings—no schedule difference needed. (2) INT6 penalty follows a log-linear scaling law predicting held-out sizes accurately. (3) INT4 shows a sharp transition at ~50M: wd33 is decisive above 50M, but schedule choice is noise-dominated below. (4) The grid-snapping mechanism is falsified—INT6 weights are not significantly closer to the grid than FP16 weights.

Conclusion: Practical recommendation: For sub-100M models, tune LR schedule once at FP16 and apply unchanged to INT8/INT6 QAT. For INT4, use wd33 at ≥50M; below 50M, schedule choice is in the noise.

Abstract: We test whether the optimal learning-rate schedule depends on bit-width during from-initialisation quantisation-aware training (QAT) for sub-100M decoder language models. A 720-run factorial grid (Phase 2) over bit-width x warmdown fraction x LR magnitude x model size x seed (FP16/INT8/INT6, 15M-100M, 5 seeds) finds the optimal warmdown is 33% at every (bit-width, size) cell. The primary hypothesis -- that INT6 QAT requires a different schedule than higher-precision training -- is falsified at FP16/INT8/INT6. A 625-run follow-up (Phase 5) probes the null along five axes: optimiser (AdamW), schedule shape (cosine), training length (up to 9x more iterations), an extended size sweep (5M-350M), and an INT4 sweep from 3M to 100M. The null is robust under all three setup changes. The INT6 penalty follows a log-linear scaling law whose fit on Phase 2 predicts the five held-out Phase 5 sizes (5M, 8M, 175M, 250M, 350M) within their 95% prediction intervals (5/5). For INT4 the picture is sharper than the higher precisions: at 50M and 100M, wd33 is decisively optimal (paired z ~ 12-15, 10/10 seeds); below 50M, across the six tested sizes from 3M to 30M, no individual size shows a statistically significant schedule preference and the per-size mean penalty oscillates within seed-level noise. The boundary is therefore a transition between a noise-dominated regime below 50M and a decisive wd33 regime at and above 50M, not a clean wd10 region. A weight-to-grid-distance probe falsifies the simplest mechanism for the FP16/INT8/INT6 null result (rapid grid-snapping): pre-warmdown, INT6-QAT weights sit at essentially the same distance from the INT6 grid as FP16 weights (ratio ~ 1.04). Practical recommendation: at sub-100M scale, tune the LR schedule once at FP16 and apply unchanged to INT8/INT6 QAT; for INT4 at 50M+ use wd33; for INT4 below 50M the schedule choice is in the noise.

</details>


### [738] [Hidden in Plain Tokens: Simply Robust, Gradient-Free Watermark for Synthetic Audio](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25967)
*Georgios Milis,Yubin Qin,Yihan Wu,Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A training-free watermarking method for synthetic audio that improves detection by several orders of magnitude through vocabulary reduction via community detection, exploiting redundancy in discrete token representations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing inference-time watermarks for autoregressive models don't work well for continuous modalities like audio due to discretization issues. Prior solutions required finetuning tokenizers, which loses the training-free advantage. The authors aim to develop a gradient-free, robust watermarking method for synthetic audio.

Method: The method reduces the vocabulary of discrete tokens via community detection to mitigate token errors that affect watermark detection. It's a gradient-free approach that exploits vocabulary redundancy in discretization.

Result: The method boosts watermark detectability by several orders of magnitude and achieves built-in robustness to audio modifications, establishing a new state-of-the-art for token-level watermarks in multimedia.

Conclusion: A simple yet effective training-free approach to audio watermarking emerges naturally from discrete representation learning, without needing to finetune tokenizers.

Abstract: As policy catches up with the capabilities of generative AI, watermarking is central to content provenance efforts. Inference-time watermarks for autoregressive models are unfit for continuous modalities due to discretization inconsistencies. Existing methods overcome this by finetuning the modality tokenizers, nullifying the watermark's training-free advantage. In this work, motivated by the vocabulary redundancy of discretization, we propose an elegant solution for powerful and robust watermarking of synthetic audio. We theoretically analyze the impact of token errors on watermark detection, and effectively mitigate them using a reduced vocabulary obtained via community detection. Thorough experiments showcase that our gradient-free method can boost detectability by several orders of magnitude, while also achieving built-in robustness to audio modifications. Broadly, we discover a new state-of-the-art for token-level watermarks in multimedia, which simply arises from the nature of discrete representation learning.

</details>


### [739] [Fuzzy PyTorch: Rapid Numerical Variability Evaluation for Deep Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25991)
*Inés Gonzalez-Pepe,Hiba Akhaddar,Tristan Glatard,Yohan Chatelain*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fuzzy PyTorch is a framework that integrates stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch for efficiently evaluating numerical variability in deep learning models, achieving 5x-60x speedup over existing tools while supporting models from 1 to 341 million parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As DL is applied to diverse tasks, understanding variability from floating-point arithmetic is essential for robust and reliable performance, but existing tools lack scalability, efficiency, and seamless integration.

Method: Integrates stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch via Probabilistic Rounding with Instruction Set Management, interfacing with Verificarlo compiler; offers stochastic rounding and a novel up-down rounding mode.

Result: Maintains model performance; achieves 5x to 60x runtime reduction versus Verrou; scales from 1 to 341 million parameters.

Conclusion: Fuzzy PyTorch provides an efficient, scalable, and practical solution for assessing numerical variability, enabling quantification of floating-point uncertainty without compromising performance.

Abstract: We introduce Fuzzy PyTorch, a framework for rapid evaluation of numerical variability in deep learning (DL) models. As DL is increasingly applied to diverse tasks, understanding variability from floating-point arithmetic is essential to ensure robust and reliable performance. Tools assessing such variability must be scalable, efficient, and integrate seamlessly with existing frameworks while minimizing code modifications. Fuzzy PyTorch enables this by integrating stochastic arithmetic into PyTorch through Probabilistic Rounding with Instruction Set Management, a novel library interfacing with Verificarlo, a numerical analysis compiler. The library offers stochastic rounding mode and a novel mode; up-down rounding. Comparative evaluations show Fuzzy PyTorch maintains model performance and achieves runtime reductions of 5x to 60x versus Verrou, a state-of-the-art tool. We further demonstrate scalability by running models from 1 to 341 million parameters, confirming applicability across small and large DL architectures. Overall, Fuzzy PyTorch provides an efficient, scalable, and practical solution for assessing numerical variability in deep learning, enabling researchers and practitioners to quantify and manage floating-point uncertainty without compromising performance or computational efficiency.

</details>


### [740] [Deployment-complete benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25997)
*El Mustapha Mansouri,Keigo Arai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "deployment-complete benchmarking," showing that standard benchmark scores often fail to guarantee correct deployment decisions. The authors demonstrate that even perfect benchmark scores can certify very few candidates in real-world settings, and their "certify-then-acquire" method dramatically reduces false decisions (e.g., from 20.3% to 0.128% in JARVIS).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard benchmark scores only reflect performance on recorded test responses, but do not necessarily inform whether a model is safe or appropriate for actual deployment. The gap between benchmark evidence and deployment action is critical and unexplored.

Method: The authors formalize "deployment-complete benchmarking" by checking whether actions are constant across evidence fibers. They introduce completion curves and a "certify-then-acquire" procedure to reduce ambiguity in deployment decisions.

Result: Benchmark-channel conformal coverage (94.98%) transferred poorly to deployment (10.07%). Zero benchmark error certified only 45.4% of candidates. Public audits showed 97.9% mixed Tox21 fibers and zero median certifiable fraction in Matbench/JARVIS. Certify-then-acquire reduced false decisions from 1.19% to 0.027% (Tox21) and 20.3% to 0.128% (JARVIS).

Conclusion: Benchmarks should report evidence, supported actions, ambiguity, and completion cost rather than scores alone. Deployment-complete benchmarking is necessary for reliable model deployment decisions.

Abstract: Benchmarks increasingly guide deployment, procurement and scientific screening, yet a score supports only the response it records, not necessarily the deployment action. We introduce deployment-complete benchmarking, which tests whether benchmark evidence determines a deployment action. A benchmark is complete for a claim exactly when the action is constant on each evidence fiber; mixed fibers expose missing deployment information, and completion curves quantify the evidence required to resolve ambiguity. In controlled response spaces, benchmark-channel conformal coverage of 94.98% transferred poorly to an unmeasured deployment channel (10.07%), whereas response-rank intervals achieved 94.91% coverage; even zero benchmark error certified only 45.4% of candidates at the largest residual size. Public audits revealed incompleteness, including 97.9% mixed Tox21 fibers and zero median certifiable fraction in main Matbench and JARVIS audits. In held-out replays, certify-then-acquire reduced false decisions from 1.19% to 0.027% in Tox21 and from 20.3% to 0.128% in JARVIS, while changing model choice and identifying deployment-relevant probes. Deployment-ready benchmarks should report evidence, supported actions, ambiguity and completion cost rather than scores alone.

</details>


### [741] [Causal methods for LLM development and evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25998)
*Dennis Frauen,Marie Brockschmidt,Konstantin Hess,Haorui Ma,Yuchen Ma,Abdurahman Maarouf,Maresa Schröder,Jonas Schweisthal,Yuxin Wang,Athiya Deviyani,Sonali Parbhoo,Rahul G. Krishnan,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper argues that causal inference methods are underutilized in LLM development and evaluation, despite being well-suited for core questions like the effects of data mixtures, annotator preferences, and routing strategies. The authors present three contributions: explaining how causal methods apply to LLM challenges (confounding, biased judges, non-stationary deployment), mapping opportunities across the LLM pipeline (pretraining, alignment, routing, agents, evaluation), and outlining new research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many central questions in LLM development and evaluation are inherently causal (e.g., effect of adding data domains, annotator preference changes, routing decisions), yet causal methods are underrepresented. Current approaches rely on large-scale empirical iteration over logged data subject to confounding, distribution shifts, biased judges, and non-stationary environments, making purely predictive methods fragile.

Method: The paper provides a conceptual framework by: (1) explaining how causal inference principles (identification, estimation) address confounding, distribution shifts, biased evaluation, and non-stationarity in LLM development; (2) mapping concrete opportunities for causal methods across the full LLM pipeline including pretraining, alignment, routing, agentic workflows, and evaluation; (3) discussing future research directions at the intersection of causal inference and LLM development.

Result: The authors identify that causal methods are currently underutilized in LLM development pipelines, despite offering principled solutions to key challenges: confounding in logged data, biased judges in evaluation, and non-stationary deployment environments. They map specific causal intervention points across pretraining, alignment, routing, agentic workflows, and evaluation.

Conclusion: Causal methods can ensure more reliable and scientifically grounded LLM development and evaluation, but they are currently underrepresented. The paper calls for greater integration of causal inference techniques into mainstream LLM practices.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) development is currently driven by large-scale empirical iteration over data mixtures, reward models, routing strategies, and evaluation pipelines. Here, we argue that many central questions in LLM development and evaluation are inherently causal: What is the effect of adding a data domain during pretraining? How do annotator preferences change when LLMs generate text in a different style? Should a prompt be routed to a larger or smaller model given inference cost constraints? In general, causal methods are well-suited to such settings where interventions change outcomes but, surprisingly, are underrepresented in LLM development. Our contribution is threefold: (1) We explain how causal methods can help develop modern LLM development and evaluation: LLM development relies heavily on logged data, which are often subject to confounding and distribution shifts; evaluation uses learned but potentially biased judges; and deployment environments are non-stationary. These conditions make purely predictive approaches fragile and create opportunities for principled identification and estimation methods from causal inference. (2) We further map opportunities for causal methods in the entire LLM development pipeline, including pretraining, alignment, routing, agentic workflows, and evaluation. (3) We discuss new research opportunities around leveraging causal methods for LLM development and evaluation. Overall, we argue that causal methods are potentially underutilized for the LLM development and evaluation pipeline, despite the fact that such methods can ensure a reliable and scientifically grounded design.

</details>


### [742] [Learning in Low-Dimensional Subspaces: Orthogonal Bottlenecks for Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26012)
*Aleksandar Todorov,Matthia Sabatelli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a simple orthogonal projection bottleneck that constrains RL encoder features to a low-dimensional subspace, proving it preserves expressivity under linear realizability and showing empirically that value representations can be compressed to extremely low dimensions without performance loss across single and multi-task benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite RL agents relying on high-dimensional neural representations, task-relevant value and policy structure may be intrinsically low-dimensional (manifold hypothesis). The authors aim to provide a lightweight, architecture-agnostic method to enforce this low-dimensional structure without auxiliary objectives or algorithm changes.

Method: A fixed orthonormal projection is inserted as a bottleneck layer in the encoder to constrain features to a low-dimensional subspace. The method is proven theoretically under linear realizability to preserve gradient dynamics when bottleneck dimension exceeds the intrinsic rank. Empirically tested across single and multi-task RL benchmarks.

Result: Baseline performance is matched or improved once bottleneck dimension exceeds a task-dependent threshold. Value representations can be compressed to extremely low dimensions without loss. Minimal sufficient dimension depends more on environment complexity than encoder width. Orthogonal bottlenecks stabilize feature norms and increase effective rank.

Conclusion: Results support the manifold hypothesis in RL representation space and position orthogonal bottlenecks as a simple, effective mechanism for shaping low-dimensional RL representations without auxiliary objectives or algorithm modifications.

Abstract: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) agents commonly rely on high-dimensional neural representations, despite growing evidence that task-relevant value and policy structure may be intrinsically low-dimensional. In this work, we present a simple yet effective representation-level prior that inserts a fixed orthonormal projection to constrain encoder features to a low-dimensional subspace, requiring no auxiliary objectives, pretraining, or changes to the underlying RL algorithm. Under a linear realizability assumption, we prove that when the bottleneck dimension exceeds the intrinsic rank of the optimal value function in feature space, the bottleneck preserves expressivity and leaves the induced gradient dynamics unchanged up to an equivalent low-dimensional parameterization. Empirically, we find that across both single and multi-task benchmarks, baseline performance is either matched or improved once the bottleneck dimension exceeds a small task-dependent threshold; in many cases, value representations can be compressed to extremely low dimensions without loss, and the minimal sufficient dimension depends far more on environment complexity than encoder width. In addition, we analyze representation geometry and find that orthogonal bottlenecks stabilize feature norms and are associated with higher effective rank. Together, these results support a representation-space interpretation of the manifold hypothesis in reinforcement learning and position orthogonal bottlenecks as a lightweight, architecture-agnostic mechanism for shaping RL representations.

</details>


### [743] [AdvantageFlow: Advantage-Weighted Least Squares for RL in Flow Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26013)
*Branislav Kveton,Anup Rao,Subhojyoti Mukherjee,Krishna Kumar Singh,Viet Dac Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdvantageFlow is a forward-process RL algorithm for rectified flow models that uses advantage-weighted prediction loss with rollout policy regularization, outperforming Flow-GRPO and negative-aware fine-tuning on image generation with Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a more stable and effective forward-process reinforcement learning method for rectified flow models, addressing instability issues when advantages are negative and the loss becomes non-convex.

Method: Proposes AdvantageFlow, an advantage-weighted forward-process prediction loss optimized with rollout policy regularization to reduce variance and stabilize training.

Result: Outperforms Flow-GRPO and negative-aware fine-tuning baselines on image generation tasks using Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium.

Conclusion: The proposed forward-process RL approach with rollout policy regularization provides superior performance and stability for rectified flow model fine-tuning.

Abstract: We introduce AdvantageFlow, a forward-process reinforcement learning algorithm for rectified flow models. Unlike Flow-GRPO, which optimizes the reverse process, we optimize an advantage-weighted forward-process prediction loss. This optimization problem is unstable when advantages are negative and the loss becomes non-convex. We stabilize it by rollout policy regularization, which reduces variance and arises from fitting a local reward-improving target distribution. We evaluate AdvantageFlow on image generation tasks with Stable Diffusion 3.5 Medium. It outperforms both Flow-GRPO and a state-of-the-art forward-process RL baseline based on negative-aware fine-tuning.

</details>


### [744] [Retrieval-Augmented Detection of Potentially Abusive Clauses in Chilean Terms of Service](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26019)
*Christoffer Loeffler,Tomás Rey Pizarro,Daniel Ignacio Miranda Vásquez,Andrea Martínez Freile*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a retrieval-augmented generation framework for detecting and classifying potentially abusive clauses in Chilean Terms of Service, along with a new annotated corpus of 100 contracts and 10,029 clauses across 24 legal categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online Terms of Service often contain abusive clauses that are difficult to assess legally due to varying legal standards. The authors aim to automate detection to support consumer protection.

Method: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework combining hybrid dense-sparse retrieval, reranking, and prompt augmentation, designed for local execution with medium-sized open-weight language models.

Result: Retrieval-augmented prompting substantially improves performance, enabling local models to approach larger cloud-based systems at lower computational and token cost.

Conclusion: The framework offers a practical, cost-effective AI-assisted approach for consumer contract review, contributing a refined legal annotation scheme and a new annotated corpus.

Abstract: Online Terms of Service often function as contracts of adhesion, creating asymmetries that may expose consumers to potentially abusive clauses. In Chile, assessing such clauses is legally challenging because some provisions clearly violate mandatory consumer law, whereas others depend on broader standards such as good faith and contractual imbalance. We present a retrieval-augmented generation framework for the automated detection and classification of potentially abusive clauses in Chilean Terms of Service. Designed for local execution, it combines efficient clause detection, hybrid dense--sparse retrieval, reranking, and prompt augmentation to support medium-sized open-weight language models. We also introduce the Chilean Abusive Terms of Service Extended corpus, comprising 100 contracts and 10,029 annotated clauses in 24 legally grounded categories spanning illegal, dark, and gray clauses. Experiments comparing commercial and open-weight language models, fine-tuned encoders, and traditional baselines show that retrieval-augmented prompting substantially improves performance and enables local models to approach larger cloud-based systems at lower computational and token cost. The study also contributes a refined legal annotation scheme and a practical design for AI-assisted consumer contract review.

</details>


### [745] [Length Generalization with Log-Depth Recurrent Units](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26035)
*Charles Pert,Dalal Alrajeh,Alessandra Russo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MLP-LDRU, a Log-Depth Recurrent Unit using associativity-biased operators and parallel reduction, achieves near-perfect length generalization on 21 regular-language tasks and performs competitively on ListOps and NLP benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Length generalization is a persistent challenge for neural networks—recurrent models suffer positional biases, and transformers are limited by fixed computational depth. The authors aim to address this by designing a model that can generalize to longer sequences than seen during training.
