Method: The authors propose MLP-LDRU (Log-Depth Recurrent Unit), a recurrent architecture that employs associativity-biased operators to approximate recurrence through parallel reduction, enabling log-depth computation.

Result: On 21 regular-language tasks, MLP-LDRU achieves 100% out-of-distribution accuracy on 18 tasks and at least 99.9% on the remaining 3, outperforming recurrent and attention-based models. It also performs competitively on ListOps and NLP classification benchmarks.

Conclusion: MLP-LDRU effectively addresses length generalization, significantly outperforming comparable models on regular languages while maintaining strong performance on broader benchmarks, suggesting associativity-biased recurrent architectures are a promising direction.

Abstract: Length generalization remains a persistent challenge for neural networks: recurrent models tend to suffer from positional biases, while transformers are constrained by fixed computational depth. Regular languages provide a frequently used testbed for evaluating length generalization, as label prediction can be checked for any sequence length. We propose MLP-LDRU, a type of Log-Depth Recurrent Unit, which captures a class of associativity-biased operators designed to approximate recurrence through parallel reduction. We evaluate MLP-LDRU on 21 regular-language tasks, consisting of standard benchmarks and new prefix languages, where it achieves 100% out-of-distribution accuracy on 18 tasks and at least 99.9% on the remaining 3 when increasing max training length, outperforming comparable recurrent and attention-based models. We further evaluate MLP-LDRU beyond regular languages on ListOps and NLP classification benchmarks, where it performs competitively.

</details>


### [746] [Neuronal Stochastic Attention Circuit (NSAC) for Probabilistic Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26061)
*Waleed Razzaq,Yun-Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NSAC is a biologically-inspired continuous-time attention architecture that models attention logits via an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck SDE, inducing Gaussian distributions over logits for probabilistic outputs and joint aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty quantification, demonstrating competitive accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty across diverse tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable uncertainty quantification in continuous-time representation learning is underdeveloped, especially within CT attention architectures, motivating a new approach.

Method: Formulates attention logit computation as solution of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck SDE modulated by input-dependent nonlinear gates from C.elegans NCP wiring; induces Gaussian distribution over logits → logistic-normal over attention weights → probabilistic output; trained with two-term objective (Gaussian NLL + epistemic-separation regularizer) for joint aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty.

Result: Competitive accuracy against baselines across 5 tasks (CT function approximation, multivariate regression, long-range forecasting, Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicle lane-keeping), with well-calibrated uncertainty estimates and interpretability at neuronal level.

Conclusion: NSAC effectively bridges biological inspiration and principled stochasticity for uncertainty-aware CT attention, achieving both performance and calibration.

Abstract: Reliable quantification of uncertainty estimates in continuous-time (CT) representation learning remains nascent, particularly within CT attention architectures. We introduce the Neuronal Stochastic Attention Circuit (NSAC), a novel biologically-inspired CT attention architecture that reformulates attention logit computation as the solution of an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic differential equation modulated by input-dependent, nonlinear interlinked gates derived from repurposed C.elegans Neuronal Circuit Policies (NCPs) wiring mechanism. It induces Gaussian distribution over logits that propagates principled stochasticity through logistic-normal distribution over attention weights to yield probabilistic output. A two-term objective function combining Gaussian negative log-likelihood with an epistemic-separation regularizer enforces higher predictive variance and enables joint quantification of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Empirically, we implement NSAC in a diverse set of learning tasks including: (i) irregular CT function approximation; (ii) multivariate regression; (iii) long-range forecasting; (iv) Industry 4.0; and (v) the lane-keeping of autonomous vehicles. We observe that the NSAC remains competitive against several baselines in terms of accuracy and produces reasonably well-calibrated uncertainty estimates while being interpretable at the neuronal cell level.

</details>


### [747] [Conditional KRR: Injecting Unpenalized Features into Kernel Methods with Applications to Kernel Thresholding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26067)
*Rustem Takhanov,Zhenisbek Assylbekov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies conditional kernel ridge regression (KRR), showing it can be reduced to standard KRR with a residual kernel plus an additional error term bounded by O(1/√N). The method outperforms standard KRR when the function class component is stronger than the residual part, demonstrated in settings with Mercer eigenfunctions and random features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conditional KRR methods have recently attracted increasing attention as they combine classical linear regression (with features specified by a function class) with KRR on residuals, but their statistical properties were not well understood.

Method: The authors reduce conditional KRR to standard KRR with a residual kernel, adding an O(1/√N) error term. They analyze two settings: (1) where the function class consists of the first k Mercer eigenfunctions of a positive definite kernel, and (2) where it consists of k random features from a random feature representation.

Result: Both theoretical analysis and experiments confirm that conditional KRR outperforms standard KRR when the function class component of the regression function is more pronounced than the residual part.

Conclusion: Conditional KRR is a viable alternative to standard KRR when prior knowledge about a function class is available, with provable guarantees through reduction to residual kernel KRR.

Abstract: Conditionally positive definite (CPD) kernels are defined with respect to a function class $\mathcal{F}$. It is well known that such a kernel $K$ is associated with its native space (defined analogously to an RKHS), which in turn gives rise to a learning method -- called conditional kernel ridge regression (conditional KRR) due to its analogy with KRR -- where the estimated regression function is penalized by the square of its native space norm. This method is of interest because it can be viewed as classical linear regression, with features specified by $\mathcal{F}$, followed by the application of standard KRR to the residual (unexplained) component of the target variable. Methods of this type have recently attracted increasing attention.
  We study the statistical properties of this method by reducing its behavior to that of KRR with another fixed kernel, called the residual kernel. Our main theoretical result shows that such a reduction is indeed possible, at the cost of an additional term in the expected test risk, bounded by $\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{N})$, where $N$ is the sample size and the hidden constant depends on the class $\mathcal{F}$ and the input distribution.
  This reduction enables us to analyze conditional KRR in the case where $K$ is positive definite and $\mathcal{F}$ is given by the first $k$ principal eigenfunctions in the Mercer decomposition of $K$. We also consider the setting where $\mathcal{F}$ consists of $k$ random features from a random feature representation of $K$. It turns out that these two settings are closely related. Both our theoretical analysis and experiments confirm that conditional KRR outperforms standard KRR in these cases whenever the $\mathcal{F}$-component of the regression function is more pronounced than the residual part.

</details>


### [748] [Rethinking Weak Supervision in Anomaly Detection: A Comprehensive Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26068)
*Xu Yao,Siyuan Zhou,Wu Zhenbo,Chaochuan Hou,Shuang Liang,Shiping wang,Hailiang Huang,Songqiao Han,Minqi Jiang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WSADBench is the first unified benchmark for Weakly Supervised Anomaly Detection, evaluating 36 algorithms across 4 modalities and 700K+ experiments, revealing strong correlations between supervision scenarios, dominance of foundation models over specialized methods except in extreme data scarcity, inconsistent utility of unlabeled data, and asymmetric sensitivity to label noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing WSAD research is fragmented across three isolated directions (incomplete, inexact, inaccurate supervision), lacking a unified framework to determine whether they share fundamental mechanisms or address distinct challenges.

Method: WSADBench establishes standardized evaluation protocols across 4 modalities, systematically varying label quantity, granularity, and quality to benchmark 36 algorithms from specialized WSAD methods to tabular foundation models, conducting over 700K experiments.

Result: (i) Strong correlations exist between weak supervision scenarios; (ii) Specialized WSAD methods only excel in extreme label scarcity but are dominated by foundation models otherwise; (iii) Unlabeled data provides marginal gains compared to label refinement; (iv) Models show asymmetric sensitivity to different label noise types.

Conclusion: WSADBench reveals that isolated WSAD research directions are intrinsically connected, and future research should reconsider the necessity of specialized WSAD methods when foundation models and general classifiers can outperform them under most conditions.

Abstract: Weakly supervised anomaly detection (WSAD) has developed in three primary directions: incomplete, inexact, and inaccurate supervision. However, these directions remain isolated, lacking a unified framework to assess whether they address unique challenges or share fundamental mechanics. This paper introduces WSADBench, the first benchmark that unifies evaluation across distinct weakly supervised scenarios, benchmarking diverse approaches from specialized WSAD methods to advanced tabular foundation models. WSADBench establishes standardized protocols to evaluate 36 algorithms across 4 modalities by systematically varying label quantity, granularity, and quality, revealing the performance boundaries of various methods. Based on over 700K experiments, WSADBench reveals four critical insights: (i) Strong intrinsic correlations exist between these weak supervision scenarios, challenging the isolation of current research directions. (ii) Specialized WSAD algorithms excel only in extreme label-scarcity regimes but are quickly dominated by tabular foundation models and general classification methods as supervision increases or in OOD scenarios. (iii) Unlabeled data shows inconsistent utility across settings, with marginal gains compared to label refinement. (iv) Models exhibit asymmetric sensitivity to different types of label noise. We release WSADBench as an open-source benchmark with code and datasets to facilitate future WSAD research: https://github.com/SUFE-AILAB/WSADBench.

</details>


### [749] [Active Query Synthesis for Preference Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26072)
*Namrata Nadagouda,Nauman Ahad,Maegan Tucker,Mark A. Davenport*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Info-Synth, a confidence-aware active query synthesis framework that generates optimal pairwise queries for efficient preference learning, along with two pool-based extension strategies (Pair M-dist and Pair Opt-dist), demonstrating versatility across synthetic and real-world tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard active learning methods are computationally expensive due to pool-based evaluation and ignore that pairwise queries between nearly identical or entirely dissimilar items yield ambiguous, low-confidence responses.

Method: Proposes a confidence-aware response model for ambiguous comparisons, an active query synthesis framework (Info-Synth) that generates optimal queries via mutual information maximization in continuous space, and two pool-based strategies (Pair M-dist and Pair Opt-dist).

Result: Demonstrated across synthetic preference learning, constrained text summarization, and continuous-space controller gain tuning for a simulated mobile robot.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses both feedback reliability and computational efficiency in active preference learning, showing strong versatility across diverse domains.

Abstract: Efficient learning of user preferences is crucial for many modern decision making systems but typically requires costly labeled data. Active learning reduces this cost, yet standard methods are computationally expensive due to pool-based evaluation. Further, most methods assume all query feedback is equally reliable, ignoring that pairwise queries between nearly identical or entirely dissimilar items yield ambiguous, low-confidence responses. To address the issue of feedback reliability, we introduce a novel confidence aware response model that explicitly accounts for these ambiguous comparisons. To overcome the computational bottleneck of pool-based evaluation, we propose an active query synthesis framework, Info-Synth that generates optimal queries by maximizing a mutual information-based objective within a continuous space. Moreover, we propose two strategies, Pair M-dist and Pair Opt-dist, that extend Info-Synth to select effective queries even when restricted to finite query pools. We demonstrate our framework's versatility and performance across synthetic preference learning, constrained text summary datasets, and subjective, continuous-space controller gain tuning for a simulated mobile robot.

</details>


### [750] [Global Convergence of Wasserstein Policy Gradient for Entropy-Regularized Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26078)
*Zhaoyu Zhu,Rui Gao,Shuang Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves global convergence of Wasserstein policy gradient (WPG) for entropy-regularized RL by leveraging Bellman structure, establishing a distributional Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition, and achieving geometric contraction with discretization bias control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite WPG's appeal for continuous-control problems, its global convergence properties remain poorly understood. Standard Langevin analyses do not directly apply because the RL objective depends on the policy through Bellman recursion rather than a static convex functional, and the Langevin drift's regularity must be controlled along policy iterates.

Method: The authors develop a global convergence theory by: (1) showing the soft Bellman residual admits a statewise KL representation w.r.t. a Gibbs policy; (2) using Bellman contraction to relate this residual to the global optimality gap; (3) employing a Bellman resolvent identity connecting value improvement to relative Fisher information; and (4) combining these with a uniform log-Sobolev inequality (LSI) for the evolving Gibbs family to yield a distributional Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition.

Result: The analysis yields geometric contraction up to a discretization bias, establishing global convergence for WPG. The Bellman recursion induces a favorable PL-type geometry that supports global convergence despite the lack of flat convexity.

Conclusion: Although entropy-regularized RL is not convex in the usual flat sense, the Bellman recursion induces a favorable Polyak-Łojasiewicz-type geometry that supports global convergence of WPG.

Abstract: Wasserstein policy gradient (WPG) is a policy optimization method for reinforcement learning (RL) that exploits the optimal-transport geometry of action distributions. For the entropy-regularized RL objective, WPG evolves each state-conditional policy by transporting it along the action gradient of the soft Q-function together with a Langevin-type diffusion. Despite its appeal for continuous-control problems, its global convergence properties remain poorly understood. Standard Langevin analyses do not directly apply, because the RL objective depends on the policy through the Bellman recursion rather than through a static convex functional, and the Langevin drift is determined by the soft Q-function, whose regularity must be controlled along the policy iterates.
  In this paper, we develop a global convergence theory for WPG by exploiting the Bellman structure of entropy-regularized RL. We show that the role usually played by convexity can be replaced by a Bellman-based argument: the soft Bellman residual admits a statewise KL representation with respect to a Gibbs policy; Bellman contraction relates this residual to the global optimality gap; and a Bellman resolvent identity connects value improvement to relative Fisher information. Combined with a uniform log-Sobolev inequality (LSI) for the evolving Gibbs family, these ingredients yield a distributional Polyak--Łojasiewicz condition. We further establish the regularity and uniform bounds needed to control the discretization error, thereby obtaining geometric contraction up to a discretization bias. Conceptually, our analysis shows that although entropy-regularized RL is not convex in the usual flat sense, the Bellman recursion induces a favorable Polyak--Lojasiewicz-type (PL) geometry that supports global convergence of WPG.

</details>


### [751] [OrpQuant: Geometric Orthogonal Residual Projection for Multiplier-Free Power-of-Two Transformer Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26092)
*Maoyang Xiang,Bo Wang,Tao Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ORP is an algorithm-hardware co-design framework that uses orthogonal residual projection with shift-and-add operations to overcome the low angular resolution problem in ultra-low-bit (sub-4-bit) Power-of-Two quantization, achieving 15-minute calibration for LLaMA-2-7B and mitigating timing bottlenecks from dense MAC arrays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ultra-low-bit PoT quantization suffers from a Low Angular Resolution Regime at sub-4-bit thresholds, degrading high-dimensional feature manifolds, while LLM/ViT deployment on edge devices is constrained by memory limits and timing bottlenecks from dense MAC arrays.

Method: Orthogonal Residual Projection (ORP) — a dual-basis geometric projection framework that adaptively synthesizes a higher-resolution residual lattice using shift-and-add operations, with an analytical solver replacing gradient-based optimization.

Result: Under 3-bit (W3/A16), ORP achieves 6.10 perplexity on LLaMA-2-7B, competitive with AWQ without asymmetric scaling; full-model calibration takes ~15 minutes; RTL synthesis at 28nm shows mitigated timing bottlenecks from dense multiplier trees.

Conclusion: ORP is an effective algorithm-hardware co-design that addresses geometric limitations of ultra-low-bit quantization, enabling efficient edge deployment without relying on MAC-intensive operations or asymmetric scaling.

Abstract: The deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision Transformers (ViTs) on edge devices is significantly constrained by memory limitations and the critical timing bottlenecks introduced by dense Multiply-Accumulate (MAC) arrays. In the ultra-low bit regime, logarithmic Power-of-Two (PoT) quantization provides a hardware-efficient alternative by replacing MAC operations with bit-shifts. However, the non-uniform exponential lattice is inherently limited by a \textbf{Low Angular Resolution Regime}, a structural flaw that becomes particularly pronounced at sub-4-bit thresholds, leading to a notable degradation of high-dimensional feature manifolds.
  To address this geometric limitation, we propose Orthogonal Residual Projection (ORP), an algorithm-hardware co-design framework. By formulating quantization as a dual-basis geometric projection, ORP adaptively synthesizes a higher-resolution residual lattice using strictly shift-and-add operations. Furthermore, ORP's analytical solver offers a practical alternative to computationally intensive gradient-based optimization, reducing the full-model calibration time for LLaMA-2-7B to approximately \textbf{15 minutes}.
  Extensive evaluations demonstrate ORP's applicability across modalities and its hardware efficiency. Under the 3-bit (W3/A16) constraint, ORP achieves a perplexity of 6.10 on LLaMA-2-7B, comparing favorably to conventional MAC-intensive baselines like AWQ without relying on asymmetric scaling, while maintaining competitive accuracy in 4-bit scenarios. At the silicon level, standard-cell RTL synthesis at a 28nm node indicates that ORP effectively mitigates the timing bottlenecks associated with dense multiplier trees.

</details>


### [752] [Goal-driven Bayesian Optimal Experimental Design for Robust Decision-Making Under Model Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26093)
*Jinwoo Go,Xiaoning Qian,Byung-Jun Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GoBOED is a goal-driven Bayesian optimal experimental design framework that optimizes experiments for downstream decision objectives rather than generic information gain, achieving equivalent decision quality with wider near-optimal design windows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard BOED maximizes information gain about model parameters, but in decision-critical settings, only specific parameter directions relevant to the objective matter — reducing irrelevant uncertainty wastes experimental resources and does not improve decisions.

Method: GoBOED combines an amortized variational posterior surrogate with a differentiable convex decision layer, enabling fully decision-focused gradient-based design optimization.

Result: Theoretically shows gradients are insensitive to decision-irrelevant parameter directions. Empirically demonstrates better alignment with downstream objectives across source localization, epidemic management, and pharmacokinetic control tasks, with substantially wider near-optimal design windows than goal-agnostic BOED.

Conclusion: Goal-driven experimental design achieves equivalent decision quality with greater design flexibility, making it more practical and efficient for decision-critical applications.

Abstract: Bayesian optimal experimental design (BOED) selects experiments to maximize information gain about model parameters. However, in decision-critical settings, reducing parameter uncertainty does not necessarily improve downstream decisions, as only specific parameter directions relevant to the objective truly matter. We propose GoBOED, a goal-driven BOED framework that directly optimizes experimental designs for a specified decision-making objective. GoBOED combines an amortized variational posterior surrogate with a differentiable convex decision layer, enabling gradient-based design optimization that is fully decision-focused. We theoretically show that GoBOED gradients are insensitive to parameter directions irrelevant to the decision objective, providing a formal justification for why goal-driven design achieves equivalent decision quality over a wider set of experimental designs than information-gain maximization. Empirically, across source localization, epidemic management, and pharmacokinetic control, GoBOED identifies designs that better align with downstream decision objectives and reveals that near-optimal design windows are substantially wider than those predicted by goal-agnostic BOED approaches.

</details>


### [753] [Forgetting in Language Models: Capacity, Optimization, and Self-Generated Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26097)
*Martin Marek,Dongkyu Cho,Shikai Qiu,Rumi Chunara,Pavel Izmailov,Andrew Gordon Wilson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Language models can use self-generated samples as replay data to nearly eliminate forgetting when finetuning on new tasks, though forgetting still occurs when models have little remaining capacity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional forgetting mitigation requires replaying stored exemplars from prior tasks, which is often impractical. The authors explore whether language models can use self-generated samples from their own training distribution as an alternative replay method.

Method: The authors use self-generated samples from language models' own training distribution as replay data during finetuning on new tasks, comparing this approach to traditional exemplar replay and examining the effects of model capacity, learning rates, and training steps.

Result: Self-generated replay nearly eliminates forgetting. However, forgetting persists when models are pretrained close to saturation (little remaining capacity). Low learning rates reduce forgetting but require substantially more training steps, while replay enables fast, high-learning-rate finetuning without forgetting.

Conclusion: Self-generated replay is an effective and practical method for mitigating forgetting in language models, breaking the tradeoff between learning rate and forgetting, though capacity limitations remain a challenge.

Abstract: Models trained on a new task typically degrade on prior tasks, a phenomenon known as forgetting. Traditionally, mitigating forgetting has required replaying stored exemplars from prior tasks, which is often impractical. By contrast, language models can sample from their own training distribution, and we show that these self-generated samples serve as effective replay data, nearly eliminating forgetting. We find that forgetting nonetheless persists when the model has little remaining capacity: models pretrained close to saturation cannot absorb new information without overwriting prior knowledge. When capacity is not the limiting factor, low learning rates reduce forgetting but require substantially more training steps. Replay breaks this tradeoff, enabling fast, high-learning-rate finetuning without forgetting.

</details>


### [754] [Looped Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26106)
*Sanghyun Lee,Chunsan Hong,Seungryong Kim,Jonghyun Lee,Jongho Park,Dongmin Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoopMDM selectively loops early-middle transformer layers in masked diffusion models, achieving up to 3.3x training efficiency gains, improved reasoning performance (e.g., +8.5 on GSM8K), and flexible inference-time compute scaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The effective design of transformer architectures for masked diffusion models (MDMs) remains underexplored; the authors aim to improve training efficiency and model performance without adding parameters.

Method: Selectively looping the early-middle transformer layers during training (depth-scaling effect, no extra parameters) and varying loop count at inference for flexible compute scaling.

Result: LoopMDM matches same-size MDMs with up to 3.3x fewer training FLOPs; outperforms on reasoning benchmarks (up to +8.5 on GSM8K); surpasses deeper non-looped MDMs; inference-time compute scaling works; adaptive looping yields additional efficiency gains.

Conclusion: Selective looping is more effective than naive depth scaling for MDMs, improving efficiency and performance, with evidence that looping promotes interactions among masked positions.

Abstract: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) have emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models for language modeling, yet the effective design of transformer architectures for MDMs remains underexplored. In this paper, we show that selectively looping the early-middle transformer layers significantly improves both training efficiency and model performance in MDMs. We call this approach LoopMDM(Looped Masked Diffusion Model), which brings two key benefits: looping layers at training-time yields a depth-scaling effect without adding parameters, while varying the number of loops at inference-time enables flexible compute scaling. Despite the simplicity, the results are striking: across multiple pre-training corpora, LoopMDM matches the performance of same-size MDMs with up to 3.3 fewer training FLOPs, while its final performance outperforms them on various reasoning benchmarks, including up to 8.5 points on GSM8K. It even surpasses deeper non-looped MDMs trained with comparable per-step compute, indicating that selective looping is more effective than naive depth scaling. Furthermore, LoopMDM can scale inference-time compute by increasing the number of loops. Adaptively adjusting the number of loops throughout the sampling process further yields additional gains in compute efficiency while maintaining performance. Lastly, with attention analysis, we provide evidence that looping is effective in MDMs by promoting interactions among masked positions. Our code and weights will be publicly released.

</details>


### [755] [Prism: A Plug-in Reproducible Infrastructure for Scalable Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.26110)
*Jun-Tao Tang,Yu-Cheng Shi,Zhen-Hao Xie,Da-Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prism is a plug-in reproducible codebase for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) that separates algorithmic development from backbone implementation via a lightweight plugin registration mechanism, eliminating engineering bottlenecks and enabling scalable, fair comparison.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MCIT research suffers from severe engineering bottlenecks: existing methods directly modify base MLLM codebases, leading to substantial implementation overhead, method-specific architectures, limited code reuse, and difficulty in fair comparison.

Method: Prism introduces a lightweight plugin registration mechanism that decouples algorithmic development from the backbone MLLM implementation. New strategies can be integrated as independent plugins without modifying the underlying codebase, natively supporting large-scale training pipelines.

Result: Prism eliminates structural fragmentation, accelerates method development, and enables reproducible and scalable MCIT experimentation through its plug-in architecture.

Conclusion: Prism provides a standardized, reusable, and fair-comparison-enabled codebase for MCIT research, addressing key engineering challenges in the field.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve versatility by reformulating diverse tasks into a unified instruction-following framework via instruction tuning. However, real-world deployment requires continuous adaptation to emerging tasks, motivating Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT). Despite its growing importance, current MCIT research is hindered by severe engineering bottlenecks. Existing methods are typically implemented by directly modifying the base MLLM codebase, which imposes substantial implementation overhead and yields method-specific architectures that severely limit code reuse and fair comparison. To address this, we introduce Prism, a plug-in reproducible codebase specifically designed for scalable MCIT research. It separates algorithmic development from the backbone implementation via a lightweight plugin registration mechanism, enabling new strategies to be integrated as independent plugins without modifying the underlying MLLM codebase, thereby eliminating structural fragmentation and accelerating method development. Prism natively supports widely used large-scale training pipeline, thereby enabling reproducible and scalable MCIT experimentation. Code is available at https://github.com/LAMDA-CL/Prism.

</details>


### [756] [NPSolver: Neural Poisson Solver with Iterative Physics Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25786)
*Bocheng Zeng,Rui Zhang,Runze Mao,Mengtao Yan,Xuan Bai,Yang Liu,Zhi X. Chen,Hao Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NPSolver is a neural Poisson solver trained without labeled data using iterative physics supervision via PCG steps, combined with a Boundary-Aware Transolver architecture, achieving superior performance on irregular 2D/3D domains compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical iterative solvers for Poisson equations suffer from prohibitive runtime on ill-conditioned irregular domains, while existing neural operators require large labeled datasets or suffer from unstable training with physics-informed losses.

Method: NPSolver uses iterative physics supervision via a small number of PCG steps to refine predictions (with stop-gradient for stability), and introduces BA-Transolver architecture with separate interior/boundary tokenization for mixed boundary conditions.

Result: Outperforms both physics-informed and data-driven baselines on 2D/3D irregular geometries, with demonstrated effectiveness in downstream thermal control tasks.

Conclusion: Iterative physics supervision with PCG steps is a stable and effective training strategy for neural PDE solvers without labeled data, and boundary-aware architecture design is crucial for handling complex boundary conditions.

Abstract: Efficiently solving Poisson equations on complex, irregular domains remains a fundamental challenge in scientific computing, as classical iterative solvers often suffer from prohibitive runtime due to ill-conditioned systems. While neural operators offer a fast alternative, they typically rely on large-scale labeled datasets or struggle with unstable training dynamics when using physics-informed residual losses. We propose \textsc{NPSolver}, a neural Poisson solver trained without solution labels via iterative physics supervision. Instead of relying on fully converged numerical solutions or raw PDE residuals, \textsc{NPSolver} utilizes a small number of preconditioned conjugate gradient (PCG) steps to refine its own predictions, providing a more stable and well-scaled training signal. Theoretical analysis confirms that this iterative supervision serves as a well-conditioned error proxy and that a stop-gradient design is essential for optimization stability. To better capture boundary-driven features under mixed boundary conditions, we further introduce the Boundary-Aware Transolver (\textsc{BA-Transolver}) architecture that explicitly separates interior and boundary tokenization. Extensive evaluations on 2D and 3D irregular geometries demonstrate that \textsc{NPSolver} outperforms both physics-informed and data-driven baselines. Furthermore, a downstream thermal control task highlights the model's capability for conducting efficient and reliable gradient-based boundary control. We will release our codes and data at https://github.com/intell-sci-comput/NPSolver.

</details>


### [757] [Small Models, Strong Priors: Architectural Inductive Bias for Parameter-Efficient Neural PDE Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25949)
*Shyam Sankaran,Hanwen Wang,Paris Perdikaris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: WaveLiT uses wavelet-based architectural priors to achieve competitive performance with 100-1000x smaller models than foundation PDE solvers, demonstrating that inductive bias matters more than scale in neural PDE solving.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that scaling model size (as done in vision and language) is a poor substitute for architectural inductive bias in neural PDE solvers, and that understanding where priors succeed/fail reveals what they capture.

Method: WaveLiT combines discrete wavelet transform for lossless multi-resolution tokenization, augmented linear attention, shared-weight multiscale feature pyramid, and wavelet-domain auxiliary loss.

Result: Bespoke 1-10M parameter WaveLiT models compete with 100-1000x larger foundation models on eight TheWell benchmarks, with best gains on wave/acoustic problems and weakest on chaotic advection-dominated flows.

Conclusion: Small-model PDE performance is shaped by architectural inductive bias rather than scale; the pattern of a prior's failures is a useful empirical signal about its content.

Abstract: Neural PDE solvers have followed the scaling trajectory of vision and language, with recent foundation models reaching billions of parameters. We argue that scale is a poor substitute for architectural inductive bias in this domain: structured priors deliver outsized parameter efficiency, and the pattern of where they succeed and fail is itself informative about what they capture. We instantiate this argument in WaveLiT, an architecture combining a discrete wavelet transform for lossless multi-resolution tokenization, an augmented linear attention block, a shared-weight multiscale feature pyramid, and a wavelet-domain auxiliary loss. Bespoke 1-10M-parameter WaveLiT models compete with foundation models of 100-1000$\times$ their size across eight TheWell benchmarks, with the largest gains on wave and acoustic-dominated benchmarks where the wavelet-multiscale prior fits the dominant dynamical structure and small per-step errors do not compound geometrically under rollout. Trained jointly across all eight benchmarks, a 10M-parameter foundation variant exhibits a structured, physically interpretable transfer pattern -- strongest where the wavelet-multiscale prior matches the dynamics, weakest on chaotic advection-dominated flows. The entire pipeline trains on a single GPU. The results suggest that small-model PDE performance is shaped by architectural inductive bias rather than scale, and that the structure of a prior's failures is a useful empirical signal about its content.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [758] [7 Tesla Quantitative MRI and Machine Learning for Exploratory Motor Subtype Stratification and Diagnosis in Parkinson's Disease](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24179)
*Anne Louise Kristoffersen,Runa Geirmundsdatter Unsgård,Marc-Antoine Fortin,Ingrid Gylterud Kvålsgard,Kjersti Eline Stige,Thanh Pierre Doan,Erik Magnus Berntsen,Charalampos Tzoulis,Pål Erik Goa*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Deep-learning-based brain segmentation combined with 7 Tesla MRI quantitative maps and feature selection significantly improves classification of Parkinson's disease subtypes (PIGD vs TD) and diagnosis, achieving up to 100% accuracy for subtype discrimination, compared to using all features.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parkinson's disease is highly heterogeneous with varying motor symptoms. The study aims to find imaging biomarkers that support objective subtype stratification, which could improve biological understanding, study design, and enable personalized treatment strategies.

Method: U-Net deep learning model for automatic brain segmentation on 7 Tesla MRI + quantitative maps. 21 HC and 24 PwP (PIGD and TD subtypes). Two classification approaches (A: all features; B: optimal feature subset) with 5-fold cross-validation across three tasks (HC vs PwP, PIGD vs TD, multiclass).

Result: U-Net achieved mean DSC of 0.86. Approach B (with feature selection) significantly outperformed Approach A: Task 1 accuracy 0.82 vs 0.69 (AUC 0.93 vs 0.73), Task 2 accuracy 1.00 vs 0.69 (AUC 1.00 vs 0.90), Task 3 accuracy 0.73 vs 0.62 (AUC 0.91 vs 0.66).

Conclusion: DL-based segmentation combined with qMRI feature selection improves classification performance, showing potential for interpretable imaging signatures for PD diagnosis and phenotype stratification. Larger multi-site studies are needed for validation.

Abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD) is a highly heterogeneous disease, including which motor symptoms are dominating. Imaging biomarkers that support subtype stratification could also improve biological understanding and study design, and enable personalized treatment strategies. This study evaluates whether deep-learning based automatic brain segmentation, in addition to quantitative maps from 7 Tesla MRI, can highlight differences between Healthy Controls (HC), Postural Instability and Gait Difficulty (PIGD) and Tremor Dominant (TD), and subsequently be used for objective PD stratification. The performance of machine learning classifiers may be improved with feature selection. 21 HC, and 24 people with PD (PwP) were included. The U-Net training was assessed with DSC. Two classification approaches using 5-fold cross-validation were defined across three tasks: (1) HC vs PwP; (2) PIGD vs TD; (3) multiclass, HC vs PIGD vs TD. Approach A used all extracted features. Approach B found the optimal subset of features for the classification tasks. The U-Net achieved mean DSC of 0.86 for all ROIs during training. Approach A: Task 1 best accuracy of 0.69 and best AUC of 0.73. Task 2 accuracy 0.69, AUC 0.90. Task 3 accuracy 0.62, AUC 0.66. Approach B: Task 1 accuracy of 0.82 and AUC of 0.93. Task 2 accuracy 1.00, AUC 1.00. Task 3 accuracy 0.73, AUC 0.91. DL-based segmentation combined with qMRI feature selection improved classification relative to using all features, supporting the potential of interpretable, low-dimensional imaging signatures for PD diagnosis support and phenotype stratification. Larger, multi-site studies are warranted to assess generalizability and stability.

</details>


### [759] [Causal Evaluation of Contributing Factors to Urban Heat Island](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24459)
*Sadaf Alavi Soltani,Saeid Niazmardi,Ali Esmaeily*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A study using Hotelling's T-square test to analyze causal relationships between UHI and its contributing factors across 22 Tehran districts (2003-2021), finding that NDWI and NDBI changes had no causal link to UHI, while other factors (precipitation, NDSI, NDVI, EVI) did contribute to UHI intensification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand causal relations between UHI and its contributing factors in order to minimize adverse environmental and health effects, given that UHI dynamics vary over time and location.

Method: A statistical method based on Hotelling's T-square test that estimates UHI trends across urban districts and compares contributing factors between districts with increasing vs. non-increasing UHI trends, interpreting significant differences as causal evidence.

Result: All 22 Tehran districts showed stable or increasing LST trends. NDWI and NDBI had no causal relationship with UHI, while precipitation, NDSI, NDVI, and EVI variations were identified as causal contributors to UHI intensification.

Conclusion: The proposed statistical test can effectively identify causal factors of UHI, which may inform targeted mitigation strategies for urban heat island effects.

Abstract: Urban heat islands (UHI) are formed due to complex interactions between various factors. UHI, its contributing factors, and their interaction vary over time and location. Accordingly, understanding the causal relation between UHI and its contributing factors is essential to minimizing its adverse effects on the environment and human health. Here, we proposed a statistical method based on Hotelling's T-square test to analyze this association. The proposed test estimates the UHI trends across different urban districts and compares the UHI contributing factors between the districts with increasing and non-increasing UHI trends. This comparison, if significantly different, can be interpreted as evidence of a causal association between the factor and UHI. This research used the proposed test to analyze the UHI and its contributing factors across 22 municipal districts of Tehran between 2003 and 2021. We examined the time series of weather conditions (measured by precipitation, NDSI, and NDWI), vegetation cover (measured by NDVI and EVI), and urban density (measured by NDBI) as factors contributing to the UHI, which was measured through nighttime LST. The results showed that all districts in Tehran exhibited stable or increasing trends in LST, leading to UHI effects. The proposed test indicated that the temporal changes in NDWI and NDBI did not have a causal relationship with UHIs. Meanwhile, variations in other factors were identified as contributing to the intensification of UHIs.

</details>


### [760] [A Signal Extraction Approach for Remote Heart Rate Variability Assessment Using Proxy Measure in a Driving Simulator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24525)
*Đorđe D. Nešković,Nadica Miljković*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This study evaluates rPPG algorithms (2SR, CHROM, POS, PCA) on superpixel facial regions in a driving simulator, introducing Lp norm and Fractional-Order Derivative peak enhancement for HRV estimation. Best results: 2SR + FOD on 20 superpixels achieved MAE of 1.92 bpm for PR, with 2SR recommended for PR and CHROM for HRV estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable robust, non-contact heart rate and HRV monitoring in dynamic driving simulator environments by evaluating and optimizing rPPG algorithms with novel peak enhancement techniques, addressing motion artifacts inherent to real-world driving conditions.

Method: Evaluated 4 rPPG algorithms (2SR, CHROM, POS, PCA) on superpixel-based facial regions (10 and 20 targets). Introduced two novel peak enhancement approaches: Lp norm and Fractional-Order Derivative. Used SNR-based quality assessment on 20s segments for data cleaning. Tested on 29 participants during baseline and driving conditions, validated against ECG.

Result: Best PR accuracy: 2SR with FOD on 20 superpixels achieved MAE 1.92 bpm (sd=1.72). Best HRV: SDNN MAE=0.061s, RMSSD MAE=0.081s. F1 score of 0.93 for inter-beat interval detection. Optimal parameters: Lp norm p=6-7, fractional orders 1.0-1.4. rPPG parameters reproduced ECG statistical structure.

Conclusion: 2SR is recommended for pulse rate estimation, while CHROM is recommended for HRV estimation using Lp norm with 20 superpixels. Caution advised for FOD due to slow rPPG waveform changes. Provides clear methodological guidance for rPPG monitoring in driving simulators.

Abstract: This study evaluates remote Photopletismography (rPPG) algorithms, Spatial Subspace Rotation (2SR), Chrominance-based method (CHROM), Plane-Orthogonal-to-Skin (POS), and Principal Component Analysis (PCA), applied to selected superpixel-based facial regions (with target counts of 10 and 20 regions) for monitoring in a driving simulator. Two novel peak enhancement approaches, based on the Lp norm and Fractional-Order Derivative (FOD), are introduced to enable robust Heart Rate Variability (HRV) estimation. A signal-to-noise ratio-based quality assessment of 20 s segments serves as a data cleaning mechanism to mitigate motion artifacts inherent to dynamic recording conditions. In a sample of 29 participants recorded during baseline and driving simulation conditions, Pulse Rate (PR) is calculated with clinically acceptable accuracy across configurations (validated against simultaneous Electrocardiography (ECG) recordings), achieving the lowest Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.92 bpm (sd = 1.72) using 2SR with FOD and 20 superpixel regions. The best-case MAE reached 0.061 s for Standard Deviation of Normal-to-Normal intervals (SDNN) and 0.081 s for Root Mean Square of Successive Differences (RMSSD), with inter-beat interval detection yielding an F1 score of 0.93. Optimal parameters clustered around p = 6-7 for Lp norm and fractional orders of 1.0-1.4. All rPPG-derived parameters reproduced the statistical structure of the reference ECG across conditions and configurations. Caution is advised when using FOD due to slow changes in the rPPG waveform. Overall, 2SR is recommended for PR, while CHROM for HRV estimation, using Lp norm with 20 superpixels, providing clear methodological guidance for rPPG monitoring in driving simulators

</details>


### [761] [SurgRFO: Foundation Model Based Compositional Synthesis of Critical Retained Foreign Objects in Intraoperative Chest X-rays](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24787)
*Yuanyun Hu,Yuli Wang,Noemi Acevedo Rodriguez,Ronald Yang,Wen-Chi Hsu,Siwei Luo,Zihao Bai,Jing Wu,Yuwei Dai,Shaoju Wu,Jonathon Lindquist,Justin Honce,Premal Trivedi,Zhicheng Jiao,Ihab Kamel,Elliott Haut,Pamela Johnson,John Eng,Cheng Ting Lin,Nan Su,Bo Chen,Sun Yu,Harrison Bai*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: SurgRFO is a two-stage synthesis framework that generates realistic intraoperative chest X-rays with retained foreign objects by fine-tuning a foundation model for background generation and compositing diverse RFO patches via conditional Poisson fusion, improving downstream detection model sensitivity while maintaining clinical realism validated by clinicians.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Critical retained foreign objects (RFOs) on intraoperative chest radiographs are rare but high-risk events. Their scarcity limits robust automated detection model training and generalization, motivating the need for realistic synthetic data generation.

Method: Two-stage synthesis: (1) Fine-tune a Roentgen chest X-ray foundation model on surgical-domain images to generate realistic RFO-free backgrounds; (2) Train a lightweight generator on localized RFO patches from limited positive cases to synthesize diverse RFO instances, then composite onto backgrounds using conditional Poisson fusion for photometric consistency.

Result: SurgRFO consistently improves sensitivity at low false-positive-per-image (FPPI) operating points on internal and external test sets. Clinician ratings indicate synthesized images achieve realism comparable to real intraoperative images.

Conclusion: The framework effectively addresses data scarcity for RFO detection by generating clinically realistic synthetic X-rays, with ablation analyses examining fusion strategies and synthesis scale, while also discussing ethical safeguards for synthetic surgical data.

Abstract: Critical retained foreign objects (RFOs) on intraoperative chest radiographs are rare but high-risk events. Their scarcity limits robust automated detection model training and generalization. We introduce SurgRFO, a two-stage synthesis framework for generating realistic RFO-present intraoperative chest X-rays. In Stage 1, a Roentgen chest X-ray foundation model is fine-tuned on surgical-domain images to generate realistic RFO-free backgrounds that preserve anatomy, indwelling lines and tubes, and intraoperative imaging characteristics. In Stage 2, a lightweight generator trained on localized RFO patches from limited positive cases synthesizes diverse RFO instances, which are composited onto generated backgrounds using conditional Poisson fusion to improve photometric consistency. We evaluate SurgRFO through (i) a blinded clinician study assessing realism and clinical plausibility, and (ii) downstream detection experiments in which synthesized data are used to augment Faster R-CNN, YOLOv8, and RetinaNet. SurgRFO consistently improves sensitivity at low false-positive-per-image (FPPI) operating points on internal and external test sets. Clinician ratings indicate that the synthesized images achieve realism comparable to real intraoperative images. Ablation analyses further examine fusion strategies and synthesis scale. Ethical safeguards for synthetic surgical data are also discussed.

</details>


### [762] [Explainable Multi-Task Retinal Imaging Reveals Microvascular Signals for Systemic Risk Stratification in Type 2 Diabetes: A Pilot Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.24913)
*Mini Han Wang,Liting Huang,Wei Hong,Boonthawan Wingwon*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This study uses an explainable multi-task deep learning framework on 11,011 retinal fundus images from 2,719 individuals to predict systemic abnormalities in Type 2 Diabetes. The model achieved modest predictive performance (best AUC 0.63 for kidney abnormality), and explainability analyses confirmed that retinal vessels are the primary predictive source, supporting retinal imaging as a potential biomarker for systemic microvascular health.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether retinal features encode biologically meaningful systemic signals that can be reliably interpreted using explainable AI, addressing the gap between retinal imaging as a non-invasive biomarker and the lack of interpretable evidence linking retinal microvascular features to systemic abnormalities in diabetes.

Method: An explainable multi-task deep learning framework with a shared neural network backbone and task-specific heads (for glycaemic status, kidney abnormality, multi-system involvement). Trained on 11,011 fundus images from 2,719 individuals. Interpretability evaluated using Grad-CAM, anatomical masking, and vessel alignment analysis.

Result: Task-dependent performance: best for kidney abnormality (AUC up to 0.63), limited for glycaemic status (AUC 0.49-0.61). Explainability localized attention to retinal vessels and peripapillary regions; masking vascular regions caused greatest performance decline. Different architectures showed heterogeneous attention patterns.

Conclusion: Retinal microvascular features contain measurable signals associated with systemic abnormalities, particularly microvascular damage. The multi-task learning with XAI validation framework advances retinal imaging toward interpretable digital biomarkers for systemic risk stratification in diabetes.

Abstract: Retinal imaging provides a non-invasive window into systemic microvascular health and has emerged as a potential biomarker for systemic diseases. However, whether retinal features encode biologically meaningful systemic signals that can be reliably interpreted using explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) remains unclear. An explainable multi-task deep learning framework was developed to investigate associations between retinal microvascular features and systemic abnormalities in Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus. A total of 11,011 fundus images from 2,719 individuals were analysed using a shared neural network with task-specific heads for glycaemic status, kidney abnormality, and multi-system involvement. Model interpretability was evaluated using Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM), anatomical masking, and vessel alignment analysis. The framework demonstrated task-dependent predictive performance, with the best discrimination observed for kidney abnormality (AUC up to 0.63), whereas glycaemic status prediction showed limited performance (AUC = 0.49-0.61). Explainability analyses consistently localized model attention to retinal vessels and peripapillary regions. Masking experiments showed that occlusion of vascular regions caused the greatest performance decline, indicating that retinal vessels were the primary predictive source. Different architectures exhibited heterogeneous attention patterns, suggesting multiple representational pathways for systemic signal encoding. This pilot study demonstrates that retinal microvascular features contain measurable signals associated with systemic abnormalities, particularly microvascular damage. By integrating multi-task learning with quantitative XAI validation, this framework advances retinal imaging toward interpretable digital biomarkers for systemic risk stratification in diabetes.

</details>


### [763] [Parameter-Efficient CT Reconstruction via Deep Graph Laplacian Regularization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25348)
*Veera Varuni Radhakrishnan,Chinthaka Dinesh,Qurat-ul-Ain Azim*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: Deep Graph Laplacian Regularization (Deep GLR) achieves 30.70 dB PSNR for LDCT reconstruction using only 91,848 parameters and 1000 training samples (2.8% of standard), offering 5.8× better parameter efficiency and 30× better data efficiency per dB improvement over benchmark methods, though a 13 dB gap remains versus state-of-the-art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tradeoff between LDCT reconstruction quality and resource requirements, as existing deep learning methods rely on over 500,000 parameters and 35,000+ scans, which is impractical for resource-constrained medical imaging scenarios.

Method: Integrates quadratic graph regularization into a Proximal Forward-Backward Splitting optimization framework with three lightweight CNN modules, using only 91,848 parameters trained on 1000 LoDoPaB-CT samples.

Result: 30.70 dB PSNR (6.33 dB improvement over FBP), 5.8× better parameter efficiency and 30× better data efficiency per dB improvement. Learned graph bandwidth ε=1.25 converges to interpretable values.

Conclusion: Graph-based regularization provides a favorable efficiency-quality tradeoff for resource-constrained medical imaging, though a significant performance gap remains versus state-of-the-art methods.

Abstract: Low-dose computed tomography (LDCT) reconstruction faces a critical tradeoff between reconstruction quality and resource requirements. While recent deep learning methods achieve state-of-the-art performance, they typically rely on over 500,000 parameters trained on large-scale datasets exceeding 35,000 scans. This work investigates whether graph-based regularization can provide meaningful noise reduction under strict resource constraints. We propose Deep Graph Laplacian Regularization (Deep GLR), integrating quadratic graph regularization into a Proximal Forward-Backward Splitting optimization framework with three lightweight CNN modules. Evaluated on the LoDoPaB-CT benchmark, Deep GLR achieves 30.70 dB PSNR, representing a 6.33 dB improvement over filtered backprojection, while using only 91,848 parameters trained on 1000 samples (2.8\% of standard training set). Compared to benchmark methods, this represents 5.8 times better parameter efficiency and 30 times better data efficiency per dB improvement. The learned graph bandwidth parameter ($ε$=1.25) converges to interpretable values, suggesting the method captures meaningful image priors rather than overfitting. While a 13 dB gap remains versus state-of-the-art methods, results demonstrate that graph-based regularization provides a favorable efficiency-quality tradeoff for resource-constrained medical imaging scenarios.

</details>


### [764] [A Clinically Validated Foundation Model for Comprehensive Lung Pathology Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25878)
*Zhengrui Guo,Zhengyu Zhang,Jiabo Ma,Yihui Wang,Fengtao Zhou,Yingxue Xu,Ling Liang,Chenglong Zhao,Qi Xie,Jinbang Li,Shujing Guo,Fangyi Han,Zhijian Cen,Ziyi Liu,Cheng Jin,Junlin Hou,Zhixuan Chen,Yu Cai,Lijuan Qu,Shifu Chen,Yueping Liu,Zhe Wang,Xiuming Zhang,Muyan Cai,Li Liang,Hao Chen*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: PulmoFoundation is a lung-specific foundation model for computational pathology, built on Virchow2 with ~40,000 lung H&E WSIs, evaluated on ~26,000 WSIs across 32 tasks. In a prospective study of 1,357 patients (11 tasks), it achieved 92.3% average AUC, reduced second-review burden (68.8% biopsies, 83.0% frozen sections), deferred 44.5% IHC orders, and in an RCT with 8 pathologists, improved diagnostic accuracy (91.7% vs 83.8%), reduced time by 19.6%, increased confidence by 8.7%, and improved inter-rater agreement (kappa 0.56→0.76).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current CPath approaches use task-specific models with isolated objectives. Pan-cancer foundation models lack subspecialty-level depth and have not been prospectively validated in real-world clinical workflows. There is a need for a lung-specific, clinically validated foundation model.

Method: PulmoFoundation was built by subspecialty-specific pretraining of Virchow2 on ~40,000 H&E-stained lung WSIs. It was evaluated on ~26,000 WSIs across 32 clinically relevant tasks. Validation included a prospective study (1,357 patients, 11 tasks), pre-specified triage thresholds for workflow reduction, and a crossover RCT with 8 pathologists (4,928 case-reader pairs).

Result: - Prospective study: average AUC 92.3% across 11 tasks.
- Triage: reduced second-review burden for 68.8% biopsies and 83.0% frozen sections; deferred 44.5% IHC stain orders (PPVs: 1.0, 0.991, 0.966).
- RCT: AI assistance improved diagnostic accuracy (91.7% vs 83.8%), reduced median diagnostic time by 19.6%, increased confidence by 8.7%, improved inter-rater agreement (kappa 0.56→0.76).

Conclusion: PulmoFoundation is a clinically validated decision-support system for lung pathology across pre-operative, intra-operative, and post-operative care, with strong prospective and RCT evidence supporting its utility.

Abstract: Pathological assessment guides lung cancer diagnosis, treatment selection, and prognostic evaluation, yet current CPath approaches rely on task-specific models for isolated objectives. Although pan-cancer foundation models offer versatility, they lack subspecialty-level depth and have not been evaluated across clinical workflows or prospectively validated in real-world settings. We introduce PulmoFoundation, a multi-center, prospectively validated, randomized controlled trial (RCT)-evaluated foundation model for comprehensive lung pathology assessment across pre-operative, intra-operative, and post-operative care. Built upon Virchow2 via subspecialty-specific pretraining using ~40,000 diagnostic H&E-stained whole-slide images (WSIs), PulmoFoundation was systematically evaluated on ~26,000 WSIs across 32 clinically relevant tasks. In addition to accurately predicting molecular markers and patient survival, our model achieves clinical-grade performance in core diagnostic tasks across biopsy, frozen section, and surgical resection slides. In a registered prospective study of 1,357 patients across 11 diagnostic tasks, our model achieved an average AUC of 92.3%. Using pre-specified triage thresholds, PulmoFoundation could reduce additional second-review burden for 68.8% of biopsies and 83.0% of frozen sections, and defer 44.5% of IHC stain orders, with PPVs of 1.0, 0.991, and 0.966. Beyond prospective validation, we conducted a crossover RCT with eight pathologists, in which AI assistance improved diagnostic accuracy across 4,928 case-reader pairs (91.7% w/ AI vs. 83.8% w/o AI). AI assistance also reduced median diagnostic time by 19.6%, increased diagnostic confidence by 8.7%, and improved inter-rater agreement from moderate (kappa = 0.56) to substantial (kappa = 0.76). Together, these evaluations support PulmoFoundation as a clinically validated decision-support system for lung pathology.

</details>


### [765] [How Accurate are Video Quality Models for Diffusion-Based Video Super-Resolution?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.25940)
*Benjamin Herb,Steve Göring,Alexander Raake,Rakesh Rao Ramachandra Rao*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates whether existing video quality models can assess diffusion-based VSR methods by comparing six upscaling methods against subjective tests, finding CNN-based full-reference models (LPIPS, DISTS, CVQA-FR) perform best but none are accurate enough to replace human evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether existing video quality assessment models can reliably evaluate the perceptual quality of diffusion-based video super-resolution methods.

Method: Six upscaling methods (Lanczos, Rhea, SCST, DOVE, SeedVR2, Starlight Mini) applied to compressed (AV1, DCVC-RT) and uncompressed low-resolution videos for UHD-1/4K display; evaluated using full-reference and no-reference quality models against subjective test ratings.

Result: CNN-based full-reference models (LPIPS, DISTS, CVQA-FR) show highest correlation with subjective ratings. VMAF fails on spatial inconsistencies from Starlight Mini. Most models overestimate SCST's overly sharp results. No model reaches sufficient accuracy to replace subjective testing.

Conclusion: Existing video quality models are inadequate for reliably assessing diffusion-based VSR methods, and complementary subjective testing remains necessary.

Abstract: Recent video super-resolution (VSR) approaches use deep neural networks to enhance low-quality input videos and recover visual detail, with diffusion-based methods in particular showing promising results. In this paper, we investigate whether existing video quality models can be used to assess the performance of these diffusion-based VSR methods, by comparing model predictions with results from a subjective test. The study compares six upscaling methods (Lanczos, Rhea, SCST, DOVE, SeedVR2, Starlight Mini) applied to both compressed (AV1 and DCVC-RT) and uncompressed low-resolution videos considering the play-out on a UHD-1/4K screen. A range of full- and no-reference quality models are used to assess their applicability to this new type of quality degradation, focusing on within-sequence performance. The results highlight that CNN-based full-reference models, such as LPIPS, DISTS, and CVQA-FR show significantly higher correlation coefficients than both conventional full- as well as the tested no-reference models. Most overestimate the overly sharp results of SCST, with VMAF mainly failing due to spatial inconsistencies introduced by Starlight Mini. None of the tested video quality models reach sufficient accuracy so as to replace complementary subjective testing. The reference, degraded and upscaled videos, as well as the user ratings and model scores are made available with the paper at https://github.com/Telecommunication-Telemedia-Assessment/AVT-VQDB-UHD-1-VSR as open data.

</details>
